> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often forget the **self-discharge term** in Part (ii), leading to a final energy of 190 kWh and SOC of 95%. While the difference is small (0.25%), it shows up in the answer key, and KTU examiners specifically check for inclusion of the $\alpha$ term. Always explicitly state the value of $\alpha$ and compute $E_{sd}$ to secure full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Boards**

- ✅ **Power Balance Equation** is the master equation: $\dfrac{dE_{st}}{dt} = P_{in} - P_{out} - P_{loss}$

- ✅ **SOC** is bounded $[0\%, 100\%]$; **DOD** is its complement; $SOC + DOD = 1$

- ✅ **C-rate** = $\vert P \vert / E_{rated}$ (units $h^{-1}$); 1C = full discharge in 1 hour

- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** = $\eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$ (NOT their average, and NOT a simple sum)

- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Power INTO storage is positive; power OUT is negative; losses are always positive

- ✅ **Self-discharge** is modeled as $P_{sd} = \alpha E_{st}$ where $\alpha$ is in $h^{-1}$

- ✅ **Three Modes:** Charging ($P_{in}>0$, $P_{out}=0$), Discharging ($P_{in}=0$, $P_{out}>0$), Idle (only $P_{sd}$ acts)

- ✅ **Energy balance over a complete cycle** gives: $\oint \frac{dE}{dt} dt = 0$, hence total input energy = total output energy + total losses

- ✅ **SOC at time $t$** is computed by integrating the power balance: $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{E_{max}} \int_0^t (P_{in} - P_{out} - P_{loss}) d\tau$

- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J = 3600 kJ; $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600$ J

- ✅ **SOH** (State of Health) is the ratio of current maximum capacity to original rated capacity — it degrades over cycles

- ✅ **Energy density** (Wh/kg) vs **Power density** (W/kg) — the Ragone plot compares them across technologies

- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Using $\eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$ for **partial cycles** — never use the shortcut unless it is a complete cycle

- ✅ **Exam Tip:** Always state the assumed initial SOC (commonly 50% or 100%) before solving time-dependent problems

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical model of storage system: modelling of power transformation system (PTS) - Central store (CS) and charge - discharge control system (CDCS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical model of storage system: modelling of power transformation system (PTS) - Central store (CS) and charge - discharge control system (CDCS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of the Energy Storage System (ESS) Architecture

An **Energy Storage System (ESS)** is mathematically modeled as an interconnected three-block architecture consisting of a **Central Store (CS)**, a **Power Transformation System (PTS)**, and a **Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS)**. This abstraction (originally formalized in the KTU-aligned energy storage literature and used widely in grid-tied storage modelling) decouples the *energy-holding capacity* of a device from the *power electronics that deliver energy* and the *control logic that decides the direction of energy flow*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The mathematical model of any storage system can be represented by three coupled functional blocks:
> 1. **Central Store (CS)** – the bulk energy reservoir (chemical, mechanical, thermal, or electrical).
> 2. **Power Transformation System (PTS)** – the bidirectional power-conditioning interface linking the CS to the external load/source.
> 3. **Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS)** – the supervisory controller that commands PTS operating mode and magnitude.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Domestic Overhead Water Tank

Imagine a household **overhead water tank** supplying your taps:
- The **tank** is the **Central Store (CS)** — it physically *holds* the water (energy) at a particular level (state of charge).
- The **pump + pipe network** that pressurises and pushes water up/down is the **Power Transformation System (PTS)** — it *converts* electrical energy into hydraulic power (and suffers some friction loss, analogous to efficiency $\eta_{PTS}$).
- The **float valve + switch** that decides *when* to fill the tank and *when* to draw from it is the **Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS)** — it *commands* the pump based on the tank's water level (analogous to SOC feedback).

> [!NOTE]
> The same three-block structure applies universally — to a Li-ion battery pack, a pumped-hydro reservoir, a flywheel, a supercapacitor bank, or a hydrogen storage vessel. The *physics* inside the CS changes, but the *mathematical interface* to the external grid is identical.

### 1.3 Role of Each Block in the KTU Reference Framework

| Block | Function | Standard Physical Quantity | Governing Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CS** | Holds bulk energy | Energy stored $E_{CS}$ (Wh or J) | State of Charge (SOC) |
| **PTS** | Bidirectional power conversion | Power $P_{PTS}$ (W) | Conversion efficiency $\eta_{PTS}$ |
| **CDCS** | Issues charge/discharge commands | Control signal $u(t)$ (0 to 1) | Reference current/power setpoint |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorise:** $E_{rated}$ in **kWh**, $P_{rated}$ in **kW**, $SOC \in [0,1]$ (dimensionless), and $\eta \in [0,1]$ (round-trip efficiency typically **0.7 – 0.95** for modern systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-by-Block Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 The Central Store (CS)

The CS is the **energy reservoir** of the system. It is treated as a single lumped state variable — the *State of Charge* (SOC).

**Why it exists:** Real storage media (batteries, flywheels, tanks) cannot be reduced to a single resistor or capacitor for grid studies. Aggregating them into a "central store" with a SOC variable keeps the model tractable while preserving the essential energy-balance physics.

**How it is modelled:** A discrete-time (or continuous-time) integrator whose input is the *net power accepted by the CS* and whose output is the *current energy content* $E_{CS}(t)$.

- The SOC is bounded: $0 \le SOC(t) \le 1$.
- Self-discharge losses are modelled as a leak term $k_{sd}$.
- The maximum storable energy $E_{max}$ is a fixed system constant.

#### 2.1.2 The Power Transformation System (PTS)

The PTS is the **bidirectional power-electronics interface** that conditions the electrical form of energy between the CS terminals and the external AC/DC bus.

**Why it exists:** A battery delivers DC at a varying terminal voltage; the grid delivers fixed-frequency AC. Power electronic converters (AC/DC, DC/DC, DC/AC) handle this translation, but every conversion incurs loss.

**How it is modelled:** As a **lossy gain block** with direction-dependent efficiency:
- Charging path: $\eta_{ch}$ — typically **0.90 – 0.97** for a modern bidirectional inverter.
- Discharging path: $\eta_{dis}$ — typically **0.90 – 0.97**.
- The round-trip efficiency is $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis}$.

#### 2.1.3 The Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS)

The CDCS is the **supervisory controller** (usually implemented in firmware, a PLC, or a digital signal processor).

**Why it exists:** A storage device on its own does not know *when* to charge or *when* to discharge. The CDCS takes high-level objectives (e.g., "peak shave between 6 PM and 9 PM", "smooth PV ramp", "regulate frequency") and translates them into a real-time power command $P_{PTS}^{ref}(t)$.

**How it is modelled:** As a function block of the form

$$u(t) = f\bigl(SOC(t),\, P_{grid}^{ref}(t),\, \text{prices},\, \text{forecast error}, \dots\bigr)$$

where $u(t) \in [-1,\,1]$ is the normalised power command and $f(\cdot)$ is typically a PI/PID controller, a rule-based scheduler, or a model-predictive controller (MPC).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** you will need to solve Module 1 problems on PTS / CS / CDCS modelling. Internalise these.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SOC definition | $SOC(t) = E_{CS}(t) / E_{max}$ | Dimensionless, range $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | CS energy dynamics (discrete) | $E_{CS}(k+1) = E_{CS}(k) + P_{CS}(k)\,\Delta t$ | Energy in Wh or kWh |
| 3 | CS energy dynamics (continuous) | $\dfrac{dE_{CS}}{dt} = P_{CS}(t) - k_{sd}\,E_{CS}(t)$ | Self-discharge constant $k_{sd}$ in $s^{-1}$ |
| 4 | Power flow: PTS → CS (charging) | $P_{CS}(t) = \eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}(t)$ | $P_{PTS}>0$ during charge |
| 5 | Power flow: CS → PTS (discharging) | $P_{CS}(t) = P_{PTS}(t) / \eta_{dis}$ | $P_{PTS}>0$ during discharge (outflow convention) |
| 6 | Net power at PCC | $P_{grid}(t) = P_{load}(t) - P_{gen}(t) - P_{PTS}(t)$ | Sign convention: +ve = import |
| 7 | CDCS normalised command | $u(t) \in [-1,\,1]$ | $-1$=full charge, $+1$=full discharge (KTU convention) |
| 8 | Reference power to PTS | $P_{PTS}^{ref}(t) = u(t) \cdot P_{rated}$ | $P_{rated}$ is the PTS nameplate rating |
| 9 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | SOC bounds | $0 \le SOC(t) \le 1$ | Hard physical constraint |
| 11 | Power bounds | $-P_{rated} \le P_{PTS}(t) \le +P_{rated}$ | Hard physical constraint |
| 12 | Maximum discharge time | $t_{dis,max} = E_{max}\,\eta_{dis} / P_{rated}$ | Hours, with $E$ in kWh, $P$ in kW |
| 13 | Maximum charge time | $t_{ch,max} = E_{max} / (P_{rated}\,\eta_{ch})$ | Hours |
| 14 | Energy throughput in interval $[t_0, t_1]$ | $E_{thru} = \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t_1} \lvert P_{PTS}(t) \rvert\,dt$ | Cumulative Wh handled by PTS |
| 15 | CDCS tracking error (PI controller) | $e(t) = P_{grid}^{ref}(t) - P_{grid}(t)$ | Error fed to PI regulator |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign-Convention Pitfall:** Many KTU students lose marks by mixing charge/discharge sign conventions. The *KTU 2024 convention* used throughout this module is: **$P_{PTS}>0$ means the system is delivering power to the external grid (discharging), and $P_{PTS}<0$ means absorbing power from the grid (charging).** Stick to this *consistently* in your answer scripts.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This CS–PTS–CDCS modelling framework is the **de-facto standard** in:
- **Utility-scale BESS (Battery Energy Storage System)** dispatch software (e.g., Tesla Autobidder, Fluence Mosaic).
- **Microgrid EMS (Energy Management Systems)** used by Siemens, ABB, Schneider.
- **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** onboard control firmware.
- **Pumped-storage hydro** scheduling at the national-grid level.

Understanding this block decomposition is the prerequisite for Module 2 (battery chemistries) and Module 3 (hybrid storage systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: State of Charge Dynamics of the Central Store

We start from the **first law of thermodynamics** (energy conservation) applied to the lumped CS.

**Step 1 — Define the state variable.**
The amount of energy remaining in the CS at time $t$ is $E_{CS}(t)$. The maximum it can hold is the nameplate capacity $E_{max}$.

$$E_{CS}(t) = SOC(t) \cdot E_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Write the power balance at the CS terminals.**
The rate of change of energy in the CS equals the net power flowing *into* the CS minus any parasitic losses (self-discharge).

$$\frac{dE_{CS}}{dt} = P_{in}(t) - P_{loss}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Express the loss term as proportional to stored energy.**
Self-discharge is modelled as a first-order leak (valid for batteries, thermal stores, and supercapacitors over short horizons):

$$P_{loss}(t) = k_{sd} \cdot E_{CS}(t)$$

where $k_{sd}$ has units of $s^{-1}$ (e.g., $k_{sd} = 10^{-5}\,s^{-1}$ for a quality Li-ion cell).

**Step 4 — Substitute the inflow from the PTS.**
When charging, power flows from PTS to CS, gaining by $\eta_{ch}$. When discharging, power leaves CS to PTS, losing by $1/\eta_{dis}$. We unify this with a single sign-convention:

$$P_{in}(t) = \begin{cases} \eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}(t), & P_{PTS}(t) < 0 \;\;(\text{charge}) \\[4pt] P_{PTS}(t) / \eta_{dis}, & P_{PTS}(t) > 0 \;\;(\text{discharge}) \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get the continuous-time CS model.**

$$\frac{dE_{CS}}{dt} = \eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}^{-}(t) + \frac{P_{PTS}^{+}(t)}{\eta_{dis}} - k_{sd}\,E_{CS}(t)$$

where $P_{PTS}^{+}$ and $P_{PTS}^{-}$ are the positive and negative parts of $P_{PTS}$.

**Step 6 — Discretise using forward Euler with time step $\Delta t$.**
This is the form KTU examiners expect for solved numerical problems.

$$E_{CS}(k+1) = E_{CS}(k) + \left[\eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}^{-}(k) + \frac{P_{PTS}^{+}(k)}{\eta_{dis}} - k_{sd}\,E_{CS}(k)\right]\Delta t$$

**Step 7 — Convert to the SOC variable (divide both sides by $E_{max}$).**

$$SOC(k+1) = SOC(k) + \frac{\Delta t}{E_{max}}\left[\eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}^{-}(k) + \frac{P_{PTS}^{+}(k)}{\eta_{dis}} - k_{sd}\,E_{max}\,SOC(k)\right]$$

This is the **final working equation** for SOC evolution in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: PTS Power-Conversion Gain Model

**Step 1 — Define the PTS input and output.**
Input: electrical power at the external bus $P_{bus}$ (W). Output: power at CS terminals $P_{CS}$.

**Step 2 — Assume a direction-dependent efficiency.**

$$P_{CS} = \begin{cases} \eta_{ch}\,P_{bus}, & \text{charging mode} \\[4pt] P_{bus} / \eta_{dis}, & \text{discharging mode} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Combine using a mode indicator $\sigma \in \{-1,\,+1\}$.**

$$P_{CS} = \left[\eta_{ch}\,\mathbb{1}(\sigma = -1) + \frac{1}{\eta_{dis}}\,\mathbb{1}(\sigma = +1)\right] \cdot P_{bus}$$

**Step 4 — Define the effective directional gain.**

$$G(\sigma) = \begin{cases} \eta_{ch}, & \sigma = -1 \\[4pt] 1/\eta_{dis}, & \sigma = +1 \end{cases}$$

So compactly: $\;P_{CS} = G(\sigma)\cdot P_{bus}$. The CDCS sets $\sigma$ each sampling instant.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: CDCS PI Control Law

**Step 1 — Define the grid-side power error.**
The CDCS aims to make the net power at the point of common coupling (PCC) follow a reference:

$$e(t) = P_{PCC}^{ref}(t) - P_{PCC}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Write the discrete PI update.**
A proportional-integral regulator generates the normalised command $u(k) \in [-1,1]$:

$$u(k) = K_p\,e(k) + K_i\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)\,\Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Saturate to respect the physical limits.**

$$u(k) = \text{clip}\bigl(u_{raw}(k),\, -1,\, +1\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Scale to the PTS rating.**

$$P_{PTS}^{ref}(k) = u(k)\cdot P_{rated}$$

**Step 5 — Anti-windup note (KTU 2024 advanced topic).**
If $u_{raw}$ saturates, the integrator must stop accumulating error to prevent "integrator wind-up":

$$\text{if } \bigl(u_{raw} = u_{sat} \bigr) \text{ and } \bigl(e\cdot u_{sat} > 0\bigr),\;\text{then } \sum e \leftarrow \sum e_{prev}.$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Exam Style)

**Given:**
- $E_{max} = 100$ kWh, $P_{rated} = 50$ kW
- $\eta_{ch} = 0.95$, $\eta_{dis} = 0.95$, $k_{sd} = 0$
- Initial SOC(0) = 0.50
- For $k = 0$ to $3$, the CDCS issues $P_{PTS} = -30, -20, +15, +40$ kW (negative = charge)
- $\Delta t = 1$ h

**Find:** SOC at the end of hour 3.

**Solution (writing out every step as the examiner wants):**

Hour 0 → 1: $P_{PTS} = -30$ kW (charging).

$$E_{CS}(1) = 100 \cdot 0.50 + 0.95 \cdot (-30) \cdot 1 = 50 - 28.5 = 21.5 \text{ kWh}$$

$$SOC(1) = 21.5 / 100 = 0.215$$

Hour 1 → 2: $P_{PTS} = -20$ kW (charging).

$$E_{CS}(2) = 21.5 + 0.95 \cdot (-20) \cdot 1 = 21.5 - 19.0 = 2.5 \text{ kWh}$$

$$SOC(2) = 2.5 / 100 = 0.025$$

Hour 2 → 3: $P_{PTS} = +15$ kW (discharging).

$$E_{CS}(3) = 2.5 + \frac{15}{0.95} \cdot 1 = 2.5 + 15.789 = 18.289 \text{ kWh}$$

$$SOC(3) = 18.289 / 100 \approx 0.183$$

**Final Answer:** $\mathbf{SOC(3) \approx 0.183}$ or **18.3 %**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Equation

| If the question says… | Use this equation set |
|---|---|
| "compute SOC after $n$ hours" | Eqs. 2 + 1 (discrete CS dynamics) |
| "find round-trip efficiency" | Eq. 9 |
| "PTS delivers X kW to grid" | Eq. 4 inverted: $P_{PTS} = P_{grid}\cdot\eta_{dis}$? No — use Eq. 5 |
| "CDCS tracks a reference" | Eq. 15 + Section 3.3 |
| "maximum backup time" | Eq. 12 |
| "size the storage for N hours of autonomy" | $E_{max} = N \cdot P_{load} / (\eta_{dis})$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The CS / PTS / CDCS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[External Source: Grid or PV] -->|P_bus_in| ptsA
    ptsA[PTS: Power Transformation System] -->|P_ch: eta_ch| csA
    csA[CS: Central Store. Holds energy E_CS] -->|P_dis: 1/eta_dis| ptsA
    ptsA -->|P_grid_out| loadA[External Load]
    socSense[SOC Sensor on CS] -->|SOC feedback| cdcsA
    cdcsA[CDCS: Charge Discharge Control System] -->|mode sigma and power ref u| ptsA
    refA[Reference: grid schedule, price, SoC setpoint] --> cdcsA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Flow with Measured Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph measLoop[Measurement Layer]
      v1[SOC: state of charge] --> ctrlCore
      v2[P_PCC: power at common coupling] --> ctrlCore
      v3[I_rms: converter current] --> prot
    end
    subgraph ctrlCore[CDCS Core Controller]
      pi[PI Regulator: Kp and Ki] --> sat[Saturation block: clip to -1 and +1]
    end
    subgraph convLayer[PTS Power Stage]
      conv[Bidirectional AC DC or DC DC Converter] --> lpf[LC Filter]
    end
    subgraph storeLayer[CS Central Store]
      batt[Storage medium: battery or flywheel or tank]
    end
    sat -->|P_ref in kW| conv
    lpf --> batt
    batt --> v1
    prot[Protection: overcurrent and overvoltage] --> conv
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Machine — CDCS Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: SOC below SOC_min
    Idle --> Discharging: SOC above SOC_max
    Charging --> Idle: SOC reached SOC_target_high
    Discharging --> Idle: SOC reached SOC_target_low
    Charging --> Fault: PTS overcurrent
    Discharging --> Fault: PTS overcurrent
    Fault --> Idle: Reset command from operator
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Dispatch Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Grid Operator
    participant CDCS as CDCS
    participant PTS as PTS
    participant CS as Central Store
    Op->>CDCS: Send P_ref and mode at t = 0
    CDCS->>CDCS: Compute u from PI on error
    CDCS->>PTS: Issue P_PTS_ref and sigma
    PTS->>CS: Convert and deliver P_CS with efficiency eta
    CS-->>CDCS: Feedback SOC
    CDCS->>Op: Acknowledge and log kWh throughput
    Note over CS,PTS: Cycle repeats every Delta_t seconds
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]**
*Define the three blocks of the energy storage system mathematical model. State one physical realisation of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Central Store (CS):** Lumped element that holds energy $E_{CS}$, characterised by SOC = $E_{CS}/E_{max}$. Realisation: battery cell, flywheel rotor, pumped-hydro upper reservoir, thermal mass.
2. **Power Transformation System (PTS):** Bidirectional power-electronics interface with charging efficiency $\eta_{ch}$ and discharging efficiency $\eta_{dis}$. Realisation: IGBT-based AC/DC inverter, DC/DC chopper.
3. **Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS):** Supervisory controller issuing a normalised command $u(t) \in [-1,1]$ that sets PTS reference power $P_{PTS}^{ref} = u \cdot P_{rated}$. Realisation: PI controller in DSP, MPC in EMS, rule-based scheduler in PLC.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
*Write the discrete-time state-of-charge update equation for the Central Store, including self-discharge and direction-dependent PTS efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$SOC(k+1) = SOC(k) + \frac{\Delta t}{E_{max}}\left[\eta_{ch}\,P_{PTS}^{-}(k) + \frac{P_{PTS}^{+}(k)}{\eta_{dis}} - k_{sd}\,E_{max}\,SOC(k)\right]$$

where $P_{PTS}^{+}=\max(P_{PTS},0)$, $P_{PTS}^{-}=\min(P_{PTS},0)$, $\eta_{ch}$ is the charge efficiency, $\eta_{dis}$ is the discharge efficiency, and $k_{sd}$ is the self-discharge constant.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive, starting from the first law of thermodynamics, the continuous-time and discrete-time SOC dynamics of the Central Store, clearly stating the role of the PTS efficiency in each mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A grid-tied BESS has $E_{max}=200$ kWh, $P_{rated}=100$ kW, $\eta_{ch}=\eta_{dis}=0.92$, and $k_{sd}=0$. At $t=0$, SOC = 0.80. The CDCS issues the following hourly power command sequence: $-50$ kW, $-30$ kW, $+40$ kW, $+60$ kW, $-20$ kW (negative = charge). With $\Delta t = 1$ h, compute and tabulate $E_{CS}(k)$ and $SOC(k)$ for $k=0$ to $5$. Comment on whether any constraint is violated. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the CS state as $E_{CS}(t)$ and define $SOC = E_{CS}/E_{max}$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the power balance $\frac{dE_{CS}}{dt} = P_{in} - P_{loss}$ with $P_{loss} = k_{sd} E_{CS}$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Express $P_{in}$ in terms of PTS power $P_{PTS}$ with direction-dependent efficiency:
- Charge: $P_{in} = \eta_{ch} P_{PTS}$ (since $P_{PTS}<0$ in our convention)
- Discharge: $P_{in} = P_{PTS}/\eta_{dis}$ (since $P_{PTS}>0$)

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Combine and discretise via forward Euler to obtain the discrete SOC update equation (see Section 3.1, Step 7).

**Step 5 (1 mark):** State bounds: $0 \le SOC \le 1$ and $-P_{rated} \le P_{PTS} \le P_{rated}$.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

| $k$ | $P_{PTS}$ (kW) | Mode | $E_{CS}$ (kWh) | $SOC$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | — | 160.000 | 0.800 |
| 1 | −50 | Charge | $160 + 0.92 \cdot (-50) \cdot 1 = 114.000$ | 0.570 |
| 2 | −30 | Charge | $114 + 0.92 \cdot (-30) \cdot 1 = 86.400$ | 0.432 |
| 3 | +40 | Discharge | $86.4 + 40/0.92 = 129.878$ | 0.649 |
| 4 | +60 | Discharge | $129.878 + 60/0.92 = 195.095$ | 0.975 |
| 5 | −20 | Charge | $195.095 + 0.92 \cdot (-20) = 176.695$ | 0.883 |

**Constraint check (1 mark):** SOC stays in $[0,1]$ throughout ✓; $|P_{PTS}| \le 100$ kW throughout ✓. **No constraint violated.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (a)**
> A common 2-mark loss happens when students **forget the self-discharge term $k_{sd}\,E_{CS}$** in the continuous-time derivation. The KTU model answer key *always* expects the leak term. If $k_{sd}=0$ is given, you should still *write the term* and *set it to zero explicitly* — that earns you full marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the Charge–Discharge Control System (CDCS) in the storage system model. Show how the normalised command $u \in [-1,1]$ is generated and how it is converted into a PTS power reference. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A microgrid has a 500 kWh CS with $P_{rated}=250$ kW, $\eta_{ch}=0.94$, $\eta_{dis}=0.96$. The CDCS uses a PI regulator with $K_p=0.6$ and $K_i=0.05\,s^{-1}$ to track a PCC reference $P_{PCC}^{ref}=100$ kW. If the current measured PCC power is $P_{PCC}=60$ kW, compute the raw and saturated control output $u$ after one PI update at $\Delta t = 1$ s, and hence find $P_{PTS}^{ref}$. The integrator state is zero at the start. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Block-diagram + control-law explanation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks)** Draw the block diagram: CDCS receives SOC feedback, $P_{PCC}^{ref}$, and measurement $P_{PCC}$. It computes the error $e = P_{PCC}^{ref} - P_{PCC}$, feeds it through a PI regulator, and produces a raw scalar $u_{raw} \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **(2 marks)** Show the saturation: $u = \text{clip}(u_{raw}, -1, +1)$.
- **(2 marks)** Show the scaling: $P_{PTS}^{ref} = u \cdot P_{rated}$, and that this command plus a mode bit $\sigma$ is sent to the PTS.
- **(1 mark)** Mention the anti-windup logic: if $u_{raw}$ is saturated and the error is driving further saturation, freeze the integrator state.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

Error:
$$e(0) = 100 - 60 = 40 \text{ kW}$$

Proportional term:
$$K_p \cdot e = 0.6 \cdot 40 = 24.0$$

Integral term (integrator was zero):
$$K_i \cdot e \cdot \Delta t = 0.05 \cdot 40 \cdot 1 = 2.0$$

Raw output:
$$u_{raw} = 24.0 + 2.0 = 26.0$$

Saturation:
$$u = \text{clip}(26.0,\,-1,\,+1) = +1.0$$

Final PTS reference:
$$P_{PTS}^{ref} = u \cdot P_{rated} = 1.0 \cdot 250 = +250 \text{ kW (full discharge)}$$

**Anti-windup note (1 mark):** Since $u_{raw}=26 \gg 1$ and $e>0$ would push $u_{raw}$ further up, the integrator must be frozen at $I=2.0$ on subsequent steps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part B (b)**
> Students frequently forget to **apply the saturation** and report $P_{PTS}^{ref} = 26 \cdot 250 = 6500$ kW. That is physically impossible (it exceeds the nameplate rating) and is treated as a **conceptual error**, costing **2 of the 7 marks** in the model answer key.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1**

- The storage system is modelled as **three coupled blocks**: **CS + PTS + CDCS**.
- **CS** holds $E_{CS}$ in kWh; its state is $SOC = E_{CS}/E_{max} \in [0,1]$.
- **PTS** is a lossy gain: charge gain $\eta_{ch}$, discharge gain $1/\eta_{dis}$.
- **CDCS** outputs a bounded command $u \in [-1, +1]$ that scales to $P_{PTS}^{ref} = u \cdot P_{rated}$.
- The **discrete-time SOC update** is the central KTU equation — memorise it verbatim.
- **Round-trip efficiency** is the product $\eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis}$ — typical Li-ion value **0.85 – 0.90**.
- **Sign convention** (KTU): $P_{PTS}>0$ ⇒ discharge to grid, $P_{PTS}<0$ ⇒ charge from grid.
- The CDCS is typically a **PI or MPC** controller, with **anti-windup** for the integral path.
- The **maximum backup time** at rated power is $t_{dis,max} = E_{max} \cdot \eta_{dis} / P_{rated}$.
- The CS dynamics reduce to a **first-order integrator with a leak term** in continuous time.
- Numerical problems always expect: **tabular work** + **constraint check** at the end.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OEEET723 / KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Econometric model of storage system. Thermal energy: General considerations - Storage media - Containment - Thermal energy storage in a power plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Econometric model of storage system. Thermal energy: General considerations - Storage media - Containment - Thermal energy storage in a power plant # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Econometric Model of Storage System ### Formal Definition An **econometric model of an energy storage system** is a mathematical-statistical framework that quantifies the relationship between the capital cost, operational cost, performance parameters, and lifecycle economics of a storage technology as a function of its rated energy capacity (E in MWh) and rated power (P in MW). It is used to forecast costs, scale systems, and evaluate financial feasibility of energy storage projects. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The econometric model is a *power-law cost model* that predicts the total installed cost of a storage system based on its rated capacity, often expressed in the form: > **C(E) = α · E^β** > where β (typically 0.6–0.9) reflects the economy of scale. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of buying water in bottles. A single 1-litre bottle might cost ₹20, but a 20-litre can costs ₹150 — not ₹400. The *unit cost drops* as you buy more. An econometric model of storage works exactly like this: doubling the capacity of a battery or thermal tank does **not** double the cost — it increases by a smaller factor. The model captures this "bulk discount" mathematically. ### Key Economic Metrics (KTU Board Favourites) - **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS):** ₹/MWh — the all-in per-unit cost of stored energy delivered. - **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX):** ₹/kW or ₹/kWh — upfront installed cost. - **Operating Expenditure (OPEX):** ₹/kW-yr or ₹/MWh — recurring annual cost. - **Round-Trip Efficiency (η_RT):** Dimensionless ratio of energy out to energy in. - **Cycle Life (N):** Number of full charge-discharge cycles before end-of-life. - **Self-Discharge Rate:** %/day or %/month — energy lost when idle. --- ## 1.2 Thermal Energy Storage (TES): General Considerations ### Formal Definition **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** is the temporary storage of thermal energy (heat or cold) in a storage medium for later use in heating, cooling, or power generation applications. It addresses the temporal mismatch between thermal energy supply and demand. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a thermos flask. You pour hot tea in at 7 AM, and even at noon it is still drinkable. A TES system is essentially a *giant, engineered thermos* that holds heat (or cold) until needed — bridging the gap between "when the sun shines" and "when electricity is demanded." ### The Three Pillars of TES > [!NOTE] > **KTU Core Classification (must memorize for Part A):** > 1. **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Energy stored by raising the temperature of a material. Example: water tanks, molten salts, rocks, concrete. > 2. **Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Energy stored during a phase change (solid↔liquid, e.g., melting/ freezing of PCM). Example: paraffin wax, salt hydrates. > 3. **Thermochemical Storage (TCS):** Energy stored via reversible chemical reactions. Example: metal hydrides, metal oxides, ammonia synthesis/decomposition. ### Why TES Matters in Power Plants - Decouples energy collection from power generation. - Enables **dispatchable solar thermal power** (e.g., Gemasolar, Spain — runs 24/7). - Reduces curtailment of waste heat in industrial processes. - Peak shaving in district heating/cooling systems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Econometric Cost Models of Storage ### 2.1.1 Power-Law (Economies of Scale) Model The most widely cited econometric model assumes cost scales as a power of capacity: $$C(E) = \alpha \cdot E^{\beta}$$ - **α (alpha):** Cost coefficient (currency units), depends on technology and year. - **β (beta):** Scaling exponent; β < 1 ⇒ economies of scale; β = 1 ⇒ linear scaling; β > 1 ⇒ diseconomies. - For most storage technologies, **0.6 ≤ β ≤ 0.9**. ### 2.1.2 Two-Variable Power-Law Model To independently capture power-rated (P) and energy-rated (E) costs: $$C(P, E) = \alpha_P \cdot P^{\beta_P} + \alpha_E \cdot E^{\beta_E}$$ - $\alpha_P$ scales the power block (inverter, BOP), and $\alpha_E$ scales the energy block (battery stack, tank). ### 2.1.3 Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS) The single most important economic metric: $$LCOS = \frac{CAPEX + \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{OPEX_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{E_{del,t}}{(1+r)^t}}$$ Where: - $r$ = discount rate (typically 8–12 % in India). - $N$ = project lifetime (years). - $E_{del,t}$ = annual energy delivered in year t (MWh). ### 2.1.4 Total Installed Cost Decomposition $$C_{total} = C_{power} + C_{energy} + C_{BOP} + C_{install}$$ - $C_{power}$: Power conversion system (PCS), inverter, transformer. - $C_{energy}$: Storage medium (battery cells / thermal mass / PCM). - $C_{BOP}$: Balance of plant — housing, controls, civil work. - $C_{install}$: Labour and commissioning. --- ## 2.2 Thermal Energy Storage — Theoretical Framework ### 2.2.1 Sensible Heat Storage Equation $$Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ - $Q_{sensible}$: Heat stored (J or kWh). - $m$: Mass of storage medium (kg). - $c_p$: Specific heat capacity (J/kg·K). - $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$: Temperature swing (K or °C). For volumetric systems, $m = \rho \cdot V$, giving: $$Q_{sensible} = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(J)}$$ ### 2.2.2 Latent Heat Storage Equation $$Q_{latent} = m \cdot \lambda$$ - $\lambda$: Latent heat of fusion / vaporization (J/kg). For combined sensible + latent (e.g., heating a PCM from solid to above its melting point): $$Q_{total} = m \left[ c_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_i) + \lambda + c_{p,l} \cdot (T_f - T_m) \right]$$ ### 2.2.3 Thermochemical Storage Equation $$Q_{TCS} = m_{reactant} \cdot \Delta H_{rxn} \cdot X_{conv}$$ - $\Delta H_{rxn}$: Enthalpy of reversible reaction (J/kg). - $X_{conv}$: Fractional conversion (0–1). ### 2.2.4 Thermal Energy Storage Capacity (Power Plant Scale) For a plant delivering $P_{out}$ (MW) for duration $t_{dis}$ (h): $$E_{TES} = P_{out} \cdot t_{dis} \quad \text{(MWh)}$$ Combined with sensible heat equation: $$V = \frac{E_{TES} \cdot 3.6 \times 10^6}{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)}$$ (Conversion: 1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^9$ J; divide by $10^6$ if using kJ.) --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Application | |---|---------|-------------|-------|--------------------------| | 1 | $C = \alpha E^{\beta}$ | Power-law cost model | ₹, \$ | Cost scaling question (4–7 marks) | | 2 | $LCOS = \dfrac{CAPEX + \sum OPEX/(1+r)^t}{\sum E_{del}/(1+r)^t}$ | Levelized cost | ₹/MWh | Numerical on cost comparison | | 3 | $Q = mc_p\Delta T$ | Sensible heat | J | Common Part B derivation | | 4 | $Q = m\lambda$ | Latent heat | J | PCM question | | 5 | $Q = m[c_{p,s}\Delta T_s + \lambda + c_{p,l}\Delta T_l]$ | Combined SHS+LHS | J | Multi-step numerical | | 6 | $V = \dfrac{E_{TES}\cdot 3.6\times10^6}{\rho c_p \Delta T}$ | Tank volume sizing | m³ | Power plant TES design | | 7 | $E_{TES} = P_{out}\cdot t_{dis}$ | Stored energy | MWh | Sizing for hours of storage | | 8 | $X_{conv} = \dfrac{m_{reacted}}{m_{initial}}$ | TCS conversion | – | Thermochemical problem | | 9 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | Comparison of technologies | | 10 | $C_{total} = C_P + C_E + C_{BOP} + C_{inst}$ | Total cost | ₹/kW, ₹/kWh | Cost decomposition | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the conversion:** 1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^9$ J = $3.6 \times 10^6$ kJ. This appears in **every** KTU numerical on thermal sizing. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **LCOS** is used by NITI Aayog, MNRE, and CEA in India to award storage tenders (e.g., SECI's 500 MWh battery tender). - **Sensible TES** powers the 110 MW Crescent Dunes (USA) and 50 MW NOOR III (Morocco) concentrated solar power plants. - **Molten salt TES** is the de-facto standard for parabolic trough and central receiver CSP plants. - **PCM TES** is being deployed in building HVAC (BEE-rated green buildings in India). - **Thermochemical TES** is at pilot stage (e.g., $\text{CaO}/\text{Ca(OH)}_2$ system at Fraunhofer). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power-Law Cost Model from First Principles ### Step 1: Define the system boundary Consider a storage technology with energy capacity $E$ (MWh). Empirically, the unit cost $\dfrac{C}{E}$ (₹/MWh) **decreases** as $E$ increases. This suggests a negative elasticity of cost with respect to size. ### Step 2: Express the elasticity Define cost elasticity $\beta$ as: $$\beta = \frac{d(\ln C)}{d(\ln E)} = \text{constant}$$ This assumption (constant elasticity) is the cornerstone of the power-law model. ### Step 3: Integrate the differential equation $$\int d(\ln C) = \beta \int d(\ln E)$$ $$\ln C = \beta \cdot \ln E + \ln \alpha$$ ### Step 4: Exponentiate both sides $$C = \alpha \cdot E^{\beta}$$ This is the final form. The constant $\alpha$ is determined by calibrating against at least two known data points: $$\alpha = \frac{C_1}{E_1^{\beta}} = \frac{C_2}{E_2^{\beta}}$$ ### Step 5: Worked Example (KTU-style) A 10 MWh battery costs ₹50 crore, and a 100 MWh battery of the same chemistry costs ₹300 crore. Find the scaling exponent $\beta$. **Solution:** $$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{E_2}{E_1}\right)^{\beta}$$ $$\frac{300}{50} = \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{\beta}$$ $$6 = 10^{\beta}$$ $$\beta = \frac{\log 6}{\log 10} = \frac{0.778}{1.000} = 0.778$$ Therefore $\alpha = \dfrac{50 \times 10^7}{10^{0.778}} = 5 \times 10^8 / 6.0 \approx 8.33 \times 10^7$ ₹/MWh. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: LCOS Simplified Form (Single-Year OPEX) For a project with constant annual OPEX and energy delivery, the geometric series collapses to: $$\sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}$$ Therefore: $$LCOS = \frac{CAPEX + OPEX \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}\right]}{E_{annual} \cdot \left[\dfrac{1 - (1+r)^{-N}}{r}\right]}$$ For $N \to \infty$ (perpetual asset, used for benchmarking): $$LCOS_{\infty} = \frac{CAPEX \cdot r + OPEX}{E_{annual}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensible Heat Storage Tank Sizing **Problem (KTU pattern):** A 50 MW solar thermal power plant needs to operate for 6 hours after sunset. The TES uses molten salt (Hitec XL: $c_p = 1.5$ kJ/kg·K, $\rho = 1990$ kg/m³) heated from 220 °C to 500 °C. Calculate the required salt volume and mass. ### Step 1: Compute total stored energy $$E_{TES} = P_{out} \cdot t_{dis} = 50 \text{ MW} \times 6 \text{ h} = 300 \text{ MWh}$$ ### Step 2: Convert to kJ $$E_{TES} = 300 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ kJ} = 1.08 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ}$$ ### Step 3: Apply sensible heat equation $$m = \frac{E_{TES}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{1.08 \times 10^9}{1.5 \times (500 - 220)}$$ $$m = \frac{1.08 \times 10^9}{420} = 2.571 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}$$ ### Step 4: Compute volume $$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{2.571 \times 10^6}{1990} \approx 1292 \text{ m}^3$$ ### Step 5: Sanity check - Volume of an Olympic swimming pool ≈ 2500 m³ → the salt tank is roughly **half a pool**, which is realistic for a 50 MW plant (matches NOOR III design). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Combined Sensible + Latent Storage **Problem:** A PCM with $T_m = 80$ °C, $\lambda = 250$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,s} = c_{p,l} = 2.0$ kJ/kg·K is heated from 30 °C to 100 °C. Find the total energy stored per kg. **Solution:** $$Q = 2.0 \cdot (80 - 30) + 250 + 2.0 \cdot (100 - 80)$$ $$Q = 100 + 250 + 40 = 390 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Note:** Latent heat dominates (64 % of total), illustrating why PCMs give high energy density in a narrow temperature window. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Technology Comparison (KTU Board Favourite) | Parameter | Li-ion Battery | Pumped Hydro | Molten Salt (TES) | PCM (Paraffin) | |-----------|----------------|--------------|-------------------|----------------| | Energy form | Electrical | Gravitational | Thermal (sensible) | Thermal (latent) | | Energy density (kJ/kg) | 540 (1.5 kWh) | 1 | ~750 | 200–250 | | Power density (W/kg) | 250–1000 | low | – | – | | Round-trip η | 85–95 % | 75–80 % | 60–80 % (incl. losses) | 70–90 % | | Self-discharge | 0.1–0.3 %/day | negligible | 1–3 %/day | low | | CAPEX (₹/kWh) | 12,000–18,000 | 5,000–8,000 | 3,000–5,000 | 8,000–15,000 | | Cycle life | 4,000–6,000 | >10,000 | >10,000 | 1,000–3,000 | | Best use | Short-duration, grid | Bulk, long-duration | CSP plants | Building HVAC | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid — Econometric Cost Modeling Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Storage Technology Selected] --> B[Define Rated Energy E MWh] B --> C[Define Rated Power P MW] C --> D[Select Cost Model: Power-Law or 2-Variable] D --> E[Calibrate alpha and beta
from historical data] E --> F[Compute CAPEX = alpha times E power beta] F --> G[Add OPEX and BOP] G --> H[Compute LCOS using discount rate r and life N] H --> I{LCOS below market threshold?} I -- Yes --> J[Project approved] I -- No --> K[Sensitivity Analysis: vary beta, r, eta_RT] K --> E ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid — Two-Tank Molten Salt TES in a CSP Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR Sun[Sun / Solar Field] --> SHeat[Heat Transfer Fluid HTF
heated to 565 C] SHeat --> ColdTank[Cold Salt Tank
290 C] ColdTank --> HX1[Heat Exchanger HX1
HTF to Cold Salt] HX1 --> SHeat HX1 --> HotSalt[Hot Molten Salt
565 C] HotSalt --> HotTank[Hot Salt Tank
Storage] HotTank --> HX2[Heat Exchanger HX2
Salt to Steam] HX2 --> Steam[Steam Generator
500 C / 100 bar] Steam --> Turbine[Rankine Turbine
Power Block] HX2 --> ColdSalt2[Cold Salt 290 C] ColdSalt2 --> ColdTank Turbine --> Grid[Electrical Grid MW] Grid --> Load[Power Demand Day Night] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid — TES Classification and Containment ```mermaid flowchart TD TES[Thermal Energy Storage TES] --> SHS[Sensible Heat] TES --> LHS[Latent Heat] TES --> TCS[Thermochemical] SHS --> S1[Water tanks] SHS --> S2[Molten salts] SHS --> S3[Rock pebble beds] SHS --> S4[Concrete modules] LHS --> L1[Organic PCMs: paraffin fatty acids] LHS --> L2[Inorganic PCMs: salt hydrates] LHS --> L3[Eutectic mixtures] TCS --> T1[CaO to CaOH2 cycle] TCS --> T2[MgH2 to Mg cycle] TCS --> T3[Ammonia synthesis decomposition] S1 --> Cont1[Containment: Steel concrete tanks] S2 --> Cont1 S3 --> Cont2[Containment: Insulated silos] S4 --> Cont3[Containment: Reinforced concrete modules] L1 --> Cont4[Containment: Encapsulated spheres in matrices] L2 --> Cont4 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid — Block Diagram: TES in a Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SF[Solar Field Subsystem] A1[Heliostats / Parabolic Troughs] end subgraph RC[Receiver and TES Subsystem] A1 --> B1[Solar Receiver 565 C] B1 --> B2[Hot Salt Storage Tank] B2 --> B3[Cold Salt Storage Tank] B3 --> B1 end subgraph PB[Power Block Subsystem] B2 --> C1[Steam Generator HX2] C1 --> C2[Turbine Generator] C2 --> C3[Condenser] C3 --> C4[Cooling Tower] end subgraph OUT[Output] C2 --> D1[Electrical Grid] end ``` > [!NOTE] > The above architecture is the **direct two-tank molten salt** configuration used at Andasol (Spain), NOOR III (Morocco), and the Indian SECI Phase-II 500 MW CSP tender projects. The CSP block is the *only* configuration where TES is dispatched at *grid scale* for **15+ hours**. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define the econometric model of an energy storage system. Mention any two key parameters used in it. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The econometric model of a storage system is a mathematical relationship that expresses the installed cost or LCOS of a storage technology as a function of its rated capacity (E) and/or power (P). (1 mark) - It is typically expressed in power-law form: $C = \alpha E^{\beta}$. (1 mark) - **Key parameters:** scaling exponent $\beta$ (economies of scale) and cost coefficient $\alpha$ (technology-specific reference cost). (1 mark) --- ### Q2. List the three main categories of thermal energy storage and give one example of a storage medium for each. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS):** Energy stored by raising temperature — Example: molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) or water. (1 mark) - **Latent Heat Storage (LHS):** Energy stored during phase change — Example: paraffin wax (PCM, $T_m \approx 60$–80 °C) or salt hydrates like Glauber's salt (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O). (1 mark) - **Thermochemical Storage (TCS):** Energy stored via reversible chemical reaction — Example: $\text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{heat}$. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **`(a)` Explain the econometric cost model of an energy storage system in detail, with the power-law formulation. Derive the scaling exponent β for a system where a 5 MWh installation costs ₹25 crore and a 50 MWh installation of the same technology costs ₹150 crore. Comment on the economy of scale. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the model (2 marks)** The econometric model relates the installed cost $C$ of a storage system to its rated energy capacity $E$ using a power-law relationship: $$C = \alpha \cdot E^{\beta}$$ where $\alpha$ is a cost coefficient (technology-specific) and $\beta$ is the scaling exponent. When $\beta < 1$, the system exhibits **economies of scale** — unit cost decreases as capacity grows. **Step 2 — Set up the ratio (2 marks)** $$\frac{C_2}{C_1} = \left(\frac{E_2}{E_1}\right)^{\beta}$$ $$\frac{150}{25} = \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^{\beta}$$ $$6 = 10^{\beta}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for β (2 marks)** $$\beta = \frac{\log 6}{\log 10} = 0.778$$ **Step 4 — Compute α (0.5 mark)** $$\alpha = \frac{25 \times 10^7}{5^{0.778}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^8}{3.31} = 7.55 \times 10^7 \text{ ₹/MWh}$$ **Step 5 — Comment (0.5 mark)** Since $\beta = 0.778 < 1$, the system shows **economies of scale** — a 10× capacity increase raises cost only ~6×, confirming bulk-discount behaviour. --- **`(b)` A concentrated solar power (CSP) plant of capacity 10 MW is to be supported by a two-tank molten salt TES. The hot salt is stored at 565 °C and the cold salt at 290 °C. The molten salt (Solar Salt, 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) has $c_p = 1.5$ kJ/kg·K and $\rho = 1870$ kg/m³. The plant must deliver rated power for 8 hours after sunset. Calculate (i) the total thermal energy to be stored in MWh, (ii) the mass of salt required, and (iii) the volume of each tank. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the stored energy (2 marks)** $$E_{TES} = P_{out} \times t_{dis} = 10 \text{ MW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 80 \text{ MWh}$$ Convert to kJ: $$E_{TES} = 80 \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 2.88 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the mass of salt (2 marks)** $$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275 \text{ °C (or K)}$$ $$m = \frac{E_{TES}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 275}$$ $$m = \frac{2.88 \times 10^8}{412.5} = 6.982 \times 10^5 \text{ kg} \approx 698.2 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the tank volume (2 marks)** $$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{6.982 \times 10^5}{1870} = 373.4 \text{ m}^3$$ Each of the two tanks (hot and cold) must hold the entire inventory of salt to enable thermosiphon-free flow: $$V_{each\,tank} = 373.4 \text{ m}^3$$ (Approximately 1.5 m × 8 m diameter × 8 m tall — typical industrial design.) **Step 4 — Verification (1 mark)** Check: 80 MWh / 373 m³ = 0.21 MWh/m³, consistent with literature values (0.15–0.25 MWh/m³) for two-tank molten salt TES. ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice* **`(a)` Describe the three modes of thermal energy storage: sensible, latent, and thermochemical. Compare their energy densities, advantages, and limitations in a tabular format. Which mode is most suited for a CSP plant, and why? (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Model Answer (7 marks):** - **Sensible Heat Storage (2 marks):** Stores energy by raising the temperature of a single-phase medium. Energy = $mc_p\Delta T$. Examples: water, molten salts, rocks, concrete. Pros: simple, low-cost, proven at scale. Cons: large volume, energy spread over a wide temperature range, self-discharge losses. - **Latent Heat Storage (2 marks):** Stores energy during a phase change, typically solid↔liquid. Energy = $m\lambda + mc_p\Delta T$. Examples: paraffin, salt hydrates, fatty acids. Pros: high energy density at nearly constant temperature, compact. Cons: low thermal conductivity, phase-segregation, costlier encapsulation. - **Thermochemical Storage (2 marks):** Stores energy by reversible endothermic/exothermic reactions. Energy = $m\Delta H_{rxn}X_{conv}$. Examples: $\text{CaO}/\text{Ca(OH)}_2$, $\text{MgH}_2/\text{Mg}$. Pros: highest energy density, near-zero losses during storage. Cons: complex reactor, slow kinetics, materials degradation. - **Best for CSP (1 mark):** **Sensible heat storage with molten salt** is most suited because (i) it operates in the CSP temperature window (290–565 °C), (ii) it is industrially mature (Andasol, NOOR III), (iii) it is non-flammable, and (iv) it can be pumped directly as HTF in newer designs. **Tabular comparison (built into marks):** | Feature | SHS | LHS | TCS | |---------|-----|-----|-----| | Energy density | Low (50–200 kJ/kg) | Medium (200–400 kJ/kg) | High (500–1500 kJ/kg) | | Storage duration | Hours–days | Hours | Days–months | | Maturity | TRL 9 (molten salt) | TRL 6–7 | TRL 4–5 | | Cost (₹/kWh_th) | 3,000–5,000 | 8,000–15,000 | 20,000+ | | Best fit | CSP, district heat | Building HVAC, electronics | Seasonal storage | --- **`(b)` Explain the concept of containment in thermal energy storage systems. Discuss suitable containment materials for high-temperature molten salt and for low-temperature PCM storage. What are the key design criteria for a TES containment vessel? (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`** **Model Answer (7 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Containment refers to the vessel, tank, or matrix that physically holds the storage medium, prevents leakage, and minimizes thermal losses to the surroundings. - **Containment for high-temperature molten salt (2.5 marks):** - Material: Austenitic stainless steel (SS 304, SS 316) for ≤565 °C; Inconel or ceramic-coated steel for higher temperatures. - Insulation: Mineral wool, calcium silicate, or vacuum-insulated panels to limit heat loss to <1–2 %/day. - Design: Hot tank at the top of hill (thermosiphon-assisted), cold tank at low elevation; pumps move salt between tanks. - **Containment for low-temperature PCM (2.5 marks):** - Material: Encapsulation in metal cans, polymer shells (HDPE, polypropylene), or macro/micro-encapsulated spheres in a matrix. - Used in building walls, ceiling tiles, and cold-chain packaging. - Must prevent leakage of melted PCM and allow repeated volume change during melting/freezing (expansion ~10 %). - **Design criteria (1 mark):** 1. Chemical compatibility with storage medium. 2. Thermal cycling resistance. 3. Low thermal conductivity (insulation). 4. Mechanical strength at operating temperature. 5. Corrosion resistance and leak-tightness. 6. Cost-effectiveness over lifetime. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks:** > 1. **Unit error in MWh → kJ conversion:** Forgetting that 1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ kJ (not $3.6 \times 10^3$) costs full marks in Part B numerics. Always write the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Using °C difference as K difference correctly** (numerically the same, but state "K" to satisfy the examiner). > 3. **Conflating SHS and LHS energy equations** — the latent term $m\lambda$ has *no* $\Delta T$ attached; students often incorrectly multiply $\lambda$ by temperature. > 4. **Forgetting to convert tonnes ↔ kg** in the volume calculation: $\rho$ is in kg/m³, so mass must be in kg. > 5. **Econometric model:** Students often write $C = \alpha E^{\beta}$ but forget to compute $\alpha$ explicitly — full 1 mark lost. > 6. **In containment questions:** Don't write "tank" alone — mention *material*, *insulation*, and *safety* for full credit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Econometric model** = power-law cost model $C = \alpha E^{\beta}$; $\beta < 1$ means economies of scale. - ✅ **LCOS** is the *single most important* economic metric — uses discount rate $r$ and lifetime $N$. - ✅ **Three TES modes:** Sensible ($mc_p\Delta T$), Latent ($m\lambda$), Thermochemical ($m\Delta H_{rxn}X_{conv}$). - ✅ **Molten salt (60/40 NaNO₃/KNO₃)** is the de-facto standard for CSP TES — $T_{hot}$ = 565 °C, $T_{cold}$ = 290 °C, $c_p$ ≈ 1.5 kJ/kg·K. - ✅ **PCM energy density** is highest near its melting point — small $\Delta T$ window, large storage. - ✅ **Containment for hot salt:** SS 304/316 + mineral wool/calcium silicate insulation; for PCM: encapsulated in metal or polymer shells. - ✅ **Design criteria for containment:** chemical compatibility, thermal cycling, low conductivity, mechanical strength, corrosion resistance. - ✅ **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^9$ J = $3.6 \times 10^6$ kJ. - ✅ **Key CSP example:** 50 MW NOOR III (Morocco) = 7.5 h TES, ~28,000 t of salt. - ✅ **Most common KTU numerical** = "calculate TES mass and volume for an N-MW plant over M hours using a given salt." Practice at least three variants before the exam. - ✅ **Comparison with battery:** Batteries store electricity; TES stores heat — but both are dispatched to the grid, and both share CAPEX/OPEX/LCOS frameworks. - ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** for TES is typically 60–80 % (lower than Li-ion 85–95 %) — a frequent viva question.

topics.title: Potential energy: Pumped hydro - Compressed Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Potential energy: Pumped hydro - Compressed Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Potential Energy Storage: The Macro-Scale Workhorse

**Potential Energy Storage (PES)** refers to a class of mechanical energy storage systems that store electrical energy by elevating a working mass (liquid or gas) against a gravitational or pressure potential field. When the energy is required, the elevated mass is allowed to fall or expand, driving a turbine/generator set to regenerate electricity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *"Potential energy storage systems store energy in the form of mechanical potential energy of a working fluid raised to a higher elevation (gravitational PES) or pressurised into an enclosed volume (pneumatic PES)."*

The two dominant commercial potential-energy technologies are:

1. **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** — Gravitational PES using water.
2. **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** — Pneumatic PES using pressurised air.

---

### 1.2 Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) — The "Water Battery"

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
Pumped Hydro Storage is a large-scale, grid-level mechanical energy storage technology in which surplus electrical energy (typically off-peak) is used to lift water from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir. During peak demand, the stored water is released through a hydraulic turbine to regenerate electricity.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a giant rechargeable battery built out of two lakes separated by a mountain. When electricity is cheap, the grid uses it to pump water "uphill" into the upper lake — *charging the battery*. When electricity is expensive, the water is allowed to flow downhill through a turbine — *discharging the battery*. The mountain's elevation is the "voltage" and the lake's volume is the "amp-hour capacity".

**Key Engineering Metrics:**

| Metric | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Round-trip efficiency | **70 % – 85 %** |
| Energy density (gravitational) | **~1 Wh / kg** (water × head) |
| Plant capacity | **100 MW – 3 000 MW** |
| Discharge duration | **6 – 24 hours** |
| Lifetime | **50 – 100 years** |

---

### 1.3 Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) — The "Air Battery"

**Definition (KTU Standard):**
Compressed Air Energy Storage is a potential-energy storage technology in which off-peak electrical energy drives a compressor train that pressurises ambient air into an underground cavern, lined rock cavern, or high-pressure vessel. During discharge, the compressed air is expanded through a turbo-expander to drive a generator.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of CAES as inflating a giant underground balloon with air. To *charge*, an electric motor compresses air and pushes it into a sealed cavern (salt cavern, depleted gas field, or aquifier). To *discharge*, a valve opens, the high-pressure air rushes out, and the kinetic/pressure energy spins a turbine. Unlike PHS, the "potential" here is *pressure potential* rather than gravitational potential.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Board-Favourite Question):**
> In **PHS**, the potential is **gravitational (mgh)**.
> In **CAES**, the potential is **pressure-volume (∫P dV)**.
> Both are forms of mechanical potential energy and are *dispatchable*, *long-duration*, and *geographically constrained*.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Potential Energy Storage

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Module 1 High-Yield Classification Tree:**

- **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)**
  - Pure (Off-Stream / Closed-Loop) PHS
  - Pump-Back (Daily Cycle) PHS
  - Seasonal PHS
- **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)**
  - Diabatic CAES (D-CAES) — Huntorf, McIntosh
  - Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES) — Heat captured and stored
  - Isothermal CAES (I-CAES) — Near-constant temperature compression
  - Advanced / Underwater CAES
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pumped Hydro Storage — Operational Logic

**Charging Mode (Pumping):**
1. Off-peak grid power energises the **motor-generator** acting as a motor.
2. The motor drives the **pump-turbine** (acting as a pump) in reverse.
3. Water is drawn from the **lower reservoir** and forced up the **penstock** to the **upper reservoir**.
4. Electrical energy is converted to **gravitational potential energy** of the elevated water mass.

**Discharging Mode (Generating):**
1. During peak demand, water is released from the **upper reservoir**.
2. The water descends through the **penstock**, gaining kinetic and pressure energy.
3. The **pump-turbine** (acting as a turbine) converts hydraulic energy to rotational mechanical energy.
4. The **motor-generator** acts as a generator, feeding electricity back to the grid.

**Key Sub-Systems (Architectural):**
- **Upper & Lower Reservoirs** (earth/rock-fill dams, concrete dams, or natural lakes).
- **Penstock** (high-pressure water conduit, typically steel-lined).
- **Pump-Turbine** (reversible Francis-type is most common).
- **Motor-Generator** (synchronous machine).
- **Surge Tank** (hydraulic transient protection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Head (H):** The vertical height difference between the upper and lower reservoirs, expressed in metres. Higher H ⇒ higher specific energy stored per unit volume of water.

---

### 2.2 Compressed Air Energy Storage — Operational Logic

**Charging Mode (Compression):**
1. Ambient air is drawn into a multi-stage **compressor train** (typically 8–12 stages).
2. The air is pressurised to **40 – 80 bar** (Huntorf) or higher.
3. In **D-CAES**, compression heat is rejected to the environment (energy lost).
4. In **A-CAES**, compression heat is captured in a **thermal energy storage (TES)** unit.
5. Pressurised air is stored in an **underground cavern** (salt cavern, aquifer, depleted gas field).

**Discharging Mode (Expansion):**
1. High-pressure air is drawn from the cavern.
2. Air is pre-heated (using stored thermal energy in A-CAES, or natural-gas combustor in D-CAES).
3. The heated, high-pressure air expands through a **turbo-expander** driving a generator.
4. Exhaust air is vented to atmosphere at near-ambient pressure.

**Cycle Thermodynamics (KTU Exam Focus):**
- **Isothermal compression/expansion:** Theoretical ideal — requires infinite heat exchange.
- **Adiabatic compression/expansion:** No heat transfer; air temperature rises/falls dramatically.
- **Polytropic process:** Practical compromise with exponent **n** (1 < n < γ for compression).
- **Diabatic (real D-CAES):** Heat rejected during compression; heat added via natural gas before expansion.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation a KTU student must memorise for Module 1 problems on PHS and CAES. Pay close attention to the SI units.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Symbol Meanings | SI Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gravitational PE stored | $E = \rho g V H$ | $\rho$ = water density, $g$ = gravity, $V$ = volume, $H$ = head | J (Joules) |
| 2 | Mass of water stored | $m = \rho V$ | Mass = density × volume | kg |
| 3 | Specific energy (PHS) | $e_{PHS} = g H$ | Energy per unit mass | J/kg |
| 4 | Volumetric energy density | $E/V = \rho g H$ | Energy per cubic metre of water | J/m³ |
| 5 | Hydraulic power (PHS) | $P = \rho g Q H \eta$ | $Q$ = flow rate, $\eta$ = turbine efficiency | W |
| 6 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{motor-gen}$ | Product of component efficiencies | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | Ideal gas law | $P V = n R T$ | $n$ = moles, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K | Pa·m³ = J |
| 8 | Polytropic relation | $P V^{n} = \text{const}$ | $n$ = polytropic index | — |
| 9 | Polytropic work | $W = \dfrac{P_{2}V_{2} - P_{1}V_{1}}{1 - n}$ | Work during compression / expansion | J |
| 10 | Isothermal compression work | $W_{iso} = P_{1} V_{1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)$ | $n=1$ limit | J |
| 11 | Adiabatic compression work | $W_{ad} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P_{1} V_{1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | $\gamma = C_{p}/C_{v} = 1.4$ for air | J |
| 12 | Adiabatic expansion work | $W_{exp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}\, P_{3} V_{3}\!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{4}}{P_{3}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$ | Indices 3,4 denote expansion inlet/outlet | J |
| 13 | Energy density of compressed air | $E_{CAES}/V = \dfrac{P}{\gamma - 1}$ | At adiabatic limit | J/m³ |
| 14 | CAES storage mass | $m = \dfrac{P V M}{R T}$ | $M$ = molar mass of air = 0.029 kg/mol | kg |
| 15 | Discharge time (CAES) | $t_{d} = \dfrac{V_{cavern}}{Q_{turbine}}$ | Cavern volume ÷ turbine mass flow | s |
| 16 | Round-trip efficiency (D-CAES) | $\eta_{D\text{-}CAES} \approx 0.45 - 0.55$ | Includes NG combustion credit | dimensionless |
| 17 | Round-trip efficiency (A-CAES) | $\eta_{A\text{-}CAES} \approx 0.60 - 0.70$ | Heat recovery retained | dimensionless |
| 18 | Round-trip efficiency (PHS) | $\eta_{PHS} \approx 0.75 - 0.85$ | Best of the three macro-storages | dimensionless |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where these systems matter in industry:**
> - **PHS** accounts for **>95 %** of all grid-scale energy storage worldwide (>160 GW installed).
> - **CAES** complements PHS where geography (no two reservoirs) prevents PHS deployment.
> - Both technologies are the **only commercially proven, multi-GW, multi-hour bulk storage** options.
> - They provide **inertia, frequency regulation, voltage support, and black-start capability** to the grid.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gravitational Potential Energy Stored in PHS

**Problem Statement (KTU typical):**
Derive an expression for the energy stored in a PHS system of head $H$ and reservoir volume $V$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Gravitational Potential Energy.**
The gravitational potential energy of an elemental mass $dm$ raised to a height $z$ above a reference datum is given by:

$$
dE = g\, z\, dm
$$

**Step 2 — Elemental Mass in a Reservoir.**
Consider an infinitesimally thin horizontal slice of water at height $z$ with cross-sectional area $A(z)$. The slice thickness is $dz$, hence:

$$
dm = \rho \, A(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into PE Expression.**

$$
dE = g \, z \, \rho \, A(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the Reservoir Height.**

$$
E = \int_{0}^{H} \rho \, g \, z \, A(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 5 — Assume Constant Cross-Section $A$ (cylindrical/conical approximation).**
If $A(z) = A$ is approximately uniform (often the case for engineered reservoirs):

$$
E = \rho \, g \, A \int_{0}^{H} z \, dz = \rho \, g \, A \left[\dfrac{z^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g A H^{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Express in Terms of Total Volume.**
Since the total stored volume $V = A \cdot H$, we have $A = V/H$. Substituting:

$$
E = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g \left(\dfrac{V}{H}\right) H^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho g V H
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on the ½ Factor:**
> When the *entire* reservoir volume $V$ is assumed to be uniformly raised to the *full* head $H$ (i.e., the water is treated as a point mass at height $H$), the formula reduces to the simpler form $E = \rho g V H$. **Both forms are accepted in KTU papers**, but the engineering-correct form for a *slab reservoir* is $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g V H$. State your assumption clearly in the answer.

**Step 7 — Accounting for Round-Trip Efficiency.**
The electrical energy recoverable from the grid is:

$$
E_{elec} = \eta_{RT} \, \rho \, g \, V \, H
$$

where $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{motor\text{-}gen}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Power Output of PHS

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Power Definition.**
Power is the time rate of energy discharge:

$$
P(t) = \dfrac{dE}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E = \rho g V H$.**
Holding $H$ constant during discharge (water level drops, but $H$ changes slowly):

$$
P(t) = \rho \, g \, H \, \dfrac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognise Volumetric Flow Rate.**
The volumetric flow rate is $Q = dV/dt$ (m³/s).

$$
P_{hydraulic}(t) = \rho \, g \, Q(t) \, H
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Turbine-Generator Efficiency.**

$$
\boxed{P_{electrical}(t) = \eta_{turb} \, \eta_{gen} \, \rho \, g \, Q(t) \, H}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Compressor Work for a Polytropic Process (CAES Foundation)

**Step 1 — Polytropic Process Definition.**
A polytropic process obeys:

$$
P V^{n} = C \quad (\text{constant})
$$

Therefore: $P = C V^{-n}$ and $V = (C/P)^{1/n}$.

**Step 2 — Boundary Conditions.**
At state 1 (intake): $P = P_{1}$, $V = V_{1}$. So $C = P_{1} V_{1}^{n} = P_{2} V_{2}^{n}$.

**Step 3 — Work Done by Compression (Boundary Work).**
For a closed-system quasi-static compression:

$$
W_{comp} = \int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} P \, dV
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P = P_{1} V_{1}^{n} V^{-n}$.**

$$
W_{comp} = P_{1} V_{1}^{n} \int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} V^{-n} \, dV
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$
\int_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} V^{-n} \, dV = \left[\dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}\right]_{V_{1}}^{V_{2}} = \dfrac{V_{2}^{1-n} - V_{1}^{1-n}}{1-n}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Back.**

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{P_{1} V_{1}^{n} V_{2}^{1-n} - P_{1} V_{1}^{n} V_{1}^{1-n}}{1-n} = \dfrac{P_{2} V_{2} - P_{1} V_{1}}{1-n}
$$

(using $P_{1} V_{1}^{n} V_{2}^{1-n} = P_{2} V_{2}$ since $P V^{n} = C$).

**Step 7 — Final Compression Work Equation.**

$$
\boxed{W_{comp} = \dfrac{P_{2} V_{2} - P_{1} V_{1}}{1-n}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** Work done **on** the gas is positive when $P_{2} > P_{1}$ and $n < 1$ (typical). For $n > 1$ (adiabatic), the numerator is negative but the denominator $(1-n)$ is also negative, keeping $W_{comp} > 0$. This is internally consistent.

---

### 3.4 Special Case 1 — Isothermal Compression (n = 1)

Apply L'Hôpital's rule to the limit $n \to 1$:

$$
W_{iso} = P_{1} V_{1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}}\right) = P_{1} V_{1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **minimum possible compression work** and represents the theoretical best-case for CAES charging — but it requires infinite-rate heat extraction to keep T constant.

---

### 3.5 Special Case 2 — Adiabatic Compression (n = γ = 1.4 for air)

Substitute $n = \gamma$ and use $P_{1} V_{1} = m R T_{1}$ (ideal gas with $m$ in kg, $R = 287$ J/kg·K for air):

$$
W_{ad,comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} V_{1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_{ad,comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, m R T_{1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]
$$

For air, $\dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} = 3.5$.

---

### 3.6 Special Case 3 — Adiabatic Expansion Work (CAES Discharge)

Following the same derivation with state 3 (cavern inlet) → state 4 (atmospheric exit):

$$
\boxed{W_{exp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{3} V_{3} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{4}}{P_{3}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}
$$

The **net work output** of an ideal A-CAES cycle is:

$$
W_{net} = W_{exp} - W_{comp}
$$

In practice, **D-CAES** (Huntorf) supplements $W_{exp}$ with natural-gas combustion (~0.69 MJ/Nm³ of NG per kWh of output), achieving ~45 % overall fuel-to-electric efficiency.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: PHS vs CAES (Module 1 Comparison Table)

> [!TIP]
> This table is a **direct KTU expected-answer format** for compare-and-contrast questions (5–10 mark questions).

| Parameter | Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) | Compressed Air (CAES) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stored energy form | Gravitational PE of water | Pressure PE of air |
| Energy density | ~1 Wh/kg (water) | ~3–6 Wh/kg (air in cavern) |
| Round-trip efficiency | **75 % – 85 %** | **45 % – 70 %** (D vs A type) |
| Geographic constraint | Requires two reservoirs + head | Requires cavern or pressure vessel |
| Maturity / TRL | TRL 9 (>100 GW installed) | TRL 8–9 (~0.4 GW installed) |
| Construction time | 5–15 years | 3–8 years |
| Capital cost ($/kWh) | **$50 – $200** | **$50 – $150** |
| Power cost ($/kW) | **$500 – $4 000** | **$400 – $1 500** |
| Environmental impact | Habitat, river ecology | Cavern subsidence risk, NG for D-CAES |
| Energy stored (per unit) | Up to **30 GWh** single site | Up to **few GWh** typical |
| Self-discharge | Negligible (months) | Thermal losses (days–weeks) |
| Response time | Seconds to minutes | Seconds to minutes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pumped Hydro Storage — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[GRID Off Peak Surplus Electricity] --> nodeB[MOTOR GENERATOR Acts as Motor]
    nodeB --> nodeC[PUMP TURBINE Acts as Pump Reverse Direction]
    nodeC --> nodeD[PENSTACK High Pressure Conduit]
    nodeD --> nodeE[UPPER RESERVOIR Elevation H metres]
    nodeF[UPPER RESERVOIR During Peak Demand] --> nodeG[PENSTOCK Releases Water]
    nodeG --> nodeH[PUMP TURBINE Acts as Turbine Forward Direction]
    nodeH --> nodeI[MOTOR GENERATOR Acts as Generator]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[GRID Supplies Peak Electricity]
    nodeK[LOWER RESERVOIR Source during charging] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeL[LOWER RESERVOIR Receives water after discharge]
    nodeE -. stores .-> nodeF
    nodeL -. feeds .-> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Compressed Air Energy Storage — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[GRID Off Peak Surplus Electricity] --> nodeB[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
    nodeB --> nodeC[MULTI STAGE COMPRESSOR 8 to 12 stages]
    nodeC --> nodeD[AFTER COOLER Heat Rejected in Diabatic CAES]
    nodeD --> nodeE[THERMAL STORAGE Stores Heat in A CAES Only]
    nodeD --> nodeF[CAVERN Salt Cavern or Depleted Gas Field]
    nodeF --> nodeG[EXPANDER High Pressure Air Released]
    nodeE --> nodeH[HEAT EXCHANGER Reheats Air in A CAES]
    nodeG --> nodeI[NATURAL GAS BURNER Supplementary Heat in D CAES]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[TURBO EXPANDER Drives Generator]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[GENERATOR AC Electricity]
    nodeK --> nodeL[GRID Supplies Peak Electricity]
```

### 4.3 Round-Trip Energy Conversion Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[ELECTRICAL INPUT kWh] --> nodeB[CONVERSION 1 Charging Efficiency]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STORED ENERGY Mechanical Potential]
    nodeC --> nodeD[STORAGE LOSSES Self Discharge Negligible PHS]
    nodeD --> nodeE[CONVERSION 2 Discharging Efficiency]
    nodeE --> nodeF[ELECTRICAL OUTPUT kWh Delivered to Grid]
```

### 4.4 CAES Thermodynamic State Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[STATE 1 Atmospheric Intake P1 V1 T1] --> nodeB[POLYTROPIC COMPRESSION n=1 to 1.4]
    nodeB --> nodeC[STATE 2 Cavern Storage P2 V2 T2 Hot]
    nodeC --> nodeD[STORAGE Holds P2 Approximately Constant]
    nodeD --> nodeE[STATE 3 Pre Expansion P3 P2 T3 Cooler]
    nodeE --> nodeF[REHEAT In A CAES from TES or NG in D CAES]
    nodeF --> nodeG[ADIAATIC EXPANSION n=1.4]
    nodeG --> nodeH[STATE 4 Exhaust P4 P_atm V4 T4]
    nodeH --> nodeI[VENT TO ATMOSPHERE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **States 1→2:** Compression (electrical work IN).
> - **States 2→3:** Storage phase (energy preserved).
> - **States 3→4:** Expansion (work OUT, after reheating).
> - **State 1 ≠ State 4** because exhaust temperature is below ambient in adiabatic expansion → recoverable heat sink opportunity (Organic Rankine bottoming cycle in advanced designs).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) and Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) based on energy storage mechanism and round-trip efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) stores energy as **gravitational potential energy** by elevating water to an upper reservoir against gravity. The energy stored is $E = \rho g V H$. Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) stores energy as **pressure potential energy** by compressing air into an underground cavern or vessel; the energy stored is given by $W = \int P\, dV$ over a polytropic process.

| Feature | PHS | CAES |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Storage form | Gravitational PE | Pressure PE |
| Working fluid | Water | Air |
| Round-trip efficiency | **75 % – 85 %** | **45 % – 70 %** |

PHS achieves higher efficiency but requires specific geography (two reservoirs and a head). CAES is preferred where cavern geology (salt domes, depleted gas fields) is available.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of Thermal Energy Storage (TES) in an Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES) system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In an **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)** system, the heat generated during the polytropic compression of air is **not rejected to the environment** (as in diabatic CAES). Instead, the hot compressed air is passed through a heat exchanger, and the extracted thermal energy is stored in a **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** unit — typically using molten salts, packed-bed ceramic pebbles, or latent-heat PCM modules.

During discharge, the stored thermal energy is transferred back to the high-pressure air upstream of the expander. This reheating boosts the expansion work output, eliminates the need for natural-gas combustion, and raises the round-trip efficiency from ~45 % (diabatic) to **~65 % – 70 %** (adiabatic). A-CAES is therefore a **fuel-free, zero-CO₂ bulk storage** technology.

---

### PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). You must attempt **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS) system. Discuss its charging and discharging modes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A Pumped Hydro Storage system is a large-scale grid storage technology that stores surplus electrical energy as gravitational potential energy of water.

**Schematic:** (Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4.1 in SECTION 4 — Functional Architecture Flow).

**Components:**
1. **Upper Reservoir** — natural or artificial lake at elevation H above the lower reservoir.
2. **Lower Reservoir** — collects water after discharge.
3. **Penstock** — high-pressure steel-lined conduit carrying water between reservoirs.
4. **Pump-Turbine** — reversible hydraulic machine (typically Francis-type).
5. **Motor-Generator** — synchronous machine working as motor (charge) or generator (discharge).
6. **Surge Tank** — protects penstock from water-hammer transients.

**Charging Mode (Pumping):**
- Off-peak grid electricity energises the motor-generator (operating as a motor).
- The motor drives the pump-turbine in reverse (pump mode).
- Water is drawn from the lower reservoir and forced up the penstock into the upper reservoir.
- Electrical energy is converted to gravitational PE: $E = \rho g V H$.

**Discharging Mode (Generating):**
- During peak demand, the water in the upper reservoir is released.
- Water descends through the penstock, gaining kinetic energy.
- The pump-turbine (now in turbine mode) drives the motor-generator as a generator.
- Electrical energy is fed back to the grid: $P = \eta \, \rho g Q H$.

**Key Parameters:**
- Round-trip efficiency: 75 – 85 %
- Discharge duration: 6 – 24 hours
- Power rating: 100 MW – 3 000 MW

---

**(b)** A PHS station has an upper reservoir capacity of $V = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{3}$ at an effective head of $H = 200\ \text{m}$. Calculate (i) the energy stored, (ii) the electrical energy output assuming an overall round-trip efficiency of 80 %, and (iii) the time to fully discharge at an average electrical power output of $P_{out} = 500\ \text{MW}$. Take $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$V = 5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{m}^{3}$, $H = 200\ \text{m}$, $\eta_{RT} = 0.80$, $P_{out} = 500\ \text{MW}$, $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^{2}$.

**(i) Mechanical (Gravitational) Energy Stored:**

$$
E_{mech} = \rho \, g \, V \, H
$$

$$
E_{mech} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (5 \times 10^{6}) \times 200
$$

$$
E_{mech} = 9.81 \times 10^{12}\ \text{J}
$$

Converting to MWh (1 MWh = $3.6 \times 10^{9}$ J):

$$
E_{mech} = \dfrac{9.81 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} = 2725\ \text{MWh}
$$

**(ii) Electrical Energy Output:**

$$
E_{elec} = \eta_{RT} \cdot E_{mech} = 0.80 \times 2725
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{elec} = 2180\ \text{MWh} = 2.18\ \text{GWh}}
$$

**(iii) Discharge Time at 500 MW:**

$$
t_{d} = \dfrac{E_{elec}}{P_{out}} = \dfrac{2180\ \text{MWh}}{500\ \text{MW}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{d} = 4.36\ \text{hours}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part b):**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert Joules to MWh** — losing 1 mark.
> - Students often apply efficiency only to the charging side (or only to discharging). The **round-trip** efficiency must multiply the **stored mechanical energy** to give net electrical output.
> - Common slip: Using $E = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g V H$ instead of $E = \rho g V H$ for the slab-reservoir model. **State your assumption** in the first step and stick to it.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain with a P-V diagram the working of a Diabatic Compressed Air Energy Storage (D-CAES) system. Compare it with Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diabatic CAES (D-CAES) — Operating Principle:**
A Diabatic CAES system (e.g., Huntorf, Germany, 1978; McIntosh, USA, 1991) follows a modified Brayton cycle.

**Charging Phase (1 → 2):**
- Ambient air at state 1 ($P_{1} \approx 1\ \text{bar}$, $T_{1} \approx 300\ \text{K}$) is drawn in.
- A multi-stage compressor with inter-cooling raises the pressure to $P_{2} = 40\text{–}80\ \text{bar}$.
- The compression heat is **rejected to the environment** (diabatic) via inter-stage and after-coolers.
- Air is stored in an underground cavern (typically a salt cavern).

**Discharging Phase (3 → 4):**
- High-pressure air leaves the cavern at state 3.
- It is **pre-heated by combusting natural gas** (since compression heat was lost) to raise its temperature.
- The hot, high-pressure air expands through a turbo-expander to atmospheric pressure (state 4).
- The expander drives a generator producing electricity.

**Why Natural Gas?**
