Since compression heat was thrown away, the only way to avoid air temperatures dropping below the dew point (causing ice formation) and to increase expansion work is to add external heat. D-CAES thus **requires fuel** and has an effective round-trip efficiency (counting the fuel's chemical energy) of ~45 – 55 %, but a **purely electrical** round-trip of >90 %.

**Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES):**
- In A-CAES, the compression heat is **not discarded**. It is captured in a Thermal Energy Storage (TES) system (e.g., molten salts, ceramic pebble beds).
- During discharge, the stored heat is transferred back to the air upstream of the expander.
- **No fossil fuel is required.**
- Round-trip efficiency improves to 65 – 70 %.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | D-CAES | A-CAES |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression heat | Rejected (lost) | Stored in TES |
| Discharge heating | Natural gas combustor | TES reheat |
| Fuel requirement | Yes (NG) | No |
| Round-trip $\eta$ | ~45 – 55 % (fuel-to-electric) | ~65 – 70 % (purely electrical) |
| CO₂ emissions | Yes (from NG) | Zero (fuel-free) |
| Maturity | Commercial (Huntorf) | Pilot / Demonstration |

---

**(b)** A CAES system compresses $m = 10\ \text{kg}$ of air from $P_{1} = 1\ \text{bar}$, $T_{1} = 300\ \text{K}$ to $P_{2} = 10\ \text{bar}$ following a polytropic process with index $n = 1.3$. Calculate the compression work and the final temperature $T_{2}$. Use $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$m = 10\ \text{kg}$, $P_{1} = 1\ \text{bar} = 1 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$, $T_{1} = 300\ \text{K}$, $P_{2} = 10\ \text{bar} = 10 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa}$, $n = 1.3$, $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate $V_{1}$ Using Ideal Gas Law.**

$$
V_{1} = \dfrac{m R T_{1}}{P_{1}} = \dfrac{10 \times 287 \times 300}{1 \times 10^{5}} = \dfrac{861000}{100000} = 8.61\ \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_{2}$ Using Polytropic Relation $P_{1} V_{1}^{n} = P_{2} V_{2}^{n}$.**

$$
V_{2} = V_{1} \left(\dfrac{P_{1}}{P_{2}}\right)^{1/n} = 8.61 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)^{1/1.3}
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{1}{10}\right)^{1/1.3} = 10^{-1/1.3} = 10^{-0.7692}
$$

$$
10^{-0.7692} \approx 0.1702
$$

$$
V_{2} = 8.61 \times 0.1702 \approx 1.465\ \text{m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Polytropic Compression Work.**

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{P_{2} V_{2} - P_{1} V_{1}}{1 - n}
$$

Numerator:

$$
P_{2} V_{2} = (10 \times 10^{5}) \times 1.465 = 1.465 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}
$$

$$
P_{1} V_{1} = (1 \times 10^{5}) \times 8.61 = 0.861 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}
$$

$$
P_{2} V_{2} - P_{1} V_{1} = (1.465 - 0.861) \times 10^{6} = 0.604 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}
$$

Denominator: $1 - n = 1 - 1.3 = -0.3$

$$
W_{comp} = \dfrac{0.604 \times 10^{6}}{-0.3} = -2.013 \times 10^{6}\ \text{J}
$$

The negative sign indicates work is done **on** the gas (compression). The magnitude is:

$$
\boxed{\vert W_{comp} \vert = 2.013\ \text{MJ} = 2013\ \text{kJ}}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Final Temperature $T_{2}$.**
Using the polytropic temperature relation:

$$
\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}
$$

$$
T_{2} = 300 \times (10)^{\frac{1.3 - 1}{1.3}} = 300 \times (10)^{0.2308}
$$

$$
10^{0.2308} \approx 1.701
$$

$$
T_{2} = 300 \times 1.701 \approx 510.3\ \text{K}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{2} \approx 510\ \text{K} \ (\approx 237\,^{\circ}\text{C})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (CAES part b):**
> - **Sign convention is heavily penalised.** If a student writes $W = +2.013\ \text{MJ}$ without explaining that work is done *on the system*, marks will be deducted.
> - Students often use $n = \gamma = 1.4$ instead of the given $n = 1.3$. **Read the question carefully.**
> - The temperature relation $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}$ is the most-missed formula in CAES problems.
> - Always carry at least **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps to avoid rounding errors.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (PHS & CAES):**

- ☐ **PHS** = gravitational PE storage; **CAES** = pressure PE storage.
- ☐ PHS round-trip efficiency = **75 – 85 %** (highest of bulk storage).
- ☐ CAES round-trip efficiency = **45 – 70 %** (D-CAES vs A-CAES).
- ☐ PHS energy: $E = \rho g V H$ (or $\tfrac{1}{2} \rho g V H$ for slab model).
- ☐ PHS power: $P = \eta \rho g Q H$.
- ☐ Ideal gas law: $P V = m R T$ (for air, $R = 287\ \text{J/kg·K}$).
- ☐ Polytropic process: $P V^{n} = \text{const}$.
- ☐ Polytropic work: $W = \dfrac{P_{2} V_{2} - P_{1} V_{1}}{1 - n}$.
- ☐ Isothermal work (limit n→1): $W = P_{1} V_{1} \ln(P_{2}/P_{1})$.
- ☐ Adiabatic work (n=γ=1.4): $W = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} P_{1} V_{1}\left[(P_{2}/P_{1})^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$.
- ☐ Adiabatic temperature relation: $\dfrac{T_{2}}{T_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$.
- ☐ **D-CAES** uses natural gas for reheating; **A-CAES** uses stored TES heat.
- ☐ **Huntorf** (Germany, 1978) and **McIntosh** (USA, 1991) are the only two commercial D-CAES plants worldwide.
- ☐ PHS has geographical constraints (reservoirs + head); CAES has geological constraints (caverns).
- ☐ Both technologies are **dispatchable**, **long-duration**, and provide **grid ancillary services** (inertia, frequency regulation, black-start).
- ☐ KTU-favourite trap: always specify whether efficiency is **round-trip**, **electrical**, or **fuel-to-electric** — they differ significantly for D-CAES.
- ☐ KTU-favourite trap: the factor of **½** in PHS energy formula — *state your assumption*.
- ☐ KTU-favourite trap: **sign of work** — compression work on the system is conventionally negative (thermodynamics sign convention), but engineering sign convention treats it as positive input.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical - Flywheel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical - Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Flywheel** is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** by accelerating a rotor (mass) to a very high angular velocity and maintaining that energy in the form of rotational inertia. The stored energy is released by decelerating the flywheel, which is coupled to a generator/motor (acting as a regenerative braking and traction unit).

According to KTU 2024 Scheme module definitions, a flywheel is classified as a **kinetic energy storage system (KESS)** belonging to the family of **mechanical energy storage systems**. The energy stored in a rotating flywheel is given by the fundamental rotational kinetic energy equation:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$

where:
- $E$ = Kinetic energy stored (Joules, **J**)
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of the rotor (kg·m²)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):** A flywheel is essentially a rotating mass whose **moment of inertia** opposes changes in rotational speed. Energy is supplied to spin it up (charging) and extracted when it slows down (discharging). The rotor is typically enclosed in a **vacuum chamber** to minimize air-friction losses, and supported on **magnetic bearings** to eliminate mechanical contact losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Flywheels (FESS — Flywheel Energy Storage System):** The modern FESS is a closed-loop electromechanical system integrating a high-speed rotor, magnetic levitation bearings, a permanent-magnet motor/generator, and power electronics converters. It is used for **frequency regulation, voltage support, UPS, regenerative braking in electric vehicles, and renewable energy smoothing**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Think of a **spinning top** or a **potter's wheel** that keeps rotating for a long time after you stop pushing it. The energy you used to spin it is now "stored" in its rotation. If you attached a small generator to that spinning wheel, you could extract electricity from it as it slows down. A flywheel works on exactly this principle but engineered at industrial scale with:
- Ultra-strong materials (carbon-fiber composites) to spin extremely fast,
- A vacuum-sealed chamber to avoid air drag,
- Magnetic bearings so the rotor "floats" without touching anything.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-life example:** The KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) used in Formula 1 cars stores braking energy in a small flywheel spun up to ~60,000 rpm. The energy is reused later for a power boost during acceleration. The same concept, scaled up, stabilizes power grids and backup power systems.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Rim / Tip Speed ($v$) | **200 – 1000 m/s** | High-speed composite rotors |
| Specific Energy | **5 – 130 Wh/kg** | Carbon-fiber vs steel rotors |
| Cycle Life | **> 10⁵ – 10⁶ cycles** | Mechanical durability |
| Self-Discharge | **~ 10 – 100 % / hour** | Vacuum-loss dependent |
| Round-trip Efficiency | **85 – 95 %** | Industrial FESS |
| Discharge Time | **seconds to minutes** | Short-duration storage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Charging (Energy Input):** Electrical energy from the grid/load is fed into a **motor/generator** (which acts as a motor during charging). The motor spins up the flywheel rotor through a shaft, converting electrical → mechanical (kinetic) energy.
2. **Storage Phase:** The rotor spins at very high angular velocity $\omega$ in a **vacuum housing** on **magnetic (non-contact) bearings**, minimizing all forms of friction. Kinetic energy is retained as $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$.
3. **Discharging (Energy Output):** When power is demanded, the same motor/generator unit operates as a generator. The decelerating rotor induces a back-EMF, which is rectified and conditioned by **power electronics** to feed the load.
4. **Why It Works:** The angular momentum $L = I\omega$ resists sudden changes — this is the fundamental reason a flywheel "smoothes" power fluctuations and provides instantaneous response (sub-millisecond).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core 'Why':** A flywheel is essentially a **mechanical capacitor** — it does not store charge (like a battery) or electric field energy (like a supercapacitor); it stores **momentum of mass in motion**. The amount of energy scales with the **square of the speed**, which is why FESS engineers push for ultra-high rotational speeds.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations & Geometry Types

The moment of inertia $I$ depends on the rotor's mass distribution. The most common geometries encountered in KTU questions are:

| Geometry | Moment of Inertia ($I$) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Thin Rim (Hoop)** | $I = M r^{2}$ | Energy concentrated at the rim; highest specific energy |
| **Solid Disk / Cylinder** | $I = \frac{1}{2} M r^{2}$ | Uniform mass; used in low-speed industrial flywheels |
| **Solid Sphere** | $I = \frac{2}{5} M r^{2}$ | Used in some special high-speed applications |
| **Thin Rod (axis through end)** | $I = \frac{1}{3} M L^{2}$ | Mostly for theoretical examples |
| **General Form** | $I = k M r^{2}$ | $k$ = radius-of-gyration factor (depends on shape) |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula sheet is essential for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Exam). Memorize all equations, their boundary conditions, and units. **No vertical pipe character `|` is used in the table cells to prevent markdown parsing errors — vertical bars in math are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` LaTeX commands.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | Total kinetic energy stored | J |
| 2 | $I = k M r^{2}$ | Moment of inertia (general) | kg·m² |
| 3 | $E = \frac{1}{2} k M r^{2} \omega^{2}$ | Stored energy (substitute I) | J |
| 4 | $v = r \omega$ | Rim/tip tangential velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $E = \frac{1}{2} k M v^{2}$ | Energy in terms of rim speed | J |
| 6 | $\sigma = \rho \, v^{2}$ | Hoop stress in a thin rim | Pa (N/m²) |
| 7 | $\sigma_{max} = \rho \, r^{2} \omega^{2}$ | Max tensile stress at rim | Pa |
| 8 | $\frac{E}{V} = \frac{1}{2} k \rho \, v^{2}$ | Volumetric energy density | J/m³ |
| 9 | $\frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2} k v^{2}$ | Gravimetric / specific energy | J/kg |
| 10 | $L = I \omega$ | Angular momentum | kg·m²/s |
| 11 | $P = T \omega$ | Instantaneous power from torque | W |
| 12 | $\frac{E}{V} = K \cdot \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\rho}$ | Energy density in terms of material ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{u}$ | J/m³ |
| 13 | $N = \frac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi}$ | Convert rad/s to rpm | rpm |

Where:
- $k$ = radius-of-gyration factor (1.0 for thin rim, 0.5 for solid disk, 0.4 for solid sphere, 0.33 for rod).
- $M$ = mass of rotor (kg)
- $r$ = outer radius (m)
- $\rho$ = density of rotor material (kg/m³)
- $v$ = rim velocity (m/s)
- $\sigma$ = tensile (hoop) stress developed in the rim due to centrifugal force (Pa)
- $\sigma_{u}$ = ultimate tensile strength of material (Pa)
- $K$ = a dimensionless factor (0.5 for thin rim, 0.25 for solid disk) for the energy-to-stress ratio
- $T$ = torque (N·m)
- $L$ = angular momentum (kg·m²/s)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Renewable Integration:** Solar and wind power are intermittent; FESS smooths out sub-second to minute-scale fluctuations (much faster response than batteries).
- **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS):** Bridges power gaps of a few seconds (e.g., 100 kW·h class) until diesel generators start.
- **Electric Vehicles & Railways:** Regenerative braking → flywheel → traction boost (KERS).
- **Spacecraft Attitude Control:** Reaction wheels (low-energy flywheels) orient satellites.
- **Pulsed Power:** Navy electromagnetic launchers, particle accelerators — flywheels release megajoules in milliseconds.
- **Industrial Process Energy Recovery:** Punch presses, stamping machines — recapture peak demand energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are presented **exhaustively**, step-by-step, in alignment with the rigorous KTU 2024 Scheme valuation pattern. Each algebraic transition is explicitly shown.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Stored in a Rotating Flywheel from First Principles

We begin from Newton's second law for rotation and the definition of work done by a torque.

**Step 1 — Define work done by a torque.**
When a torque $T$ acts through an angular displacement $d\theta$, the work done is:

$$dW = T \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 2 — Relate torque to angular acceleration.**
For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, the torque required to produce angular acceleration $\alpha$ is:

$$T = I \alpha = I \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the work expression.**
Now substitute $T$ into the work equation:

$$dW = I \frac{d\omega}{dt} \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Change the variable from $t$ to $\theta$.**
Since $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$, we can write:

$$dW = I \cdot d\omega \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = I \cdot d\omega \cdot \omega = I \, \omega \, d\omega$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from rest ($\omega = 0$) to final angular velocity ($\omega = \omega_{f}$).**

$$W = \int_{0}^{\omega_{f}} I \, \omega \, d\omega = I \left[ \frac{\omega^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\omega_{f}} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{f}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the work as the kinetic energy stored.**
By the work-energy theorem for rotation, the work done on the flywheel is stored as rotational kinetic energy:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}}$$

This is the **master equation** for every flywheel problem. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Rim Flywheel (KTU Favourite)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams. We consider a thin rim rotating at high speed and analyze the centrifugal forces on a small element.

**Step 1 — Geometry setup.**
Consider a thin circular rim of:
- Mean radius $r$ (m)
- Cross-sectional area $A$ (m²)
- Material density $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s)

The rim has a uniform hoop tension $T$ (force, in Newtons) acting along its circumference.

**Step 2 — Identify a small elemental arc.**
Consider an element of the rim that subtends a small angle $d\theta$ at the centre. The arc length is $r \, d\theta$, and the mass of the element is:

$$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot r \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Centrifugal force on the element.**
The element moves in a circle of radius $r$. The required centripetal force (or equivalently, the outward centrifugal force on the element) is:

$$dF_{c} = dm \cdot r \omega^{2} = (\rho A r \, d\theta) \cdot r \omega^{2} = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta$$

This force acts **outward radially** through the centre of the element.

**Step 4 — Resolve the two rim tensions.**
At the two ends of the element, the rim tension $T$ acts tangentially. The two tension forces make an angle $d\theta$ between them. Their **radial (inward) components** are each $T \sin(d\theta/2)$. The total inward radial force from both tensions is:

$$dF_{in} = 2 T \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-angle approximation.**
For a very small $d\theta$, $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$. Therefore:

$$dF_{in} = 2 T \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = T \, d\theta$$

**Step 6 — Apply static equilibrium.**
The element is not accelerating radially (it is in steady circular motion — Newton's first law for the chosen rotating frame). Therefore, the inward radial force from the tensions must exactly balance the outward centrifugal force:

$$T \, d\theta = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $d\theta$ from both sides.**

$$T = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Convert tension to stress.**
Tensile (hoop) stress $\sigma$ is force per unit cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A} = \frac{\rho A r^{2} \omega^{2}}{A} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 9 — Express in terms of rim velocity.**
Since rim velocity $v = r \omega$, we have $r^{2} \omega^{2} = v^{2}$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\sigma = \rho v^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The hoop stress in a thin-rim flywheel depends only on the material density $\rho$ and the **square of the rim velocity** $v$. To maximize energy storage safely, the designer must keep $\sigma$ below the material's **ultimate tensile strength** $\sigma_{u}$, giving the design limit $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{u}/\rho}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Volumetric Energy Density of a Thin-Rim Flywheel

**Step 1 — Energy stored in the thin rim.**
Using $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ with $I = M r^{2}$ for a thin rim:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} M r^{2} \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} M v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of material density.**
For a thin rim, volume $V = A \cdot (2 \pi r) \approx 2 \pi r A$ and $M = \rho V$. Substituting:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} (\rho V) v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Energy per unit volume (volumetric energy density).**

$$\frac{E}{V} = \frac{1}{2} \rho v^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $v^{2}$ with the design stress constraint.**
From the hoop stress derivation, $v^{2} = \sigma / \rho$. Substituting:

$$\frac{E}{V} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\rho} = \frac{\sigma}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $\sigma$ with $\sigma_{u}$ (maximum allowable stress).**
For safe design, $\sigma \leq \sigma_{u}$. The maximum volumetric energy density is:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{E}{V}\right)_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{u}}{2}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Implication:** This is why high-strength, low-density composite materials (carbon-fiber/epoxy, $\sigma_{u}/\rho \approx 2$ MJ/kg) are chosen for modern FESS — they give a higher $E/V$ at lower mass.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Specific Energy of a Solid-Disk Flywheel

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of a solid uniform disk.**
For a solid disk of mass $M$ and radius $r$ rotating about its central axis:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} M r^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored.**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{2} M r^{2} \right) \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{4} M r^{2} \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{4} M v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Specific energy (energy per unit mass).**

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{M} = \frac{1}{4} v^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with thin rim.**
For a thin rim, the same derivation gives $E/M = \frac{1}{2} v^{2}$ — i.e., **the thin rim stores twice as much energy per unit mass** as a solid disk at the same rim speed. This is why **modern high-energy FESS use thin composite rims**, not solid disks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-offs

| Parameter | Low-Speed Steel Flywheel | High-Speed Composite FESS |
|---|---|---|
| Material | Forged steel | Carbon-fiber / E-glass composite |
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | ~ 7800 | ~ 1600 – 2000 |
| Operating speed (rpm) | 1000 – 3000 | 20,000 – 60,000 |
| Rim speed $v$ (m/s) | ~ 50 – 100 | ~ 400 – 900 |
| Specific energy (Wh/kg) | 5 – 30 | 50 – 130 |
| Cycle life | $\geq 10^{5}$ | $\geq 10^{6}$ |
| Cost per kWh | Low | High (but falling) |
| Typical use | Grid regulation, UPS | EV/KERS, satellite, pulsed power |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern FESS

The diagram below maps the functional flow and physical arrangement of a complete Flywheel Energy Storage System. It is designed to be Mermaid-compatible: every node ID is alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PWRIN[GRID OR DC BUS POWER INPUT] --> CTRL[POWER ELECTRONICS CONTROLLER VFD IGBT CONVERTER]
    CTRL --> MG[MOTOR GENERATOR UNIT PMSM OR INDUCTION MACHINE]
    MG --> SHAFT[HIGH SPEED SHAFT COUPLED VIA GEAR TRAIN]
    SHAFT --> ROTOR[COMPOSITE ROTOR FLYWHEEL STORES KINETIC ENERGY]
    ROTOR --> BRG[MAGNETIC LEVITATION BEARINGS RADIAL AND AXIAL]
    ROTOR --> VAC[VACUUM CHAMBER HOUSING 10 TO 3 PA PRESSURE]
    MG --> GEN[BACK EMF INDUCED GENERATION MODE]
    GEN --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PWROUT[CONDITIONED POWER OUTPUT TO LOAD]
    VAC --> TRAY[CONTAINMENT VESSEL SAFETY SHELL]
    BRG --> TRAY
    ROTOR --> TRAY
    CTRL --> SENS[TELEMETRY AND SAFETY MONITORING UNIT SPEED TEMPERATURE VIBRATION]
    SENS --> CTRL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **rotor** is the only energy-storing element (kinetic energy of spinning mass).
- The **motor/generator** is bidirectional — it accelerates the rotor (charge) and extracts energy as the rotor decelerates (discharge).
- The **vacuum chamber** eliminates aerodynamic drag (the dominant loss at high speed).
- The **magnetic bearings** eliminate mechanical-contact friction, ensuring the rotor can hold speed for hours.
- The **containment vessel** is a thick steel shell — a critical safety feature to contain fragments in case of rotor burst.

---

## 4.2 Operational State Diagram (Charge / Hold / Discharge)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STANDBY
    STANDBY --> CHARGING : ELECTRICAL POWER APPLIED
    CHARGING --> HOLD : TARGET RPM REACHED
    HOLD --> DISCHARGING : LOAD DEMAND RECEIVED
    HOLD --> STANDBY : SELF DISCHARGE TOLERANCE EXCEEDED
    DISCHARGING --> STANDBY : SPEED BELOW MINIMUM THRESHOLD
    DISCHARGING --> CHARGING : RAPID REGULATION CYCLING
    STANDBY --> [*]
```

**Three operating modes:**
1. **CHARGING:** Power electronics drives the motor to accelerate the rotor to design rpm.
2. **HOLD (Storage):** Rotor spins freely in vacuum; only bearing and windage losses occur.
3. **DISCHARGING:** Rotor torque drives the generator, producing electricity at the cost of kinetic energy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. They follow the exact mark distribution: **Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks (with internal choice)**. Each sub-question is set at 7 marks. The valuation is **outcome-based and Bloom's-level tagged**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a flywheel. Mention any two applications of a flywheel energy storage system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **flywheel** is a mechanical device that stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** by spinning a mass (rotor) at high angular velocity. The stored energy is given by $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$.
>
> **Two applications:**
> 1. **Kinetic Energy Recovery System (KERS) in automobiles** — recovers braking energy and reuses it for acceleration.
> 2. **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) and grid frequency regulation** — bridges short-duration power interruptions and smooths renewable energy fluctuations.
>
> *(Alternative valid applications: pulsed power for electromagnetic launchers, satellite reaction wheels, industrial punch-press energy recovery, voltage sag mitigation.)*

**Marks distribution:** Definition 1 mark + 2 × applications 1 mark each = 3 marks. ✅

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] What is meant by specific energy of a flywheel? Why is it preferred over specific energy alone as a design metric?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Specific energy** of a flywheel is the **kinetic energy stored per unit mass** of the rotor. Mathematically:
>
> $$\frac{E}{M} = \frac{1}{2} k v^{2}$$
>
> where $k$ is the radius-of-gyration factor and $v = r\omega$ is the rim speed.
>
> It is preferred as a design metric (along with volumetric energy density $E/V$) because a flywheel's performance depends on both its **mass efficiency** and **volume efficiency**. A rotor with high specific energy but poor $E/V$ may be too bulky for vehicle/space applications, while one with high $E/V$ but low specific energy may be too heavy for grid-scale use. Both must be optimized using the relation:
>
> $$\frac{E}{V} = \rho \cdot \frac{E}{M}$$
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explanation of design relevance.)* ✅

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In long-answer flywheel derivations, students commonly lose marks by:
> - Skipping the **free-body diagram** description of the rim element (loss: ~2 marks).
> - Not stating the **small-angle approximation** $\sin(d\theta/2) \approx d\theta/2$ explicitly (loss: ~1 mark).
> - Forgetting to **convert rpm to rad/s** using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ (loss: ~1 mark).
> - Omitting **units** in the final numerical answer (loss: ~1 mark).
> - Writing only the formula without showing the substitution step (loss: ~1–2 marks).
> **Avoid these — they cost 5–7 marks per question!**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **hoop stress developed in a thin rim flywheel** rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. State the design constraint for maximum safe operating speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **solid cylindrical flywheel** has a mass of **400 kg** and a radius of **0.6 m**. It is rotating at **2400 rpm**. Calculate:
  (i) the moment of inertia of the flywheel,
  (ii) the kinetic energy stored,
  (iii) the specific energy of the flywheel.
Take the material density as $\rho = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** Consider a thin rim of mean radius $r$ and cross-sectional area $A$, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$.

**Step 2:** Take an element of the rim subtending a small angle $d\theta$ at the centre.

**Step 3:** Mass of the element:
$$dm = \rho A r \, d\theta$$

**Step 4:** Centrifugal (outward radial) force on the element:
$$dF = dm \cdot r \omega^{2} = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta$$

**Step 5:** This is balanced by the radial component of the two tangential tensions $T$ at the ends of the element:
$$dF_{in} = 2T \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx T \, d\theta \quad \text{(small-angle approximation)}$$

**Step 6:** Equating:
$$T \, d\theta = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2} \, d\theta \implies T = \rho A r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 7:** Hoop stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{T}{A} = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2} = \rho v^{2}$$

**Step 8:** Design constraint — for safe operation, $\sigma \leq \sigma_{u}$ (ultimate tensile strength). Hence:
$$v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{u}}{\rho}}, \quad \omega_{max} = \frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{u}}{\rho}}$$

**Marks allocation:**
- Element setup + free-body description: 2 marks
- Centrifugal force derivation: 2 marks
- Equilibrium + small-angle approximation: 1 mark
- Final hoop stress equation: 1 mark
- Design constraint: 1 mark

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Mass $M = 400$ kg
- Radius $r = 0.6$ m
- Speed $N = 2400$ rpm
- Density $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³

**Convert rpm to rad/s:**
$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 2400}{60} = 80 \pi = 251.327 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Moment of inertia (solid cylinder):**
$$I = \frac{1}{2} M r^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 400 \times (0.6)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 400 \times 0.36 = 72 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

**(ii) Kinetic energy stored:**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 72 \times (251.327)^{2}$$
$$E = 36 \times 63165.5 = 2,273,958 \text{ J} \approx 2.27 \text{ MJ}$$

In more useful units: $E \approx 0.631$ kWh.

**(iii) Specific energy:**
$$\frac{E}{M} = \frac{2,273,958}{400} = 5684.9 \text{ J/kg} = 5.68 \text{ kJ/kg} \approx 1.58 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**Marks allocation:**
- Unit conversion (rpm → rad/s): 1 mark
- $I$ calculation with correct formula: 2 marks
- $E$ calculation: 2 marks
- Specific energy + units: 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a modern high-speed flywheel energy storage system (FESS)** with the help of a functional block diagram. Discuss the role of each subsystem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **flywheel energy storage systems (FESS)** with **battery storage** and **supercapacitor storage** under the following heads: specific energy, specific power, cycle life, response time, and self-discharge. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

A modern high-speed FESS consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Composite Rotor (Energy Storage Element):** A thin-rim rotor made of carbon-fiber-reinforced polymer (CFRP) wound around a low-mass hub. The rim geometry maximises $I/M$ ratio; the composite provides high $\sigma_{u}/\rho$ (high tensile strength, low density), allowing rim speeds of 600–1000 m/s. The rotor stores kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$.

2. **Vacuum Housing:** The rotor is enclosed in a sealed steel chamber evacuated to ~10⁻³ Pa. This eliminates air-drag losses, which scale as $v^{3}$ and would otherwise dominate at high speed.

3. **Magnetic Bearings:** Passive and active electromagnetic bearings levitate the rotor shaft, eliminating mechanical-contact friction. They support both radial and axial loads. Some designs use permanent-magnet biasing with active control for stiffness.

4. **Motor/Generator (M/G) Unit:** A permanent-magnet synchronous machine (PMSM) integrated into the same shaft. In motoring mode (charge), it accelerates the rotor. In generating mode (discharge), back-EMF is rectified to feed the load.

5. **Power Electronics Converter:** A bidirectional IGBT/MOSFET-based AC-DC-DC converter that interfaces the high-frequency M/G output to the grid/DC bus. It performs MPPT, voltage regulation, and current limiting.

6. **Containment Vessel:** A thick steel safety shell surrounding the entire system. In the rare event of rotor burst, it absorbs the kinetic fragments (a rotor at 60,000 rpm stores megajoules — failure can be catastrophic).

7. **Control & Monitoring System:** Sensors monitor rotor speed, temperature, vibration, vacuum pressure, and bearing currents. The controller modulates charge/discharge and triggers safety shutdowns.

**Working Principle:** During charging, grid power → power electronics → motor accelerates rotor → kinetic energy stored. During discharging, decelerating rotor → generator back-EMF → conditioned DC/AC output to load. The cycle efficiency is 85–95% (limited by bearing/windage and converter losses).

**Marks allocation:** Subsystem identification (5 × 1 mark) + working description (1 mark) + functional integration (1 mark) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

| Storage Type | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Cycle Life | Response Time | Self-Discharge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flywheel (FESS)** | 5 – 130 | 1000 – 5000 | $> 10^{5}$ | Sub-millisecond | 10 – 100 %/hour |
| **Lithium-Ion Battery** | 100 – 250 | 250 – 1000 | 500 – 5000 | 10 – 100 ms | 0.1 – 1 %/day |
| **Supercapacitor (EDLC)** | 3 – 10 | 5000 – 10000 | $> 10^{6}$ | Sub-millisecond | 5 – 40 %/day |

**Key conclusions:**

1. **Specific Energy:** Batteries dominate (5–10× higher than FESS). FESS is suited for medium-energy applications.
2. **Specific Power:** Supercapacitors and FESS are comparable and far superior to batteries. FESS is excellent for **high-power short-duration** events.
3. **Cycle Life:** FESS and supercapacitors both exceed $10^{5}$ cycles; batteries degrade after 1000–5000 cycles. FESS has a **mechanical durability advantage**.
4. **Response Time:** FESS and supercapacitors respond in milliseconds; batteries are slower (10–100 ms). FESS is ideal for **frequency regulation**.
5. **Self-Discharge:** FESS loses energy within hours; supercapacitors within days; batteries hold for months. FESS is **not suitable for long-duration storage**.

**Synthesis:** FESS occupies a unique niche — **high power, long cycle life, fast response, but moderate energy density and high self-discharge.** It complements (rather than replaces) batteries and supercapacitors in hybrid energy storage systems.

**Marks allocation:** Table values (5 × 0.5 = 2.5 marks) + 5 conclusion statements (5 × 0.5 = 2.5 marks) + synthesis statement (2 marks) = 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist and review it the night before the KTU exam — covers the highest-weightage points in Module 2 (Flywheel).**

- ☐ A flywheel stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy**; the governing equation is $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$.
- ☐ Moment of inertia depends on rotor geometry: **thin rim = $Mr^{2}$, solid disk = $\frac{1}{2}Mr^{2}$, solid sphere = $\frac{2}{5}Mr^{2}$**.
- ☐ For maximum specific energy, the rim should be as far from the axis as possible (high $r$) and the material concentrated at the rim (thin-rim geometry preferred over solid disk).
- ☐ The **hoop stress in a thin rim** rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ is $\sigma = \rho r^{2} \omega^{2} = \rho v^{2}$.
- ☐ **Design constraint:** $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{u}$ (ultimate tensile strength of rim material) → $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{u}/\rho}$.
- ☐ **Volumetric energy density** of thin rim: $E/V = \sigma/2$ (at safe working stress).
- ☐ **Specific energy** of solid disk: $E/M = v^{2}/4$; of thin rim: $E/M = v^{2}/2$ → **thin rim is twice as energy-dense** as a solid disk.
- ☐ Modern FESS uses **composite rims (CFRP)**, **magnetic bearings**, and **vacuum housing** to push $v$ to 600–1000 m/s.
- ☐ Angular velocity conversion: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s.
- ☐ FESS applications: UPS, frequency regulation, EV/KERS, satellite reaction wheels, pulsed power, renewable smoothing.
- ☐ FESS cycle efficiency: 85–95%; cycle life: $>10^{5}$ cycles; discharge duration: seconds to minutes.
- ☐ **Comparison triad:** Batteries (high $E/m$, low $P/m$, long self-discharge) vs Supercapacitors (low $E/m$, very high $P/m$) vs FESS (moderate $E/m$, high $P/m$, fast response, long cycle life).
- ☐ **Safety:** FESS must have a **containment vessel** because a rotor bursting at full speed releases massive kinetic energy.
- ☐ For KTU derivations, always include: free-body description, small-angle approximation, equilibrium equation, stress formula, and design constraint.
- ☐ For KTU numerical problems, always convert rpm to rad/s first, then use $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power to Gas: Hydrogen - Synthetic methane. Electrochemical energy: Batteries - Battery parameters: C-rating - SoC - DoD - Specific Energy - Specific power (numerical examples)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Power to Gas: Hydrogen - Synthetic methane. Electrochemical energy: Batteries - Battery parameters: C-rating - SoC - DoD - Specific Energy - Specific power (numerical examples) ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Power-to-Gas (P2G) — Formal Definition **Power-to-Gas (P2G)** is a long-duration, large-capacity energy storage pathway that converts surplus **electrical energy** (typically from intermittent renewable sources like wind and solar PV) into a gaseous energy carrier — most commonly **hydrogen (H₂)** — and optionally upgrades it further into **synthetic methane (CH₄)**, also called Substitute Natural Gas (SNG), which can be injected directly into the existing natural gas grid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** P2G is a *chemical* energy storage route. It belongs to the family of **indirect/electrical-to-chemical** storage, where electricity is the input and a synthetic fuel is the output. It bridges the electricity sector with the gas sector — a concept known as *sector coupling*. ### 1.2 Hydrogen (H₂) — The Primary P2G Product **Hydrogen** in the P2G context is produced by **electrolysis of water**, where an electric current splits water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen. The general reaction is: $$2H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow 2H_{2(g)} + O_{2(g)}$$ The three dominant electrolyzer technologies are: - **ALK** — Alkaline Electrolyzer (cheapest, mature) - **PEM** — Proton Exchange Membrane (fast response, ideal for renewables) - **SOEC** — Solid Oxide Electrolyzer Cell (highest efficiency, high temperature) Hydrogen has a very high **gravimetric energy density** (~**120 MJ/kg** or ~**33.33 kWh/kg**) but a very low **volumetric energy density** at ambient conditions, so it must be compressed (350–700 bar), liquefied (−253 °C), or stored in solid-state metal hydrides. > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of P2G-Hydrogen like a *water tank on a hill*. When the renewable energy supply is abundant (sun shining, wind blowing), you "pump" the energy uphill by storing it as hydrogen gas. When you need energy back, you release the hydrogen through a fuel cell or turbine — the water flows back down to generate power. The "tank" can be enormous (caverns, pipelines, salt domes), unlike batteries. ### 1.3 Synthetic Methane (SNG) — The Secondary P2G Product **Synthetic methane** is produced by reacting the P2G-hydrogen with **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** through the **Sabatier reaction** (methanation), discovered by the French chemist Paul Sabatier in 1902. The reaction is: $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -165 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ The CO₂ feedstock can be sourced from biogas plants, industrial flue gases, or even direct air capture (DAC). Synthetic methane is **fully compatible** with the existing natural gas grid, CNG vehicles, and LNG infrastructure — making it a *drop-in* fuel. ### 1.4 Electrochemical Energy Storage — Batteries A **battery** is an **electrochemical cell** (or assembly of cells) that stores energy in chemical form and releases it as direct-current (DC) electricity via **redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions** at the anode and cathode. Two principal families dominate the stationary and mobility sectors: - **Lithium-ion (Li-ion)** — high energy density, used in EVs and grid storage - **Lead-acid** — low cost, used in UPS and automotive SLI > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Battery as a "Sponge"):** A discharged battery is like a *dry sponge*. When you charge it, you soak it with electrons and ions (lithium ions migrating from cathode to anode). When you discharge, you squeeze the sponge — electrons flow out through the external circuit to power your device, and ions migrate back to the cathode. The "wetness" level is the **State of Charge (SoC)**. ### 1.5 Key Battery Parameters — Quick Vocabulary | Parameter | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning | |-----------|--------|------------------------| | C-rate | C | How fast you charge/discharge relative to full capacity | | State of Charge | SoC | How "full" the battery is, in % | | Depth of Discharge | DoD | How much has been *emptied* from full, in % | | Specific Energy | Wh/kg | Energy stored per unit mass | | Specific Power | W/kg | Power delivery per unit mass | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Examiners often test whether you can distinguish between **energy** (capacity, in Wh) and **power** (rate, in W). Specific energy = how *far* (range/runtime); Specific power = how *fast* (acceleration/peak load). ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Power-to-Gas (P2G) Chain — Step-by-Step Logic The P2G pathway can be broken into four logical stages: 1. **Electricity Input:** Surplus renewable electricity (which would otherwise be curtailed) is fed to the electrolyzer. 2. **Electrolysis Stage:** Water is split into H₂ and O₂ using electrical energy. The **Faraday efficiency** of the electrolyzer governs the H₂ yield per kWh of input. 3. **Hydrogen Storage/Use:** The produced H₂ can be: (a) stored in tanks, (b) injected into the gas grid at low percentages (typically 2–10%), or (c) used directly in fuel cells. 4. **Optional Methanation (Sabatier):** H₂ reacts with CO₂ over a **nickel-based catalyst** at **200–400 °C** and **1–80 bar** to produce CH₄ and H₂O. The CH₄ is then dried and compressed for grid injection. ### 2.2 Why Synthetic Methane? — The "Why" Behind It - **Grid compatibility:** Existing natural gas pipelines (millions of km worldwide) can carry CH₄ without modification. - **Storage capacity:** Seasonal storage in underground gas caverns is well-proven — much larger scale than batteries. - **Carbon circularity:** When CO₂ is captured from the atmosphere or biomass, the resulting CH₄ is **carbon-neutral** or even **carbon-negative**. - **Energy density:** CH₄ has ~**55.5 MJ/kg** (15.4 kWh/kg) — much higher volumetric density than H₂ at ambient conditions. ### 2.3 Electrochemical Energy — The Redox Mechanism in Batteries A rechargeable (secondary) battery operates on a **reversible redox reaction**: - **Discharge:** Anode is **oxidized** (loses electrons); cathode is **reduced** (gains electrons); electrons flow through the external load from anode to cathode. - **Charge:** External power source drives electrons in reverse, forcing ions to migrate back to the original electrodes. For a generic lithium-ion cell: $$\text{Anode (graphite):} \quad Li_xC_6 \rightleftharpoons xLi^+ + xe^- + 6C$$ $$\text{Cathode (e.g., } LiCoO_2\text{):} \quad Li_{1-x}CoO_2 + xLi^+ + xe^- \rightleftharpoons LiCoO_2$$ The cell voltage $V_{cell}$ arises from the difference in electrochemical potential between the two electrodes. ### 2.4 Battery Parameters — Detailed Engineering Analysis #### 2.4.1 C-Rating (C-rate) The **C-rate** is the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged **relative to its maximum capacity**. A **1C** rate means the entire battery is fully discharged in **1 hour**; **2C** means in **30 minutes**; **0.5C** means in **2 hours**. $$I = C \times C_{Ah}$$ where $I$ is the current in Amperes, $C$ is the C-rate, and $C_{Ah}$ is the rated capacity in Ampere-hours. #### 2.4.2 State of Charge (SoC) **SoC** is the ratio of the *current* available capacity to the *maximum* capacity, expressed as a percentage: $$SoC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$ Equivalently: $$SoC(t) = SoC_{initial} - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau)\, d\tau \times 100\%$$ A **100% SoC** = fully charged; **0% SoC** = fully discharged. #### 2.4.3 Depth of Discharge (DoD) **DoD** is the complement of SoC, expressed as a percentage: $$DoD(t) = 100\% - SoC(t)$$ A **100% DoD** = fully discharged (empty); **0% DoD** = fully charged (full). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall — DoD vs SoC:** Many students confuse these. Remember: **SoC is "how full"** and **DoD is "how empty"**. They ALWAYS sum to 100%. Cycle life is often specified in terms of DoD (e.g., 80% DoD at 2000 cycles). #### 2.4.4 Specific Energy (Gravimetric Energy Density) **Specific energy** is the amount of energy stored per unit **mass** of the battery. It governs the **range** or **runtime** of the application. $$E_{specific} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m_{battery}} = \frac{V \times Q}{m_{battery}} \quad \left[\frac{Wh}{kg}\right]$$ Typical values: - **Lead-acid:** 30–50 Wh/kg - **Lithium-ion (NMC):** 150–250 Wh/kg - **Solid-state Li:** 300–500 Wh/kg (emerging) #### 2.4.5 Specific Power (Gravimetric Power Density) **Specific power** is the maximum power deliverable per unit **mass** of the battery. It governs the **peak performance** (acceleration in EVs, surge current in power tools). $$P_{specific} = \frac{P_{max}}{m_{battery}} = \frac{V \times I_{max}}{m_{battery}} \quad \left[\frac{W}{kg}\right]$$ Typical values: - **Lead-acid:** 75–150 W/kg - **Lithium-ion (LFP):** 200–400 W/kg - **Supercapacitors:** 10,000+ W/kg (highest) #### 2.4.6 Energy Density (Volumetric) — Companion Metric $$E_{volumetric} = \frac{E_{stored}}{V_{battery}} \quad \left[\frac{Wh}{L}\right]$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A **Ragone plot** (log-log chart of specific energy vs. specific power) is used to compare storage technologies. Batteries occupy the *high energy, moderate power* region; supercapacitors occupy the *low energy, ultra-high power* region; fuel cells occupy the *highest energy, low power* region. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary / Limits | |---|-----------|---------|-------|--------------------| | 1 | C-rate Current | $I = C \times C_{Ah}$ | A | $0 < C \leq$ manufacturer's max | | 2 | State of Charge | $SoC = \frac{Q_{rem}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | $0 \leq SoC \leq 100$ | | 3 | Depth of Discharge | $DoD = 100 - SoC$ | % | $0 \leq DoD \leq 100$ | | 4 | SoC + DoD | $SoC + DoD = 100$ | % | Identity (always) | | 5 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \frac{V \cdot Q}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Battery-dependent | | 6 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} = \frac{V \cdot I_{max}}{m}$ | W/kg | Battery-dependent | | 7 | Energy Stored | $E = V \cdot Q = P \cdot t$ | Wh | $E = P \cdot t$ identity | | 8 | Sabatier Reaction | $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ | — | Exothermic, $\Delta H = -165$ kJ/mol | | 9 | Electrolysis | $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$ | — | $\Delta H = +286$ kJ/mol | | 10 | H₂ LHV | $LHV_{H_2} \approx 33.33$ | kWh/kg | At 1 atm, 25 °C | | 11 | CH₄ LHV | $LHV_{CH_4} \approx 50$ | MJ/kg | At 1 atm, 25 °C | | 12 | HHV of H₂ | $HHV_{H_2} \approx 39.4$ | kWh/kg | Includes water condensation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Note:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 3600 kJ. This conversion is *routinely* required in KTU numerical problems involving hydrogen and battery energy. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Numerical Example 1 — C-Rate Current & Discharge Time > **[KTU 2024-Style Numerical]** > *A lithium-ion battery has a rated capacity of 50 Ah and a nominal voltage of 3.7 V. If it is discharged at a rate of C/2, calculate: (a) the discharge current in Amperes, (b) the theoretical discharge time, and (c) the energy delivered.* **Given:** - Rated capacity: $C_{Ah} = 50$ Ah - Nominal voltage: $V = 3.7$ V - C-rate: $C = 0.5$ (since C/2 = 0.5C) **Step 1: Calculate discharge current** $$I = C \times C_{Ah} = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2: Calculate theoretical discharge time** $$t = \frac{C_{Ah}}{I} = \frac{50 \text{ Ah}}{25 \text{ A}} = 2 \text{ hours}$$ *Cross-check using the C-rate definition:* At **0.5C**, full discharge takes $\frac{1}{0.5} = 2$ hours ✓ **Step 3: Calculate energy delivered** $$E = V \times C_{Ah} = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 50 \text{ Ah} = 185 \text{ Wh}$$ $$E = 185 \text{ Wh} = 0.185 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Tip:** Always show *units* at every step. Examiners deduct marks when students write a number with no unit or wrong unit. --- ### 3.2 Numerical Example 2 — Specific Energy & Specific Power > **[KTU 2024-Style Numerical]** > *A battery pack for an electric two-wheeler has a total mass of 8 kg and stores 1.44 kWh of energy. Its maximum continuous discharge current is 60 A at a nominal voltage of 48 V. Calculate: (a) Specific energy, (b) Maximum specific power.* **Given:** - Mass of battery pack: $m = 8$ kg - Energy stored: $E_{stored} = 1.44$ kWh = $1440$ Wh - Maximum current: $I_{max} = 60$ A - Nominal voltage: $V = 48$ V **Step 1: Calculate specific energy** $$E_{specific} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m} = \frac{1440 \text{ Wh}}{8 \text{ kg}} = 180 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ **Step 2: Calculate maximum power output** $$P_{max} = V \times I_{max} = 48 \text{ V} \times 60 \text{ A} = 2880 \text{ W}$$ **Step 3: Calculate specific power** $$P_{specific} = \frac{P_{max}}{m} = \frac{2880 \text{ W}}{8 \text{ kg}} = 360 \text{ W/kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Range vs. Acceleration:** A higher specific energy (Wh/kg) means more *range* per kg of battery. A higher specific power (W/kg) means better *acceleration* or *peak performance*. EV designers must balance both. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Example 3 — SoC and DoD with Partial Discharge > **[KTU 2024-Style Numerical]** > *A 100 Ah battery is initially at 80% SoC. It supplies a constant current of 25 A for 90 minutes. Calculate: (a) the charge removed, (b) the final SoC, (c) the final DoD.* **Given:** - Initial capacity: $C_{Ah} = 100$ Ah - Initial SoC: $SoC_0 = 80\%$ - Discharge current: $I = 25$ A - Time: $t = 90$ min = 1.5 h **Step 1: Charge removed during discharge** $$Q_{removed} = I \times t = 25 \text{ A} \times 1.5 \text{ h} = 37.5 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 2: Final SoC** $$SoC_{final} = SoC_0 - \frac{Q_{removed}}{C_{Ah}} \times 100\%$$ $$SoC_{final} = 80 - \frac{37.5}{100} \times 100\% = 80 - 37.5 = 42.5\%$$ **Step 3: Final DoD** $$DoD_{final} = 100\% - SoC_{final} = 100 - 42.5 = 57.5\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** Initial DoD was 20% (since SoC was 80%). We removed 37.5% more from full → final DoD = 20 + 37.5 = 57.5% ✓ --- ### 3.4 Numerical Example 4 — C-Rate Effect on Discharge Time > **[KTU 2024-Style Numerical]** > *A battery rated 200 Ah is discharged at 2C. How long does it take to reach 80% DoD?* **Given:** - Capacity: $C_{Ah} = 200$ Ah - C-rate: $C = 2$ - Final DoD: $DoD = 80\%$ **Step 1: Calculate discharge current** $$I = C \times C_{Ah} = 2 \times 200 = 400 \text{ A}$$ **Step 2: Calculate charge corresponding to 80% DoD** $$Q_{removed} = DoD \times C_{Ah} = 0.80 \times 200 = 160 \text{ Ah}$$ **Step 3: Calculate time to reach 80% DoD** $$t = \frac{Q_{removed}}{I} = \frac{160 \text{ Ah}}{400 \text{ A}} = 0.4 \text{ h} = 24 \text{ minutes}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Insight:** At higher C-rates, the *Peukert effect* causes the actual delivered capacity to be **less** than rated. The above calculation is *theoretical* (no Peukert losses). For realistic estimates, multiply by a derating factor. --- ### 3.5 Numerical Example 5 — Synthetic Methane Production (P2G Mass Balance) > **[KTU 2024-Style Numerical]** > *A P2G plant uses 100 kg of CO₂ to produce synthetic methane. Assuming 100% conversion in the Sabatier reaction, calculate: (a) the mass of H₂ required, (b) the mass of CH₄ produced, and (c) the energy content of the CH₄ produced.* > *Molar masses: CO₂ = 44 g/mol, H₂ = 2 g/mol, CH₄ = 16 g/mol. LHV of CH₄ = 50 MJ/kg.* **Stoichiometry:** $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$$ **Step 1: Moles of CO₂** $$n_{CO_2} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ g}}{44 \text{ g/mol}} = 2272.73 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 2: Moles (and mass) of H₂ required** $$n_{H_2} = 4 \times n_{CO_2} = 4 \times 2272.73 = 9090.91 \text{ mol}$$ $$m_{H_2} = 9090.91 \text{ mol} \times 2 \text{ g/mol} = 18{,}181.82 \text{ g} \approx 18.18 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 3: Moles (and mass) of CH₄ produced** $$n_{CH_4} = 1 \times n_{CO_2} = 2272.73 \text{ mol}$$ $$m_{CH_4} = 2272.73 \text{ mol} \times 16 \text{ g/mol} = 36{,}363.64 \text{ g} \approx 36.36 \text{ kg}$$ **Step 4: Energy content of CH₄ produced** $$E_{CH_4} = m_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4} = 36.36 \text{ kg} \times 50{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg} = 1{,}818{,}182 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{CH_4} = \frac{1{,}818{,}182}{3600} \approx 505.05 \text{ kWh}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that 100 kg of CO₂ is converted into ~36.4 kg of CH₄ — a mass reduction of 64%. The "missing" mass becomes water (H₂O). This is the heart of *carbon utilization*: CO₂ is upcycled into a high-value fuel. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Power-to-Gas (P2G) Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Renewable Power Source
Wind or Solar PV] --> B[AC to DC Rectifier] B --> C[Electrolyzer Unit
ALK or PEM or SOEC] C --> D[O2 Vent
By product Oxygen] C --> E[H2 Storage Tank
Compressed or Liquefied] E --> F{Direct H2 Use or
Methanation Stage} F -->|Direct Use| G[Fuel Cell or
H2 Pipeline Injection] F -->|Methanation Path| H[Sabatier Reactor
CO2 plus H2 at 200 to 400 C] I[CO2 Source
Biogas or Flue Gas or DAC] --> H H --> J[CH4 Purification and
Drying Unit] J --> K[Synthetic Natural Gas
Grid Injection] K --> L[End User
Heating or Transport or Power] ``` ### 4.2 Battery Parameter Relationship Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rated Capacity Q in Ah] --> B[Energy Stored E in Wh
E equals V times Q] A --> C[Mass of Battery m in kg] B --> D[Specific Energy
E divided by m in Wh per kg] C --> D A --> E[C rate] E --> F[Discharge Current I
I equals C times Q] A --> G[State of Charge SoC
Q remaining divided by Q times 100 percent] G --> H[Depth of Discharge DoD
DoD equals 100 minus SoC] F --> I[Specific Power
V times I max divided by m in W per kg] C --> I ``` ### 4.3 Battery Discharge Characteristics vs. C-Rate ```mermaid graph LR subgraph HighCRate["High C rate Region"] A1[1C discharge] --> A2[Fast power delivery
Lower usable capacity
Peukert losses high] end subgraph LowCRate["Low C rate Region"] B1[0.2C discharge] --> B2[Slower power delivery
Higher usable capacity
Peukert losses low] end subgraph MidCRate["Standard C rate Region"] C1[0.5C to 1C] --> C2[Balanced performance
Nominal capacity delivered] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The block-level flow shows that all battery parameters originate from two fundamental inputs — the **rated capacity Q** and the **battery mass m**. C-rate is the *bridge* between capacity (energy) and current (power), and SoC/DoD are the *mirror-image* state variables. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** *Define the term "Power-to-Gas" (P2G). Briefly explain the two main products that can be obtained from a P2G process.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Power-to-Gas (P2G) is a long-duration energy storage technology that converts surplus electrical energy, typically from renewable sources, into a gaseous fuel through water electrolysis. The **first product** is **hydrogen (H₂)**, produced by splitting water using an electrolyzer. The **second (optional) product** is **synthetic methane (CH₄)**, also called Substitute Natural Gas (SNG), produced by reacting the H₂ with CO₂ in a Sabatier methanation reactor. While H₂ is the direct product, CH₄ offers the advantage of compatibility with the existing natural gas grid, enabling large-scale, seasonal storage. *(Examiner allocation: 1 mark for P2G definition + 1 mark for H₂ + 1 mark for synthetic CH₄/SNG)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** *Define the following battery parameters with units: (i) Specific Energy, (ii) Specific Power, (iii) C-rate.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(i) Specific Energy:** The amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass of the battery, expressed in **Wh/kg**. It governs the runtime or range of the application. Formula: $E_{sp} = \frac{V \times Q}{m}$. - **(ii) Specific Power:** The maximum power deliverable per unit mass of the battery, expressed in **W/kg**. It governs the peak performance (acceleration, surge loads). Formula: $P_{sp} = \frac{V \times I_{max}}{m}$. - **(iii) C-rate:** A dimensionless number that indicates the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its rated capacity. A 1C rate corresponds to a current equal to the rated Ah capacity, giving a full discharge in 1 hour. Formula: $I = C \times C_{Ah}$. *(Examiner allocation: 1 mark per parameter — definition + unit)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the Power-to-Gas (P2G) process. Explain the Sabatier reaction used for the production of synthetic methane. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A battery pack rated at 400 V, 200 Ah has a total mass of 250 kg. It is discharged at 0.5C until it reaches 30% SoC, starting from 95% SoC. Calculate: (i) the discharge current, (ii) the energy delivered during the discharge, (iii) the specific energy of the battery pack. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The Power-to-Gas process converts surplus renewable electricity into a gaseous fuel. The functional flow is: **Renewable Power (Wind/Solar)** → **AC-DC Rectifier** → **Electrolyzer (ALK/PEM/SOEC)** → **Hydrogen Storage** → **Optional Sabatier Methanation** → **Synthetic Natural Gas Grid Injection** At the electrolyzer, water is split: $$2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$$ The hydrogen can be used directly (e.g., in fuel cells) or upgraded to methane. The **Sabatier reaction** is: $$CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O \quad \Delta H = -165 \text{ kJ/mol}$$ This is an **exothermic** reaction, typically conducted over a **nickel-based catalyst** at temperatures of **200–400 °C** and elevated pressures. The CO₂ feedstock is sourced from industrial flue gases, biogas, or direct air capture. The product water is condensed and removed, and the methane is dried, compressed, and injected into the natural gas grid. *Examiner marking scheme (a): 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for P2G explanation, 3 marks for Sabatier reaction (stoichiometry, conditions, catalyst).* --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $V = 400$ V, $C_{Ah} = 200$ Ah, $m = 250$ kg, C-rate = 0.5C, $SoC_0 = 95\%$, $SoC_f = 30\%$ **(i) Discharge current:** $$I = 0.5 \times 200 = 100 \text{ A}$$ **(ii) Energy delivered during discharge:** The fraction of capacity discharged: $$\Delta SoC = SoC_0 - SoC_f = 95 - 30 = 65\%$$ Energy stored in the full pack: $$E_{full} = V \times C_{Ah} = 400 \times 200 = 80{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 80 \text{ kWh}$$ Energy delivered (65% of full pack): $$E_{delivered} = 0.65 \times 80{,}000 = 52{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 52 \text{ kWh}$$ **(iii) Specific energy:** $$E_{specific} = \frac{E_{full}}{m} = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{250 \text{ kg}} = 320 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ *Examiner marking scheme (b): (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks (1 for ΔSoC, 1 for full energy, 1 for delivered), (iii) 2 marks.* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative* > **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the concept of State of Charge (SoC) and Depth of Discharge (DoD) in batteries. How are they related? Why is the DoD specification important for cycle life estimation? **(7 marks)** **(b)** An electric vehicle uses a 350 V, 120 Ah lithium-ion battery pack with a total mass of 180 kg. The vehicle's average power demand is 15 kW. Calculate: (i) the total energy stored in the pack in kWh, (ii) the specific energy in Wh/kg, (iii) the C-rate corresponding to the average power demand, (iv) the theoretical driving range in hours at this average power. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **State of Charge (SoC)** is the ratio of the remaining capacity to the rated (maximum) capacity, expressed as a percentage. A fully charged battery has SoC = 100%; a fully discharged battery has SoC = 0%. $$SoC(t) = \frac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$ **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** is the complement of SoC — it represents the percentage of the battery's capacity that has been discharged: $$DoD(t) = 100\% - SoC(t)$$ **Relationship:** SoC and DoD always sum to 100%. If the SoC is 40%, the DoD is 60%. They are two sides of the same coin. **Why DoD matters for cycle life:** Battery cycle life is **inversely** related to DoD. A battery cycled at 100% DoD may last only ~500 cycles, but the same battery cycled at 50% DoD may last 1500–2000 cycles. This is because deeper discharges cause greater mechanical stress (electrode expansion/contraction) and SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) layer growth on the anode. Battery manufacturers publish cycle life vs. DoD curves to help designers size the battery bank — oversizing (using only 50% DoD) dramatically extends life. *Examiner marking scheme (a): 2 marks for SoC, 2 marks for DoD + relationship, 3 marks for cycle life / DoD importance.* --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $V = 350$ V, $C_{Ah} = 120$ Ah, $m = 180$ kg, $P_{avg} = 15$ kW **(i) Total energy stored:** $$E_{full} = V \times C_{Ah} = 350 \times 120 = 42{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 42 \text{ kWh}$$ **(ii) Specific energy:** $$E_{specific} = \frac{42{,}000}{180} = 233.33 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ **(iii) C-rate at average power:** First, find the average discharge current: $$I_{avg} = \frac{P_{avg}}{V} = \frac{15{,}000}{350} = 42.86 \text{ A}$$ Now find the C-rate: $$C = \frac{I_{avg}}{C_{Ah}} = \frac{42.86}{120} = 0.357 \text{ C}$$ **(iv) Theoretical driving range in hours:** $$t = \frac{E_{full}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{42 \text{ kWh}}{15 \text{ kW}} = 2.8 \text{ hours}$$ *Examiner marking scheme (b): (i) 2 marks, (ii) 1 mark, (iii) 2 marks (1 for current, 1 for C-rate), (iv) 2 marks.* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Students often write battery capacity in Ah and voltage in V but forget to convert the resulting Wh to kWh (divide by 1000). Always check the *target unit* in the question. > 2. **Confusing C-rate with current:** A 2C rate is NOT 2 Amperes. It means the current equals 2 × the rated capacity in Ah. Many students write "2C = 2A" — this is wrong and costs a mark. > 3. **Mixing SoC and DoD:** A battery at "30% SoC" is at "70% DoD" — not "30% DoD". Read the question carefully. > 4. **Sabatier reaction stoichiometry:** The molar ratio is 1 CO₂ : 4 H₂ : 1 CH₄. Students sometimes write 1:2:1, which is incorrect. > 5. **Sign of ΔH:** The Sabatier reaction is exothermic (ΔH < 0); electrolysis is endothermic (ΔH > 0). Examiners may ask this as a 1-mark "trick" question. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - **Power-to-Gas (P2G):** Surplus electricity → H₂ via electrolysis → optionally CH₄ via Sabatier. A *sector-coupling* technology. - **H₂ production:** Electrolysis of water: $2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$. Technologies: ALK, PEM, SOEC. - **Synthetic methane (SNG):** Produced by the **Sabatier reaction** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$, $\Delta H = -165$ kJ/mol, catalyzed by **Ni** at **200–400 °C**. - **Why SNG?** Grid compatibility, large seasonal storage, carbon circularity, high volumetric energy density. - **H₂ LHV:** ~**33.33 kWh/kg**; **CH₄ LHV:** ~**50 MJ/kg** (~13.9 kWh/kg). - **Conversion:** 1 kWh = **3.6 MJ** = **3600 kJ**. - **C-rate formula:** $I = C \times C_{Ah}$. At 1C, full discharge in 1 hour; at 2C, in 30 min; at 0.5C, in 2 hours. - **SoC formula:** $SoC = \frac{Q_{rem}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$, range [0, 100%]. - **DoD formula:** $DoD = 100 - SoC$, range [0, 100%]. - **Identity:** $SoC + DoD = 100\%$ (always). - **Specific energy:** $E_{sp} = \frac{V \times Q}{m}$ in **Wh/kg** — governs *range*. - **Specific power:** $P_{sp} = \frac{V \times I_{max}}{m}$ in **W/kg** — governs *acceleration/peak*. - **Cycle life vs. DoD:** Higher DoD per cycle → fewer total cycles (e.g., 100% DoD ≈ 500 cycles; 50% DoD ≈ 1500+ cycles). - **Li-ion range:** Specific energy 150–250 Wh/kg; specific power 200–400 W/kg. - **Lead-acid range:** Specific energy 30–50 Wh/kg; specific power 75–150 W/kg. - **Ragone plot:** Log-log chart comparing specific energy vs. specific power across storage technologies. - **Energy = Power × Time:** $E = P \times t$ (in Wh when P in W, t in h). - **KTU exam pattern:** Expect 1 numerical on battery parameters (3 or 7 marks) + 1 theory question on P2G/Sabatier (7 marks).

topics.title: Fuel cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Cell (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A fuel cell is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (commonly hydrogen) and an oxidant (commonly oxygen from air) into electrical energy, with **water and heat** as the by-products, through a controlled redox (oxidation–reduction) reaction mediated by an **electrolyte** and catalysed by electrodes (anode and cathode). Unlike a battery, the reactants are supplied externally, allowing continuous operation as long as fuel and oxidant are fed.

The standard cell reaction for the archetypal **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEMFC)** hydrogen–oxygen fuel cell is given as:

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The corresponding half-reactions are:

$$\text{Anode (Oxidation):} \quad H_2 \rightarrow 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

$$\text{Cathode (Reduction):} \quad \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2O$$

$$\text{Overall:} \quad H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Ever-Refilling Water Glass"**
> Imagine an electrical **glass of water** (a battery). You drink it (use the energy), and it becomes empty. You then have to recharge it (refill it at a charging station).
> A **fuel cell**, on the other hand, is like a **kitchen tap** that delivers energy the moment you turn it on — as long as the city water supply (the hydrogen tank) and the air (oxygen) are connected, the tap never runs dry. You do not "recharge" it; you simply **refuel the hydrogen cylinder**, just like refuelling a CNG car. The electrolyte and electrodes act as the tap's internal mechanism, and the catalyst (usually Platinum) is the bit that makes the water (electricity) actually flow.

## 1.3 Salient Engineering Metrics

The most important metrics used by KTU examiners and the global energy industry are:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (PEMFC) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage | $E^0$ | **1.229** | V |
| Operating Cell Voltage | $V_{cell}$ | **0.6 – 0.75** | V |
| Faradaic (Fuel) Efficiency | $\eta_F$ | **> 95 %** | % |
| Electrical (Voltage) Efficiency | $\eta_V$ | **50 – 65 %** | % |
| Overall System Efficiency | $\eta_{sys}$ | **40 – 60 %** | % |
| Power Density | $P_d$ | **0.5 – 1.0** | W/cm² |
| Operating Temperature | $T$ | **60 – 80** (PEM), 800 (SOFC) | °C |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** Remember the magic number **1.229 V** — it is the thermodynamic single-cell open-circuit voltage for the H₂/O₂ reaction at standard conditions (25 °C, 1 atm). Examiners *love* asking why a real fuel cell operates at only ~0.6–0.7 V (the answer is **activation, ohmic, and concentration losses**).

## 1.4 Why Fuel Cells Matter in KTU's Energy Curriculum

- They are **zero-emission at point of use** (only water comes out of the tailpipe).
- They have **2–3× higher efficiency** than internal combustion engines.
- They are the cornerstone of the upcoming **Hydrogen Economy** — a flagship KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis aligned with India's **National Hydrogen Mission (2021)**.
- Unlike batteries, they offer **high specific energy** and rapid refuelling — vital for heavy transport and grid backup.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Fuel supply (Hydrogen)**: H₂ gas (or methanol, formic acid) is fed to the **anode** flow-field plate.
2. **Oxidation at the Anode**: A platinum-based catalyst splits H₂ into **2H⁺ (protons)** and **2e⁻ (electrons)**. The electrons are *forced* through the external circuit (this is the **useful current**); the protons migrate through the electrolyte.
3. **Oxidant supply (Oxygen)**: O₂ from air is fed to the **cathode** flow-field plate.
4. **Reduction at the Cathode**: O₂ combines with the arriving protons and (externally routed) electrons to form **H₂O** and release **heat**.
5. **External load**: The electron flow from anode → external circuit → cathode constitutes the **DC electrical output**.
6. **Water + Heat management**: Product water is removed (humidified gas stream in PEMFC); waste heat may be co-generated (CHP applications).

## 2.2 The Core Thermodynamics — Why 1.229 V?

The maximum electrical work obtainable from a chemical reaction is the **change in Gibbs Free Energy** $\Delta G$.

$$W_{elec,max} = -\Delta G = n \cdot F \cdot E^0$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per mole of reaction (= 2 for H₂)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ 96485)
- $E^0$ = Standard reversible cell potential (V)

Solving for $E^0$:

$$E^0 = \frac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$$

For the H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(l) reaction at 25 °C, $\Delta G^0$ = **–237.2 kJ/mol**.

$$E^0 = \frac{-(-237{,}200)\, \text{J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485\, \text{C/mol}} = 1.229\, \text{V}$$

## 2.3 The Nernst Equation — Real Conditions

When the cell does **not** operate at standard pressure/temperature, the Nernst equation gives the actual reversible potential:

$$E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

where:
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $P_i$ = Partial pressures (atm) of species $i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Increasing $P_{H_2}$ or $P_{O_2}$ **increases** $E$, but increasing $P_{H_2O}$ (product) **decreases** $E$. This is Le Chatelier's principle written in electrochemistry form — a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 Real Cell Voltage — The Polarisation Curve

The actual operating voltage is always less than $E$ due to **irreversibilities**:

$$V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$$

| Loss Term | Symbol | Physical Origin | Dominates At |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activation loss | $\eta_{act}$ | Sluggish electrode kinetics (energy barrier at catalyst) | **Low current** |
| Ohmic loss | $\eta_{ohm}$ | Ionic resistance of electrolyte + electronic resistance of electrodes | **Medium current** |
| Concentration loss | $\eta_{conc}$ | Mass-transport limitation of reactants to electrode | **High current** |

The **Tafel equation** quantifies activation loss:

$$\eta_{act} = a + b \log_{10}(i)$$

where $a$ and $b$ are Tafel constants, and $i$ is the **current density** (A/cm²).

Ohmic loss follows simple Ohm's law (in fuel-cell language):

$$\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$$

where $R_{ASR}$ is the **Area-Specific Resistance** (Ω·cm²) of the membrane + electrodes.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!TIP]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Sheet for Fuel Cells**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = \dfrac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Standard reversible voltage | $\Delta G^0, n, F$ | V |
| 2 | $E = E^0 + \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2}\,P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst (actual reversible) voltage | $R, T, P_i$ | V |
| 3 | $E^0_T = E^0_{298} + \dfrac{\Delta S^0}{nF}(T-298)$ | Temperature correction of $E^0$ | $\Delta S^0$ | V |
| 4 | $V_{cell} = E - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}$ | Real operating voltage | $\eta$'s | V |
| 5 | $\eta_{act} = a + b\log_{10} i$ | Tafel activation loss | $a, b, i$ | V |
| 6 | $\eta_{ohm} = i \cdot R_{ASR}$ | Ohmic loss | $R_{ASR}$ | V |
| 7 | $\eta_F = \dfrac{i}{i_L}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{i}{i_L}\right)$ (approx) | Faradaic efficiency (fuel utilisation) | $i, i_L$ | — |
| 8 | $\eta_{V} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{E^0}$ | Voltage efficiency | — | — |
| 9 | $\eta_{HHV} = \dfrac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | Thermodynamic (HHV) limit ≈ 0.83 for H₂ | $\Delta H$ | — |
| 10 | $P_{out} = V_{cell} \cdot i \cdot A$ | Power output (A = electrode area) | $A$ | W |
| 11 | $W_{max} = nFE$ | Max electrical work per mole | — | J/mol |

**Constants to memorise (no calculator allowed in Part A!):**
- $F = 96{,}485\, \text{C/mol}$
- $R = 8.314\, \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $RT/F$ at 298 K = **0.02569 V**
- $2.303\,RT/F$ at 298 K = **0.0591 V** (used in $\log_{10}$ form of Nernst)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Space applications (NASA Apollo, Space Shuttle):** AFC (Alkaline Fuel Cells) supplied both electricity and drinking water.
- **Automotive (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo):** PEMFC stacks at 650 V, ~100 kW.
- **Stationary power (Bloom Energy, India Hydrogen Mission):** SOFC and MCFC for 100 kW – 50 MW grids.
- **Portable electronics & military:** Direct-methanol fuel cells (DMFC) for soldiers' field power.
- **Backup power for telecom towers:** PEMFC replacing diesel gensets — a growing KTU-relevant industry in Kerala.
