| Round-trip efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = E_{out} \div E_{in}$ | dimensionless (%) | All storage systems |
| Specific energy | $\rho_E = E/m$ | Wh/kg | Mass-critical (EV, aerospace) |
| Energy density (volumetric) | $\rho_{Ev} = E/V$ | Wh/L | Space-critical (portable) |
| Specific power | $\rho_P = P/m$ | W/kg | Power-burst applications |
| Discharge time | $\tau = E/P$ | hours | Classifies system type |
| Self-discharge rate | $\sigma = \Delta E_{idle} \div E_{0}$ | %/month | Standby loss assessment |
| State of Charge | $SOC = E_{remaining} \div E_{rated}$ | dimensionless | Battery management |
| Depth of Discharge | $DOD = E_{discharged} \div E_{rated}$ | dimensionless | Cycle life impact |
| Cycle life to 80% capacity | $N_{80} = f(\text{chemistry}, DOD, T)$ | cycles | Warranty planning |
| Levelized Cost of Storage | $LCOS = (CAPEX + \sum OPEX_t/(1+r)^t) \div \sum E_{out,t}/(1+r)^t$ | \$/kWh | Economic comparison |
| Peukert's law (lead-acid) | $I^n \cdot t = C$ | — | Capacity vs. discharge rate |
| Energy stored in flywheel | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | J | Mechanical storage |
| Energy stored in capacitor | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Capacitive storage |
| Magnetic energy (SMES) | $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$ | J | Inductive storage |
| Pumped hydro potential | $E = \eta \cdot m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J | Gravitational storage |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently confuse **energy density** (Wh/kg) with **power density** (W/kg). Remember: a **supercapacitor** can have 10× the power density of a Li-ion battery but only 1/20th the energy density. They are **not interchangeable**.

## 2.5 Selection Logic — "Matching Technology to Application"

Comparative analysis ultimately serves **selection**. The KTU 2024 module teaches students to use a **decision matrix**:

1. **Identify the application profile** (energy vs. power dominant).
2. **Assign weights** to each parameter based on application needs.
3. **Score each technology** (0–10) on each parameter.
4. **Compute weighted scores** to rank technologies.
5. **Perform sensitivity analysis** to validate robustness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use Case:** The Southern California Edison (SCE) grid uses a **hybrid Li-ion + flywheel** system at the Tehachapi wind farm. The comparative analysis showed neither alone was optimal — Li-ion for energy shifting, flywheel for frequency regulation — and the hybrid out-performs either in LCOS and response time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Round-Trip Efficiency from First Principles

Let us derive the round-trip efficiency considering charging and discharging losses.

**Step 1 — Define the charging process.**
During charging, the input energy $E_{in}$ is delivered to the storage device. Due to internal losses (resistive $I^2R$, electrochemical overpotentials, thermal dissipation), only a fraction reaches the stored energy reservoir.

$$
E_{stored} = E_{in} \cdot \eta_{ch}
$$

Where $\eta_{ch}$ is the coulombic + voltage efficiency during charging.

**Step 2 — Define the discharging process.**
During discharging, the stored energy $E_{stored}$ is converted back, but again with internal losses.

$$
E_{out} = E_{stored} \cdot \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine the two stages.**
Substituting Step 1 into Step 2:

$$
E_{out} = (E_{in} \cdot \eta_{ch}) \cdot \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract the round-trip efficiency.**
The round-trip efficiency is defined as the ratio of output to input:

$$
\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example (KTU-type problem).**
Suppose a battery charges at 90% efficiency and discharges at 95% efficiency. Then:

$$
\eta_{RT} = 0.90 \times 0.95 = 0.855 = 85.5\%
$$

This single number tells the engineer: for every **100 kWh** put in, only **85.5 kWh** comes out. The remaining **14.5 kWh** is lost as heat.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Characteristic Discharge Time $\tau$

**Step 1 — Start from definitions.**

$$
\rho_E = \frac{E}{m}, \quad \rho_P = \frac{P}{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\frac{\rho_E}{\rho_P} = \frac{E/m}{P/m} = \frac{E}{P}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize E/P as discharge time.**

$$
\tau = \frac{E}{P} \quad [\text{hours}]
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation table.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau > 4\ \text{h} &\Rightarrow \text{Energy-oriented (PHS, CAES, batteries)} \\
0.1\ \text{h} < \tau < 4\ \text{h} &\Rightarrow \text{Energy/Power hybrid (Li-ion, NaS)} \\
\tau < 0.1\ \text{h} &\Rightarrow \text{Power-oriented (supercapacitor, flywheel, SMES)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — LCOS Simplification for KTU Problems

In exam problems, a simplified LCOS formula is often used:

$$
LCOS = \frac{CAPEX + OPEX \cdot N}{E_{rated} \cdot DOD \cdot \eta_{RT} \cdot N \cdot (1 - degradation)^N}
$$

**Step-by-step simplified computation:**

Given:
- $CAPEX = \$200{,}000$
- $OPEX = \$5{,}000/\text{year}$
- $N = 15$ years
- $E_{rated} = 1{,}000$ kWh
- $DOD = 80\% = 0.8$
- $\eta_{RT} = 0.85$
- Annual cycle degradation = 2% (capacity fade)

**Step 1 — Compute total cost over lifetime.**

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 200{,}000 + 5{,}000 \times 15 = 200{,}000 + 75{,}000 = \$275{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute useful energy delivered per year (Year 1).**

$$
E_{year,1} = 1{,}000 \times 0.8 \times 0.85 = 680\ \text{kWh/cycle}
$$

For 365 cycles/year (one cycle per day):

$$
E_{annual,1} = 680 \times 365 = 248{,}200\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply degradation factor over 15 years (geometric series).**

$$
\sum_{t=1}^{15} (1 - 0.02)^{t-1} = \sum_{t=0}^{14} (0.98)^t = \frac{1 - 0.98^{15}}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{1 - 0.7419}{0.02} = 12.905
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total lifetime energy delivered.**

$$
E_{lifetime} = 248{,}200 \times 12.905 = 3{,}203{,}441\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute LCOS.**

$$
LCOS = \frac{275{,}000}{3{,}203{,}441} = \$0.0858/\text{kWh} \approx \$0.086/\text{kWh}
$$

**Conclusion:** This storage system delivers energy at approximately **8.6 cents per kWh** over its lifetime — a benchmark value competitive with peak-grid electricity tariffs.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU Board's most-tested topic in Module 2.** Memorize the relative ordering of each metric.

| Parameter | PHS | CAES | Lead-Acid | Li-ion | NaS | VRFB | Flywheel | Supercapacitor | H₂ / Fuel Cell | Thermal (PCM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 0.5–1.5 | 30–60 | 30–50 | 100–250 | 150–240 | 20–50 | 5–80 | 2–10 | 500–3000 (incl. tank) | 50–200 |
| **Specific Power (W/kg)** | — | — | 75–300 | 200–1500 | 100–250 | 50–100 | 500–5000 | 5000–15000 | — | — |
| **Round-Trip Efficiency** | 70–85% | 42–55% | 70–85% | 85–95% | 85–90% | 70–85% | 85–95% | 90–98% | 35–50% | 30–60% |
| **Cycle Life (cycles)** | >50,000 | >10,000 | 300–1500 | 1000–10,000 | 2500–4500 | >10,000 | >100,000 | >100,000 | — | 1000–5000 |
| **Self-Discharge** | ~0% | ~0% | 3–20%/mo | 1–5%/mo | ~0% | ~0% | 20–100%/h | 5–40%/h | Very low | ~0% |
| **Response Time** | s–min | s–min | ms | ms | ms | ms | ms | $\mu$s–ms | s–min | min |
| **Calendar Life (yrs)** | 50–100 | 30–50 | 5–15 | 10–20 | 15–20 | 15–20 | 15–25 | 10–20 | — | 10–30 |
| **CAPEX (\$/kWh)** | 5–100 | 50–200 | 100–300 | 200–600 | 300–500 | 300–800 | 500–2000 | 500–2000 | 500–1500 | 20–100 |
| **CAPEX (\$/kW)** | 500–2500 | 500–1500 | 200–1000 | 500–2000 | 1000–3000 | 700–2500 | 300–1000 | 100–500 | 1000–5000 | 200–800 |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 | 8 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 6–7 | 7 |
| **Best Suited For** | Bulk grid | Bulk grid | Backup, UPS | EVs, residential | Grid, industrial | Long-duration | Frequency regulation | Power bursts | Long-duration seasonal | Solar thermal |

**Note:** Values are representative engineering ranges and may vary by manufacturer and design.

## 3.5 Application-Based Selection Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Application} &\rightarrow \textbf{Recommended Storage} \\
\text{EV Traction} &\rightarrow \text{Li-ion (high energy + power density)} \\
\text{Solar PV residential} &\rightarrow \text{Li-ion or Lead-acid} \\
\text{Grid frequency regulation} &\rightarrow \text{Flywheel or Li-ion (fast response)} \\
\text{Seasonal storage} &\rightarrow \text{PHS or H}_2/\text{fuel cell} \\
\text{Power quality / UPS} &\rightarrow \text{Supercapacitor + Lead-acid} \\
\text{Off-grid microgrid} &\rightarrow \text{Hybrid: Li-ion + supercapacitor + H}_2 \\
\text{Concentrated Solar Power} &\rightarrow \text{Thermal (molten salt / PCM)}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Comparative Analysis Decision Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application Requirements] --> B{Energy or Power Dominant?}
    B -->|Energy Dominant| C{Response Time Required?}
    B -->|Power Dominant| D{Frequency Regulation or Power Quality?}
    C -->|Slow Minutes| E[Long-Duration Storage]
    C -->|Fast Milliseconds| F[Hybrid: Battery + Capacitor]
    D -->|Frequency Regulation| G[Flywheel Energy Storage]
    D -->|Power Quality| H[Supercapacitor or SMES]
    E -->|Hours Needed| I[Pumped Hydro Storage PHS]
    E -->|Days to Seasons| J[Hydrogen Fuel Cell System]
    E -->|Moderate 4-10 Hours| K[Li-ion or Flow Battery]
    F --> L[Battery + Supercapacitor Hybrid]
    G --> M[Flywheel FESS]
    H --> N[Supercapacitor EDLC]
    I --> O[Final Selection: PHS]
    J --> P[Final Selection: H2 FC]
    K --> Q[Final Selection: Li-ion]
    L --> R[Final Selection: Hybrid Pack]
    M --> S[Final Selection: Flywheel]
    N --> T[Final Selection: Supercapacitor]
    O --> U[Validate with LCOS and LCA]
    P --> U
    Q --> U
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Engineering Decision Finalized]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Ragone Plot Architecture (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighPowerLowEnergy["High Power - Low Energy Region"]
        SC[Supercapacitor]
        FW[Flywheel]
        SMES[SMES]
    end
    subgraph MidRegion["Mid Energy - Mid Power Region"]
        LA[Lead-Acid]
        LI[Li-ion]
        NAS[NaS Battery]
    end
    subgraph HighEnergyLowPower["High Energy - Low Power Region"]
        H2[Hydrogen Fuel Cell]
        VRFB[Vanadium Redox Flow]
    end
    subgraph BulkRegion["Bulk Energy Region"]
        PHS[Pumped Hydro]
        CAES[Compressed Air]
    end
    XAxis[Specific Energy Wh/kg increasing] --> HighPowerLowEnergy
    HighPowerLowEnergy --> MidRegion
    MidRegion --> HighEnergyLowPower
    HighEnergyLowPower --> BulkRegion
    YAxis[Specific Power W/kg increasing] --> HighPowerLowEnergy
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Hybrid Energy Storage System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Renewable Source Solar Wind] --> CONV1[Power Conversion Unit 1]
    CONV1 --> BUS[DC Bus]
    BUS --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    BMS --> BAT[Li-ion Battery Bank]
    BUS --> SUP[Supercapacitor Bank]
    BUS --> INV[Inverter]
    INV --> LD[AC Load Grid]
    BAT -.->|Bidirectional Flow| BUS
    SUP -.->|Bidirectional Flow| BUS

    SUP --- CTRL

    CTRL -->|Peak Shaving Logic| SUP
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define round-trip efficiency of an energy storage system. Why is it a critical parameter in comparative analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
Round-trip efficiency $\eta_{RT}$ is the ratio of the energy delivered by a storage system during discharge to the energy supplied during charging, expressed as a percentage. It is mathematically given as $\eta_{RT} = (E_{out}/E_{in}) \times 100\%$. It is critical in comparative analysis because it directly determines the **usable energy yield** per unit of input energy, which in turn governs the **LCOS**, the **operating cost**, and the **carbon footprint** of the storage system. A higher $\eta_{RT}$ means lower losses and better economic return. *(3 marks)*

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a Ragone plot? Explain its significance in comparing energy storage technologies.**

**Model Answer:**
A Ragone plot is a logarithmic scatter plot with **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis and **specific power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis. Each energy storage technology occupies a unique region on this plot, allowing engineers to visually compare energy-versus-power trade-offs. Its significance lies in enabling **instant technology selection** based on application demands — for instance, fuel cells lie in the high-energy region, while supercapacitors dominate the high-power region. The plot also reveals the existence of "empty zones," which motivate the development of **hybrid storage systems**. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. Students often present the comparison table without **justifying** the parameter choice. Always explain *why* each metric is relevant.
> 2. Numerical LCOS problems are frequently attempted but contain **arithmetic errors in the geometric series** of degradation. Double-check $(1-d)^N$ terms.
> 3. Ragone plots are asked but students draw linear axes — **always use log-log scale** for both axes.
> 4. Hybrid system questions require **clear functional partitioning** of energy vs. power duties.
> 5. Confusing **CAPEX/kWh** with **CAPEX/kW** is a recurrent, costly mistake. kWh = energy cost; kW = power cost.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare the following energy storage technologies on the basis of (i) energy density, (ii) power density, (iii) cycle life, (iv) round-trip efficiency, and (v) self-discharge rate: **Pumped Hydro Storage, Lithium-ion Battery, and Supercapacitor**. Present the answer in a tabular form with appropriate units. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the concept of a **Ragone plot**. Discuss how a hybrid battery-supercapacitor system can overcome the limitations of either technology when used independently. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Pumped Hydro (PHS) | Li-ion Battery | Supercapacitor |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 0.5–1.5 | 100–250 | 2–10 |
| **Specific Power (W/kg)** | Very low | 200–1500 | 5000–15000 |
| **Cycle Life (cycles)** | >50,000 | 1000–10,000 | >100,000 |
| **Round-Trip Efficiency** | 70–85% | 85–95% | 90–98% |
| **Self-Discharge Rate** | Negligible (~0%/mo) | 1–5%/month | 5–40%/hour |
| **Response Time** | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds | Microseconds to ms |
| **Best Application** | Bulk grid energy storage | EVs, residential, grid | Power bursts, regen braking |

**Key inferences (for full marks):**
- PHS has excellent cycle life and zero self-discharge but is geographically constrained and slow.
- Li-ion offers the best energy-power compromise for mobile and residential use.
- Supercapacitor dominates in power density and cycle life but stores very little energy per kg. *(2 marks for inference and conclusion)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — Ragone Plot & Hybrid System (7 marks)

**Ragone Plot — Definition (2 marks):**
The Ragone plot is a **log-log plot of specific power (W/kg) vs. specific energy (Wh/kg)**. Each technology occupies a distinct region, providing a visual benchmark for technology comparison. A sample sketch:

```
Specific Power (W/kg)
  10^6 |
       |       SMES
  10^5 |    SUPERCAPACITOR
       |  FLYWHEEL
  10^4 |
       |
  10^3 |          Li-ion
       |    NaS   |
  10^2 |  Lead-acid
       |
  10^1 |                   VRFB
       |                          H2 Fuel Cell
  10^0 |________________________________________
       10^0  10^1  10^2  10^3  10^4  10^5
                 Specific Energy (Wh/kg)
```

**Hybrid System — How it overcomes limitations (3 marks):**
1. The **supercapacitor** handles **high-power, short-duration** peaks (acceleration, braking regeneration, frequency spikes) — protecting the battery from high C-rate stress.
2. The **Li-ion battery** handles **steady-state, long-duration** energy demand — leveraging its high energy density.
3. Combined, the hybrid system **moves the effective operating point into the upper-right quadrant** of the Ragone plot, achieving both high energy and high power.
4. The result is **extended battery life, lower LCOS, and improved dynamic response**.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** The hybrid configuration is the engineering solution when no single technology can simultaneously meet energy, power, and cost targets.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)**. A battery system has the following specifications: CAPEX = ₹25,00,000, annual OPEX = ₹1,00,000, lifetime N = 12 years, rated energy = 500 kWh, depth of discharge = 80%, round-trip efficiency = 88%, cycles per year = 350, and annual capacity degradation = 1.5%. Compute the LCOS. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the role of **comparative analysis in the design of a hybrid renewable microgrid** for a remote village. Suggest a suitable storage combination and justify your selection. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — LCOS Concept and Calculation (7 marks)

**Concept of LCOS (2 marks):**
LCOS is the **per-unit-energy-delivered lifetime cost** of a storage system, expressed in ₹/kWh or \$/kWh. It allows fair economic comparison between technologies with different CAPEX, OPEX, lifetimes, and efficiencies. It is computed by **amortizing total lifetime costs (CAPEX + discounted OPEX) over the total lifetime energy actually delivered** (factoring in efficiency, DOD, and degradation).

**Numerical Computation (5 marks):**

**Given:**
- $CAPEX = ₹25,00,000$
- $OPEX = ₹1,00,000/\text{year}$
- $N = 12$ years
- $E_{rated} = 500$ kWh
- $DOD = 0.8$
- $\eta_{RT} = 0.88$
- $n_{cycles} = 350/\text{year}$
- Annual capacity fade = 1.5%

**Step 1 — Total cost (assuming r = 0 for simplicity as per KTU problem):**

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 25{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}00{,}000 \times 12) = 25{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = ₹37{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Useful energy per cycle (Year 1):**

$$
E_{cycle,1} = 500 \times 0.8 \times 0.88 = 352\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy delivered (Year 1):**

$$
E_{annual,1} = 352 \times 350 = 1{,}23{,}200\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Degradation-weighted lifetime energy (geometric sum):**

$$
\sum_{t=0}^{11} (0.985)^t = \frac{1 - 0.985^{12}}{1 - 0.985} = \frac{1 - 0.834}{0.015} = 11.066
$$

$$
E_{lifetime} = 1{,}23{,}200 \times 11.066 = 13{,}63{,}331\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — LCOS:**

$$
LCOS = \frac{37{,}00{,}000}{13{,}63{,}331} = ₹2.71/\text{kWh}
$$

**Conclusion:** The battery delivers energy at approximately **₹2.71 per kWh** over its 12-year life — useful for benchmarking against diesel generation (₹15–25/kWh in remote areas) to justify renewable-battery microgrids.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Microgrid Design (7 marks)

**Role of Comparative Analysis (2 marks):**
Comparative analysis identifies which storage technology best satisfies the microgrid's load profile, which may include base load (slow), peak demand (fast), seasonal storage (long-duration), and frequency regulation (sub-second). A single technology rarely meets all needs.

**Suitable Hybrid Storage Combination (3 marks):**
For a remote village microgrid (e.g., solar PV + small wind):

1. **Li-ion battery bank** — for daily solar energy time-shifting (5–10 kWh per household, 4–8 hours discharge).
2. **Supercapacitor bank** — for handling cloud-edge solar ramps, motor starting transients, and microgrid stabilization (<30 s events).
3. **Optional: Hydrogen electrolyzer + fuel cell** — for long-duration seasonal storage (weeks of low solar/wind).

**Justification (2 marks):**
- The Li-ion battery handles **bulk energy needs** cost-effectively.
- The supercapacitor provides **power quality and fast response**, extending battery life by absorbing high-C-rate events.
- Hydrogen provides **seasonal resilience** when renewable generation is poor for extended periods.
- Comparative analysis (Ragone plot, LCOS, cycle life) confirms that the **hybrid configuration** outperforms any single technology in both technical and economic terms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Comparative analysis benchmarks ESS technologies using **performance, economic, and sustainability** parameters.
- **Key Metrics (The "Vital 8"):** Energy density, power density, round-trip efficiency, cycle life, self-discharge, response time, CAPEX (₹/kWh and ₹/kW), LCOS.
- **Ragone Plot:** Log-log plot of specific power vs. specific energy; the **single most important visualization** in ESS comparison.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency:** $\eta_{RT} = \eta_{charge} \times \eta_{discharge}$; always less than 100%.
- **Characteristic Time $\tau = E/P$:** Classifies systems as energy-oriented ($\tau > 4$ h) or power-oriented ($\tau < 0.1$ h).
- **LCOS Formula:** Lifetime cost ÷ lifetime energy delivered; the fairest economic comparator.
- **No Universal Winner:** Each technology has a niche. PHS for bulk, Li-ion for EVs, supercapacitor for power, H₂ for seasonal.
- **Hybrid Systems:** Combining technologies (e.g., battery + supercapacitor) fills the empty upper-right of the Ragone plot.
- **Peukert's Law** applies to lead-acid: capacity drops at high discharge rates.
- **SMES & Supercapacitors** are the fastest responding technologies ($\mu$s to ms).
- **Hydrogen/Fuel Cell systems** have low round-trip efficiency (35–50%) due to electrolysis and fuel cell losses.
- **Thermal storage** (molten salt, PCM) is uniquely coupled with concentrated solar power (CSP).
- **CAPEX/kWh ≠ CAPEX/kW** — the first is energy cost, the second is power cost.
- **Cycle life is degraded by DOD** — operating at 50% DOD doubles cycle life vs. 100% DOD for most chemistries.
- **KTU-Favorite Question Type:** "Compare three technologies on five parameters" (Part A, 3 marks) and "Ragone plot + hybrid system" (Part B, 14 marks).
- **Always use log-log scale** when drawing the Ragone plot in the exam.
- **Sustainability angle:** Lithium, cobalt, and rare-earth dependency is a growing comparative factor for 2024+ KTU questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When the question asks for a comparison, **always use a table** with consistent units in the header. The KTU Board Examiner awards marks for: (1) correct parameter selection (2 marks), (2) tabulated data with units (3 marks), (3) justification/inference paragraph (2 marks). Total 7 marks for comparison sub-questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental impacts of different technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Environmental impacts of different technologies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Environmental Impact (EI) of Energy Storage Technologies** is defined as the cumulative set of measurable alterations to natural and human environments resulting from the complete life cycle of an energy storage system (ESS). This life cycle encompasses **raw material extraction**, **material processing**, **manufacturing**, **transportation**, **operational use**, **recycling**, and **end-of-life disposal** phases, evaluated using standardized frameworks such as **ISO 14040/14044 (Life Cycle Assessment)** and **Cradle-to-Grave (CTG)** analysis. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, the assessment of environmental impacts is quantified using the **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** methodology, which evaluates a technology across **five standardized impact categories**: | # | Impact Category | Unit (Standard Metric) | |---|---|---| | 1 | Global Warming Potential (GWP) | **kg CO₂-equivalent / kWh** | | 2 | Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) | **MJ / kWh** | | 3 | Water Consumption | **litres / kWh** | | 4 | Toxicity Potential (Human & Eco) | **kg 1,4-DB-eq / kWh** | | 5 | Resource Depletion (Abiotic) | **kg Sb-eq / kWh** | The standard benchmark for any storage technology is the **Embodied Energy**, defined as the total primary energy consumed to produce **1 kWh of usable storage capacity**, typically ranging from **5 MJ/kWh** (pumped hydro) to over **1000 MJ/kWh** (advanced supercapacitors). ## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Carbon Backpack" of a Battery:** > Imagine every energy storage device carrying an invisible **"Carbon Backpack"** on its journey from the mine to the recycling plant. A **pumped hydro reservoir** carries a small backpack because it uses gravity, water, and concrete. A **lithium-ion battery**, however, carries a heavy backpack filled with lithium mined from salt flats, cobalt from underground mines, fluorinated electrolytes, and the electricity burned in gigafactories. When the device is used, the backpack does not get lighter — it stays on the environment. **Our goal as engineers is to lighten that backpack** at every stage of the life cycle. ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies environmental sustainability as a **Program Outcome (PO-7: Environment and Sustainability)** competency. The choice of a storage technology in modern grid applications is no longer driven purely by **energy density** or **cost**, but by a **triple-bottom-line** evaluation: 1. **Economic viability** (LCOE — Levelized Cost of Energy) 2. **Technical performance** (round-trip efficiency, cycle life) 3. **Environmental footprint** (LCA emissions, toxicity, recyclability) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Under KTU 2024 Module 2, students are expected to compare battery, supercapacitor, hydrogen, pumped hydro, CAES, flywheel, thermal, and SMES technologies **not only on performance parameters but explicitly on their environmental burden profiles**, including end-of-life management. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Five-Phase Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) Framework Every energy storage technology is evaluated across the following **sequential phases**: ### Phase 1 — Raw Material Extraction (Cradle) - Mining of lithium, cobalt, nickel, lead, cadmium, rare earths - Earth-moving, water consumption, habitat disruption - Example: **1 tonne of lithium** requires approximately **2 million litres of water** in brine extraction (Salar de Atacama, Chile) ### Phase 2 — Material Processing & Cell Manufacturing - Refining, electrolyte synthesis, electrode coating - High-temperature kiln operations in cathode production - Solvent emissions (NMP — N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone) in electrode drying ### Phase 3 — Transportation & Installation - Global shipping of cells/modules - On-site assembly at grid-scale installations - Cold-chain logistics for some chemistries ### Phase 4 — Operational Use (Gate-to-Gate) - Charging-discharging cycles (round-trip efficiency losses) - Self-discharge and parasitic loads - On-site emissions (rare in electrochemical, common in thermal) ### Phase 5 — End-of-Life (Grave) - Recycling, refurbishment, second-life applications - Landfill disposal (worst case) - Controlled incineration (selective cases) ## 2.2 Environmental Impact Quantification Equations > [!NOTE] > The following equations form the **analytical backbone** of any LCA comparison in KTU examination contexts. ### 2.2.1 Total Cradle-to-Grave Emissions The total greenhouse gas emissions of a storage technology per unit of energy delivered over its lifetime: $$E_{total} = E_{manuf} + E_{oper} \times N_{cycles} + E_{EOL}$$ Where: - $E_{total}$ = total **kg CO₂-eq** per kWh of storage delivered - $E_{manuf}$ = embodied emissions from manufacturing (kg CO₂-eq/kWh) - $E_{oper}$ = operational emissions per cycle (kg CO₂-eq/kWh/cycle) - $N_{cycles}$ = total cycle life of the device - $E_{EOL}$ = end-of-life emissions (recycling credit as negative value) ### 2.2.2 Embodied Energy per kWh Delivered $$\varepsilon_{delivered} = \frac{E_{embodied}}{\eta_{RT} \times DoD \times N_{cycles}}$$ Where: - $E_{embodied}$ = total energy used in manufacturing (MJ) - $\eta_{RT}$ = round-trip efficiency - $DoD$ = depth of discharge (fraction) - $N_{cycles}$ = total cycle life ### 2.2.3 Energy Payback Time (EPBT) The time required for the storage system to "generate" the energy equivalent to what was used to manufacture it: $$EPBT = \frac{E_{embodied}}{E_{annual\_saved} - E_{annual\_operational}}$$ Where $E_{annual\_saved}$ is the emissions avoided annually by displacing fossil-fuel-based peaking plants. ### 2.2.4 Cumulative Energy Demand (CED) $$CED = \sum_{i} (m_i \times e_i)$$ Where: - $m_i$ = mass of material $i$ (kg) - $e_i$ = specific energy intensity of material $i$ (MJ/kg) ### 2.2.5 E-Waste Generation Index $$EWI = \frac{M_{device}}{E_{lifetime\_delivered}}$$ Where: - $M_{device}$ = total mass of the device (kg) - $E_{lifetime\_delivered}$ = total energy delivered over its lifetime (kWh) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range / Value | Unit | Key Technology | |---|---|---|---|---| | Embodied Energy | $E_{emb}$ | 5 – 1500 | **MJ/kWh** | All ESS | | GWP — Manufacturing | $GWP_{manuf}$ | 20 – 250 | **kg CO₂-eq/kWh** | Li-ion: ~150 | | GWP — Pumped Hydro | $GWP_{PHS}$ | 1 – 10 | **kg CO₂-eq/kWh** | PHS | | GWP — Lead-acid | $GWP_{LA}$ | 50 – 100 | **kg CO₂-eq/kWh** | Lead-acid | | Cycle Life | $N_{cyc}$ | 500 – 100000 | **cycles** | Li-ion / Flow | | Round-trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT}$ | 0.60 – 0.95 | **dimensionless** | PHS / Li-ion | | Recycling Rate | $R_r$ | 50 – 95 | **%** | Lead-acid > 95% | | Toxicity Index | $TI$ | varies | **kg 1,4-DB-eq/kWh** | NiCd highest | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note:** Always express absolute values using $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX (e.g., $\vert E_{total} \vert$). When writing KTU examination answers, do NOT use the vertical pipe $\vert$ directly in markdown table cells as it breaks table rendering — use the LaTeX `\vert` macro. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The EIA framework directly informs: - **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** — mandates carbon footprint declarations - **India's National Mission on Advanced Chemistry Cell (ACC)** — PLI scheme tied to local manufacturing emissions - **Tesla's Recycle Loop at Gigafactory Nevada** — closed-loop cobalt recovery - **IEC 62933 series** standards for environmental testing of grid-integrated ESS --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive LCA Derivation for a Grid-Scale Li-ion Battery Bank > [!NOTE] > The following worked derivation is the **gold standard** for KTU Part B answers on this topic. ### Problem Statement A **10 MWh grid-scale Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP) battery bank** has the following specifications: - Manufacturing emissions: $E_{manuf}$ = **120 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** - Operational emissions per cycle: $E_{oper}$ = **0.05 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** - Total cycles over lifetime: $N_{cycles}$ = **6000** - End-of-life recycling credit: $E_{EOL}$ = **−15 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** - Round-trip efficiency: $\eta_{RT}$ = **0.92** - Depth of discharge: $DoD$ = **0.80** **Calculate:** (a) Total cradle-to-grave emissions per kWh delivered (b) Embodied energy per kWh delivered (assuming embodied energy = 250 MJ/kWh) (c) Energy payback time, given annual emissions displaced = 8000 tonnes CO₂ ### Step-by-Step Solution #### Part (a): Total Emissions We begin with the master LCA equation: $$E_{total} = E_{manuf} + E_{oper} \times N_{cycles} + E_{EOL}$$ Substituting the given numerical values: $$E_{total} = 120 + (0.05) \times (6000) + (-15)$$ Step 1 — Evaluate the operational term: $$E_{oper} \times N_{cycles} = 0.05 \times 6000 = 300 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$$ Step 2 — Sum all three terms: $$E_{total} = 120 + 300 - 15$$ $$E_{total} = 405 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight:** Notice that the operational term ($E_{oper} \times N_{cycles}$ = 300) dominates the LCA. This is because **per-cycle losses accumulate over the lifetime**. Engineers therefore minimize **$E_{oper}$** by selecting high-$\eta_{RT}$ chemistries. #### Part (b): Embodied Energy per kWh Delivered $$\varepsilon_{delivered} = \frac{E_{embodied}}{\eta_{RT} \times DoD \times N_{cycles}}$$ Substituting: $$\varepsilon_{delivered} = \frac{250}{(0.92) \times (0.80) \times (6000)}$$ Step 1 — Compute the denominator: $$(0.92) \times (0.80) \times (6000) = 4416$$ Step 2 — Compute the ratio: $$\varepsilon_{delivered} = \frac{250}{4416}$$ $$\varepsilon_{delivered} \approx 0.0566 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 56.6 \text{ kJ/kWh}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Even with a heavy 250 MJ/kWh manufacturing footprint, the LFP battery "amortizes" this energy over thousands of cycles, yielding only 56.6 kJ of embodied energy per kWh actually delivered. This is the **magic of high cycle life**. #### Part (c): Energy Payback Time The 10 MWh bank has total embodied emissions: $$E_{total\_bank} = E_{manuf} \times 10000 = 120 \times 10000 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} = 1200 \text{ tonnes}$$ Now apply: $$EPBT = \frac{E_{embodied}}{E_{annual\_saved} - E_{annual\_operational}}$$ Step 1 — Annual operational emissions of the bank: $$E_{annual\_oper} = E_{oper} \times N_{annual\_cycles} \times 10000$$ Assuming one cycle per day for 365 days: $$E_{annual\_oper} = 0.05 \times 365 \times 10000 = 182{,}500 \text{ kg} = 182.5 \text{ tonnes}$$ Step 2 — Net annual savings: $$E_{net\_saved} = 8000 - 182.5 = 7817.5 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq}$$ Step 3 — Compute EPBT: $$EPBT = \frac{1200}{7817.5} = 0.1535 \text{ years}$$ $$\boxed{EPBT \approx 56 \text{ days}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The 10 MWh LFP battery repays its entire carbon manufacturing debt in approximately **2 months**, after which it provides **net-negative emissions** for the grid. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix Across ESS Technologies | Technology | $E_{manuf}$ (kg CO₂/kWh) | $\eta_{RT}$ | Toxicity Risk | Recyclability | Land Use (m²/kWh) | Water Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Pumped Hydro (PHS)** | 1 – 10 | 0.75 – 0.82 | Low | High | 0.5 – 2.0 | High | | **CAES** | 5 – 30 | 0.42 – 0.55 | Medium (oil in diabatic) | Medium | 0.05 – 0.2 | Low | | **Li-ion (NMC)** | 100 – 200 | 0.85 – 0.95 | Medium | Medium (50%) | 0.001 – 0.01 | Medium | | **Li-ion (LFP)** | 80 – 130 | 0.90 – 0.95 | Low | Medium (60%) | 0.001 – 0.01 | Medium | | **Lead-acid** | 50 – 100 | 0.70 – 0.85 | **Very High (Pb)** | **Very High (>95%)** | 0.01 | Low | | **NaS** | 40 – 90 | 0.75 – 0.90 | Medium | Medium | 0.005 | Low | | **Vanadium Redox Flow** | 60 – 120 | 0.65 – 0.80 | Medium (V₂O₅) | High (90%) | 0.02 | Medium | | **Supercapacitor** | 200 – 500 | 0.90 – 0.98 | Low | Medium | 0.002 | Low | | **Hydrogen (H₂)** | 30 – 80 | 0.30 – 0.45 | Low | High | 0.001 | High | | **Flywheel** | 50 – 200 | 0.85 – 0.95 | Low | High | 0.003 | Negligible | | **SMES** | 100 – 400 | 0.90 – 0.97 | Low | High | 0.001 | Negligible | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Most students lose marks by comparing technologies **only on cost or efficiency**. Always present environmental impact as a **multi-criteria matrix** with at least **4 impact dimensions**: GWP, toxicity, water, recyclability. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Cradle-to-Grave LCA Flow Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Raw Material Extraction
Mining of Li Co Ni Mn] --> A2[Material Processing
Refining and Chemical Synthesis] A2 --> A3[Cell Manufacturing
Gigafactory Assembly Lines] A3 --> A4[Transportation
Global Logistics Network] A4 --> A5[Operational Use
Grid Scale Cycling] A5 --> A6[End of Life Phase
Recycling or Disposal] A6 --> A7[Second Life Application
or Landfill] B1[Environmental Burden] -.-> A1 B2[Carbon Emissions] -.-> A3 B3[Water Consumption] -.-> A2 B4[Toxic Waste] -.-> A6 B5[Recycling Credit] -.-> A7 style A1 fill:#ffcccc style A3 fill:#ffe599 style A6 fill:#b6d7a8 style B1 fill:#d9d9d9 style B5 fill:#a4c2f4 ``` ## 4.2 Technology-to-Impact Mapping Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ESS_Tech[Energy Storage Technologies] T1[Pumped Hydro] T2[Li-ion Battery] T3[Lead Acid] T4[Supercapacitor] T5[Hydrogen] T6[Flywheel] end subgraph Impact_Cat[Environmental Impact Categories] I1[GWP
kg CO2 eq] I2[Toxicity
Heavy Metals] I3[Water Use
Litres per kWh] I4[Land Use
Square m per kWh] I5[Recyclability
Percent] end T1 --> I4 T1 --> I3 T2 --> I1 T3 --> I2 T4 --> I1 T5 --> I3 T6 --> I4 style T1 fill:#cfe2f3 style T2 fill:#f9cb9c style T3 fill:#d5a6bd style T4 fill:#b6d7a8 style T5 fill:#fff2cc style T6 fill:#d9d2e9 style I1 fill:#f4cccc style I2 fill:#f4cccc style I3 fill:#f4cccc style I4 fill:#f4cccc style I5 fill:#f4cccc ``` ## 4.3 Decision Logic: Selecting a Green ESS ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Storage Requirement Identified]) --> q1{Is Duration
More than 4 Hours?} q1 -- Yes --> q2{Location Has
Topography?} q1 -- No --> q3{Is Power Density
Priority?} q2 -- Yes --> sol1[Select PHS] q2 -- No --> sol2[Select CAES or Flow Battery] q3 -- Yes --> sol3[Select Supercapacitor or Flywheel] q3 -- No --> sol4{Is Carbon Footprint
Critical KPI?} sol4 -- Yes --> sol5[Select LFP or Sodium Ion] sol4 -- No --> sol6[Select NMC Li-ion] style start fill:#a4c2f4 style sol1 fill:#b6d7a8 style sol2 fill:#b6d7a8 style sol3 fill:#ffe599 style sol5 fill:#d5a6bd style sol6 fill:#d5a6bd ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** > **"Define Cradle-to-Grave Life Cycle Assessment in the context of energy storage systems. List its five sequential phases."** **Model Answer:** Cradle-to-Grave (CTG) Life Cycle Assessment is a comprehensive ISO 14040/14044 standardized methodology that quantifies the **total environmental burden** of an energy storage system from the **extraction of raw materials (cradle)** to its **final disposal or recycling (grave)**. The **five sequential phases** are: 1. **Raw Material Extraction** — Mining of lithium, cobalt, nickel, lead, etc. 2. **Material Processing & Manufacturing** — Refining, synthesis, cell fabrication 3. **Transportation & Installation** — Logistics, on-site assembly 4. **Operational Use** — Charge/discharge cycles and parasitic losses 5. **End-of-Life Management** — Recycling, second-life use, or disposal **Mark Distribution (KTU Key):** - Definition with ISO standard: **1.5 marks** - Five phases with examples: **1.5 marks** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **"Why is lead-acid battery recycling considered an environmental success story despite the high toxicity of lead?"** **Model Answer:** Despite the **extreme neurotoxicity** of lead (Pb), lead-acid batteries achieve a **closed-loop recycling rate exceeding 95%** globally, making them the **most recycled consumer product on Earth**. The reasons are: 1. **Economic incentive** — Lead has high scrap value (~$2000/tonne) 2. **Mature infrastructure** — Established smelting networks in over 60 countries 3. **Strict regulations** — EU Battery Directive 2006/66, India's E-Waste Rules 2016 4. **Technical simplicity** — Lead smelting at 1100°C is well-understood 5. **Performance equivalence** — Recycled lead has identical electrochemical properties to virgin lead **Mark Distribution:** - Recycling rate statistic: **1 mark** - Three contributing factors: **2 marks** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Compliance:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) for **7 marks** and (b) for **7 marks**, following the official KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination template. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 2]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat block diagram the **five phases of Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** applied to a grid-scale lithium-ion battery installation. Discuss the major emissions contributors in each phase. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] A 5 MWh NMC (Nickel Manganese Cobalt) battery installation has the following data: > - Manufacturing emissions: **150 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** > - Operational emissions per cycle: **0.08 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** > - Total cycles: **4000** > - Recycling credit: **−20 kg CO₂-eq/kWh** > > Calculate: > 1. Total cradle-to-grave emissions per kWh > 2. If the battery displaces **4000 tonnes of CO₂ annually**, find the energy payback time **Model Solution — Part (a):** The LCA framework consists of **five sequential phases**: 1. **Raw Material Extraction (Cradle):** - Mining of lithium from brine pools or spodumene ore - Cobalt extraction (often from DRC — Democratic Republic of Congo, with associated social concerns) - Nickel laterite mining (Indonesia, Philippines) - **Major emissions:** Diesel fuel for heavy machinery, mine dewatering pumps 2. **Material Processing:** - Conversion of spodumene to Li₂CO₃ (calcination at 1100°C) - Cathode precursor synthesis (NMC hydroxide co-precipitation) - **Major emissions:** Process heat, chemical precursors 3. **Cell & Pack Manufacturing:** - Electrode coating (NMP solvent recovery) - Cell formation cycling (multiple charge-discharge cycles) - Module and rack assembly - **Major emissions:** Electricity consumption (often from coal in Asia) 4. **Transportation:** - Shipping from Asian gigafactories to global markets - **Major emissions:** Bunker fuel from container vessels 5. **End-of-Life:** - Hydrometallurgical recycling (acid leaching) - Pyrometallurgical smelting - **Major emissions:** Acid waste, slag, energy for pyrometallurgy **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Five phases explained: **5 × 1 = 5 marks** - Block diagram: **1 mark** - Major emissions in each: **1 mark** **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Total Emissions per kWh** Using the master equation: $$E_{total} = E_{manuf} + E_{oper} \times N_{cycles} + E_{EOL}$$ $$E_{total} = 150 + (0.08) \times (4000) + (-20)$$ $$E_{total} = 150 + 320 - 20 = 450 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/kWh}$$ **Step 2 — Total Bank Embodied Emissions** $$E_{bank} = 450 \times 5000 = 2{,}250{,}000 \text{ kg} = 2250 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 3 — Annual Operational Emissions of the Bank** $$E_{annual\_oper} = 0.08 \times 365 \times 5000 = 146{,}000 \text{ kg} = 146 \text{ tonnes}$$ **Step 4 — Net Annual Savings** $$E_{net} = 4000 - 146 = 3854 \text{ tonnes/year}$$ **Step 5 — Energy Payback Time** $$EPBT = \frac{2250}{3854} = 0.584 \text{ years}$$ $$\boxed{EPBT \approx 7 \text{ months}}$$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** - Part (b)(i) calculation: **3 marks** - Part (b)(ii) calculation with EPBT: **4 marks** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Module 2]** > **(a)** [7 Marks] Compare the environmental impacts of **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** and **Lithium-ion Battery** systems across **four impact categories** with appropriate quantitative values. > > **(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **toxicity and end-of-life recycling challenges** specific to: > 1. Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd) batteries > 2. Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries (VRFB) > 3. Hydrogen storage (with reference to leakage) **Model Answer — Part (a):** A four-category comparison: | Impact Category | PHS | Li-ion (LFP) | |---|---|---| | **GWP (kg CO₂/kWh)** | 1 – 10 | 80 – 130 | | **Land Use (m²/kWh)** | 0.5 – 2.0 | 0.001 – 0.01 | | **Water Consumption** | High (evaporation losses) | Medium (electrolyte production) | | **Recyclability (%)** | >90% (concrete, steel) | ~60% (LFP) – 50% (NMC) | **Engineering conclusion:** PHS wins on **recyclability and land efficiency for short-term discharge**, but its **geographical constraints** (need for elevation and water) limit deployment. Li-ion offers **flexibility and modularity** at the cost of higher embodied carbon. **Mark Distribution:** - Four categories correctly tabulated: **4 marks** - Quantitative values: **2 marks** - Engineering conclusion: **1 mark** **Model Answer — Part (b):** 1. **NiCd Batteries:** - **Cadmium** is a **Category 1 carcinogen** under IARC classification - Disposal in municipal landfills leads to **soil and groundwater contamination** - EU Battery Directive **banned NiCd** for most consumer applications in 2009 - Recycling requires **dedicated hydrometallurgical facilities** (e.g., Saft, France) 2. **Vanadium Redox Flow Batteries (VRFB):** - Electrolyte is **V²⁺/V³⁺/VO²⁺/VO₂⁺** in sulfuric acid (~5M) - Spent electrolyte is **highly acidic** and requires neutralization - Vanadium itself is **non-toxic** but its mining causes land disturbance - Advantage: **Electrolyte can be reused indefinitely** with periodic rebalancing 3. **Hydrogen Storage:** - **H₂ leakage** to atmosphere has **GWP₁₀₀ of 11.6** (methane reference = 28) - Indirect GHG effect due to tropospheric ozone formation - Liquid hydrogen boil-off losses (~0.1%/day) contribute to leakage - Storage tank manufacturing (carbon fiber) has high embodied energy **Mark Distribution:** - Three technologies × ~2 marks each: **6 marks** - Common theme synthesis: **1 mark** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes in KTU Exam Answers on this Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing GWP with CO₂ emissions** — GWP is a *normalized* metric over 100 years; raw CO₂ is not. Always specify the **time horizon** (GWP₁₀₀). > 2. **Forgetting the recycling credit sign** — $E_{EOL}$ is **negative** (carbon saved through material recovery). Students frequently add it as positive, doubling the apparent footprint. > 3. **Quoting only manufacturing emissions** — In KTU 2024, the question **"what is the LCA of a battery?"** expects *all five phases*. Merely listing $E_{manuf}$ earns only **40% marks**. > 4. **Ignoring round-trip efficiency in EPBT** — Energy delivered ≠ energy stored. Always multiply by $\eta_{RT} \times DoD$. > 5. **Comparing technologies in different units** — Convert all to **per-kWh-delivered** basis before comparison. > 6. **Neglecting water as a critical resource** — KTU 2024 emphasizes India's water-stress context; PHS in arid regions is environmentally questionable. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Environmental Impacts** - [ ] **LCA has 5 phases**: Extraction → Processing → Manufacturing → Use → EOL - [ ] **Master equation**: $E_{total} = E_{manuf} + E_{oper} \cdot N_{cycles} + E_{EOL}$ - [ ] **Embodied energy per kWh delivered**: divide by $\eta_{RT} \cdot DoD \cdot N_{cycles}$ - [ ] **Pumped hydro** has lowest **GWP** (1–10) but highest **land & water** use - [ ] **Lead-acid** has highest **recycling rate** (>95%) — most recycled product on Earth - [ ] **Li-ion (LFP)** is greener than **NMC** — no cobalt, lower GWP - [ ] **NiCd** is banned in EU due to **cadmium carcinogenicity** - [ ] **Vanadium flow** electrolyte is **acidic** but **infinitely reusable** - [ ] **Hydrogen leakage** has GWP₁₀₀ ≈ 11.6 (often underestimated) - [ ] **Supercapacitors** have **high embodied energy** (200–500 MJ/kWh) but **long cycle life** - [ ] **Recycling credit is NEGATIVE** — subtract from total emissions - [ ] **Always normalize** comparison to **kg CO₂-eq per kWh delivered** - [ ] **Cradle-to-Gate** vs **Cradle-to-Grave** — know the difference - [ ] **ISO 14040/14044** is the official LCA framework — name it in answers - [ ] **EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542** mandates carbon footprint declarations - [ ] **India's ACC PLI scheme** ties subsidies to local manufacturing emissions ---

topics.title: Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌊 WAVE ENERGY: A MECHANICAL RENEWABLE STORAGE PARADIGM

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wave Energy Storage** is defined as the process of **capturing kinetic and potential energy from ocean surface gravity waves**, converting it into a storable form (mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, or electrical), and holding it for later use. In KTU 2024 scheme, it is classified under **Mechanical / Renewable Energy Storage Systems** within Module 3.

**Wave Energy Conversion (WEC)** systems use the oscillatory motion of ocean waves as the prime mover. The energy exists in two interconvertible forms at the ocean surface:
- **Kinetic Energy (KE)** – due to water particle motion within the wave.
- **Potential Energy (PE)** – due to displacement of the water surface from the mean sea level.

The **total mechanical energy per unit horizontal area** of a wave is given by:

$$E = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \;\; (\text{J/m}^2)$$

where $\rho$ = density of seawater ($\approx$ **1025 kg/m³**), $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**), $H$ = significant wave height (m).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why waves are excellent energy carriers:** Ocean waves have an energy density ~10–20× higher than wind or solar per unit area, and unlike solar/wind, wave energy arrives in a continuous, predictable, diurnal cycle (lulls are short).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting on a pier watching waves roll in. Each wave lifts a small boat up and pushes it forward, then drops it back. That gentle rhythmic push is **kinetic + potential energy** in motion. Now imagine attaching a buoy (float) connected to a piston via a cable.

- When the buoy rises → piston compresses fluid → fluid is stored in an **accumulator** (storage).
- When the buoy falls → fluid flows back → drives a turbine → electricity.

> **In one sentence:** A wave energy storage system is essentially a "**mechanical lung**" that inhales (absorbs) wave motion and exhales electricity on demand.

**Key advantages:**
- Highest energy density among renewables (~30–50 kW/m of wave front).
- Predictability (waves arrive with regularity tied to wind patterns).
- Low visual/auditory footprint compared to wind turbines.

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost, marine corrosion, harsh sea conditions.
- Transmission losses to shore.

---

## 1.3 Standard Wave Power Metric (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wave Power Formula (Empirical – KTU Reference):**
> 
> $$P_w \approx 0.5 \cdot H_s^2 \cdot T_e \;\; (\text{kW per meter of wave crest})$$
> 
> where $H_s$ = significant wave height (m), $T_e$ = energy period (s). This is the **most quoted rule-of-thumb in KTU board exams** for wave power estimation.

The **global theoretical wave energy resource** is estimated at ~**2 TW** continuous.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Ocean Wave (Linear Wave Theory – Airy Theory)

Ocean surface waves are described by **Airy / Linear Wave Theory**, which assumes:
- Inviscid, incompressible fluid.
- Small amplitude ($H \ll \lambda$).
- Irrotational flow.

The **free surface elevation** is sinusoidal:

$$\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

where:
- $k$ = wave number = $\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ (rad/m)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency = $\frac{2\pi}{T}$ (rad/s)
- $T$ = wave period (s)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)

### Dispersion Relation (Deep Water)

$$\omega^2 = gk \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} \approx 1.56 \, T^2$$

This is the **foundation of all wave energy analysis** — it relates wave size to wave period.

---

## 2.2 Energy and Power Derivations (Step-by-Step Logic)

### Step 1: Kinetic Energy per unit area
For deep-water Airy waves, KE and PE per unit area are **equal**:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2 \quad ; \quad E_p = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

### Step 2: Total Energy per unit horizontal area
Adding both:

$$E_{total} = E_k + E_p = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$$

### Step 3: Group Velocity (energy transport speed)
Energy is transported at **half** the phase velocity in deep water:

$$c_g = \frac{1}{2}c = \frac{g}{2\omega} = \frac{\lambda}{2T}$$

### Step 4: Wave Power per unit crest length

$$P = E_{total} \cdot c_g = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64\pi}$$

This is the **fundamental wave power equation** in deep water.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Condition |
|---|----------|--------|---------|------|-----------|
| 1 | Surface elevation | $\eta$ | $\frac{H}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$ | m | Linear theory |
| 2 | Wavelength (deep) | $\lambda_0$ | $\frac{gT^2}{2\pi}$ | m | Deep water |
| 3 | Wave number | $k$ | $\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | rad/m | All depths |
| 4 | Angular frequency | $\omega$ | $\frac{2\pi}{T}$ | rad/s | All depths |
| 5 | Energy per unit area | $E$ | $\frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ | J/m² | Linear theory |
| 6 | Group velocity | $c_g$ | $\frac{g}{2\omega}$ | m/s | Deep water |
| 7 | Wave power / length | $P$ | $\frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64\pi}$ | W/m | Deep water |
| 8 | Empirical power | $P_w$ | $0.5 \cdot H^2 \cdot T$ | kW/m | KTU rule-of-thumb |
| 9 | Phase velocity | $c$ | $\frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{gT}{2\pi}$ | m/s | Deep water |
| 10 | Capture width ratio | CWR | $\frac{\text{Power captured}}{P \cdot D}$ | – | WEC performance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Safeguard:** The empirical rule $P = 0.5 H^2 T$ gives **kW/m** directly when $H$ is in metres and $T$ in seconds. The deep-water formula gives **W/m** when SI base units are used.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Wave Energy |
|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Island grids** | Diesel replacement in remote islands (e.g., Azores, Maldives) |
| **Desalination** | Direct mechanical drive for RO pumps |
| **OTEC hybrid** | Combined with thermal ocean gradients |
| **Subsea charging** | Powering AUVs/underwater observatories |
| **Hydrogen production** | Wave → electrolysis → green H₂ (long-duration storage) |
| **Pumped storage** | Wave-driven pumping to elevated reservoirs |

> **The "Why":** Waves are essentially a *concentrated form of wind energy*. Over thousands of km of ocean fetch, winds of 5–10 m/s accumulate into swells carrying power densities of 30–70 kW/m — orders of magnitude denser than solar (≈0.2 kW/m²) or wind (≈1–2 kW/m² at the rotor disk).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Wave Power from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** Derive the wave power per unit length of wave crest in deep water using Airy wave theory.

**Step A — Velocity Potential**

For an inviscid, irrotational flow, we define a velocity potential $\phi$ such that $\vec{u} = \nabla \phi$. Solving Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ with the free-surface boundary conditions yields:

$$\phi(x,z,t) = \frac{H g}{2\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh k(z+d)}{\cosh kd} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step B — Pressure Field (Linearized Bernoulli)**

$$p = -\rho \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} - \rho g z$$

Substituting $\phi$ and evaluating at the free surface $z = \eta$ (and using the linearized dynamic free-surface boundary condition) gives:

$$p = \frac{\rho g H}{2} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step C — Horizontal Velocity at the Surface**

$$u_x(x,0,t) = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = \frac{H g k}{2\omega}\cos(kx - \omega t) = \frac{H\omega}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

(using deep-water dispersion $\omega^2 = gk$).

**Step D — Vertical Velocity at the Surface**

$$u_z = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\bigg\vert_{z=0} = -\frac{H\omega}{2}\sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step E — Total Energy per Unit Area (sum of KE and PE)**

The instantaneous KE density integrated over depth:

$$E_k = \int_{-d}^{0} \frac{1}{2}\rho (u_x^2 + u_z^2) \, dz$$

After depth integration (and applying $\int_{-d}^{0}\cosh^2 k(z+d)\,dz = \frac{\sinh 2kd + 2kd}{4k}$), and in the deep-water limit $kd \to \infty$, we obtain:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

The PE density averaged over one wave period:

$$E_p = \frac{1}{16}\rho g H^2$$

So:

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$$

**Step F — Energy Flux (Power per unit crest length)**

Energy propagates at the **group velocity** $c_g$:

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{\omega/k}{2} = \frac{g}{2\omega} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

Therefore, the **power per unit crest length**:

$$P = E_{total} \cdot c_g = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64\pi} \;\; (\text{W/m})}$$

**Step G — Numerical Substitution (standard wave)**

Take $H = 2$ m, $T = 8$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$P = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2 \times (2)^2 \times 8}{64\pi}$$

Computing stepwise:
- $(9.81)^2 = 96.2361$
- $1025 \times 96.2361 = 98642.0$
- $\times 4 = 394568$
- $\times 8 = 3156544$
- $/ (64\pi) = 3156544 / 201.062 \approx 15700$ W/m

$$\boxed{P \approx 15.7 \text{ kW/m}}$$

Cross-check with empirical rule: $0.5 \times H^2 \times T = 0.5 \times 4 \times 8 = 16$ kW/m ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Energy in a Single Wavelength

For a wave of length $\lambda$, energy per unit crest length per wavelength is:

$$E_\lambda = E_{total} \cdot \lambda = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \lambda$$

Using $\lambda = gT^2/(2\pi)$:

$$E_\lambda = \frac{\rho g^3 H^2 T^2}{16\pi}$$

**Numerical Example:** $H = 2$ m, $T = 8$ s, $\lambda = 1.56 \times 64 = 99.84$ m.

$$E_\lambda = \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 99.84$$
$$= 0.125 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4 \times 99.84$$
$$\approx 502{,}000 \text{ J/m} \approx 502 \text{ kJ/m}$$

This is the energy arriving at a 1 m wide line of a coastline in **one wavelength** — a vivid demonstration of wave energy's massive density.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| WEC Type | Operating Principle | Capacity Range | TRL* | Storage Coupling |
|----------|--------------------|----------------|------|-------------------|
| **Point Absorber** | Buoy heaving | 50 kW – 1 MW | 7–8 | Hydraulic accumulator / flywheel |
| **Attenuator** (Pelamis) | Multi-segment hinged | 750 kW | 7 | Hydraulic + generator |
| **Terminator** (Oyster) | Oscillating flap | 315 kW | 7 | Hydraulic → generator |
| **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** | Air compression | 100 kW – 1 MW | 8–9 | Compressed air / Wells turbine |
| **Overtopping** (Wave Dragon) | Reservoir fill | 4–24 MW | 6 | Pumped hydro-style |
| **Submerged Pressure Differential** | Bulge wave | 100 kW | 5 | Direct generator |

*TRL = Technology Readiness Level (1–9).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Wave Energy Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ocean Wave Input] --> B[Wave Energy Capture]
    B --> C[Primary Power Take Off PTO]
    C --> D[Rectification and Conditioning Unit]
    D --> E{Storage Mode Selector}
    E --> F1[Hydraulic Accumulator]
    E --> F2[Compressed Air CAES]
    E --> F3[Flywheel KERS]
    E --> F4[Battery / Supercapacitor]
    F1 --> G[Secondary PTO Stage]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Inversion and Grid Sync]
    H --> I[Grid Output 50 Hz AC]
    B --> J[Mooring and Anchoring Subsystem]
    J -.stabilizes.-> B
```

## 4.2 Detailed Schematic: Oscillating Water Column (OWC) with CAES Storage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Incident Wave] --> C[Capture Chamber Partially Submerged]
    C -->|air pushed up| V1[One way Valve]
    V1 --> T1[Wells Turbine Bidirectional]
    T1 --> G1[Generator 1 Direct AC]
    C -->|air sucked down| V2[Second one way Valve]
    V2 --> T2[Secondary Air Turbine]
    T2 --> CMP[Air Compressor Stage]
    CMP --> RES[Underground Air Reservoir CAES]
    RES -.release on demand.-> T2
    G1 --> INV[Power Electronics Inverter]
    T2 --> INV
    INV --> GRID[Grid Output]
    G1 --> BAT[Battery Bank Short Term]
    INV --> BAT
```

## 4.3 WEC Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Wave Energy Converters WECs]
    root --> A1[Fixed Structure]
    root --> A2[Floating Structure]
    A1 --> A11[Oscillating Water Column]
    A1 --> A12[Overtopping Device]
    A1 --> A13[Submerged Pressure Diff]
    A2 --> A21[Point Absorber]
    A2 --> A22[Attenuator]
    A2 --> A23[Terminator Oscillating Flap]
    A21 --> A211[Bulge Wave Type]
    A21 --> A212[Floating Buoy Type]
```

## 4.4 Energy Conversion Chain (Cascaded Storage View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Sea Wave KE and PE] -->|mechanical| E2[Buoy / Flap Motion]
    E2 -->|hydraulic| E3[Pressurized Fluid]
    E3 -->|pneumatic| E4[Compressed Air]
    E3 -->|mechanical| E5[Flywheel Rotation]
    E4 -->|on demand| E6[Air Turbine]
    E5 -->|on demand| E6
    E6 -->|electromagnetic| E7[Generator Output]
    E7 -->|AC DC AC| E8[Grid Ready Power]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define wave energy. State the empirical formula for wave power per unit crest length used in KTU board examinations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Wave energy** is the renewable mechanical energy carried by ocean surface gravity waves, comprising both kinetic energy of water-particle motion and potential energy of the displaced free surface.
>
> The **empirical wave power formula** (most commonly used in KTU exams) is:
>
> $$P_w = 0.5 \cdot H_s^2 \cdot T_e \;\; (\text{kW/m})$$
>
> where $H_s$ is the significant wave height (m) and $T_e$ is the energy period (s).

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks formula explanation)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Pattern]**
**List any three classifications of Wave Energy Converters (WECs) with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Point Absorber** – single small buoy, e.g., PowerBuoy (Ocean Power Technologies).
2. **Attenuator** – long multi-segment floating device aligned with wave direction, e.g., Pelamis.
3. **Terminator** – device whose long axis is perpendicular to wave direction, e.g., Oscillating Water Column (OWC) / Oyster flap.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each classification)*

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the expression for wave power per unit length of wave crest in deep water using Airy wave theory. State all assumptions clearly.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) For a wave with significant height 2.5 m and energy period 9 s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³), calculate (i) the wavelength, (ii) the wave power per unit crest length using both the rigorous and the empirical formula, and (iii) the total energy per unit area.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
- Incompressible, inviscid fluid.
- Irrotational flow.
- Small amplitude (linear) waves: $H \ll \lambda$.
- Deep water: $d > \lambda/2$ (water depth > wavelength/2).

**Steps (5.5 marks):**

The free surface elevation is $\eta(x,t) = \frac{H}{2}\cos(kx - \omega t)$.

The linear velocity potential in deep water is:

$$\phi = \frac{H g}{2\omega} e^{kz} \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Total energy per unit area (KE + PE):

$$E_{total} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$$

Group velocity in deep water:

$$c_g = \frac{g}{2\omega} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

Wave power per unit crest length:

$$P = E_{total} \cdot c_g = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 \cdot \frac{gT}{4\pi}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64\pi} \;\; (\text{W/m})}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $H = 2.5$ m, $T = 9$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Wavelength (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_0 = \frac{gT^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \times 81}{2\pi} = \frac{794.61}{6.2832}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_0 \approx 126.48 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Wave power (3.5 marks):**

Rigorous formula:

$$P = \frac{1025 \times 96.2361 \times 6.25 \times 9}{64\pi}$$

Numerator: $1025 \times 96.2361 \times 6.25 \times 9 = 1025 \times 96.2361 \times 56.25$
- $1025 \times 96.2361 = 98642.0$
- $98642.0 \times 56.25 = 5548612.5$

Denominator: $64\pi = 201.062$

$$P = \frac{5548612.5}{201.062} \approx 27596 \text{ W/m} \approx 27.6 \text{ kW/m}$$

Empirical formula:

$$P_w = 0.5 \times H^2 \times T = 0.5 \times 6.25 \times 9 = 28.125 \text{ kW/m}$$

$$\boxed{P_{rigorous} \approx 27.6 \text{ kW/m}, \quad P_{empirical} \approx 28.1 \text{ kW/m}}$$

**(iii) Energy per unit area (2 marks):**

$$E = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2 = \frac{1}{8} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25$$

$$= 0.125 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 6.25 = 0.125 \times 62860.78$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 7857.6 \text{ J/m}^2 \approx 7.86 \text{ kJ/m}^2}$$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy system coupled with a compressed air energy storage (CAES) subsystem.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Compare Point Absorber, Attenuator, and Terminator type wave energy converters. Discuss their relative merits and demerits with respect to capture width ratio and survivability.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — OWC + CAES (7 marks)**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
An OWC consists of a partially submerged hollow chamber with an opening below the waterline. As waves enter the chamber, the water column rises and falls, alternately compressing and decompressing the air column trapped above. This bidirectional air flow is routed through a **Wells turbine**, which spins in the same direction regardless of flow direction, driving a generator.

**CAES Coupling (3 marks):**
Part of the compressed air is diverted to a **compressor train** that stores it in an underground cavern or pressure vessel. During periods of low wave activity, the stored air is released back through an expander turbine to produce electricity. This effectively **decouples generation from wave availability**, giving the system storage characteristics similar to pumped hydro.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to **Section 4.2 Mermaid schematic** in these notes.

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks)**

| Feature | Point Absorber | Attenuator | Terminator |
|---------|----------------|------------|------------|
| **Size relative to $\lambda$** | Small (~$<\lambda/4$) | Long (several $\lambda$) | Wide (~$>\lambda/2$) |
| **Orientation** | Any | Aligned with wave direction | Perpendicular to wave direction |
| **CWR** | High (50–90%) | Moderate (40–60%) | High (60–80%) |
| **Survivability** | Good (small target) | Moderate (long structure) | Moderate |
| **Examples** | PowerBuoy, Wavebob | Pelamis, AWS | OWC, Oyster |
| **Cost** | Low to moderate | High (multiple joints) | Moderate |
| **Maintenance access** | Easy | Difficult (offshore) | Moderate |

**Merits of Point Absorber:** Compact, omni-directional, simple mooring.
**Demerits of Attenuator:** Complex articulated joints, high failure risk.
**Demerits of Terminator:** Sensitive to wave direction, large structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing W/m and kW/m between the rigorous and empirical formulas. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting assumptions of Airy theory:** Examiners always check for the 4–5 assumptions in derivation. (–1.5 marks)
> 3. **Confusing group velocity with phase velocity:** Use $c_g = c/2$ in deep water; not $c$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the KTU empirical formula:** Most students derive the rigorous one but miss the 0.5·H²·T rule-of-thumb. (–1 mark)
> 5. **No diagram in descriptive answers:** A neat labelled block diagram carries full marks in (a) of Part B. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting density value for seawater:** Use $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, not 1000. (–0.5 mark)

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Wave energy** is renewable mechanical energy in ocean surface gravity waves; energy density is 10–20× wind/solar.
- [x] **Airy / Linear wave theory** governs the math; key assumption is $H \ll \lambda$.
- [x] **Total energy per unit area** $E = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ — this is the most tested equation.
- [x] **Group velocity** in deep water = $c/2$ = $\frac{gT}{4\pi}$.
- [x] **Wave power** $P = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{64\pi}$ (W/m) **or** $0.5 H^2 T$ (kW/m).
- [x] **Wavelength** $\lambda_0 = 1.56 T^2$ m (deep water).
- [x] **5 WEC families**: Point Absorber, Attenuator, Terminator, OWC, Overtopping.
- [x] **Storage coupling**: OWC ↔ CAES, Point Absorber ↔ Hydraulic Accumulator, all WECs ↔ Battery/Flywheel.
- [x] **Empirical KTU rule** $P \approx 0.5 H^2 T$ kW/m — must be memorized.
- [x] **Seawater density** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³; $g = 9.81$ m/s² — both are KTU constants.
- [x] **OWC** uses bidirectional **Wells turbine**; air is the working fluid.
- [x] **Capture Width Ratio (CWR)** is the standard WEC performance metric.
- [x] **Energy per wavelength** $E_\lambda = \frac{\rho g^3 H^2 T^2}{16\pi}$ — useful for very-long-baseline problem solving.
- [x] **Real-world deployments**: Azores (Portugal), Mutriku (Spain), Wave Hub (UK), King Island (Australia).
- [x] **TRL status**: OWC is the most mature (TRL 8–9); submerged pressure differential is least (TRL 5).
- [x] **Engineering challenges**: corrosion, biofouling, storm survivability, grid integration.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wind

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Wind Energy** is the kinetic energy possessed by moving air masses (wind) which can be converted into useful forms of power, primarily electricity, through the use of Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS). A WECS transforms the aerodynamic force exerted by wind on rotating blades of a turbine into mechanical shaft power, which is subsequently converted into electrical energy by a coupled generator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Definition:** Wind energy is classified as an *indirect* form of mechanical energy storage because modern WECS almost always requires a complementary storage medium (batteries, supercapacitors, hydrogen, or pumped hydro) to overcome the inherent intermittency of wind and match supply with grid demand.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing on a bridge with a steady stream of cars moving across. Each car carries **kinetic energy** ($\frac{1}{2}mv^2$). If you could somehow "skim" a fraction of that energy off each passing car without stopping traffic, you would be doing exactly what a wind turbine does to air molecules. The wind is a **river of air** flowing over the Earth's surface, and a wind turbine is essentially a submerged "water wheel" that intercepts a slice of this flowing fluid, extracting a portion of its kinetic energy while letting the rest continue downstream.

The three knobs that determine how much power a wind stream carries are:
1. **How dense the air is** ($\rho$) — like the number of cars per kilometer of road.
2. **How big the "interception window" is** ($A$) — the swept area of the blades.
3. **How fast the air is moving** ($v$) — the speed limit on the highway.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard air density at sea level | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Betz coefficient (theoretical maximum $C_p$) | $C_{P,max}$ | **16/27 ≈ 0.5926** | dimensionless |
| Typical operational $C_p$ (modern turbines) | $C_P$ | **0.35 – 0.45** | dimensionless |
| Cut-in wind speed | $v_{in}$ | **3 – 4** | m/s |
| Rated wind speed | $v_{rated}$ | **12 – 15** | m/s |
| Cut-out wind speed | $v_{out}$ | **25 – 30** | m/s |
| Specific power of wind at 12 m/s | $P/A$ | **≈ 1058** | W/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Betz Coefficient matters in KTU exams:** You will frequently be asked "What is the maximum fraction of wind power that an *ideal* turbine can extract?" The answer is **16/27 (≈ 59.3%)**, derived by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919. No real turbine can exceed this limit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Wind Power — Step-by-Step Logic

The conversion of wind energy to electricity follows a strict thermodynamic chain. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU exam success.

- **Step 1 — Kinetic Energy Reservoir:** Wind is simply air in bulk motion. Every cubic meter of moving air carries kinetic energy equal to $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ joules.
- **Step 2 — Mass Flow Through the Rotor Disc:** In one second, a cylindrical column of air of cross-section $A$ and length $v$ passes through the rotor. Its mass is $\dot{m} = \rho A v$.
- **Step 3 — Available Power in the Wind:** Combining Steps 1 and 2 gives the gross aerodynamic power flowing through the swept area every second: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$.
- **Step 4 — Power Extraction by the Turbine:** The turbine can only extract a fraction of this, governed by the power coefficient $C_P$. Hence: $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$.
- **Step 5 — Mechanical-to-Electrical Conversion:** Gearbox, generator, and power electronics introduce further losses ($\eta_{mech}$, $\eta_{gen}$), so the *electrical* output is: $P_{el} = C_P \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$.
- **Step 6 — Energy Storage Buffering:** Because wind is intermittent, the electrical output is either sent to the grid or routed to a storage subsystem (battery / supercapacitor / hydrogen).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cube-law principle (v³):** A small increase in wind speed produces a *huge* increase in available power. Doubling the wind speed (e.g., from 6 m/s to 12 m/s) increases the available power by a factor of **8** (2³). This is why site selection and hub-height design are critical in wind farm engineering.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Gross power in the wind stream | W |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Swept area of a rotor of radius R | m² |
| 3 | $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Mechanical power captured by the rotor | W |
| 4 | $C_{P,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz theoretical limit | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass flow rate of air through disc | kg/s |
| 6 | $E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P(t)\, dt$ | Energy harvested over a time interval | J (or Wh) |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Tip-speed ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $P_{el} = \eta_{total}\, C_P\, \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ | Net electrical output | W |
| 9 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | Capacity factor of a wind farm | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v(h) = v_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Power-law wind shear profile | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX tip for KTU answer sheets:** The absolute value $\vert x \vert$ must NEVER be written using the keyboard pipe character `|` inside your LaTeX table — always use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the table syntax intact.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wind-to-electrical energy conversion underpins three large industries in 2024–2026:

1. **Utility-scale wind farms** (50 MW – 2 GW) feeding directly into high-voltage transmission grids, often paired with Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) or Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS).
2. **Off-grid distributed wind** (1 – 100 kW) for telecom towers, remote villages, and island microgrids coupled with hybrid battery-diesel storage.
3. **Offshore floating wind** — emerging 12 MW+ platforms requiring subsea HVDC cables and large-scale hydrogen production for storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Context (NEP 2020):** The wind-storage pairing is a *mandatory* design consideration in Module 3. Any WECS without storage is treated as a "non-dispatchable" source and cannot guarantee firm power delivery. Energy storage transforms WECS from a *variable* source to a *dispatchable* asset.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power in the Wind Stream

**Starting Premise:** Consider a uniform wind of speed $v$ (m/s) blowing perpendicular to a circular disc of area $A$ (m²) representing the rotor swept area. We want to find the kinetic power carried by this wind stream.

**Step 1 — Write the kinetic energy of a moving mass of air.**

A mass $m$ of air moving at speed $v$ has kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.

**Step 2 — Determine the mass of air that crosses the disc per second.**

In one second, a column of air of length $v$ and cross-section $A$ passes through the disc. The volume of this column is $V = A \cdot v$. The mass is therefore $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho A v$.

**Step 3 — Identify the mass flow rate.**

By definition, the mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = \dfrac{dm}{dt} = \rho A v$ (kg/s), since in each second a fresh mass $\rho A v$ passes through the disc.

**Step 4 — Compute the kinetic energy flux (power).**

The power in the wind is the rate at which kinetic energy crosses the disc:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= \frac{dE_k}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{dm}{dt} \cdot v^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \quad \text{(Watts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify dimensional consistency.**

$[\rho] = \text{kg/m}^3$, $[A] = \text{m}^2$, $[v^3] = \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$. Multiplying: $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \text{m}^2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}^3} = \dfrac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^2}{\text{s}^3} = \text{Watts}$ ✓

> **Engineering Insight:** The cubic dependence on $v$ is the single most important reason why a 1% error in wind speed estimation produces nearly a 3% error in power estimation. Accurate anemometry is therefore mission-critical in wind farm design.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Betz Limit ($C_{P,max} = 16/27$)

**Starting Premise:** We use the **actuator disc model** (Rankine–Froude theory). The wind turbine is modelled as a porous disc that slows the air from an upstream velocity $v_1$ to a downstream velocity $v_2$, with a velocity $v$ at the disc itself.

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass (continuity equation).**

The mass flow rate must be the same through any cross-section of the stream tube:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho A_1 v_1 &= \rho A v = \rho A_2 v_2 \\
\therefore A_1 v_1 &= A v = A_2 v_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the momentum theorem (Euler's turbine equation).**

The thrust force $F$ on the disc equals the rate of change of momentum of the air:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's equation on the upstream and downstream sides.**

Neglecting rotational losses, the pressure jump across the disc is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p &= p_2 - p_1 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_1^{\,2} - v_2^{\,2})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Express thrust in terms of the pressure jump.**

The force on the disc is also $F = A (p_2 - p_1)$. Equating the two expressions for $F$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho A v (v_1 - v_2) &= A \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_1^{\,2} - v_2^{\,2}) \\
2 v (v_1 - v_2) &= (v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $v_1 \neq v_2$ (otherwise no power is extracted), we can divide by $(v_1 - v_2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 v &= v_1 + v_2 \\
\therefore v &= \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Write the power extracted by the disc.**
