The power extracted equals thrust times disc velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= F \cdot v = \rho A v (v_1 - v_2) \cdot v \\
&= \rho A v^2 (v_1 - v_2) \\
&= \rho A \left(\frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}\right)^2 (v_1 - v_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Express everything in terms of $v_1$ and the induction factor $a$.**

Define the axial induction factor $a$ such that $v = v_1(1 - a)$. From Step 4, $v_2 = v_1(1 - 2a)$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \rho A \, v_1^{\,3} (1-a) \cdot \bigl[(1-a) - (1-2a)\bigr] \cdot \bigl[\text{combining } (1-a) \text{ factor}\bigr] \\
&= \rho A v_1^{\,3} \cdot 4a(1-a)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the power coefficient.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^{\,3}} = \frac{4a(1-a)^2}{1/2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\
&= 4a(1-a)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $a$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dC_P}{da} &= 4(1-a)^2 + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) \\
&= 4(1-a)^2 - 8a(1-a) \\
&= 4(1-a)\bigl[(1-a) - 2a\bigr] \\
&= 4(1-a)(1 - 3a)
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dC_P}{da} = 0$ gives either $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

**Step 9 — Substitute $a = 1/3$ into $C_P$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{P,max} &= 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 \\
&= \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} \\
&= \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Result:** The Betz limit, $C_{P,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$, states that *no* wind turbine — no matter how perfectly designed — can convert more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in wind into useful mechanical work. The remaining ~40.7% must remain in the downstream wake to keep the air moving away from the rotor.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Wind Turbine Classification

| Class | Axis Orientation | Typical Use | $C_P$ Range | Storage Compatibility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HAWT (Horizontal Axis)** | Rotor shaft parallel to wind | Utility-scale, offshore | 0.40 – 0.48 | High — easy BESS/PHS integration |
| **VAWT (Vertical Axis)** | Rotor shaft perpendicular to wind | Distributed, urban rooftops | 0.20 – 0.35 | Medium — needs dedicated BESS |
| **Darrieus (egg-beater)** | Vertical axis, lift-based | Research, niche | 0.25 – 0.35 | Low–Medium |
| **Savonius (drag-based)** | Vertical axis, drag-based | Low-power, education | 0.10 – 0.20 | Low |
| **Floating Offshore** | HAWT on semi-submersible | Deep-sea wind farms | 0.45 – 0.50 | Very High — hydrogen storage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wind Resource v m/s] --> B[Rotor Blades Swept Area A m2]
    B --> C[Hub and Main Shaft Mechanical Power]
    C --> D[Gearbox Speed Multiplier]
    D --> E[Generator AC Induction or PMSG]
    E --> F[Power Electronics Rectifier Inverter]
    F --> G{Dispatch Logic Controller}
    G -->|High demand| H[Grid Step up Transformer]
    G -->|Low demand| I[Battery Energy Storage System BESS]
    G -->|Long term surplus| J[Hydrogen Electrolyzer and Fuel Cell]
    I --> K[Bi directional Inverter]
    J --> K
    H --> L[End User Load]
    K --> L
    M[SCADA and Wind Sensors Anemometer] --> G
    N[Pitch and Yaw Control System] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds from the *primary energy source* (wind) on the left to the *end user load* on the right, with the dispatch controller (center) acting as the brain that routes energy to either the grid or to one of the storage subsystems. The SCADA feedback loop ensures the rotor blades are pitched for optimal $C_P$, and the yaw system keeps the nacelle pointed into the prevailing wind.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Wind Turbine Subsystem Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SYS[Wind Turbine System] --> R1[Rotor Subsystem]
    SYS --> R2[Drive Train Subsystem]
    SYS --> R3[Generator and Power Subsystem]
    SYS --> R4[Control and Safety Subsystem]
    SYS --> R5[Tower and Foundation Subsystem]

    R1 --> R1A[Blades Aerofoils]
    R1 --> R1B[Hub and Pitch Mechanism]
    R1 --> R1C[Nacelle Yaw Drive]

    R2 --> R2A[Low Speed Shaft]
    R2 --> R2B[Gearbox Step up]
    R2 --> R2C[High Speed Shaft]
    R2 --> R2D[Mechanical Brake]

    R3 --> R3A[Generator PMSG or DFIG]
    R3 --> R3B[Converter AC DC AC]
    R3 --> R3C[Transformer and Switchgear]

    R4 --> R4A[SCADA Controller]
    R4 --> R4B[Anemometer and Wind Vane]
    R4 --> R4C[Pitch and Yaw Actuators]
    R4 --> R4D[Safety Shutdown System]

    R5 --> R5A[Tubular Steel Tower]
    R5 --> R5B[Foundation Pile or Raft]
    R5 --> R5C[Access Ladder and Service Lift]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The five top-level subsystems correspond directly to the modules of a modern multi-megawatt wind turbine. Each subsystem is engineered as an *independent* engineering package, which is why the wind industry has been able to scale rapidly using parallel design teams.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Wind Power vs. Wind Speed Curve (Power Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Region 1 v less than v_in Cut in] --> P2[Region 2 v_in to v_rated Partial Load]
    P2 --> P3[Region 3 v_rated to v_out Rated Load]
    P3 --> P4[Region 4 v greater than v_out Shutdown]

    P1 --> P1D[No output P equals 0]
    P2 --> P2D[Output follows cube law P proportional to v cubed]
    P3 --> P3D[Output is constant at P_rated Pitch control active]
    P4 --> P4D[Output is zero Safety shutdown]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This four-region power curve is a *mandatory* KTU topic. Region 1 = idle, Region 2 = cube-law extraction, Region 3 = constant rated power (the pitch controller feathering blades to dump excess wind), Region 4 = safety cut-out to prevent structural damage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*State and explain the Betz limit for a wind turbine. Why is it impossible to extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Betz limit states that the maximum fraction of the kinetic energy in wind that an *ideal* turbine can convert into useful mechanical work is **$C_{P,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$** (1 mark). It is impossible to extract 100% because if all the kinetic energy were removed, the air would have to come to a complete standstill at the rotor disc (1 mark). A stationary air mass would block the incoming flow, choking the turbine — this is called the "plug-flow" condition. Therefore, a minimum downstream velocity must be preserved to allow continuous air flow through the rotor (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplied Paper]**
*Define "Capacity Factor" of a wind farm. A wind farm has a rated capacity of 50 MW and generates 1.314 × 10⁸ kWh in one year. Calculate its capacity factor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **capacity factor (CF)** is the ratio of the *actual* energy generated in a year to the *maximum possible* energy that could have been generated if the farm operated at full rated power for 8760 hours (1 mark).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy at rated power} &= P_{rated} \times 8760 = 50 \times 8760 = 4.38 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh} = 4.38 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh} \\
CF &= \frac{1.314 \times 10^8}{4.38 \times 10^8} = 0.30 = 30\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks for calculation)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Wind Power Calculation and Betz Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the power available in the wind stream and explain why wind speed is the most critical parameter in wind energy harvesting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. Consider a uniform wind of velocity $v$ m/s flowing perpendicular to a rotor disc of swept area $A$ m². **(1 mark)**
2. The volume of air passing through the disc per second is $V = A \cdot v$ m³/s. **(1 mark)**
3. The mass flow rate is $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ kg/s, where $\rho$ is air density. **(1 mark)**
4. The kinetic energy per unit mass is $\frac{1}{2}v^2$, so the power in the wind is: **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 \text{ (W)}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. Wind speed is the most critical parameter because of the **cube-law dependence** on $v$. A 10% error in wind speed produces a 33% error in power. **(1 mark)**
6. Site selection, anemometer height, and wind resource assessment therefore dominate wind farm economics. **(1 mark)**
7. Cubic dependence also makes turbine rating highly sensitive to wind regime — small increases in average wind speed dramatically improve annual energy production (AEP). **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, and the power coefficient is 0.40. Calculate: (i) the swept area, (ii) the gross wind power, (iii) the actual mechanical power captured, and (iv) the Betz-limited maximum mechanical power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(i) **Swept Area:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 5026.55 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

(ii) **Gross Wind Power:** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wind} &= \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \\
&\approx 5.32 \times 10^6 \text{ W} = 5.32 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Actual Mechanical Power Captured:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mech} &= C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 = 0.40 \times 5.32 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 2.13 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iv) **Betz-Limited Maximum Power:** **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Betz} &= \frac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \times 10^6 \\
&\approx 3.15 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Wind Turbine Classification and Storage Integration*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) with respect to orientation, $C_P$, wind direction dependence, and suitability for storage-coupled microgrids. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | HAWT | VAWT |
|---|---|---|
| Shaft orientation | Parallel to wind | Perpendicular to wind |
| Self-starting | Requires initial push | Yes (Darrieus needs push, Savonius self-starts) |
| Typical $C_P$ | 0.40 – 0.48 | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| Yaw mechanism | Required (costly) | Not required (omnidirectional) |
| Maintenance | Tower-top (costly) | Ground-level (cheap) |
| Wind direction sensitivity | High | Low |
| Storage microgrid suitability | Excellent | Moderate |
| Rated capacity range | 1 kW – 14 MW | 100 W – 100 kW |

**(4 marks) for the comparison table and (3 marks) for the engineering justification, including:**
- HAWTs are preferred for utility-scale BESS/PHS integration because of higher $C_P$ and proven reliability. **(1 mark)**
- VAWTs are preferred for urban microgrids and rooftop-BESS systems where omnidirectional acceptance reduces control complexity. **(1 mark)**
- HAWTs require complex yaw and pitch control which is non-trivial when integrated with storage. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** Explain the four regions of a wind turbine power curve. Why is energy storage *mandatory* in regions 2 and 4 for grid stability? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Region 1: Below cut-in speed ($v < v_{in}$, typically 3 m/s).** **(1.5 marks)**
The kinetic energy in the wind is too low to overcome friction and internal losses. The turbine is idling and the controller keeps the rotor in free-wheel mode. Output power = 0.

**Region 2: Partial load ($v_{in} \leq v < v_{rated}$).** **(1.5 marks)**
The turbine operates to **maximize $C_P$** by adjusting blade pitch to a fine-tuned angle. Output power follows the **cube law**: $P = C_P(v) \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$. The BESS is charged during this region to smooth the power delivered to the grid.

**Region 3: Rated load ($v_{rated} \leq v < v_{out}$).** **(1.5 marks)**
The turbine produces its **rated power** constantly. Blade pitch is actively adjusted to "spill" excess aerodynamic energy — the blades are feathered so the rotor disc presents less area to the wind. Storage is bypassed in this region; all power goes to the grid.

**Region 4: Cut-out ($v \geq v_{out}$, typically 25 m/s).** **(1.5 marks)**
The turbine is **fully shut down** to prevent structural damage. The blades are pitched to 90° (feathered) and the rotor is mechanically braked. The BESS must discharge to maintain grid supply during this "no-wind" forced outage.

**Why storage is mandatory in Regions 2 and 4:** **(1 mark)**
- In Region 2, wind speed fluctuates second-by-second, producing **power output flicker**. The grid code (e.g., Indian Electricity Grid Code 2023) requires frequency stability within ±0.5 Hz. A BESS smooths this in real time using bi-directional inverter control.
- In Region 4, generation drops to **zero abruptly**. Without storage, the grid would suffer an under-frequency event. The BESS acts as an "uninterruptible power supply" for the wind farm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** In Part B numerical questions, *always* write the final answer with units (W, kW, or MW). Marks are deducted for unit-less final answers.
> 2. **Confusing cut-in and rated speed:** Cut-in (≈ 3–4 m/s) is where generation *starts*; rated speed (≈ 12–15 m/s) is where generation *plateaus*. Mixing these up is a classic 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Stating Betz limit as 59.3% without showing the derivation step (a = 1/3):** Examiners expect at least the induction factor and the substitution step. A bare "answer = 16/27" with no working loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention the cube-law implication in Region 2 of the power curve:** This is the most common reason KTU students lose 1 mark in the 14-mark power curve question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wind Power Formula:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3$ — a *cube-law* dependence on wind speed.
- **Betz Limit:** Maximum theoretical $C_P = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$, achieved at axial induction factor $a = 1/3$.
- **Power Coefficient $C_P$:** Real turbines operate at $C_P = 0.35 - 0.45$, well below the Betz limit due to mechanical, aerodynamic, and electrical losses.
- **Swept Area:** $A = \pi R^2$ — doubling rotor diameter *quadruples* power output.
- **Tip-Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — optimal TSR is 6–8 for modern three-blade HAWTs.
- **Power Curve Regions:** Cut-in (Region 1) → Partial-load cube-law (Region 2) → Rated-load (Region 3) → Cut-out safety (Region 4).
- **Storage Coupling:** BESS, supercapacitors, hydrogen, and pumped hydro storage are *essential* to make WECS dispatchable; without storage, wind is a non-firm power source.
- **Capacity Factor:** Typical Indian wind farm CF = 25–35%; offshore wind farms achieve 45–55%.
- **Wind Shear Law:** $v(h) = v_{ref} (h/h_{ref})^{\alpha}$ — taller hub heights capture stronger, more consistent winds.
- **Classification:** HAWT (utility-scale, high $C_P$) vs VAWT (urban microgrid, omnidirectional, low $C_P$).
- **Key Subsystems:** Rotor → Drive train → Generator → Power electronics → Control → Tower/foundation.
- **KTU Must-Know Constants:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea-level, 15°C), $C_{P,max} = 16/27$, $v_{in} = 3$ m/s, $v_{rated} = 12$ m/s, $v_{out} = 25$ m/s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tidal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Tidal # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Tidal Energy** is a form of **renewable mechanical-to-electrical energy conversion** technology that exploits the periodic rise and fall of sea water levels — caused by the **gravitational interaction between the Earth, the Moon, and the Sun** — to drive low-head hydro turbines coupled to electrical generators. It is classified as a **predictable, dispatchable renewable source** and is frequently discussed under the umbrella of *Energy Storage Systems* because of its inherent cyclic storage characteristic (water stored in a basin at high tide acts as a potential energy reservoir). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > "Tidal power is the power generated by converting the energy of tidal movements (rise and fall of tides and tidal currents) into useful forms of energy, primarily electricity, through the use of hydraulic turbines and generators." **Key Sub-Technologies Recognized in Module 3:** | Sub-Technology | Operating Principle | Head Range | |---|---|---| | **Tidal Barrage** | Uses a dam-like barrier across an estuary | 3 m – 10 m | | **Tidal Stream (Current)** | Underwater turbines in open channel flow | Kinetic (no head) | | **Tidal Lagoon** | Coastal impoundment built offshore | 3 m – 8 m | | **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)** | Long dam perpendicular to coast | Theoretical | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview **Real-World Analogy — "The Bathroom Bucket Water Wheel" 🪣** Imagine a giant bucket (the *tidal basin*) connected to the ocean through a small water wheel (the *turbine*). - When the tide **rises** (flood tide), the ocean level becomes higher than the bucket. Water flows **IN** through the wheel, spinning it. - When the tide **falls** (ebb tide), the bucket has more water than the ocean. Water flows **OUT** through the wheel, spinning it again. - The wheel spins **twice a day**, predictably, forever — driven not by the sun, wind, or rain, but by the **Moon's gravity pulling the ocean around the Earth**. This is exactly what happens at the **La Rance Tidal Power Station (France)** — except the "bucket" is a 22 km² estuary, and the "wheel" is a row of **24 bulb turbines** producing 240 MW. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Distinction for KTU Exams:** > - Tidal **Energy** ≠ Tidal **Power**. *Energy* is the integral of power over time (kWh or MJ). *Power* is the instantaneous rate (kW or MW). > - The "energy" stored in a tidal basin is **gravitational potential energy** of the elevated water column. ## 1.3 Essential Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Constants for KTU Calculations** > - Density of seawater, **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** (NOT 1000 kg/m³) > - Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²** > - Tidal period (semi-diurnal), **T = 12 hours 25 minutes ≈ 12.42 h = 44,712 s** > - Number of tidal cycles per day, **n = 2** (semi-diurnal) > - Spring–Neap cycle period, **T_cycle = 14.77 days** (fortnightly) > - Typical tidal range (macrotidal), **H = 4 m to 12 m** > - Typical tidal current velocity, **v = 1.5 m/s to 3.0 m/s** --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of Tides — The Driving Physics The tides are generated by the **differential gravitational force** (tidal force) exerted primarily by the **Moon (~67%)** and the **Sun (~33%)** on the Earth's oceans. **Three Tidal Patterns You Must Know:** 1. **Semi-diurnal tides** — Two high + two low tides per day (e.g., most Atlantic coasts). 2. **Diurnal tides** — One high + one low tide per day (e.g., Gulf of Mexico). 3. **Mixed semi-diurnal tides** — Two unequal highs and lows per day (e.g., Pacific coasts). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this matter for KTU?** > Because **Spring tides** (when Sun + Moon align → new/full moon) produce the *maximum* tidal range and *maximum* available energy, while **Neap tides** (Sun–Moon at 90°) produce *minimum* range. The average power plant must therefore be designed for the **mean tidal range**, not the maximum. ## 2.2 Modes of Power Generation in a Tidal Barrage A tidal barrage operates in **three distinct cycles** (KTU favourite!): | Cycle | Flow Direction | Head Condition | Power Generated? | |---|---|---|---| | **Ebb Generation** | Basin → Sea (outgoing) | Basin higher than sea | ✅ **YES** | | **Flood Generation** | Sea → Basin (incoming) | Sea higher than basin | ✅ **YES** | | **Two-Way Generation** | Both directions | Bidirectional | ✅ **YES (both)** | | **Pumping Mode** | Sea → Basin (against head) | Forced using grid power | ❌ NO (consumes) | > [!NOTE] > **Pumping Mode = Energy Storage** > When excess grid power is available (e.g., during low-demand periods), reversible turbines pump water from sea to basin. This **artificially raises the head**, increasing the energy available at the next generation phase. This is the **storage function** of a tidal plant. ## 2.3 Available Energy in a Tidal Basin — Conceptual Steps **Step-by-step logical breakdown:** 1. **Water enters the basin** during flood tide → potential energy increases as height `h` rises. 2. **Maximum potential energy** is stored at **high tide**, when `h = H` (tidal range). 3. **Water is released** through turbines during ebb tide → kinetic energy of flow → mechanical torque on turbine blades → electrical energy. 4. **Net extractable energy per cycle** depends on the **usable head**, which is always less than `H` due to: - Turbulence losses in sluice gates - Submergence of turbine draft tubes - Frictional losses in the approach channel 5. **Not all water is drained** — typically only the water between the **turbine cut-in head** and the **sill level** passes through the turbines. The remaining water in the basin is "stranded" energy. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this table. These formulas appear in almost every KTU numerical.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Mass of water in basin** | $m = \rho_{sw} \cdot A \cdot h$ | kg | $A$ = basin area, $h$ = instantaneous head | | 2 | **Potential energy stored** | $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} \, g \, A \, H^2$ | J (Joules) | Maximum at high tide | | 3 | **Average power per cycle** | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\rho_{sw} \, g \, A \, H^2}{2T}$ | W (Watts) | $T$ = tidal period (s) | | 4 | **Theoretical energy per cycle** | $E_{th} = \rho_{sw} \, g \, A \, \int_0^H h \, dh = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g A H^2$ | J | Integral form | | 5 | **Available energy (single-basin, ebb only)** | $E_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} g A H^2 \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_g$ | J | Includes turbine + generator efficiency | | 6 | **Tidal stream power density** | $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} \, A_s \, v^3 \, C_p$ | W | $A_s$ = swept area, $C_p$ = power coefficient | | 7 | **Betz limit for tidal stream** | $C_{p,max} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | — | Theoretical maximum | | 8 | **Plant capacity factor** | $CF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times 8760}$ | — | Typical CF for tidal = 0.22 to 0.28 | | 9 | **Head loss (sluice)** | $\Delta h = K \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | m | $K$ = loss coefficient | | 10 | **Daily energy (2 cycles)** | $E_{day} = 2 \times E_{cycle}$ | J | Two semi-diurnal cycles | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where Tidal Energy Fits in the Modern Grid:** > - **Predictability**: Tides are astronomically predictable for centuries (no weather dependency). > - **Dispatchability**: Tidal plants can be synchronized with grid demand unlike solar/wind. > - **Storage Function**: Tidal lagoons with pumping act as **giant gravitational batteries**. > - **Hybrid Plants**: Combined with offshore wind and solar, tidal forms a stable baseload. > - **Coastal Desalination**: Tidal energy can directly drive reverse-osmosis pumps. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Power from a Tidal Basin (Ebb Generation) **Problem Setup:** A tidal basin of area `A` (m²) has a tidal range `H` (m). Water is released through turbines during ebb generation. Find the **average power** generated per tidal cycle. **Given:** - Basin area: `A` (m²) - Tidal range: `H` (m) - Sea-water density: **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²** - Tidal period: `T` (s) **Step 1:** Consider an elemental slice of water of height `dh` above the basin floor. The mass of this slice is: $$dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot dh$$ The gravitational potential energy of this slice relative to the basin floor (taking the floor as the zero-potential reference) is: $$dE_p = dm \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \, dh$$ **Step 2:** Integrate from `h = 0` (basin floor) to `h = H` (high tide level) to find the total potential energy stored at high tide: $$E_p = \int_{0}^{H} \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot h \, dh$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integral. Since ρ, A, and g are constants with respect to h: $$E_p = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \int_{0}^{H} h \, dh$$ The antiderivative of `h` is `h²/2`: $$E_p = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \left[ \frac{h^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H}$$ **Step 4:** Apply the limits (upper limit `H`, lower limit `0`): $$E_p = \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot \left( \frac{H^2}{2} - \frac{0^2}{2} \right)$$ $$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot H^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the master formula. Memorize it: $E_p = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho g A H^2$** **Step 5:** The average power is energy divided by the period of one tidal cycle `T`: $$P_{avg} = \frac{E_p}{T} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot H^2}{2T}$$ **Step 6:** Insert numerical constants for seawater and g: $$P_{avg} = \frac{1025 \times A \times 9.81 \times H^2}{2 \times T} = \frac{5027.6 \cdot A \cdot H^2}{T} \;\; \text{(in Watts)}$$ **Step 7:** Sanity check the proportionality: - Power ∝ **A** (linear — double the area → double the power) - Power ∝ **H²** (quadratic — double the tidal range → **quadruple** the power) - This is why **macrotidal sites** (H > 5 m) are vastly preferred. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Annual Energy Output of a Tidal Plant **Step 1:** Number of tidal cycles per year (using semi-diurnal T = 12.42 h): $$N_{cycles} = \frac{365.25 \times 24 \text{ h}}{12.42 \text{ h/cycle}} = 705.7 \text{ cycles/year}$$ **Step 2:** Annual theoretical energy: $$E_{annual} = N_{cycles} \times E_p = 705.7 \times \frac{1}{2} \rho g A H^2$$ **Step 3:** Apply overall efficiency η_total = η_turbine × η_generator × η_transmission: $$E_{annual,actual} = \eta_{total} \times 705.7 \times \frac{1}{2} \rho g A H^2$$ **Step 4:** Substitute numerical values for a typical plant (A = 20 km², H = 8 m, η_total = 0.30): First, compute theoretical energy per cycle: $$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times 8^2$$ $$E_p = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2 \times 10^7 \times 64$$ Compute step by step: - $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$ - $512.5 \times 9.81 = 5027.625$ - $5027.625 \times 2 \times 10^7 = 1.00553 \times 10^{11}$ - $1.00553 \times 10^{11} \times 64 = 6.4354 \times 10^{12}$ J So $E_p \approx 6.44$ TJ per cycle. **Step 5:** Annual theoretical energy: $$E_{annual} = 705.7 \times 6.4354 \times 10^{12} = 4.541 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}$$ **Step 6:** Apply efficiency (η = 0.30): $$E_{annual,actual} = 0.30 \times 4.541 \times 10^{15} = 1.362 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}$$ Convert to MWh: divide by $3.6 \times 10^9$: $$E_{annual,actual} = \frac{1.362 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 378{,}333 \text{ MWh} \approx 378 \text{ GWh}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Tidal vs. Other Renewables | Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream | Offshore Wind | Solar PV | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Energy density (W/m²)** | 3 – 10 | 5 – 15 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 0.15 – 0.25 | | **Capacity factor** | 0.22 – 0.28 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.15 – 0.25 | | **Predictability** | Centuries-ahead | Decades-ahead | Hours-ahead | Minutes-ahead | | **Storage capability** | ✅ Yes (pumping) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | | **Construction cost ($/kW)** | 4000 – 8000 | 5000 – 9000 | 3000 – 6000 | 800 – 1500 | | **LCOE ($/MWh)** | 150 – 300 | 200 – 400 | 80 – 150 | 40 – 80 | | **Visual impact** | High | Low (subsea) | Moderate | Low | | **Marine ecology impact** | High | Moderate | Moderate | None | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU 2-Mark Conversion Question **Question:** A tidal basin has an area of 5 km² and a mean tidal range of 6 m. Calculate the average power available. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify and convert units: - $A = 5 \text{ km}^2 = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$ - $H = 6 \text{ m}$ - $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 12.42 \times 3600 = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$ **Step 2:** Apply the average power formula: $$P_{avg} = \frac{\rho \cdot A \cdot g \cdot H^2}{2T}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute: $$P_{avg} = \frac{1025 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 9.81 \times 6^2}{2 \times 44{,}712}$$ **Step 4:** Compute the numerator: - $1025 \times 5 \times 10^6 = 5.125 \times 10^9$ - $5.125 \times 10^9 \times 9.81 = 5.0276 \times 10^{10}$ - $5.0276 \times 10^{10} \times 36 = 1.8100 \times 10^{12}$ **Step 5:** Compute the denominator: - $2 \times 44{,}712 = 89{,}424$ **Step 6:** Divide: $$P_{avg} = \frac{1.8100 \times 10^{12}}{8.9424 \times 10^4} = 2.024 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 20.24 \text{ MW}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Answer: $P_{avg} \approx 20.24$ MW** > After applying η_total ≈ 0.30, the electrical output would be ~6 MW. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Tidal Barrage Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Sea / Ocean Tidal Source] --> B[Tidal Basin Entrance Channel] B --> C{Sluice Gates
Control Flow} C --> D[Tidal Basin
Reservoir A m2] D --> E[High Pressure Penstock] E --> F[Bulb Turbine Assembly
Kaplan Type] F --> G[Speed Multiplier Gearbox] G --> H[Synchronous Generator
3-Phase AC] H --> I[Step-Up Transformer] I --> J[Grid Transmission Line] H --> K[Control and SCADA System] C --> K D --> K H --> L[Auxiliary Loads
Pumps and Lighting] F --> M[Draft Tube and Tailrace] M --> N[Sea Outflow Channel] N --> A D -.->|Pumping Mode
Off-Peak Hours| F F -.->|Reverse Power| H ``` ## 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram — Tidal Stream Generator ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Tidal Current
Velocity v m/s] --> B[Rotor Blades
3 Blade Rotor] B --> C[Variable Pitch Hub] C --> D[Gearbox
1:40 Ratio] D --> E[Permanent Magnet
Synchronous Generator] E --> F[Power Converter
AC-DC-AC] F --> G[Subsea Cable] G --> H[Onshore Substation] H --> I[Step-Up Transformer] I --> J[Utility Grid] B -.->|Yaw Control| K[Pitch and Yaw Actuator] K -.->|Feedback Signal| C E --> L[Condition Monitoring
Vibration and Temp] L --> M[SCADA Control Center] M --> K ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow of a Tidal Energy Storage Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Tidal Cycle Begins]) --> Q1{Tide Phase?} Q1 -->|Flood| F1[Sea Level Rising] F1 --> F2{Sluice Gates Open?} F2 -->|Yes| F3[Water Fills Basin
No Generation] F2 -->|No - Held Back| F4[Basin Level Maintained Low] F4 --> F5[Stored Potential Energy
Increases] Q1 -->|Ebb| E1[Sea Level Falling] E1 --> E2{Basin Higher than Sea?} E2 -->|Yes| E3[Turbines Open
Generation Mode] E3 --> E4[Bulb Turbines Spin
Generator Produces AC] E4 --> E5[Power Exported to Grid] E2 -->|No - Basin Lower| E6[No Generation
Wait for Next Phase] E5 --> End([Cycle Complete]) E6 --> End F3 --> End F5 --> End ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{Tidal Range Available?} Q1 -->|Greater than 5 m| A1[Macrotidal Site] Q1 -->|2 to 5 m| A2[Mesotidal Site] Q1 -->|Less than 2 m| A3[Micotidal Site
Not Viable] A1 --> B1{Basin Geometry?} B1 -->|Wide Estuary| C1[Tidal Barrage
Recommended] B1 -->|Narrow Channel| C2[Tidal Stream
Recommended] A2 --> B2{Water Velocity?} B2 -->|Greater than 2 m/s| C3[Tidal Stream
Viable] B2 -->|Less than 2 m/s| C4[Tidal Lagoon
Recommended] C1 --> D[Deploy Low-Head
Kaplan Turbines] C2 --> E[Deploy Horizontal
Axis Turbines] C3 --> E C4 --> F[Deploy Reversible
Bulb Turbines] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **"Define tidal energy. List any three advantages and two disadvantages of tidal power plants."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Tidal energy is a form of renewable energy that is harnessed by converting the potential and kinetic energy of ocean tides — caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun on the Earth's water bodies — into useful electrical energy using low-head hydraulic turbines coupled to generators. **Advantages (1½ Marks — any 3):** 1. **Renewable and inexhaustible** — Tides are caused by astronomical forces and will continue for billions of years. 2. **Highly predictable** — Unlike solar and wind, tidal cycles can be predicted centuries in advance with very high accuracy. 3. **No fuel cost** — Once installed, the "fuel" (tidal water) is free. 4. **Long plant life** — Properly maintained tidal plants have a service life of 75–100 years. 5. **Low carbon emissions** — Minimal greenhouse gas emissions during operation. 6. **Storage capability** — Tidal lagoons can act as gravitational energy storage. **Disadvantages (½ Mark — any 2):** 1. **High initial capital cost** — Barrage construction is expensive. 2. **Site-specific** — Requires a high tidal range (>5 m) and suitable geography. 3. **Environmental impact** — Affects marine ecosystems, fish migration, and sedimentation. 4. **Intermittent generation** — Power is produced in pulses aligned with tides, not continuously. 5. **Corrosion and biofouling** — Salt water is highly corrosive to metal components. --- ### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **"Explain the difference between spring tides and neap tides. Why is this distinction important in the design of a tidal power plant?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Spring Tides (1 Mark):** Spring tides occur during the **new moon** and **full moon** phases, when the Sun, the Earth, and the Moon are aligned in a straight line (syzygy). The gravitational forces of the Sun and Moon **reinforce** each other, producing the **maximum tidal range** of the month (up to 1.4 times the mean range). **Neap Tides (1 Mark):** Neap tides occur during the **first quarter** and **third quarter** moon phases, when the Sun and Moon are at right angles (90°) relative to the Earth. Their gravitational forces **partially cancel** out, producing the **minimum tidal range** (about 0.6 times the mean range). **Design Importance (1 Mark):** - The plant's **rated capacity** is determined by the **mean tidal range**, not the spring tide range, to avoid over-sizing. - The **sluice gate dimensions** and **turbine cut-in head** must be designed for **neap tide** conditions to ensure year-round generation. - The **energy storage reservoir (basin)** is sized to smooth out the spring-neap cycle variability. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]** **(a) With a neat schematic, explain the working of a single-basin tidal barrage power plant using the ebb generation mode. Discuss the role of sluice gates and bulb turbines. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (2 Marks):** A single-basin tidal barrage is the most commercially proven tidal energy technology. It consists of a dam-like barrier (the *barrage*) constructed across a natural estuary or bay. The barrage contains: - **Sluice gates** — for filling and emptying the basin with minimum loss. - **Bulb turbines** — reversible low-head Kaplan turbines that generate electricity. - **Ship locks** — to maintain navigation. - **Embankments and coffer dams** — for civil construction. **Working Principle — Ebb Generation Mode (3 Marks):** 1. **Flood Phase (Filling):** During the rising tide, the **sluice gates are opened** and the turbines are kept closed. Sea water flows freely into the basin through the sluice gates until the sea level reaches its **maximum (high tide)**. The basin is now "charged" with potential energy. 2. **Hold Phase (Waiting):** After high tide, the **sluice gates are closed** to hold the water inside the basin. There is a short waiting period (typically 1–2 hours) until the sea level drops enough to create a usable head difference (typically 1–2 m). 3. **Ebb Phase (Generation):** The **turbines are opened** and water flows from the basin to the sea under the head difference. The bulb turbines rotate, driving the generators to produce electricity. Generation continues until the head difference falls below the **turbine cut-in threshold** (typically 0.5–1 m). 4. **Equalization Phase:** After generation stops, the sluice gates are re-opened to equalize levels, ready for the next cycle. **Role of Sluice Gates (1 Mark):** - Allow free flow of water during filling (flood) and emptying (equalization) **without passing through turbines**, minimizing head loss. - Enable **timing control** of when generation begins and ends. - Reduce turbine wear by separating flow regulation from power generation. **Role of Bulb Turbines (1 Mark):** - **Reversible** — can generate on ebb or flood, and pump in reverse. - **Low-head operation** — efficient at heads of 3–10 m. - **Compact design** — generator housed inside a waterproof bulb in the water passage, eliminating the need for a separate powerhouse. --- **(b) A tidal basin has an area of 10 km² and a mean tidal range of 8 m. Calculate (i) the total potential energy stored at high tide, and (ii) the average power output per tidal cycle. Assume sea-water density = 1025 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s². (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Basin area, $A = 10 \text{ km}^2 = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 10^7 \text{ m}^2$ - Tidal range, $H = 8 \text{ m}$ - $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - Tidal period, $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$ **Part (i) — Total Potential Energy (3 Marks):** Apply the derived formula: $$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H^2$$ Substitute values: $$E_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 8^2$$ **Step-by-step calculation:** - $8^2 = 64$ - $\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 = 512.5$ - $512.5 \times 9.81 = 5027.625$ - $5027.625 \times 10^7 = 5.0276 \times 10^{10}$ - $5.0276 \times 10^{10} \times 64 = 3.2177 \times 10^{12}$ J $$\boxed{E_p \approx 3.218 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 3217.7 \text{ GJ} \approx 893.8 \text{ MWh}}$$ **Part (ii) — Average Power (4 Marks):** Apply the average power formula: $$P_{avg} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot H^2}{2T}$$ Substitute values: $$P_{avg} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 64}{2 \times 44{,}712}$$ **Step-by-step calculation:** - Numerator: $1025 \times 9.81 \times 10^7 \times 64$ - $1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25$ - $10{,}055.25 \times 10^7 = 1.0055 \times 10^{11}$ - $1.0055 \times 10^{11} \times 64 = 6.4354 \times 10^{12}$ - Denominator: $2 \times 44{,}712 = 89{,}424$ $$P_{avg} = \frac{6.4354 \times 10^{12}}{8.9424 \times 10^4}$$ $$P_{avg} = 7.197 \times 10^7 \text{ W} = 71.97 \text{ MW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 72 \text{ MW (theoretical)}}$$ **With practical efficiency η_total = 0.30:** $$P_{actual} = 0.30 \times 72 = 21.6 \text{ MW}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Forgetting to convert km² to m² costs 1 mark immediately. (1 km² = 10⁶ m², NOT 10⁴ m²). > 2. **Using freshwater density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** — loses ½ mark and gives ~2.4% error. > 3. **Forgetting to convert hours to seconds** in the period T — results in 3600× wrong answer. > 4. **Omitting the ½ factor** — confusing average power with peak power. > 5. **Not specifying the time period** (per cycle, per day, per year) in the final answer. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style] — INTERNAL CHOICE** **(a) Explain the working principle of a tidal stream energy conversion system. Compare its performance with a tidal barrage system in terms of energy density, environmental impact, and site requirements. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle of Tidal Stream System (3 Marks):** A tidal stream (or tidal current) system extracts kinetic energy from the horizontal flow of water during flood and ebb tides — analogous to an underwater wind turbine. **Components:** - **Rotor** with 2 or 3 blades, similar to a wind turbine but designed for water density (which is ~832 times denser than air). - **Nacelle** housing the gearbox and generator. - **Subsea support structure** (monopile, tripod, or gravity base). - **Subsea cable** for power export to shore. **Operation:** 1. Tidal currents flow past the rotor blades at velocity `v`. 2. The **lift force** on the blades causes rotation. 3. A **gearbox** steps up the slow rotor speed (~10–20 rpm) to generator speed (~1500 rpm). 4. A **permanent magnet synchronous generator (PMSG)** converts mechanical power to electrical power. 5. **Power electronics** (AC-DC-AC converter) stabilize the variable-frequency output to grid frequency. 6. Power is exported via subsea cable to the onshore grid. **Power Equation:** $$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho_{sw} \cdot A_s \cdot v^3 \cdot C_p$$ Where $C_p$ is the power coefficient (max 16/27 = 0.593 by Betz's law). **Comparison Table (4 Marks):** | Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream | |---|---|---| | **Energy form** | Potential energy (head) | Kinetic energy (current) | | **Energy density (W/m²)** | 3 – 10 | 5 – 15 (higher per unit area) | | **Civil works required** | Massive (dam construction) | Minimal (subsea piling) | | **Construction cost** | Very high | Moderate | | **Visual impact** | High (large dam) | Low (mostly underwater) | | **Marine ecology** | Severe (blocks migration) | Moderate (collision risk) | | **Site requirement** | Macrotidal estuary | High-velocity narrow channel | | **Storage capability** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | | **Maturity (TRL)** | 9 (commercial) | 7–8 (pre-commercial) | | **Examples** | La Rance (France), Sihwa (S. Korea) | SeaGen (UK), MeyGen (Scotland) | --- **(b) A tidal stream site has a flow velocity of 2.5 m/s. A tidal stream turbine with rotor diameter 20 m operates at a power coefficient of 0.40. Calculate the electrical power output if the generator and gearbox combined efficiency is 92%. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Flow velocity, $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$ - Rotor diameter, $D = 20 \text{ m}$ - Power coefficient, $C_p = 0.40$ - Combined efficiency, $\eta = 0.92$ - $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Step 1: Calculate the swept area of the rotor (2 Marks):** $$A_s = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi$$ $$A_s = 100 \times 3.1416 = 314.16 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2: Calculate the available hydraulic power (2 Marks):** $$P_{hydraulic} = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot A_s \cdot v^3$$ Calculate $v^3$: $$v^3 = 2.5^3 = 15.625 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3$$ Substitute: $$P_{hydraulic} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 314.16 \times 15.625$$ Step-by-step: - $\frac{1}{2} \times 1025 = 512.5$ - $512.5 \times 314.16 = 161{,}007$ - $161{,}007 \times 15.625 = 2{,}515{,}734$ W $$P_{hydraulic} \approx 2.516 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 3: Apply power coefficient for mechanical power at the rotor shaft (1 Mark):** $$P_{rotor} = C_p \times P_{hydraulic} = 0.40 \times 2.516 = 1.006 \text{ MW}$$ **Step 4: Apply combined gearbox and generator efficiency for electrical power (2 Marks):** $$P_{electrical} = \eta \times P_{rotor} = 0.92 \times 1.006$$ $$\boxed{P_{electrical} = 0.926 \text{ MW} \approx 926 \text{ kW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:** > 1. **Swept area of a circular rotor** uses $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, NOT $A = \pi r^2$ with r in cm. Watch your units. > 2. **Order of application matters:** Power coefficient $C_p$ applies to the **mechanical rotor power** — efficiency $\eta$ applies to the **electrical output** after the gearbox. Don't multiply them in the wrong sequence. > 3. **Don't forget to cube the velocity** — a common mistake is to use $v$ or $v^2$ instead of $v^3$. This error gives answers off by factors of 2.5 or 6.25. > 4. **Always state the final units clearly** (kW or MW) — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for ambiguous units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🔥 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for Tidal Energy** ### **Core Concepts to Memorize** - ✅ Tidal energy = potential + kinetic energy of seawater, driven by **Moon (67%) + Sun (33%)** gravitational forces. - ✅ Tidal period **T = 12 hours 25 minutes** (semi-diurnal). - ✅ Two tidal cycles per day → **705 cycles per year**. - ✅ **Spring tides** = Sun + Moon aligned → maximum range. **Neap tides** = Sun–Moon at 90° → minimum range. - ✅ Tidal barrage operates in **ebb generation, flood generation, two-way, and pumping modes**. - ✅ Pumping mode converts tidal plant into a **gravitational energy storage system**. ### **Master Formulas (No Excuses — Memorize!)** - ✅ Potential energy stored: $E_p = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A H^2$ - ✅ Average power per cycle: $P_{avg} = \frac{\rho g A H^2}{2T}$ - ✅ Tidal stream power: $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_s v^3 C_p$ - ✅ Betz limit: $C_{p,max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ - ✅ Annual energy: $E_{annual} = 705.7 \times \eta \times \frac{1}{2} \rho g A H^2$ ### **Critical Constants** - ✅ $\rho_{seawater} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (not 1000) - ✅ $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - ✅ $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44{,}712 \text{ s}$ - ✅ Macrotidal threshold: $H > 4 \text{ m}$ (some sources use 5 m) - ✅ Tidal stream cut-in velocity: $v \geq 1 \text{ m/s}$; rated: $v \approx 2.5 \text{ m/s}$ ### **Real-World Examples (KTU loves asking these!)** - ✅ **La Rance (France)** — World's first large-scale tidal barrage, 240 MW, 24 bulb turbines, operating since 1966. - ✅ **Sihwa Lake (South Korea)** — World's largest, 254 MW. - ✅ **MeyGen (Scotland)** — World's first commercial tidal stream array, 6 MW. - ✅ **Annapolis Royal (Canada)** — Single-turbine Straflo turbine, 20 MW. ### **Frequent KTU Exam Keywords & Their Meanings** - ✅ **"Sluice gate"** — Gate that controls water flow into/out of basin without passing through turbines. - ✅ **"Bulb turbine"** — Kaplan-type turbine with the generator housed inside a waterproof bulb in the water passage. - ✅ **"Sill level"** — Minimum water level in the basin below which turbines cannot operate. - ✅ **"Tidal prism"** — Total volume of water that flows into/out of the basin during a tidal cycle. - ✅ **"Capacity factor"** — Ratio of actual energy generated to the energy that would be generated at rated power for 24/7. - ✅ **"Two-way generation"** — Generating on both flood and ebb phases; requires reversible turbines. ### **Common Mistakes to Avoid** - ❌ Using freshwater density instead of seawater density. - ❌ Confusing tidal range `H` (m) with instantaneous head `h` (m). - ❌ Forgetting to convert km² to m² (factor = 10⁶) or hours to seconds (factor = 3600). - ❌ Mixing up tidal stream (kinetic) and tidal barrage (potential) formulas. - ❌ Reporting power without specifying the time period (per cycle vs. per day vs. per year). - ❌ Ignoring the Betz limit — claiming a turbine can extract 100% of the kinetic energy. ---

topics.title: Hydroelectric - Solar thermal technologies and Photovoltaics, Storage role in isolated power systems with renewable power sources, Storage role in an integrated power system with grid-connected renewable power sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hydroelectric - Solar thermal technologies and Photovoltaics, Storage role in isolated power systems with renewable power sources, Storage role in an integrated power system with grid-connected renewable power sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydroelectric Energy Systems

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Hydroelectric power is the generation of electrical energy through the conversion of the potential energy of water stored at an elevation into kinetic energy, which then drives a hydraulic turbine coupled to an electrical generator. In the context of energy storage, **Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS)** is the most mature and widely deployed large-scale energy storage technology, where water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper reservoir during periods of excess electrical supply and released back to generate electricity during periods of peak demand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** PHS accounts for over **95%** of the world's installed bulk electrical energy storage capacity, with round-trip efficiencies typically in the range of **70% to 85%**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a giant rechargeable battery made of water. When the electricity grid has too much power (e.g., at night when nobody uses electricity), we use that surplus electricity to pump water uphill into a high mountain lake. Later, when everyone turns on their air conditioners and the grid is short on power, we simply open valves and let the water rush downhill through turbines to generate electricity. The mountain lake acts like the "charged" state of a battery, while the lower reservoir acts like the "discharged" state.

**Standard Physical Metrics:**
- Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Hydroelectric constant: **1 m³/s of water falling 1 m ≈ 9.81 kW theoretical power**

---

## 1.2 Solar Thermal Technologies

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Solar thermal technologies (also called **Concentrated Solar Power / CSP** or **Solar Thermal Electricity / STE**) convert solar radiation into thermal energy using mirrors or lenses to concentrate sunlight onto a small receiver, where the heat is used to drive a heat engine (typically a steam turbine or Stirling engine) connected to an electrical generator.

**The four principal CSP architectures:**

| Technology | Concentration Ratio | Receiver Type | Operating Temperature |
|------------|--------------------:|---------------|----------------------:|
| Parabolic Trough | 30 – 100 | Line-focus, synthetic oil | 400 – 600 °C |
| Solar Power Tower | 300 – 1000 | Point-focus, molten salt | 500 – 1000 °C |
| Dish-Stirling | 1000 – 3000 | Point-focus, gas (H₂/He) | 600 – 800 °C |
| Linear Fresnel Reflector | 10 – 40 | Line-focus, water/steam | 250 – 400 °C |

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of using a magnifying glass on a sunny day to burn a hole in a leaf — except instead of burning a leaf, we focus enormous amounts of sunlight to boil water. The resulting steam spins a giant pinwheel (turbine) which spins a generator. The "smart" part is that we can store the heat in molten salt tanks and use it to make electricity even when the sun is not shining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition:** **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** in CSP typically uses a **two-tank molten salt system** with solar salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃), allowing 6–15 hours of full-load storage.

---

## 1.3 Photovoltaic (PV) Systems

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Photovoltaics is the direct conversion of photon energy from sunlight into electricity using the **photovoltaic effect** in semiconductor materials (most commonly crystalline silicon). When photons of energy greater than the semiconductor band-gap strike the PV cell, electron-hole pairs are generated, separated by the built-in electric field of a p-n junction, and collected at external electrodes as direct current (DC).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine solar cells as tiny one-way turnstiles for electrons. Sunlight acts like a push — when a photon hits a silicon atom, it knocks an electron loose, but the cell's internal "valve" only lets the electron flow in one direction (out through the wire). Millions of these microscopic turnstiles wired together produce a useful electric current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** 1000 W/m² irradiance, **25 °C** cell temperature, AM 1.5 spectrum. Every PV module datasheet is rated under STC.

---

## 1.4 Storage Role — Isolated (Off-Grid) Power Systems

**Formal Definition:**
An **isolated power system** (also called off-grid, autonomous, or islanded system) is an electrical network that is **not connected to the main utility grid**, relying entirely on local generation sources — typically a mix of renewable generators (PV, wind, micro-hydro) and diesel gensets, supported by Energy Storage Systems (ESS) to balance supply and demand.

**Core Functions of Storage in Isolated Systems:**
1. Time-shift renewable generation from daytime/peak-wind hours to evening load
2. Provide **spinning reserve** and frequency stability
3. Allow diesel generator **minimum-run-time reduction** (fuel saving)
4. Smooth the **duck-curve ramp rate** of PV during sunrise/sunset

---

## 1.5 Storage Role — Grid-Connected Integrated Power Systems

**Formal Definition:**
A **grid-connected integrated power system** is a large interconnected network where renewable generators, conventional generators, and Energy Storage Systems are all tied to the main utility grid. Storage in such systems provides grid-services such as **load levelling, peak shaving, frequency regulation, voltage support, spinning reserve, and renewable firming**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
In an isolated system, the storage is the *only* shock-absorber; in a grid-connected system, the storage is one of many tools the grid operator uses to keep the whole orchestra in tune.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The economic value of storage increases dramatically when it can stack multiple services — e.g., providing frequency regulation and capacity simultaneously. This is called **value-stacking**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydroelectric — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Available Power and Energy

The theoretical hydraulic power available from a flow of water through a height drop:

$$P_{hydro} = \eta_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{generator} \cdot \eta_{transformer} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{net}$$

where:
- $P_{hydro}$ = electrical power output (W)
- $\eta_{turbine}$ = turbine efficiency (typically 0.85 – 0.95)
- $\eta_{generator}$ = generator efficiency (typically 0.95 – 0.98)
- $\rho$ = water density = **1000 kg/m³**
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = volume flow rate (m³/s)
- $H_{net}$ = net head (gross head minus hydraulic losses), in metres

The energy stored in an upper reservoir of volume $V$ at mean head $H$:

$$E_{stored} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V \cdot H \cdot \eta_{cycle}$$

where $\eta_{cycle}$ is the round-trip efficiency of PHS.

### 2.1.2 Pumped Hydro Storage — Modes of Operation

| Mode | Action | Grid Condition |
|------|--------|----------------|
| **Pump (Charging)** | Motor drives pump → water moves to upper reservoir | Surplus generation (off-peak) |
| **Generate (Discharging)** | Water released → turbine + generator | Peak demand |
| **Synchronous Condenser** | Pumped unit runs un-watered, supplies reactive power | Voltage support |
| **Standby / Spinning Reserve** | Synchronized but at zero output | Frequency/emergency reserve |

**Why PHS matters:** Discharge duration of **4 – 20 hours**, cycle life **> 15,000 cycles**, energy density (per unit mass of water) is low, but energy density *per unit volume* is acceptable because of high head.

---

## 2.2 Solar Thermal (CSP) — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.2.1 Concentration Ratio

$$C = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}}$$

where $A_{aperture}$ is the area of mirrors/lenses collecting sunlight, $A_{receiver}$ is the area that absorbs it.

### 2.2.2 Receiver Heat Gain

$$\dot{Q}_{receiver} = \eta_{optical} \cdot C \cdot I_{DNI} \cdot A_{aperture} - \dot{Q}_{losses}$$

where:
- $\eta_{optical}$ = mirror reflectivity × tracking accuracy × cleanliness (~ 0.7)
- $I_{DNI}$ = Direct Normal Irradiance (W/m²)
- $\dot{Q}_{losses}$ = convection + radiation losses (highly temperature-dependent)

### 2.2.3 Thermal Energy Stored in Molten Salt

$$Q_{TES} = m_{salt} \cdot c_{p,salt} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

where:
- $m_{salt}$ = mass of molten salt (kg)
- $c_{p,salt}$ = specific heat ≈ **1.5 kJ/(kg·K)** for solar salt
- $T_{hot}$ ≈ 565 °C, $T_{cold}$ ≈ 290 °C

**Hourly discharge energy** from a TES tank:

$$E_{TES} = \dot{m}_{salt} \cdot c_{p,salt} \cdot \Delta T \cdot \Delta t$$

### 2.2.4 Power-Block Efficiency (Carnot-derived limit)

$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$$

Practical Rankine cycle efficiency: **30 – 40%** for solar thermal plants.

---

## 2.3 Photovoltaic — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.3.1 Photovoltaic Effect & Band-gap

A photon of energy $E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ generates an electron-hole pair **only if** $E_{photon} \ge E_g$ (band-gap energy).

| Material | Band-gap $E_g$ (eV) |
|----------|---------------------:|
| Crystalline Si | 1.12 |
| Amorphous Si | 1.7 |
| CdTe | 1.45 |
| CIGS | 1.0 – 1.1 |
| Perovskite (CH₃NH₃PbI₃) | 1.55 |

### 2.3.2 I–V Characteristic of a PV Cell

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:
- $I_{ph}$ = photo-generated current (proportional to irradiance)
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current
- $q$ = electron charge = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 – 2)
- $R_s$, $R_{sh}$ = series and shunt resistances

### 2.3.3 Maximum Power Point (MPP)

PV output is non-linear; the operating point that maximizes $P = V \cdot I$ is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. The voltage at MPP is roughly 70–80% of open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$.

$$P_{mpp} = V_{mpp} \cdot I_{mpp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$$

where **Fill Factor** (FF) is typically 0.6 – 0.85 for good cells.

### 2.3.4 Temperature & Irradiance Effects

$$P_{out} = P_{STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}} \cdot [1 + \gamma \cdot (T_{cell} - 25)]$$

where:
- $G$ = plane-of-array irradiance (W/m²), $G_{STC}$ = 1000 W/m²
- $\gamma$ = power temperature coefficient (≈ –0.4 %/°C for c-Si)
- $T_{cell}$ = cell temperature (°C)

---

## 2.4 Storage in Isolated Power Systems

### 2.4.1 Energy Balance Equation

For an autonomous microgrid:

$$\sum_{i} P_{gen,i}(t) + P_{ESS}^{dis}(t) - P_{ESS}^{ch}(t) = P_{load}(t) + P_{loss}(t)$$

The ESS State of Charge evolves as:

$$SOC(t+\Delta t) = SOC(t) + \frac{\eta_{ch} P_{ch}\Delta t - \frac{1}{\eta_{dis}} P_{dis}\Delta t}{E_{rated}}$$

### 2.4.2 Diesel Generator Sizing Logic

$$E_{ESS} \ge E_{load,daily} \cdot (1 - R_{RE}) \cdot \frac{T_{autonomy}}{24}$$

where $R_{RE}$ is the renewable penetration fraction and $T_{autonomy}$ is the desired days of autonomy (commonly 1 – 3 days).

### 2.4.3 Frequency Response from BESS

Inertia constant equivalent:

$$H_{virtual} = \frac{E_{rated}}{2 \cdot S_{base}}$$

BESS responds within **milliseconds**, vastly faster than diesel gensets (1 – 10 s).

---

## 2.5 Storage in Grid-Connected Integrated Systems

### 2.5.1 Grid-Services Taxonomy

| Service | Response Time | Storage Technology |
|---------|--------------:|--------------------|
| Frequency Regulation (primary) | < 1 s | BESS, flywheel |
| Load Following | seconds – minutes | BESS, PHS |
| Peak Shaving | 1 – 4 h | BESS, PHS |
| Energy Arbitrage | 4 – 12 h | PHS, CAES |
| Voltage / Reactive Support | < 0.1 s | STATCOM+BESS, PHS (sync. condenser mode) |
| Black Start | minutes | BESS, diesel, PHS |

### 2.5.2 Renewable Firming

Renewable firming converts variable renewable output into a near-constant "firm" power commitment:

$$P_{firm}(t) = P_{RE,avg}(t) - P_{ESS}^{ch/dis}(t)$$

The required ESS rating to firm a renewable plant:

$$E_{ESS} \ge \int_{0}^{T} \max(0, P_{RE,avg} - P_{RE}(t)) \, dt$$

### 2.5.3 Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS)

$$LCOS = \frac{CAPEX + \sum_{t} \frac{OPEX_t}{(1+r)^t}}{\sum_{t} \frac{E_{delivered,t}}{(1+r)^t}}$$

where $r$ is the discount rate and $E_{delivered,t}$ is the energy actually discharged.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{hydro} = \eta_t \eta_g \rho g Q H_{net}$ | Hydro power | W |
| 2 | $E_{PHS} = \rho g V H \eta_{cycle}$ | Stored energy in upper reservoir | J |
| 3 | $C = A_{aperture}/A_{receiver}$ | Concentration ratio | – |
| 4 | $Q_{TES} = m_{salt} c_p \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J |
| 5 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_{cold}/T_{hot}$ | Ideal thermal efficiency | – |
| 6 | $E_{photon} = hc/\lambda \ge E_g$ | Photovoltaic threshold | J |
| 7 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT)-1]$ | PV diode equation | A |
| 8 | $P_{mpp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ | Max PV power | W |
| 9 | $P_{out} = P_{STC}(G/G_{STC})[1+\gamma(T-25)]$ | Real PV output | W |
| 10 | $LCOS = \frac{\Sigma C_t (1+r)^{-t}}{\Sigma E_t (1+r)^{-t}}$ | Storage cost | ₹/MWh |
| 11 | $SOC_{t+1} = SOC_t + \frac{\eta_{ch} P_{ch}\Delta t - P_{dis}\Delta t/\eta_{dis}}{E_{rated}}$ | ESS dynamics | % |
| 12 | $\eta_{cycle} = \eta_{ch} \cdot \eta_{dis} \cdot \eta_{aux}$ | Round-trip efficiency | – |

> For KTU problems, **always state units explicitly** and convert kWh ↔ MJ using **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydroelectric Power from First Principles

**Starting point:** A mass of water $m$ at elevation $h$ possesses gravitational potential energy:

$$E_p = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

If the mass flows at rate $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ (kg/s), the available mechanical power is:

$$P_{mech} = \frac{dE_p}{dt} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot h = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot h$$

Applying turbine efficiency $\eta_t$, generator efficiency $\eta_g$, and transformer efficiency $\eta_{tr}$:

$$P_{elec} = \eta_t \cdot \eta_g \cdot \eta_{tr} \cdot \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot h_{net}$$

where $h_{net} = h_{gross} - h_{friction} - h_{weir}$.

**Worked Example (KTU-Style):**

> A run-of-river hydro plant has a net head of 25 m, design flow of 12 m³/s, turbine efficiency 88%, generator efficiency 95%, and transformer efficiency 99%. Calculate the rated electrical output.

**Step 1 — Substitute into formula:**
$$P_{elec} = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 \times 1000 \times 12 \times 9.81 \times 25$$

**Step 2 — Group constants:**
$$\eta_{overall} = 0.88 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.8276$$

**Step 3 — Multiply hydraulic term:**
$$\rho Q g h_{net} = 1000 \times 12 \times 9.81 \times 25 = 2{,}943{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication:**
$$P_{elec} = 0.8276 \times 2{,}943{,}000 = 2{,}435{,}507 \text{ W} \approx 2.44 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy (assuming 70% capacity factor):**
$$E_{annual} = 2.44 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.70 = 14.95 \text{ GWh}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Stored in a PHS Upper Reservoir

Consider an upper reservoir of surface area $A$ and water depth $H$, with the turbine centre-line at depth $h_{min}$ below water surface.

The water mass stored is:
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot H$$

The available head varies from $H_{max}$ (when full) to $H_{min}$ (when emptied to minimum operating level). The average head for energy extraction:
$$\bar{H} = \frac{H_{max} + H_{min}}{2}$$

Energy that can be extracted in one full discharge cycle:
$$E_{discharge} = m \cdot g \cdot \bar{H} \cdot \eta_{gen} \cdot \eta_{turb}$$

Energy required to pump water back up:
$$E_{charge} = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot \bar{H}}{\eta_{pump} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$

**Round-trip efficiency:**
$$\eta_{cycle} = \frac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} = \eta_{gen} \eta_{turb} \eta_{pump} \eta_{motor}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-Style):**

> A PHS plant has an upper reservoir of effective volume 5 × 10⁶ m³ and mean head 300 m. Round-trip efficiency is 78%. Calculate the stored energy in MWh.

**Step 1 — Stored hydraulic energy:**
$$E_{hydraulic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot V \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 5\times10^6 \times 300$$

$$E_{hydraulic} = 1.4715 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MWh:**
$$E_{hydraulic} = \frac{1.4715 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 4087.5 \text{ MWh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply round-trip efficiency:**
$$E_{deliverable} = 0.78 \times 4087.5 = 3188.25 \text{ MWh} \approx 3.19 \text{ GWh}$$

**Step 4 — Continuous discharge rating for 6 h:**
$$P_{max,6h} = \frac{3188.25 \text{ MWh}}{6 \text{ h}} = 531.4 \text{ MW}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CSP Molten-Salt TES Capacity

**Sensible heat storage equation:**
$$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

**Derivation from first law:** For a control mass with no phase change and constant $c_p$:
$$Q = \int_{T_{cold}}^{T_{hot}} m c_p \, dT = m c_p (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$$

**Worked Example (KTU-Style):**

> A 50 MWe parabolic-trough CSP plant uses 28,000 tonnes of solar salt to provide 7.5 hours of full-load storage. Hot tank 565 °C, cold tank 290 °C. $c_p$ = 1.5 kJ/(kg·K). Verify the storage capacity and the round-trip thermal efficiency.

**Step 1 — Mass of salt:**
$$m = 28{,}000 \text{ tonnes} = 2.8 \times 10^7 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature difference:**
$$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Heat stored:**
$$Q_{stored} = 2.8 \times 10^7 \times 1500 \times 275 = 1.155 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

$$Q_{stored} = \frac{1.155 \times 10^{13}}{3.6 \times 10^9} = 3208.3 \text{ MWh}_{th}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to electrical MWh (using plant thermal-to-electrical efficiency ≈ 38%):**
$$E_{el} = 0.38 \times 3208.3 = 1219.2 \text{ MWh}_{e}$$

**Step 5 — Verify duration at 50 MW:**
$$\text{Duration} = \frac{1219.2}{50} = 24.4 \text{ h}$$

> The calculation gives more than 7.5 h, indicating only part of the salt is cycled daily (typical design — full capacity used for partial-load extension).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: PV Cell I–V Equation

The diode current equation from semiconductor physics:

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{qV_D}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

The photo-generated current is approximately proportional to irradiance:
$$I_{ph} = K \cdot G$$

Kirchhoff's current law at the output terminal:
$$I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}$$

where $I_{sh} = (V + IR_s)/R_{sh}$. Substituting yields the **single-diode model**:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-Style):**

> A PV module has 36 cells in series. At STC, $V_{oc}$ = 21.6 V, $I_{sc}$ = 3.6 A, FF = 0.72. Calculate $P_{mpp}$.

$$P_{mpp} = FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} = 0.72 \times 21.6 \times 3.6 = 55.99 \text{ W} \approx 56 \text{ W}$$

**Temperature-corrected output at $T_{cell} = 55$ °C (γ = –0.4 %/°C, G = 800 W/m²):**

$$P_{out} = 56 \times \frac{800}{1000} \times [1 + (-0.004)(55-25)]$$

$$P_{out} = 56 \times 0.8 \times [1 - 0.12] = 56 \times 0.8 \times 0.88 = 39.42 \text{ W}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Storage Role Comparison

| Parameter | Isolated System | Grid-Connected System |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Primary storage purpose | Energy sufficiency | Grid services & economics |
| ESS sizing driver | Days of autonomy | Power capacity / response time |
| Typical ESS duration | 4 – 24 hours | 0.25 – 4 hours |
| Economic value | Avoided diesel fuel | Energy arbitrage + ancillary |
| Cycling frequency | 1 – 2 cycles/day | 1 – 5 cycles/day |
| Inertia contribution | Must be provided virtually | Shared with synchronous machines |
| Dominant storage tech | Li-ion BESS, lead-acid | Li-ion BESS, PHS, flywheel |
| Renewable penetration | 50 – 100% (with storage) | 20 – 80% (grid-limited) |
| Voltage/frequency support | Critical (weak grid) | Supplementary (strong grid) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Storage penetration transforms isolated renewable systems from *intermittent* to *dispatchable*. In grid-connected systems, storage penetration transforms renewables from *variable* to *firm*.
