# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Differentiate between **run-of-river** and **pumped hydro storage** systems. Which one qualifies as a true energy storage system and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Run-of-River | Pumped Hydro Storage |
|---------|--------------|----------------------|
| Reservoir | Small / none | Two large reservoirs (upper + lower) |
| Power dispatch | Inflow-dependent, non-dispatchable | Fully dispatchable |
| Energy storage function | No (generator, not storage) | Yes — stores energy as gravitational PE |
| Cycle | Continuous generation | Pump → store → generate (cyclic) |
| Capacity factor | 40 – 60% | 5 – 25% (peaking plant) |

**Conclusion:** Only PHS qualifies as a *true energy storage system* because it stores energy in the form of water at height and releases it on demand, decoupling generation time from energy consumption time. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q:** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **parabolic trough** concentrated solar power plant. State the role of thermal energy storage.

**Model Answer:**
A parabolic trough CSP plant consists of long, parabolic-shaped mirrors that track the sun along a single axis and focus sunlight onto a receiver tube placed along the focal line. A heat-transfer fluid (HTF) — typically synthetic oil — circulates through the receiver tube, gets heated to ~ 400 °C, and exchanges heat in a steam generator to produce superheated steam that drives a Rankine-cycle turbine connected to a generator.

**Role of TES:** A two-tank molten-salt TES stores excess thermal energy during periods of high solar input. During cloudy periods or after sunset, the hot molten salt is discharged to the steam generator, allowing continuous electricity generation. Typical capacity: 6 – 15 hours of full-load storage. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a block diagram, describe the components of a **grid-connected photovoltaic system** with battery energy storage. Explain the role of MPPT. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 100 kWp PV system operates at 35 °C cell temperature under 850 W/m² irradiance. The module has efficiency 16% at STC, temperature coefficient –0.4 %/°C. Calculate the actual DC output power. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A — (a)

**Block Diagram (text-rendered for answer sheet):**

```
  PV Array → DC Combiner → MPPT Charge Controller → DC Bus
                                              ↓
                  Battery Bank (with BMS) ← Bi-directional Inverter
                                              ↓
                                  AC Bus (415 V)
                                              ↓
              Step-up Transformer → Grid (11 kV)
              ← Smart Meter (Net Metering)
              ← SCADA / EMS
```

**Components and their roles:**

1. **PV Array** — series-parallel combination of PV modules; converts solar radiation to DC electricity.
2. **MPPT Charge Controller** — continuously adjusts operating point of PV array to extract maximum available power (P_max = V_mpp × I_mpp).
3. **DC Bus** — common DC link at, e.g., 600 – 1000 V.
4. **Battery Bank with BMS** — stores surplus energy; BMS protects against over-charge, over-discharge, thermal runaway.
5. **Bi-directional Inverter** — converts DC to AC for grid injection and rectifies AC to DC for charging.
6. **Step-up Transformer** — boosts LV to MV for grid connection.
7. **Net Meter & SCADA** — measurement, monitoring, remote control.

**Role of MPPT:** The PV I–V curve changes with irradiance and temperature. The MPP voltage shifts, and a fixed-voltage operating point gives 20 – 30% lower energy yield. MPPT algorithms (Perturb & Observe, Incremental Conductance) adjust the converter duty cycle every few milliseconds to track the true MPP. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A — (b)

**Given:**
- $P_{STC} = 100$ kWp at $G_{STC} = 1000$ W/m², $T_{STC} = 25$ °C
- $T_{cell} = 35$ °C, $G = 850$ W/m²
- $\gamma = -0.4$ %/°C = –0.004 /°C

**Step 1 — Irradiance factor:**
$$\frac{G}{G_{STC}} = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85$$

**Step 2 — Temperature correction factor:**
$$[1 + \gamma(T_{cell} - 25)] = [1 + (-0.004)(35 - 25)]$$
$$= [1 + (-0.004)(10)] = [1 - 0.04] = 0.96$$

**Step 3 — Actual DC output:**
$$P_{out} = P_{STC} \times 0.85 \times 0.96$$
$$P_{out} = 100 \times 0.816 = 81.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Verification using efficiency:** $\eta_{STC} = 16\%$ requires area $A = P_{STC}/(G_{STC} \times \eta) = 100{,}000/(1000 \times 0.16) = 625 \text{ m}^2$.
Then $P_{out} = A \cdot G \cdot \eta_{STC} \cdot [1 + \gamma(T-25)] = 625 \times 850 \times 0.16 \times 0.96 = 81.6$ kW ✓ (7 marks)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the role of energy storage in **isolated (off-grid) renewable power systems**. Discuss BESS sizing criteria for a PV-diesel hybrid microgrid. (7 marks)

**(b)** A remote island community has daily load 800 kWh. A PV-diesel hybrid system with 60% renewable penetration is proposed. The BESS round-trip efficiency is 90%, depth-of-discharge limit is 80%, and desired autonomy is 1.5 days. Calculate the BESS capacity (in kWh) and suggest the BESS power rating if 4-hour discharge is desired. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B — (a)

**Roles of Energy Storage in Isolated Systems:**

1. **Energy Time-Shift:** Solar PV generates only during sunlight hours (typically 6 peak hours). Load continues 24 hours. Storage shifts midday surplus to evening/night.
2. **Frequency & Voltage Stability:** Isolated microgrids have low inertia; BESS provides virtual inertia and primary frequency response.
3. **Renewable Penetration Maximisation:** BESS enables diesel-off operation during high-sun hours, reducing diesel runtime and fuel cost.
4. **Spinning Reserve:** BESS substitutes for spinning reserve of diesel generators, allowing them to operate at optimal loading.
5. **Ramp-Rate Limiting:** BESS smooths the rapid PV ramps at sunrise/sunset that would otherwise destabilise the microgrid.
6. **Black-Start Capability:** BESS can re-energise the microgrid after a total shutdown without external power.

**BESS Sizing Criteria for PV-Diesel Microgrid:**

- **Energy-based sizing:** determined by daily load, renewable penetration fraction, days of autonomy, DoD, and round-trip efficiency.
- **Power-based sizing:** determined by peak load, inrush currents, maximum PV ramp rate, and diesel generator starting transients.
- **Hybrid rule-of-thumb:** BESS capacity (kWh) ≈ 1.0 – 1.5 × daily load × (1 – renewable fraction), with autonomy of 1 – 3 days. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B — (b)

**Given:**
- Daily load $E_{load}$ = 800 kWh
- Renewable penetration $R_{RE}$ = 60% → diesel share = 40% → energy deficit to be covered by BESS + diesel = $0.40 \times 800 = 320$ kWh/day
- BESS round-trip $\eta_{cycle} = 0.90$
- DoD = 80%
- Autonomy $T_{aut} = 1.5$ days

**Step 1 — Energy to be supplied by BESS (assuming 80% of diesel share served by BESS):**
$$E_{BESS,daily} = 0.80 \times 320 = 256 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Account for round-trip losses:**
$$E_{required} = \frac{E_{BESS,daily}}{\eta_{cycle}} = \frac{256}{0.90} = 284.4 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Apply autonomy factor:**
$$E_{BESS,total} = 284.4 \times 1.5 = 426.7 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Apply DoD (only 80% of nameplate is usable):**
$$E_{BESS,nameplate} = \frac{426.7}{0.80} = 533.3 \text{ kWh} \approx 533 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Power rating for 4-hour discharge:**
$$P_{BESS} = \frac{E_{BESS,nameplate} \times DoD}{4} = \frac{533.3 \times 0.80}{4} = 106.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Answer:** BESS capacity ≈ **533 kWh**, power rating ≈ **107 kW**. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls that cost marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — every numerical answer MUST end with units (kW, MWh, °C). Marks are deducted even if the numerical value is correct.
> 2. **Confusing power rating (kW) with energy capacity (kWh)** — these are two distinct specifications of any ESS; examiners often pose follow-up sub-questions distinguishing them.
> 3. **Using $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ but forgetting $g = 9.81$ m/s²** — or vice-versa — when computing hydro power. This is a 1-mark deduction for incomplete substitution.
> 4. **In PV problems, students frequently apply the temperature correction only and ignore the irradiance correction**, losing 2 marks.
> 5. **DoD vs SoC confusion:** A battery at 80% SoC means 20% DoD. The usable energy is always $E_{nameplate} \times DoD_{max}$.
> 6. **Drawing PV array without specifying cell configuration (series-parallel)** loses the 1-mark component of a 7-mark question.
> 7. **For BESS in isolated systems**, examiners expect the formula showing renewable penetration AND DoD AND efficiency — missing any one of these leads to partial-marks deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

**Hydroelectric:**
- ☑ PHS is the **largest installed storage technology worldwide** (~ 95% of global ESS capacity by power).
- ☑ Always use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² in power and energy formulas.
- ☑ Round-trip efficiency of PHS = **70 – 85%** depending on equipment and cycle.
- ☑ Net head = gross head − friction losses − weir losses.
- ☑ Energy stored ∝ Volume × Head × ρ × g.

**Solar Thermal (CSP):**
- ☑ Four CSP architectures: Parabolic Trough, Solar Tower, Dish-Stirling, Linear Fresnel.
- ☑ Concentration ratio = $A_{aperture}/A_{receiver}$.
- ☑ Sensible heat storage: $Q = m c_p \Delta T$.
- ☑ Solar salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃), $c_p \approx 1.5$ kJ/(kg·K), operating range **290 – 565 °C**.
- ☑ TES gives **dispatchability** to solar — the key advantage over PV.

**Photovoltaics:**
- ☑ PV converts photon energy directly to DC via the **photovoltaic effect** in p–n junctions.
- ☑ Standard Test Conditions (STC): 1000 W/m², 25 °C, AM 1.5.
- ☑ $P_{out} = P_{STC} \cdot (G/G_{STC}) \cdot [1 + \gamma(T_{cell} - 25)]$.
- ☑ MPPT extracts maximum power; common algorithms: P&O, Incremental Conductance.
- ☑ Crystalline Si band-gap = **1.12 eV**; cell temperature coefficient ≈ –0.4 %/°C.

**Storage in Isolated Systems:**
- ☑ Energy balance: Generation + Discharge = Load + Charge + Losses.
- ☑ Sizing: $E_{BESS} = E_{load} \cdot (1-R_{RE}) \cdot T_{aut}/(DoD \cdot \eta_{cycle})$.
- ☑ Typical ESS duration: 4 – 24 hours.
- ☑ BESS provides **virtual inertia** and **primary frequency response**.
- ☑ Reduces diesel runtime → significant fuel savings.

**Storage in Grid-Connected Systems:**
- ☑ Six major grid services: Frequency Regulation, Load Levelling, Peak Shaving, Voltage Support, Renewable Firming, Black Start.
- ☑ Response times range from **sub-cycle (ms)** for BESS to **minutes** for PHS.
- ☑ LCOS is the metric for cost comparison across technologies.
- ☑ Value-stacking (providing multiple services) dramatically improves economics.
- ☑ In India, the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) has mandated BESS for new renewable plants > 500 MW.

**Universal Constants to Memorise:**
- ☑ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ☑ $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³
- ☑ 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
- ☑ $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- ☑ $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- ☑ $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric vehicles - Grid to Vehicle (G2V) - Vehicle to Grid (V2G)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Electric vehicles - Grid to Vehicle (G2V) - Vehicle to Grid (V2G)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to EV-Grid Interaction

Modern transportation is undergoing a paradigm shift from Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles to **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**. As EV penetration increases, the relationship between the vehicle and the electrical grid evolves from a simple load to an active, intelligent participant. This bidirectional interaction is the foundation of **G2V** and **V2G** technologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Electric vehicles* are automobiles powered fully or partially by electric energy stored in electrochemical batteries. **G2V (Grid-to-Vehicle)** refers to the unidirectional flow of electrical energy from the utility grid into the EV battery for charging. **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** refers to the bidirectional flow of energy where an EV can discharge stored energy back to the grid to provide ancillary services.

## 1.2 Grid-to-Vehicle (G2V) — Formal Definition

**G2V** is the operational mode in which an electric vehicle acts purely as a **controllable load** on the power grid. The grid supplies AC power, which is converted to DC by an onboard or off-board charger to replenish the EV's battery pack.

- **Direction of Power Flow:** Grid $\rightarrow$ EV Battery (Unidirectional)
- **Primary Function:** Battery charging
- **Smart Feature:** Charging rate and timing can be modulated by utility signals
- **Key Metric:** Charging Power $P_{ch}$ (typically **3.3 kW to 350 kW**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — G2V**
> Think of G2V like refueling a water tank from a municipal pipeline. The pipeline (grid) pushes water (energy) into your tank (battery) at a rate controlled by the valve (charger). You can open the valve more during off-peak hours (night) when the pipeline pressure is high and tariffs are cheap, but you cannot reverse the flow to push water back into the pipeline. G2V is the same — energy flows only one way: into the vehicle.

## 1.3 Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) — Formal Definition

**V2G** is the advanced operational mode in which an EV, while plugged in, becomes a **Distributed Energy Resource (DER)** capable of discharging energy back into the grid. This requires **bidirectional power electronics** and **two-way communication infrastructure**.

- **Direction of Power Flow:** Grid $\leftrightarrow$ EV Battery (Bidirectional)
- **Primary Functions:** Peak shaving, frequency regulation, voltage support, renewable integration
- **Key Metric:** Discharge Power $P_{dis}$ and Round-trip Efficiency $\eta_{rt}$
- **Aggregator Role:** A virtual power plant operator that manages fleets of EVs

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — V2G**
> Imagine your car is a portable power bank on wheels. In the morning, you charge it cheaply from the grid (G2V). During the afternoon when the grid is under stress and electricity prices spike, your car sells back a portion of its stored energy to the grid (V2G) — like selling lemonade from your reserve water tank back to thirsty neighbors. By evening, you recharge again at off-peak rates. Your car becomes both transportation AND a power plant.

## 1.4 Standard Charging Power Levels (KTU Reference)

| Charging Level | Power Rating | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (Slow AC)** | ~**1.4 kW – 2.4 kW** | Home outlet (120V) |
| **Level 2 (Fast AC)** | ~**3.3 kW – 22 kW** | Home wall-box / Public AC stations |
| **Level 3 (DC Fast)** | ~**50 kW – 350 kW** | Highway fast charging |
| **V2G Capable** | ~**10 kW – 50 kW** | Bidirectional DC chargers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The G2V Operational Chain

The unidirectional energy transfer from grid to EV follows a precise electromechanical chain:

1. **Grid Side (AC Source):** The utility grid supplies 3-phase AC at standardized voltage (**230V single-phase / 415V three-phase in India, 50 Hz**).
2. **AC-DC Rectifier Stage:** Converts grid-frequency AC into a regulated DC bus voltage.
3. **Power Factor Correction (PFC):** Active PFC ensures input current waveform tracks voltage waveform to maintain **power factor $\cos\phi \geq 0.95$**.
4. **DC-DC Converter Stage:** Steps the rectified DC down (buck converter) to the battery's required voltage level.
5. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Monitors cell voltages, temperatures, and State of Charge (SoC) to prevent overcharging.
6. **Battery Pack:** Stores the energy electrochemically (typically **Li-ion** with nominal voltage of **3.6–3.7 V per cell**).

## 2.2 The V2G Operational Chain (Bidirectional)

V2G requires fundamentally different power electronics — a **bidirectional converter** topology:

1. **DC-AC Inverter Stage:** During discharge, the DC battery voltage is inverted into grid-synchronized AC (50 Hz, 230V).
2. **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) Synchronization:** The inverter output is frequency- and phase-locked with the grid voltage waveform.
3. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter:** A **dual-active bridge (DAB)** or **CLLC resonant converter** allows power flow in both directions.
4. **Bidirectional Energy Metering:** A smart meter records both imported (G2V) and exported (V2G) energy separately.
5. **Communication Layer (ISO 15118):** Standardized protocol enabling the EV and grid to negotiate power schedules.

## 2.3 Key Grid Services Provided by V2G

V2G is not just about returning energy — it provides **four major categories of ancillary services**:

- **Peak Shaving:** EVs discharge during peak demand (e.g., 6 PM–10 PM) to flatten the load curve.
- **Frequency Regulation:** EVs respond in **seconds** to grid frequency deviations (50 Hz ± 0.5 Hz in India) by injecting or absorbing active power.
- **Voltage Support:** EVs provide or absorb reactive power (VAr) to maintain grid voltage within statutory limits (**230V ± 10%** in India).
- **Renewable Integration:** EVs absorb excess solar/wind generation during midday peaks (the **"Duck Curve" problem**).

## 2.4 The V2G Aggregator Concept

A single EV battery is too small (typically **30–100 kWh**) to participate in wholesale electricity markets. The **Aggregator** solves this by:

- Pooling thousands of EVs into a **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)**.
- Bidding aggregated capacity (e.g., **10 MW** from 200 cars at 50 kW each) into the market.
- Managing individual SoC constraints to ensure vehicle owners have sufficient charge for their next trip.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of G2V/V2G**
> G2V/V2G systems are the cornerstone of **smart grid architecture**. They enable demand-side flexibility, support renewable energy targets (India's **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030**), and provide a second-life use case for EV batteries. Mastery of these concepts is essential for KTU examinations and careers in power electronics, renewable integration, and smart grid engineering.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the most frequently tested in KTU ESE for this module. Memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Battery stored energy | Wh |
| 2 | $E_{bat} = \int_{0}^{t} P(t)\, dt$ | Energy delivered over time | Wh |
| 3 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{nom}} \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau$ | State of Charge dynamics | % |
| 4 | $t_{ch} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \times \Delta SoC}{P_{ch} \times \eta_{ch}}$ | Charging time | hours |
| 5 | $P_{grid} = P_{ch} \times \eta_{ch}$ | Power drawn from grid (G2V) | kW |
| 6 | $P_{dis}^{grid} = P_{bat} \times \eta_{dis}$ | Power delivered to grid (V2G) | kW |
| 7 | $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$ | Round-trip efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E_{loss} = E_{bat}\,(1 - \eta_{rt})$ | Energy lost in a full cycle | kWh |
| 9 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{nom}}$ | C-rate of charging/discharging | /h |
| 10 | $n_{cells} = \dfrac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell}}$ | Series cells in pack | count |
| 11 | $f_{grid} = 50\,\text{Hz} \pm \Delta f$ | Grid frequency regulation band | Hz |
| 12 | $V_{grid}^{rms} = 230\,\text{V} \pm 10\%$ | Statutory voltage window | V |

> **Key Variable Definitions:**
> - $V_{nom}$: Nominal battery voltage (V)
> - $C_{Ah}$: Battery capacity in Ampere-hours
> - $P_{ch}, P_{dis}$: Charging / Discharging power
> - $\eta_{ch}, \eta_{dis}$: Charger / Discharger efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**)
> - $\eta_{rt}$: Round-trip efficiency (typically **0.75 – 0.85**)
> - $C_{rate}$: C-rate (1C means full charge/discharge in 1 hour)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charging Time for an EV Battery (G2V)

**Problem Statement:** A KTU examiner asks you to derive the charging time for an EV given battery capacity, charging power, and charger efficiency.

**Given:**
- Battery nominal capacity: $C_{bat} = 60\,\text{kWh}$
- Initial State of Charge: $SoC_0 = 20\%$
- Final State of Charge: $SoC_f = 80\%$
- Charger power rating: $P_{ch} = 11\,\text{kW}$ (Level 2 AC)
- Charger efficiency: $\eta_{ch} = 0.92$

**Step 1: Define the change in State of Charge.**

The required $\Delta SoC$ is the difference between the final and initial states of charge.

$$
\Delta SoC = SoC_f - SoC_0
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\Delta SoC = 80\% - 20\% = 60\% = 0.60
$$

**Step 2: Compute the required energy to be stored in the battery.**

The energy the battery must absorb is its total capacity multiplied by the SoC change.

$$
E_{required} = C_{bat} \times \Delta SoC
$$

$$
E_{required} = 60\,\text{kWh} \times 0.60 = 36\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3: Account for charger losses.**

Because the charger has efficiency $\eta_{ch}$, the actual energy that must be drawn from the grid exceeds the energy stored in the battery. The relationship is:

$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{required}}{\eta_{ch}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{36\,\text{kWh}}{0.92} = 39.13\,\text{kWh}
$$

The wasted energy is therefore:

$$
E_{loss} = E_{grid} - E_{required} = 39.13 - 36.00 = 3.13\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the charging time.**

Since the charger delivers power $P_{ch}$ from the grid, the charging time is the total grid energy divided by charger power.

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{grid}}{P_{ch}}
$$

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{39.13\,\text{kWh}}{11\,\text{kW}} = 3.558\,\text{hours}
$$

Converting to hours and minutes:

$$
t_{ch} \approx 3\,\text{h}\,33\,\text{min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A 60 kWh EV battery charged from 20% to 80% using an 11 kW Level 2 charger with 92% efficiency requires **3.56 hours**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Round-Trip Efficiency of a V2G System

**Problem Statement:** Show that the round-trip efficiency of a V2G cycle equals the product of charge and discharge efficiencies, and compute the energy loss for a typical daily cycle.

**Step 1: Energy taken from the grid (G2V phase).**

During G2V, the energy that enters the battery from the grid is reduced by the charging efficiency.

$$
E_{bat} = E_{grid} \times \eta_{ch}
$$

**Step 2: Energy returned to the grid (V2G phase).**

When the battery discharges, only a fraction of the stored energy reaches the grid, reduced by the discharge efficiency.

$$
E_{returned} = E_{bat} \times \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the first equation into the second.**

This gives the total energy that returns to the grid after one complete cycle.

$$
E_{returned} = (E_{grid} \times \eta_{ch}) \times \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 4: Define round-trip efficiency.**

Round-trip efficiency is the ratio of energy returned to the grid to the energy originally drawn from the grid.

$$
\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{returned}}{E_{grid}}
$$

Substituting the expression for $E_{returned}$:

$$
\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{grid} \times \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}}{E_{grid}}
$$

Cancelling $E_{grid}$ from numerator and denominator:

$$
\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation.**

Assume $\eta_{ch} = 0.94$ and $\eta_{dis} = 0.93$, both realistic values for a bidirectional EV charger.

$$
\eta_{rt} = 0.94 \times 0.93 = 0.8742
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta_{rt} \approx 87.42\%
$$

**Step 6: Energy loss per cycle for a 50 kWh dispatch.**

If the EV dispatches 50 kWh into the grid in a single V2G event, the energy loss is:

$$
E_{loss} = E_{bat}\,(1 - \eta_{rt}) = 50 \times (1 - 0.8742) = 50 \times 0.1258
$$

$$
E_{loss} = 6.29\,\text{kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A round-trip efficiency of ~87% means that for every 100 kWh the EV draws from the grid, only 87.4 kWh can be returned. The 12.6 kWh loss appears as heat in the power electronics and as internal losses in the battery's electrochemical cycling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: C-Rate Relationship to Charging Time

**Step 1: Definition of C-rate.**

The C-rate is defined as the ratio of the charging current to the nominal capacity of the battery.

$$
C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{ch}}{C_{nom}}
$$

**Step 2: Relating charging time to C-rate.**

The time required to fully charge a battery is the inverse of the C-rate.

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}
$$

**Step 3: Verification with an example.**

A battery with $C_{nom} = 50\,\text{Ah}$ charged at $I_{ch} = 50\,\text{A}$ has a C-rate of:

$$
C_{rate} = \dfrac{50}{50} = 1\,\text{C}
$$

The corresponding charging time is:

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{1}{1\,\text{C}} = 1\,\text{hour}
$$

For fast charging at 2C ($I_{ch} = 100\,\text{A}$):

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5\,\text{hours} = 30\,\text{minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Higher C-rates (faster charging) accelerate battery degradation. Most KTU questions on V2G emphasize that **shallow cycles at moderate C-rates (0.3C–0.5C) preserve battery life**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: G2V vs. V2G Comparison

| Parameter | G2V (Grid-to-Vehicle) | V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Flow Direction** | Unidirectional | Bidirectional |
| **Converter Topology** | Rectifier + Buck | Dual-Active Bridge / Bidirectional Inverter |
| **Communication Standard** | OCPP / IEC 61851 | ISO 15118 (Plug & Charge) |
| **Battery SoC Effect** | Increases | Decreases (controlled) |
| **Primary Grid Impact** | Load (demand increase) | Source + Load (flexibility) |
| **Use Case** | Daily home/workplace charging | Peak shaving, ancillary services |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low to Moderate | High |
| **Efficiency Concern** | Charging efficiency (~92–95%) | Round-trip efficiency (~85–88%) |
| **Regulatory Status** | Fully commercial | Pilot / Early commercial |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 G2V Power Flow Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the unidirectional energy chain from the grid to the EV battery in a G2V configuration.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1[Utility Grid AC 230V 50Hz] --> rect1[AC-DC Rectifier with PFC]
    rect1 --> dcdc1[Buck DC-DC Converter]
    dcdc1 --> bms1[Battery Management System BMS]
    bms1 --> bat1[EV Battery Pack 400V Li-ion]
    ctrl1[Grid Signal Tariff Time-of-Use] -.-> rect1
    ctrl1 -.-> dcdc1
    mon1[SoC and Temperature Monitor] -.-> bms1
```

**Operational Description:**
- The **Utility Grid** supplies 230V AC at 50 Hz.
- The **Rectifier with PFC** converts AC to DC and corrects the power factor.
- The **Buck DC-DC Converter** steps the voltage down to the battery's charging level.
- The **BMS** monitors the battery to prevent overcharge and thermal runaway.
- **Dashed arrows** represent control and communication signals (not power flow).

## 4.2 V2G Bidirectional Power Flow Architecture

The V2G system is more complex because the same hardware must allow power to flow in **both directions**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    grid2[Utility Grid 230V 50Hz] --> biacdc[Bidirectional AC-DC Inverter]
    biacdc --> bidcdc[Bidirectional DC-DC Converter DAB]
    bidcdc --> bat2[EV Battery Pack 400V 100 kWh]
    bat2 --> bidcdc
    bidcdc --> biacdc
    biacdc --> grid2
    iso2[ISO 15118 Communication Protocol] -.-> biacdc
    iso2 -.-> bidcdc
    aggr2[V2G Aggregator VPP] -.-> iso2
    bms2[BMS SoC Protection] -.-> bidcdc
```

**Key Subsystems:**
- **Bidirectional AC-DC Inverter:** Acts as rectifier during G2V, inverter during V2G.
- **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter (DAB):** A Dual-Active Bridge that allows energy to flow in either direction with high efficiency and galvanic isolation.
- **ISO 15118:** Standardized communication protocol for Plug & Charge, power schedule negotiation, and authentication.
- **V2G Aggregator:** Aggregates many vehicles into a market-biddable entity.

## 4.3 G2V vs. V2G Comparison Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{EV Plugged In}
    start --> comm[Communication with Grid]
    comm --> req{Grid Request Type}
    req --> low[Low Tariff Period]
    req --> high[High Tariff or Stress]
    low --> g2v[G2V Mode: Charge Battery]
    g2v --> chmon[Monitor SoC]
    chmon --> stop1{Reaches Target SoC}
    stop1 --> yes1[Stop Charging]
    stop1 --> no1[Continue G2V]
    high --> v2g[V2G Mode: Discharge Battery]
    v2g --> dismon[Monitor SoC and Price]
    dismon --> stop2{SoC reaches minimum threshold}
    stop2 --> yes2[Stop V2G]
    stop2 --> no2[Continue V2G]
```

**Interpretation:** The same physical charging port dynamically toggles between G2V and V2G depending on grid conditions, electricity prices, and the driver's required range.

## 4.4 V2G Grid Services Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    v2gservices[V2G Grid Services]
    v2gservices --> s1[Peak Shaving MW scale Hours]
    v2gservices --> s2[Frequency Regulation MW scale Seconds]
    v2gservices --> s3[Voltage Support MVar scale Seconds]
    v2gservices --> s4[Renewable Energy Integration MW scale Hours]
    v2gservices --> s5[Backup Power kW scale Hours]
    s2 --> fcr1[Primary FCR within 30s]
    s2 --> fcr2[Secondary FCR within minutes]
```

**Engineering Note:** Frequency regulation is the **highest-value** service that V2G can provide because it requires very fast response times (sub-second to seconds) — exactly what battery energy storage excels at.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define V2G and list two grid services it can provide.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** is a smart energy concept in which an electric vehicle, while connected to the grid via a bidirectional charger, can discharge stored electrical energy from its battery back to the grid. The power flow in V2G is **bidirectional**, and the system is governed by communication protocols like **ISO 15118**.

**Two major grid services provided by V2G:**

1. **Peak Shaving:** During periods of high electricity demand, aggregated EVs discharge into the grid, reducing the peak load that conventional power plants must serve. This flattens the load curve and reduces generation costs.

2. **Frequency Regulation:** EVs respond within seconds to deviations in grid frequency (from the nominal **50 Hz** in India) by automatically injecting or absorbing active power. This stabilizes the grid in real time and is the most lucrative ancillary service that V2G can offer.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of a V2G aggregator?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**Model Answer:**

A **V2G Aggregator** is an intermediary entity that pools a large number of electric vehicles into a single **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)**. Its primary roles are:

- **Market Bidding:** A single EV (30–100 kWh) is too small to bid into wholesale electricity markets. An aggregator combines thousands of EVs to offer **MW-scale** capacity to the grid operator.
- **SoC Management:** The aggregator ensures that each participating EV retains enough charge for the driver's next trip, while maximizing revenue from grid services.
- **Scheduling and Dispatch:** It coordinates real-time charging and discharging schedules based on grid signals, electricity prices, and individual vehicle availability.
- **Revenue Distribution:** It collects payments from the grid operator and distributes them proportionally to the EV owners who participated in V2G events.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse G2V and V2G.** G2V is **unidirectional** (grid to vehicle only). V2G is **bidirectional**.
> - When asked for charging time, **always include the charger efficiency** in your calculation. Skipping $\eta_{ch}$ costs you 2 marks.
> - When asked for round-trip efficiency, the correct expression is $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$, **not** $\eta_{ch} + \eta_{dis}$.
> - Always **state the direction of power flow** explicitly in your diagrams.
> - For SoC problems, **specify whether SoC is increasing or decreasing** to avoid ambiguity.

---

### Question A: Comprehensive Problem on G2V Charging and V2G Discharging

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester, Module 4]**

An electric vehicle has the following specifications:
- Battery capacity: $C_{bat} = 75\,\text{kWh}$
- Initial SoC: $SoC_0 = 30\%$
- Charger rating: $P_{ch} = 22\,\text{kW}$ (Level 2 AC, three-phase)
- Charging efficiency: $\eta_{ch} = 0.93$
- Discharging efficiency: $\eta_{dis} = 0.91$

#### Part (a) — G2V Charging [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (i):** Calculate the energy that must be drawn from the grid to charge the EV from 30% to 90% SoC. [3 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The change in SoC is:

$$
\Delta SoC = 90\% - 30\% = 60\% = 0.60
$$

The required battery energy is:

$$
E_{bat} = C_{bat} \times \Delta SoC = 75 \times 0.60 = 45\,\text{kWh}
$$

The grid energy required (accounting for charger losses) is:

$$
E_{grid} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{\eta_{ch}} = \dfrac{45}{0.93} = 48.39\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Answer:** The grid must supply **48.39 kWh** to deliver 45 kWh to the battery.

**Sub-part (ii):** Determine the charging time and the energy lost as heat in the charger. [4 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Charging time is:

$$
t_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{grid}}{P_{ch}} = \dfrac{48.39}{22} = 2.20\,\text{hours}
$$

Converting to hours and minutes:

$$
t_{ch} = 2\,\text{h}\,12\,\text{min}
$$

The energy lost in the charger is:

$$
E_{loss} = E_{grid} - E_{bat} = 48.39 - 45.00 = 3.39\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Answer:** Charging time is **2 hours 12 minutes**, and **3.39 kWh** is lost as heat.

#### Part (b) — V2G Discharging and Round-Trip Efficiency [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (i):** The same EV is now used for a V2G event. From 90% SoC, it discharges down to 40% SoC at a discharge power rating of $P_{dis} = 10\,\text{kW}$. Calculate the energy delivered back to the grid and the duration of the V2G event. [4 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The energy extracted from the battery:

$$
E_{bat}^{dis} = 75 \times (0.90 - 0.40) = 75 \times 0.50 = 37.5\,\text{kWh}
$$

The energy delivered to the grid (after discharge losses):

$$
E_{grid}^{V2G} = E_{bat}^{dis} \times \eta_{dis} = 37.5 \times 0.91 = 34.125\,\text{kWh}
$$

The duration of the V2G event:

$$
t_{V2G} = \dfrac{E_{bat}^{dis}}{P_{dis}} = \dfrac{37.5}{10} = 3.75\,\text{hours}
$$

**Answer:** The V2G event delivers **34.13 kWh** to the grid over **3.75 hours**.

**Sub-part (ii):** Compute the round-trip efficiency of the full G2V + V2G cycle and the total energy lost. [3 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

The total energy drawn from the grid during G2V was 48.39 kWh. The total energy returned during V2G is 34.125 kWh.

$$
\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{E_{grid}^{V2G}}{E_{grid}} = \dfrac{34.125}{48.39} = 0.7052
$$

Expressed as a percentage:

$$
\eta_{rt} \approx 70.52\%
$$

Alternatively, using the product rule:

$$
\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis} = 0.93 \times 0.91 = 0.8463
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Examiner:** The slight discrepancy between the two methods (70.52% vs. 84.63%) arises because the second method assumes the **same** amount of energy is cycled through both directions, whereas the first method uses the **actual different SoC windows** for charging and discharging. For full credit, students should show both methods and explain the difference.

Total energy lost in the cycle:

$$
E_{loss}^{total} = E_{grid} - E_{grid}^{V2G} = 48.39 - 34.125 = 14.265\,\text{kWh}
$$

---

### Question B: Conceptual and Analytical Essay on V2G Grid Services

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester, Module 4 Alternative]**

#### Part (a) — V2G Architecture and Communication [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (i):** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture of a V2G system. Identify the role of the bidirectional inverter, the BMS, and the aggregator. [4 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A complete V2G system consists of the following blocks:

1. **Utility Grid:** The AC network that receives or supplies power. The grid operates at **230V single-phase / 415V three-phase, 50 Hz** in India.

2. **Bidirectional AC-DC Inverter:** This is the heart of the V2G charger. During G2V, it operates as a **rectifier** converting AC to DC. During V2G, it operates as an **inverter** converting the battery's DC into grid-synchronized AC. It must maintain frequency, phase, and voltage synchronization with the grid using a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)**.

3. **Bidirectional DC-DC Converter (DAB or CLLC):** This stage matches the battery voltage to the DC bus voltage. It allows power to flow in both directions, provides galvanic isolation (via a high-frequency transformer), and maintains high efficiency (~96–98%).

4. **Battery Management System (BMS):** Protects the battery from overcharge, overdischarge, thermal runaway, and excessive C-rates. It also enforces the SoC limits (typically **20% minimum, 90% maximum**) to preserve battery longevity.

5. **V2G Aggregator:** Coordinates thousands of EVs into a VPP. It receives grid signals (prices, frequency, regulation requests) and dispatches individual EVs accordingly. It is the **commercial and operational brain** of the V2G system.

6. **Communication Protocol (ISO 15118):** Enables Plug & Charge authentication, encrypted billing, and dynamic power schedule negotiation between the EV and the grid.

**Sub-part (ii):** Differentiate between frequency regulation and peak shaving as V2G services. [3 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Frequency Regulation | Peak Shaving |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Scale** | Seconds to minutes | Hours |
| **Trigger** | Deviation of grid frequency from 50 Hz | Predicted daily demand peak |
| **Direction** | Can be up or down (charge or discharge) | Predominantly discharge (V2G) |
| **Value to Grid** | Very high (grid stability critical) | Moderate (load shifting) |
| **Payment Model** | Capacity + Performance payment | Energy arbitrage (price differential) |
| **EV Impact** | Small energy, high cycles | Large energy, few cycles |

#### Part (b) — Economic and Technical Challenges [7 Marks]

**Sub-part (i):** Discuss the technical challenges in implementing V2G at scale. [4 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

1. **Battery Degradation:** Each V2G cycle causes minor electrochemical wear. Although shallow cycles are gentler, real-world fleets still lose capacity over time. Most studies project **5–10% extra capacity loss per year** of active V2G participation.

2. **Bidirectional Charger Cost:** A V2G-capable bidirectional charger is approximately **2–3× more expensive** than a unidirectional G2V charger due to additional power electronics, sensors, and control circuitry.

3. **Standardization:** Widespread adoption requires harmonized standards (ISO 15118, IEC 61850, OCPP 2.0.1) across automakers, charger manufacturers, and utilities. Interoperability remains a key hurdle.

4. **Grid Stability Concerns:** Aggregating millions of EVs without proper coordination could create **new grid disturbances** (e.g., simultaneous discharge causing voltage rise). Coordinated control via aggregators is essential.

**Sub-part (ii):** A bidirectional EV charger operates for one complete cycle: it charges a 40 kWh battery from the grid with 94% efficiency, then discharges 90% of the stored energy back to the grid with 92% efficiency. Calculate the round-trip efficiency and the total energy lost. [3 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

Energy stored in the battery:

$$
E_{bat} = 40 \times 0.94 = 37.6\,\text{kWh}
$$

Energy delivered to the grid (90% of stored energy):

$$
E_{grid}^{V2G} = 37.6 \times 0.90 \times 0.92 = 31.13\,\text{kWh}
$$

Round-trip efficiency:

$$
\eta_{rt} = \dfrac{31.13}{40} = 0.778 = 77.8\%
$$

Total energy lost:

$$
E_{loss} = 40 - 31.13 = 8.87\,\text{kWh}
$$

**Answer:** $\eta_{rt} \approx 77.8\%$, with **8.87 kWh** lost per cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Your Exam!**

- [ ] **G2V = Unidirectional** (Grid → Vehicle); **V2G = Bidirectional** (Grid ↔ Vehicle)
- [ ] Three charging levels: Level 1 (~1.4 kW), Level 2 (~3.3–22 kW), Level 3 DC fast (~50–350 kW)
- [ ] Indian grid standards: **230V single-phase / 415V three-phase, 50 Hz**
- [ ] **Charging time formula:** $t_{ch} = \dfrac{C_{bat} \times \Delta SoC}{P_{ch} \times \eta_{ch}}$
- [ ] **Round-trip efficiency formula:** $\eta_{rt} = \eta_{ch} \times \eta_{dis}$ (typical range: **75–88%**)
- [ ] **V2G Aggregator** combines many EVs into a Virtual Power Plant (VPP)
- [ ] **Communication standard:** ISO 15118 enables Plug & Charge and bidirectional power negotiation
- [ ] **V2G grid services:** Peak shaving, frequency regulation, voltage support, renewable integration, backup power
- [ ] **Frequency regulation** is the most valuable V2G service (response time: seconds)
- [ ] **Bidirectional converter topologies:** Dual-Active Bridge (DAB), CLLC resonant converter
- [ ] **Battery protection:** SoC window typically limited to **20%–90%** to extend cycle life
- [ ] **Power electronics efficiency:** ~96–98% per stage; losses appear as heat
- [ ] **C-rate definition:** $C_{rate} = I_{ch} / C_{nom}$; charging time $t_{ch} = 1/C_{rate}$
- [ ] **Series cell count:** $n_{cells} = V_{pack} / V_{cell}$ (e.g., 400V pack / 3.7V cell ≈ 108 cells)
- [ ] V2G business model: **Aggregator → Grid Operator → Revenue → EV Owner**
- [ ] **Duck Curve problem:** EVs can absorb midday solar surplus via smart G2V and discharge in evening peak via V2G

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Management and control hierarchy of storage systems. Aggregating energy storage systems and distributed generation (Virtual Power Plant Energy Management with storage systems)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Management and control hierarchy of storage systems. Aggregating energy storage systems and distributed generation (Virtual Power Plant Energy Management with storage systems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Storage System Management, Aggregation & Virtual Power Plants

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Storage System Management Hierarchy** refers to the multi-layered supervisory and control architecture that orchestrates the operation, protection, balancing, and dispatch of individual storage cells, modules, packs, racks, and aggregated plants. It integrates **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**, **Power Conversion System (PCS) controllers**, **Plant-level Energy Management Systems (EMS)**, **SCADA**, and **Utility/ISO dispatch platforms** into a coordinated control pyramid.

**Distributed Energy Resources (DER) Aggregation** is the process of pooling geographically dispersed but electronically coordinated storage units, photovoltaic (PV) inverters, wind turbines, micro-turbines, and controllable loads so that the ensemble behaves as a single dispatchable entity.

**Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** is a cloud-and-communications-based aggregation framework that uses software-defined intelligence to combine the flexible generation, storage, and demand-side resources of many small DERs into a unified portfolio that can bid into wholesale electricity markets and provide grid services — *without requiring them to be physically co-located*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A VPP is *not* a physical power station. It is an **information-and-energy orchestration layer** that creates "synthetic" capacity, firming, and ancillary services from fragmented DERs by using storage as the principal flexibility enabler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra Conductor"

Imagine an orchestra where every violinist, flautist, and percussionist is sitting in a different room across the city.

- Each musician (a **battery cell** or **small PV inverter**) is individually weak and uncoordinated.
- Each musician has a **music stand** (a **local BMS/inverter controller**) that keeps their personal tune correct — voltage, current, temperature, SoC.
- The **concert hall amplifiers and section leaders** form the **module and rack controllers**, ensuring every section stays in tune with adjacent sections.
- The **section conductor** corresponds to the **plant-level PCS + local EMS**, deciding how much power flows in/out and when.
- The **chief conductor listening on a headset via internet** is the **VPP aggregator / cloud EMS** — coordinating *dozens* of such halls (plants) into one grand symphony that responds in real-time to the grid's beat (frequency, price signal, dispatch instruction).

The **chief conductor has no physical instrument**, yet controls the entire orchestra. That is precisely what a VPP does for storage and DERs.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in This Topic

- **C-rate**: ratio of charge/discharge current to nominal capacity (unitless, e.g., 1C, 0.5C).
- **State of Charge (SoC)**: stored energy / usable energy, dimensionless, **0 ≤ SoC ≤ 1**.
- **State of Health (SoH)**: present max capacity / original capacity, **0 ≤ SoH ≤ 1**.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD)**: 1 − SoC_min, dimensionless.
- **Round-trip efficiency (η_rt)**: energy out / energy in, typically **0.80 – 0.92** for Li-ion.
- **Frequency droop constant (R)**: Hz / MW or Hz / p.u., depends on grid code.
- **Aggregation latency**: **≤ 1 second** for primary frequency response; **seconds to minutes** for secondary/tertiary control.
- **Round-trip time of dispatch signal (Telecom)**: typically **≤ 4 seconds** under IEC 61850 GOOSE.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Management & Control Hierarchy of a Storage System

Modern grid-scale storage plants are governed by a **four-to-five-layer control pyramid** moving from the cell level (milliseconds, hardware) up to the market level (minutes to hours, software/business).

### Layer 1 — Cell-Level Protection & Balancing (Hardware, ms)
Performed inside each lithium-ion, lead-acid, or flow-battery cell by a **Battery Management System (BMS)** slave board.

Functions:
- Per-cell voltage monitoring
- Per-cell temperature monitoring
- Over-current / over-voltage / under-voltage cut-off
- Passive or active cell **balancing** (SoC equalization)
- Coulomb counting for SoC estimation

### Layer 2 — Module / Pack Controller (Hardware, ms–s)
A BMS master board collects data from cell-level slaves and computes pack SoC/SoH, propagates limits to the PCS.

### Layer 3 — Power Conversion System (PCS) Local Controller (ms–s)
- Controls IGBT/MOSFET gating of the bidirectional DC–AC inverter
- Implements **P–f** and **Q–V** droops
- Executes active power set-points and reactive power references
- Provides **LVRT/HVRT** (low/high voltage ride-through) and **anti-islanding**

### Layer 4 — Plant Energy Management System (P-EMS) (seconds–minutes)
- Schedules charge/discharge against a price curve or renewable forecast
- Performs peak-shaving, load-levelling, self-consumption maximization
- Manages State-of-Charge (SoC) **operating window** (e.g., 10%–90%)
- Sends setpoints to multiple PCSs within the plant

### Layer 5 — Aggregation / VPP / Market Layer (minutes–hours)
- Receives dispatch signals from utility, ISO/RTO, or aggregator
- Optimizes portfolio economics across many plants and DERs
- Bids into day-ahead, intra-day, ancillary markets
- Issues **set-point commands** down to each P-EMS

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Draw and explain the management and control hierarchy of a battery energy storage system." — Always draw the pyramid with **bidirectional communication arrows** between every adjacent layer.

## 2.2 SoC Dynamics — The Foundational Equation

For any storage device, the fundamental state equation governing SoC over time is:

$$
SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) + \frac{1}{E_{\text{rated}}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \bigl( \eta_c \, P_c(\tau) - \tfrac{1}{\eta_d} \, P_d(\tau) \bigr) \, d\tau
$$

where:
- $E_{\text{rated}}$ = rated energy capacity of the storage (MWh)
- $P_c(\tau)$ = charging power (MW, ≥ 0)
- $P_d(\tau)$ = discharging power (MW, ≥ 0)
- $\eta_c$ = Coulombic charge efficiency (typically 0.95–0.99)
- $\eta_d$ = Coulombic discharge efficiency (typically 0.95–0.99)
- Round-trip efficiency $\eta_{rt} = \eta_c \cdot \eta_d$ (typical **0.85–0.92** for Li-ion systems)

## 2.3 Power Dispatch with Frequency Droop (Primary Response)

For a storage unit participating in **primary frequency control**, the droop characteristic gives:

$$
P(t) \;=\; P_{\text{ref}} \;+\; \frac{1}{R} \bigl( f_{\text{ref}} - f(t) \bigr)
$$

where:
- $P(t)$ = injected active power (MW)
- $P_{\text{ref}}$ = scheduled dispatch set-point (MW)
- $R$ = droop constant in **Hz/MW** (e.g., 0.05 Hz/MW)
- $f_{\text{ref}}$ = nominal frequency (**50 Hz** in India)
- $f(t)$ = measured grid frequency (Hz)

When grid frequency drops (under-frequency), the storage discharges; when it rises, the storage charges — providing **synthetic inertia** and **fast frequency response (FFR)**.

## 2.4 Reactive Power — Voltage Droop

$$
Q(t) \;=\; Q_{\text{ref}} \;+\; k_q \bigl( V_{\text{ref}} - V(t) \bigr)
$$

where $k_q$ is the Q–V droop gain (MVAR/p.u.).

## 2.5 Aggregation — Portfolio Model

Consider **N** heterogeneous storage units aggregated into a VPP. The **aggregated power output** is:

$$
P_{\text{VPP}}(t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i(t) \quad \text{subject to} \quad SoC_i^{\min} \le SoC_i(t) \le SoC_i^{\max}
$$

For market bidding, the aggregator solves a **stochastic optimization** problem:

$$
\max_{P_i,\, b_i} \; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \lambda_t \, P_{\text{VPP}}(t) \cdot \Delta t \;-\; C_{\text{deg}}\bigl( \text{DoD}_i, N_{cyc,i} \bigr)
$$

where:
- $\lambda_t$ = locational marginal price (LMP) at hour *t* (₹/MWh)
- $b_i$ = binary charge/discharge decision for unit *i*
- $C_{\text{deg}}$ = battery degradation cost function (₹/MWh throughput)
- $N_{cyc,i}$ = equivalent full cycles of unit *i*

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|----------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) + \dfrac{1}{E_r}\!\int (\eta_c P_c - \tfrac{1}{\eta_d}P_d)\,dt$ | Energy-balance state equation | $0 \le SoC \le 1$ |
| 2 | $E_{\text{usable}} = E_{\text{rated}} \cdot \text{DoD}$ | Energy that can be cyclically used | MWh |
| 3 | $\eta_{rt} = \eta_c \cdot \eta_d$ | Round-trip efficiency | 0.80 – 0.92 typical |
| 4 | $P(t) = P_{\text{ref}} + \dfrac{1}{R}(f_{\text{ref}} - f(t))$ | P–f droop / primary response | R in **Hz/MW** |
| 5 | $Q(t) = Q_{\text{ref}} + k_q(V_{\text{ref}} - V(t))$ | Q–V droop / voltage support | $k_q$ in **MVAR/p.u.** |
| 6 | $P_{\text{VPP}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$ | Aggregated VPP dispatch | MW |
| 7 | $C_{\text{deg}} = a \cdot \text{DoD}^b$ (Wöhler-like) | Cycle-life degradation | ₹/MWh |
| 8 | $\text{SoH}(t) = 1 - \dfrac{\int \vert P \vert \,dt}{E_{\text{lifetime}}}$ | State of Health aging | 0 – 1 |
| 9 | $\Delta f(t) = -\dfrac{1}{2H_s}\, \int \bigl( P_m - P_e \bigr) dt$ | Synthetic inertia swing eq. | Hz, $H_s$ in **s** |
| 10 | $\text{Response time} \le 1\,\text{s}$ | Primary FFR (Indian Draft CEA) | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) inside table cells** — absolute value is rendered as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Aggregator Economic Dispatch with SoC Coupling

The aggregator's classical problem: maximize revenue across *N* heterogeneous storage units over horizon *T*, subject to per-unit SoC dynamics and power limits.

### Step 1 — Define Objective Function

The revenue from selling $P_i(t)$ into the market at price $\lambda_t$ minus the cycling degradation cost is:

$$
\Pi \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{T} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Bigl[ \lambda_t \, P_i^{\text{dis}}(t) - \lambda_t \, P_i^{\text{ch}}(t) - c_{\text{deg},i}\,\bigl(P_i^{\text{ch}}(t) + P_i^{\text{dis}}(t)\bigr) \Bigr]\,\Delta t
$$

Step 1 explanation: We separate charging and discharging powers ($P_i^{\text{ch}}, P_i^{\text{dis}} \ge 0$) so we can price energy purchase during charge and revenue during discharge, while charging a degradation penalty on both because every cycle ages the battery.

### Step 2 — Per-Unit Power Limits

$$
0 \;\le\; P_i^{\text{ch}}(t) \;\le\; P_{i}^{\text{ch,max}}, \qquad 0 \;\le\; P_i^{\text{dis}}(t) \;\le\; P_{i}^{\text{dis,max}}
$$

Step 2 explanation: Each storage unit has nameplate charge/discharge ratings enforced by the PCS and BMS.

### Step 3 — SoC Coupling Constraint

From the SoC dynamic equation in §2.2, we obtain a discrete-time coupling between consecutive hours:

$$
SoC_i(t+1) \;=\; SoC_i(t) \;+\; \frac{\Delta t}{E_{r,i}}\Bigl( \eta_c\,P_i^{\text{ch}}(t) \;-\; \frac{1}{\eta_d}\,P_i^{\text{dis}}(t) \Bigr)
$$

Step 3 explanation: The discrete Euler integration of the continuous SoC equation gives a linear coupling between $t$ and $t+1$. This is what makes the optimization a **Linear Program (LP)** when the objective is linear.

### Step 4 — SoC Operating Envelope

$$
SoC_i^{\min} \;\le\; SoC_i(t) \;\le\; SoC_i^{\max}
$$

with typical values $SoC_i^{\min}=0.10$ and $SoC_i^{\max}=0.90$ to protect cycle life.

Step 4 explanation: This envelope reserves a buffer SoC that the optimizer cannot touch. It is the *safety margin* the BMS uses for primary frequency response — leaving headroom to absorb unexpected over-frequency and under-frequency events.

### Step 5 — Terminal Condition

$$
SoC_i(T) \;\ge\; SoC_i^{\text{final}}
$$

Step 5 explanation: The optimizer must restore each battery to at least its final SoC at the end of the horizon so that the next day's market window starts from a known energy reserve.

### Step 6 — Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Stationarity

Forming the Lagrangian and taking the gradient w.r.t. $P_i^{\text{dis}}(t)$:

$$
\lambda_t \;-\; c_{\text{deg},i} \;+\; \frac{\mu_i^{\text{SoC}}(t)}{\eta_d \, E_{r,i}}\,\Delta t \;-\; \nu_i^{\text{dis}}(t) \;=\; 0
$$

Step 6 explanation: The optimal dispatch occurs when the market price equals the marginal degradation cost plus the shadow price of SoC. If $\lambda_t$ rises above this sum, the unit discharges; if it falls below, the unit charges (a symmetric condition governs $P_i^{\text{ch}}$). This is the **economic merit-order** of storage dispatch inside a VPP.

### Step 7 — Practical Closed-Form (Two-Period Arbitrage Example)

For $N = 1$ and $T = 2$ (charge hour, discharge hour), the optimal SoC trajectory becomes:

$$
SoC(t_2) \;=\; SoC(t_0) \;+\; \frac{\Delta t}{E_r}\bigl( \eta_c \, P_{\text{ch}} - \tfrac{1}{\eta_d}\, P_{\text{dis}} \bigr)
$$

To break-even on price spread alone:

$$
\lambda_{\text{dis}} \;\ge\; \lambda_{\text{ch}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\eta_{rt}} \;+\; c_{\text{deg}}
$$

Step 7 explanation: The discharge price must exceed the charge price divided by round-trip efficiency plus the per-MWh degradation cost. This is the classic **arbitrage trigger condition** taught in every energy economics module and is a guaranteed KTU short-question.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Layer-Wise Decision Variables and Time Constants

| Control Layer | Decision Variables | Time Constant | Hardware/Software | Failure Cost |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|--------------|
| Cell (BMS slave) | Per-cell V, I, T | **ms** | Analog front-end + ADC | Cell fire/explosion |
| Pack (BMS master) | Pack SoC, SoH | **100 ms** | Microcontroller + CAN | Pack outage |
| PCS local ctrl | Gate pulses, $P$, $Q$ | **1 ms** (inner current loop) | FPGA / DSP | Trip, ride-through failure |
| Plant EMS (P-EMS) | Set-points, schedules | **1 s – 1 min** | Industrial PLC + SCADA | Lost revenue, constraint violation |
| Aggregator / VPP | Portfolio bids, dispatch | **5 min – 1 h** | Cloud servers + APIs | Market penalties, imbalance |

> [!NOTE]
> The **time-constants rise monotonically** as we climb the pyramid. The KTU board often asks students to *justify* why a higher layer does not — and should not — directly control a lower layer's fast variable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Storage Management & Control Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5["LAYER 5
Aggregation and VPP
Market Interface
Hour to Day Time Scale"]
    L4["LAYER 4
Plant Energy Management System P-EMS
Second to Minute Time Scale"]
```
