```mermaid
Power Conversion System PCS Controller
Millisecond Time Scale"]
    L2["LAYER 2
Pack BMS Master Board
100 ms Time Scale"]
    L1["LAYER 1
Cell BMS Slave Board
Voltage Current Temperature
ms Time Scale"]
    MK["Utility or ISO or DISCOM
Load Frequency Control Market
Dispatch Signal and Price Signal"]
    NET["Distribution Grid 11 kV or 33 kV
Point of Common Coupling"]
    EMS["Energy Storage Modules
Lithium-ion or Flow Battery"]
    PV["Solar PV Rooftop 100 kW"]
    WT["Wind Turbine 250 kW"]
    DG["Diesel Genset 500 kW"]
    LD["Smart Controllable Loads
Industrial and Residential"]

    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5

    MK --> L5
    L5 --> MK
    L5 --> EMS
    L5 --> PV
    L5 --> WT
    L5 --> DG
    L5 --> LD
    L3 --> NET
    PV --> NET
    WT --> NET
    DG --> NET
    EMS --> NET
    LD --> NET
```

### Interpretation of the Diagram
- **Layer 1** (bottom-most block): cell-level sensing — the only layer that *physically touches* the cell terminals.
- **Layer 5** (top-most block): market-facing decisions; communicates with utility/ISO via **IEC 61850**, **OpenADR 2.0b**, or **IEEE 2030.5** protocols.
- The double-headed arrows between every adjacent layer indicate **bidirectional command-and-telemetry flow**.
- DERs (PV, wind, genset, load) all join at the **VPP layer** because the VPP treats them as fungible portfolio members.

## 4.2 Virtual Power Plant with Storage — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FLD["FIELD LAYER"]
        B1["Battery Bank A
5 MWh Li-ion"]
        B2["Battery Bank B
2 MWh Flow Battery"]
        PV1["Solar Plant 1
10 MWp"]
        PV2["Solar Plant 2
5 MWp"]
        WT1["Wind Park
8 MW"]
        LD1["Smart Loads
Curtailable 2 MW"]
    end
    subgraph LOC["LOCAL CONTROL LAYER"]
        BMS1["BMS Bank A"]
        BMS2["BMS Bank B"]
        INV1["PV Inverter Plant 1"]
        INV2["PV Inverter Plant 2"]
        WTC["Wind SCADA"]
        LDC["Load Controller"]
    end
    subgraph PLT["PLANT EMS LAYER"]
        PEMS1["P-EMS Plant 1"]
        PEMS2["P-EMS Plant 2"]
        PEMS3["P-EMS Plant 3"]
    end
    subgraph AGG["AGGREGATION LAYER"]
        VPP["VPP Cloud EMS
Portfolio Optimizer
Bid Engine"]
        ANA["Analytics and Forecasting
Weather Load and Price"]
        CRM["Market Interface
Day Ahead and Real Time"]
    end
    subgraph GRD["GRID"]
        ISO["ISO or Utility
Dispatch Center"]
    end

    B1 --> BMS1 --> PEMS1 --> VPP
    B2 --> BMS2 --> PEMS1
    PV1 --> INV1 --> PEMS1
    PV2 --> INV2 --> PEMS2
    WT1 --> WTC --> PEMS2
    LD1 --> LDC --> PEMS3
    VPP --> ISO
    ISO --> VPP
    ANA --> VPP
    VPP --> CRM --> ISO
```

### Interpretation of the Diagram
- Three colour-coded sub-graphs (`FLD`, `LOC`, `PLT`, `AGG`, `GRD`) map the physical-to-cyber chain.
- **Storage units (B1, B2)** appear in *every* plant because they provide the time-shift, ramp-rate control, and frequency response that turn intermittent renewables into **dispatchable capacity**.
- The **VPP Cloud EMS** is the *only* block that talks directly to the ISO — there is no physical busbar behind the VPP, only **information aggregation**.

## 4.3 Information Flow Sequence During a VPP Frequency Event

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ISO as ISO or Utility
    participant VPP as VPP Cloud EMS
    participant PEMS as Plant EMS
    participant PCS as PCS Controller
    participant BAT as Battery Modules
    ISO->>VPP: Frequency nadir event f=49.5 Hz
    VPP->>PEMS: Aggregate dispatch 20 MW FFR
    PEMS->>PCS: Power set-point ramp 0 to 8 MW in 0.5 s
    PCS->>BAT: Discharge command respecting SoC window
    BAT-->>PCS: Telemetry voltage current temperature
    PCS-->>PEMS: Ack and revised headroom
    PEMS-->>VPP: Actual delivered power
    VPP-->>ISO: Telemetry to settlement system
```

This diagram shows the **end-to-end latency budget**: from frequency nadir detection to power injection must occur in **≤ 1 second** to qualify for **Fast Frequency Response (FFR)** payments under the Indian Draft CEA Regulation 2024.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Virtual Power Plant (VPP). How does it differ from a conventional microgrid?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** is a *software-orchestrated* aggregation of geographically dispersed distributed energy resources (DERs) — including storage, PV, wind, and controllable loads — that operates as a *single dispatchable entity* in electricity markets.

| Feature | Microgrid | Virtual Power Plant |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| Geographic footprint | **Co-located** within a defined electrical boundary | **Dispersed** across wide area |
| Physical coupling | Common busbar / point of common coupling | Only **information-coupled** |
| Islanding | Yes (intentional islanding supported) | Not typically supported |
| Market role | Behind-the-meter consumer or prosumer | Wholesale market participant |
| Key enabler | Local EMS + protection | Cloud EMS + telemetry |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for tabular difference, 1 for key insight on storage role.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the layers of a battery storage plant management hierarchy and state the time-constant of each layer.**

**Model Answer:**

| Layer | Function | Time Constant |
|-------|----------|---------------|
| 1 — Cell BMS slave | Voltage / current / temperature sensing | **ms** |
| 2 — Pack BMS master | SoC/SoH, balancing, pack protection | **~100 ms** |
| 3 — PCS controller | Inverter gating, P/Q control | **~1 ms** inner loop |
| 4 — Plant EMS | Dispatch schedule, SoC window mgmt. | **1 s – 1 min** |
| 5 — Aggregator / VPP | Portfolio optimization, bidding | **5 min – 1 h** |

*(3 marks: 1 for naming, 1 for time-constants, 1 for explaining why time-constants increase up the pyramid.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Choose either Question A OR Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **management and control hierarchy of a grid-scale battery energy storage system**. Explain the role of each layer and the typical time-constants.

**(b) [7 marks]** A **10 MWh / 5 MW** Li-ion BESS has $\eta_c = 0.97$, $\eta_d = 0.97$, $SoC_0 = 0.30$, and a **SoC operating window of [0.10, 0.90]**. It must deliver **4 MW** for 1 hour starting at $t = 1$ h. Verify whether the unit can honour the dispatch without violating SoC limits. If not, calculate the deficit energy.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- Draw the **five-layer pyramid** (Cell → Pack → PCS → P-EMS → VPP).
- Identify that **bidirectional arrows** exist between adjacent layers (commands down, telemetry up).
- State the **time-constants**: ms / 100 ms / 1 ms / 1 s–1 min / 5 min–1 h.
- Emphasise that **storage provides the cross-layer flexibility** that makes upper-layer optimization meaningful.
- Mention communication protocols: **CAN** (cells), **Modbus / DNP3** (plant), **IEC 61850 / OpenADR** (grid).

*(Distribution: 2 marks diagram, 2 marks layers, 2 marks time-constants, 1 mark role of storage.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)
**Step 1 — Discharge energy required:**

$$
E_{\text{req}} \;=\; P \cdot t \;=\; 4\;\text{MW} \times 1\;\text{h} \;=\; 4\;\text{MWh}
$$

Step 1 explanation: The delivery contract is 4 MW × 1 h, i.e., 4 MWh of *output* energy.

**Step 2 — Available stored energy (SoC-limited):**

$$
E_{\text{available}} \;=\; E_{\text{rated}} \cdot \bigl( SoC_0 - SoC^{\min} \bigr) \;=\; 10 \times (0.30 - 0.10) \;=\; 2\;\text{MWh}
$$

Step 2 explanation: From initial SoC 0.30 down to the lower limit 0.10, only 20% of the 10 MWh is usable = 2 MWh.

**Step 3 — Account for discharge efficiency** $\eta_d$:

$$
E_{\text{available,out}} \;=\; E_{\text{available}} \cdot \eta_d \;=\; 2 \times 0.97 \;=\; 1.94\;\text{MWh}
$$

Step 3 explanation: Of the 2 MWh extracted from the cells, only 97% reaches the AC bus due to PCS + battery internal losses.

**Step 4 — Compare with requirement:**

$$
E_{\text{available,out}} \;=\; 1.94\;\text{MWh} \;<\; 4\;\text{MWh} \;=\; E_{\text{req}}
$$

Step 4 explanation: The unit **cannot** deliver the contracted 4 MWh.

**Step 5 — Deficit energy:**

$$
E_{\text{deficit}} \;=\; E_{\text{req}} - E_{\text{available,out}} \;=\; 4 - 1.94 \;=\; 2.06\;\text{MWh}
$$

Step 5 explanation: The aggregator (or VPP layer) must dispatch **2.06 MWh** from other units in the portfolio to honour the full 4 MWh contract.

**Final Answer:** The BESS cannot honour the full dispatch; **deficit = 2.06 MWh**. The aggregator should top up from another storage unit or curtail the contract to 1.94 MWh.

*(Distribution: 1 mark Step 1, 2 marks Step 2, 1 mark Step 3, 1 mark Step 4, 1 mark Step 5, 1 mark conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)**. Discuss the role of **energy storage** in a VPP and explain how aggregation enables **stacking of revenue streams** (energy arbitrage, frequency regulation, peak shaving, capacity payment).

**(b) [7 marks]** A VPP aggregates **three** storage units with parameters:
- Unit 1: 5 MWh / 2.5 MW, SoC₀ = 0.5
- Unit 2: 3 MWh / 1.5 MW, SoC₀ = 0.8
- Unit 3: 8 MWh / 4.0 MW, SoC₀ = 0.2
Assume η_rt = 0.90 and SoC operating window [0.10, 0.90]. The VPP receives a **dispatch instruction of 6 MW for 2 hours**. Determine the optimal allocation using a *greedy merit-order based on available headroom*.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)
- Define VPP: an information-coupled aggregation that appears as a single dispatchable entity.
- Storage's role: provides **time-shift, ramp control, and frequency response** to firm up variable renewables.
- Revenue stacking: arbitrage (price spread), FFR/regulation (€/MW capacity payment), peak shaving (demand-charge reduction), capacity market (€ / MW availability).
- Emphasise that storage is the *only* resource in the VPP that can stack **all four** streams simultaneously because it is the only fully *bidirectional* and *fast-responding* asset.

*(Distribution: 2 marks VPP definition, 2 marks storage role, 2 marks stacking concept, 1 mark example.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute available discharge energy for each unit (down to SoC_min = 0.10):**

$$
E_{\text{dis},i} \;=\; E_{r,i} \cdot (SoC_{0,i} - 0.10) \cdot \eta_d
$$

Step 1 explanation: We use η_d = √η_rt = √0.90 ≈ 0.949 (assuming symmetric charging/discharging losses).

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:**

| Unit | $E_{r,i}$ (MWh) | $SoC_0$ | $SoC_0 - 0.10$ | $E_{\text{dis},i}$ (MWh) | Power (MW) |
|------|----------------|---------|----------------|--------------------------|------------|
| 1 | 5 | 0.50 | 0.40 | $5 \times 0.40 \times 0.949 = 1.898$ | 2.5 |
| 2 | 3 | 0.80 | 0.70 | $3 \times 0.70 \times 0.949 = 1.993$ | 1.5 |
| 3 | 8 | 0.20 | 0.10 | $8 \times 0.10 \times 0.949 = 0.759$ | 4.0 |

Step 2 explanation: Each unit's available *discharge* energy is its accessible SoC range multiplied by its energy capacity and discharge efficiency.

**Step 3 — Greedy allocation by headroom (largest available energy first):**

- Total demand: $6\;\text{MW} \times 2\;\text{h} = 12\;\text{MWh}$
- Sum of available discharge energy: $1.898 + 1.993 + 0.759 = 4.650\;\text{MWh}$

Step 3 explanation: The total **available** discharge energy across the portfolio is only 4.65 MWh, but the **dispatch instruction** demands 12 MWh.

**Step 4 — Constraint check by power rating:**

Demand = 6 MW. Sort units by available discharge energy / available duration merit:

- Unit 2: 1.993 MWh over 2 h ⇒ max continuous $P = 1.5$ MW (nameplate). So Unit 2 can deliver its full 1.5 MW for 2 h (= 3 MWh), but only 1.993 MWh is *available* before SoC floor → at 1.5 MW it lasts $1.993/1.5 = 1.33$ h, **insufficient for 2 h**.
- Unit 1: 1.898 MWh available. At 2.5 MW nameplate, can sustain $1.898/2.5 = 0.76$ h only.
- Unit 3: 0.759 MWh. At 4 MW nameplate, only $0.76/4 = 0.19$ h.

Step 4 explanation: A pure *power-merit-order* allocation (largest MW first: Unit 3 → Unit 1 → Unit 2) would deplete SoC of each unit in tens of minutes, violating the 2 h delivery requirement.

**Step 5 — Reallocate using energy-based merit-order:**

Allocate by *largest available discharge energy first*:

- Unit 2 → assign 1.5 MW for entire 2 h ⇒ uses 3 MWh demand but only 1.993 MWh available ⇒ **deficit 1.007 MWh**.
- Unit 1 → assign 2.5 MW for entire 2 h ⇒ uses 5 MWh demand but only 1.898 MWh available ⇒ **deficit 3.102 MWh**.
- Unit 3 → assign 4.0 MW for entire 2 h ⇒ uses 8 MWh demand but only 0.759 MWh available ⇒ **deficit 7.241 MWh**.

Step 5 explanation: Total deficit = $12 - 4.65 = 7.35$ MWh. The aggregator must either (a) accept partial curtailment, (b) recall stored energy from renewable curtailment, or (c) call on additional contracted units.

**Step 6 — Realistic revised dispatch:**

The VPP can deliver at most **4.65 MWh / 2 h ≈ 2.325 MW sustained for 2 h**, or alternatively **6 MW for 0.78 h**. The aggregator reports a **partial-acceptance** of the dispatch instruction.

**Final Answer:** Optimal (greedy) allocation = full power from all three units, total **deliverable energy = 4.65 MWh** vs **12 MWh requested**; deficit = **7.35 MWh**. Aggregator should issue a **partial-acceptance** signal.

*(Distribution: 1 mark Step 1, 2 marks Step 2 table, 1 mark Step 3, 1 mark Step 4, 1 mark Step 5, 1 mark final answer.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not confuse η_rt with η_d alone** — round-trip efficiency splits into charge and discharge halves. Markers specifically check whether you applied η_d for *output* energy and 1/η_c for *input* energy.
> 2. **Always quote the SoC operating window** explicitly. Forgetting the [0.10, 0.90] window loses 1–2 marks in Part B numericals.
> 3. **Never draw the VPP as a physical busbar**. VPP is *information-coupled*. Many students lose marks by drawing wires between units.
> 4. **Mention communication protocols** (IEC 61850, OpenADR, IEEE 2030.5) at least once in any descriptive answer — examiners award "extra" awareness marks.
> 5. **For aggregation numerics, always compute *both* power-feasibility and energy-feasibility.** Many portfolios fail on energy, not on power.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ The **management hierarchy** of a storage plant has **five layers**: Cell BMS slave → Pack BMS master → PCS controller → Plant EMS → VPP/Aggregator.
- ☐ Time-constants **increase monotonically** as we move up: ms → 100 ms → 1 ms → seconds–minutes → minutes–hours.
- ☐ Storage is the **principal flexibility enabler** in any aggregation: it is the only asset that is *bidirectional*, *fast*, and *programmable* in time.
- ☐ A **Virtual Power Plant (VPP)** is a *software-defined* aggregation — it is **not** a microgrid (microgrid is *geographically* co-located; VPP is *informationally* co-located).
- ☐ Core SoC equation: $\,SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) + \tfrac{1}{E_r}\!\int (\eta_c P_c - \tfrac{1}{\eta_d}P_d)dt$.
- ☐ Primary frequency response droop: $P(t) = P_{\text{ref}} + \tfrac{1}{R}(f_{\text{ref}} - f(t))$.
- ☐ Aggregator objective = maximise $\sum \lambda_t P_i(t) \Delta t - C_{\text{deg}}$, subject to SoC limits.
- ☐ **Arbitrage break-even**: $\lambda_{\text{dis}} \ge \lambda_{\text{ch}}/\eta_{rt} + c_{\text{deg}}$.
- ☐ Two feasibility checks for any dispatch: **power-feasibility** (nameplate) AND **energy-feasibility** (SoC window × η).
- ☐ Communication protocols: **CAN** (cell), **Modbus/DNP3** (plant), **IEC 61850 / OpenADR 2.0b / IEEE 2030.5** (VPP↔ISO).
- ☐ Round-trip efficiency for Li-ion ≈ **0.85–0.92**; for flow batteries ≈ **0.65–0.80**; for pumped hydro ≈ **0.75–0.82**.
- ☐ Standard India grid frequency = **50 Hz**; allowable band = **49.5 – 50.5 Hz**; FFR response time ≤ **1 s**.
- ☐ KTU 2024 syllabus stresses the *interaction* of storage with **renewables** and **demand response** inside a VPP — always mention **revenue stacking**.
- ☐ Be ready to draw the **VPP functional architecture** with field, local control, plant EMS, aggregation, and grid layers — examiners expect *bidirectional arrows*.
- ☐ Numerical traps: forgetting η, forgetting SoC window, treating VPP power as if bounded by a single physical busbar.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery SCADA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Battery SCADA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Battery SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, hierarchical software-hardware automation architecture deployed in Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and battery banks that continuously acquires real-time field telemetry from the Battery Management System (BMS), performs supervisory control commands, logs historical operational data, and triggers alarms upon the violation of preset safety/operational thresholds. It acts as the **digital nervous system** of any medium-to-large scale battery installation, bridging the low-level cell electronics with the grid/plant operator's Human-Machine Interface (HMI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> *"Battery SCADA refers to the integrated monitoring, control, and data acquisition framework used to supervise battery banks and Battery Energy Storage Stations, providing real-time visibility of State of Charge (SOC), State of Health (SOH), voltage, current, temperature, and alarm status to the plant operator."*

The standard SCADA architecture, when adapted for batteries, follows the **Three-Tier Hierarchical Model** prescribed by **IEEE C37.1** and **IEC 62443**:

1. **Field Level (Level 0):** Current sensors (Hall-Effect), voltage transducers, thermocouples, BMS slave boards.
2. **Automation Level (Level 1):** Remote Terminal Units (RTUs), Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Bay Control Units (BCUs).
3. **Supervisory Level (Level 2):** Master Terminal Unit (MTU), SCADA Server, Engineering Workstation, Historian Database, HMI.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU" Model

To grasp Battery SCADA intuitively, imagine a large **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** in a hospital:

- **The Patient:** Your battery bank (a stack of Li-ion cells).
- **The Vitals Monitor (BEEP-BOOP):** Each bedside monitor measures heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen — analogously, the **BMS** measures cell voltage, current, temperature, and SOC for every cell.
- **The Central Nursing Station (SCADA HMI):** All bedside monitors are wired to one giant screen at the nurse's station. The nurse sees all patients at once, gets **alarms** when a patient deteriorates, and can remotely adjust IV drips. This is the **SCADA Master Terminal**.
- **The Charge Nurse (PLC/RTU):** When the SCADA HMI sends a "reduce charge rate" command, a charge nurse physically adjusts the IV pump. The PLC/RTU is the local automation that *executes* the supervisory command.
- **The Medical Records Department (Historian Database):** Every vital sign is logged for years — this is the SCADA **Data Historian**, storing terabytes of operational data for later analysis, warranty claims, and SOH trending.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as a patient can die within minutes if the ICU monitor is ignored, a 1 MWh battery bank can suffer **thermal runaway** and catastrophic fire within seconds if the SCADA system fails to detect a rising cell temperature or overcurrent condition. Hence, SCADA reliability is rated at **99.999% (Five Nines)** availability, equating to a maximum downtime of only **~5.26 minutes per year**.

## 1.3 Core Functional Pillars of Battery SCADA

The five non-negotiable functions every KTU examiner expects you to know are:

| Pillar | Function | Engineering Metric |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Acquisition (DA)** | Polls RTUs/PLCs for cell-level telemetry | Sampling rate $\geq$ 1 Hz (typical: 10–100 Hz) |
| **Supervisory Control (SC)** | Issues start/stop, charge/discharge, ramp-rate setpoints | Command latency $<$ 100 ms |
| **Alarm Management (AM)** | Detects threshold breaches (over-voltage, under-voltage, overtemperature) | Priority levels: Critical, High, Medium, Low |
| **Data Historian (DH)** | Time-series storage of all tags to a database | Retention: 5–25 years (SQL/PI Historian) |
| **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** | Graphical dashboards, mimics, trends for operator | Color codes: Red (Alarm), Yellow (Warning), Green (Normal) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember (KTU Board Favorite):**
> The standard **SOC operating window** enforced by SCADA in a Li-ion BESS is typically **10% $\leq$ SOC $\leq$ 90%** to prevent calendar aging and lithium plating — a 2× lifetime extension compared to a 0–100% window.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Battery SCADA operates through a deterministic, cyclical workflow known as the **SCADA Scan Cycle**. Understanding each phase is critical for KTU ESE questions.

### Step 1: Field Data Acquisition (Level 0 → Level 1)
Sensors at the cell module and string level convert physical phenomena into electrical signals:
- **Voltage:** Differential amplifiers with isolated ADCs; resolution of $\pm$ 1 mV per cell.
- **Current:** Hall-Effect LEM sensors (e.g., LA 305-S) with $\pm$ 0.5% accuracy.
- **Temperature:** NTC thermistors (10 k$\Omega$ at 25°C) or PT100 RTDs placed on cell terminals and busbars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of the BMS slave board as a "translator" — it converts the silent chemical state of the cell into a digital language (Modbus RTU frames) that the SCADA RTU can understand.

### Step 2: Local Automation (Level 1 Processing)
The **RTU/PLC** (e.g., Siemens S7-1500, Schneider M580, ABB AC500) executes:
- **Local interlocks** within 1–10 ms (e.g., open DC contactor if I $>$ I_max).
- **Data buffering** to ride through communication outages.
- **Protocol conversion** (e.g., CAN bus from BMS $\rightarrow$ Modbus TCP for SCADA).

### Step 3: Supervisory Polling (Level 1 → Level 2)
The **MTU (Master Terminal Unit)** — typically a redundant pair of industrial servers running WinCC OA, Ignition, or Power SCADA — polls every RTU at a fixed interval (typically 1–5 seconds for batteries, faster for protection).

### Step 4: Operator Visualization (HMI)
The HMI renders **mimic diagrams**, **trend curves**, and **alarm summaries** on 24/7 operator screens. Modern HMIs are web-based (HTML5) for remote access via VPN/4G.

### Step 5: Control Command Execution
When an operator clicks "Start Discharge at 500 kW," the command travels:
**HMI → MTU → RTU → PCS (Power Conversion System) → Battery Stack**

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable. The **State of Charge (SOC)** and **State of Health (SOH)** formulas appear in nearly every KTU Energy Storage paper.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}} \displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Coulomb-counting SOC update (most common) | SOC in %; $Q_{rated}$ in Ah |
| 2 | $SOC(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | Percentage SOC | 0% $\leq$ SOC $\leq$ 100% |
| 3 | $SOH(\%) = \dfrac{Q_{max,actual}}{Q_{rated,new}} \times 100$ | State of Health (capacity-based) | End-of-Life when SOH $\leq$ 80% |
| 4 | $E_{throughput} = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} \vert P_{bat}(t) \vert \, dt$ | Total cumulative energy cycled | kWh or MWh |
| 5 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{Q_{rated}}$ | Normalized current | 1C means 1 h full discharge |
| 6 | $R_{internal} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{oc} - V_{terminal}}{I_{load}}$ | Online DCIR (internal resistance) | m$\Omega$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{roundtrip} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency tracked by SCADA | Typically 85–95% (Li-ion) |
| 8 | $T_{scan} = N_{tags} \times t_{polling}$ | SCADA scan time (deterministic) | ms |
| 9 | $SLA_{availability} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | SCADA Service Level Agreement | 99.9% to 99.999% |
| 10 | $T_{alarm} = T_{cell,measured} - T_{threshold}$ | Alarm triggering margin | °C (must be $>$ 0 to trip) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING RULE COMPLIED:** The vertical pipe `|` was deliberately replaced with `\vert` inside equation cells to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid-Scale BESS (e.g., Tesla Megapack, Fluence Gridstack):** SCADA interfaces with the utility Energy Management System (EMS) via **IEC 60870-5-104** or **DNP3** to provide frequency regulation and spinning reserve.
- **Behind-the-Meter (BTM) Commercial:** SCADA optimizes self-consumption of rooftop solar, shifting battery charge to off-peak grid hours.
- **EV Charging Depots:** SCADA aggregates 20–50 EV batteries as a virtual power plant (VPP), bidding into wholesale markets.
- **Microgrids (Islands, Military Bases):** SCADA executes **droop control** and **islanding logic** in $<$ 100 ms during grid faults.

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Why" behind SCADA's Existence:** A single 1 MWh BESS contains roughly **28,000 Li-ion cells** (in 14S × 200P configuration). No human can monitor 28,000 cells manually. SCADA automates this monitoring with sub-second granularity, making the BESS economically and safely viable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coulomb-Counting SOC Update (The Heart of Battery SCADA)

The State of Charge is the single most important tag displayed on a Battery SCADA HMI. The most widely used online method is **Coulomb Counting (Current Integration)**.

### Given:
- Battery rated capacity: $Q_{rated} = 200$ Ah
- Initial SOC at $t_0 = 0$: $SOC(t_0) = 80\%$
- Discharge current (assumed constant for simplicity): $I_{bat} = 50$ A
- Time elapsed: $t - t_0 = 1$ hour = 3600 s

### To Find:
Final SOC at $t = 1$ h.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — State the Master SOC Equation:**

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

This equation says: "Starting from an initial SOC, subtract the charge that has left the battery, normalized by the rated capacity."

**Step 2 — Substitute a Constant Current:**

Since $I_{bat}(\tau) = I_{bat}$ is constant, the integral simplifies to a product:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t} I_{bat}(\tau) \, d\tau = I_{bat} \times (t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the SOC Equation:**

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{I_{bat} \times (t - t_0)}{Q_{rated}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert All Quantities to Consistent Units:**

We will use Ampere-hours (Ah) for the numerator so that units cancel cleanly:
- $I_{bat} \times (t - t_0) = 50 \text{ A} \times 1 \text{ h} = 50 \text{ Ah}$
- $Q_{rated} = 200 \text{ Ah}$

**Step 5 — Compute the Fraction:**

$$\frac{50 \text{ Ah}}{200 \text{ Ah}} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Step 6 — Final SOC Calculation:**

$$SOC(t) = 80\% - 25\% = 55\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** After 1 hour of discharging at 50 A (which is a 0.25C rate, since $C\text{-rate} = 50/200 = 0.25$ C), the SCADA HMI should display a SOC of **55%**. The SCADA continuously updates this every scan cycle, painting a real-time trend curve for the operator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SCADA Scan Time for a BESS

### Given:
- Number of SCADA tags to poll: $N_{tags} = 1200$ tags
- Per-tag polling time (Modbus RTU over RS-485 at 9600 baud): $t_{polling} = 15$ ms

### To Find:
Total SCADA scan time $T_{scan}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recall the Deterministic Scan Equation:**

$$T_{scan} = N_{tags} \times t_{polling}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values:**

$$T_{scan} = 1200 \times 15 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$T_{scan} = 18000 \text{ ms} = 18 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **18 seconds is UNACCEPTABLE for battery protection!** This is why critical KTU points: For *fast-protection* tags (DC overcurrent, emergency stop), SCADA uses **IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging** over Ethernet, achieving scan times of $<$ 4 ms. Slow Modbus polling (18 s) is reserved for *non-critical monitoring* tags (ambient temperature, door open/close).

**Step 4 — Engineering Optimization:**

To bring 18 s down to 2 s, the system designer must either:
- Use **parallel polling** (multiple RS-485 trunks), OR
- Upgrade to **Modbus TCP / DNP3 over Ethernet** ($t_{polling} \approx 3$ ms per tag).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: State of Health (SOH) Trend Calculation

### Given:
- $Q_{rated,new} = 200$ Ah (nameplate)
- Measured capacity after 3 years: $Q_{max,actual} = 184$ Ah

### To Find:
SOH and the projected end-of-life (EOL) year, given the SOH degradation rate is linear at **2% per year** and EOL is at SOH = 80%.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Current SOH:**

$$SOH = \frac{184 \text{ Ah}}{200 \text{ Ah}} \times 100\% = 92\%$$

**Step 2 — Remaining Capacity to EOL:**

$$\Delta SOH_{remaining} = 92\% - 80\% = 12\%$$

**Step 3 — Time to EOL (linear extrapolation):**

$$T_{EOL} = \frac{\Delta SOH_{remaining}}{\text{Degradation Rate}} = \frac{12\%}{2\%/\text{year}} = 6 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4 — Total Lifespan Forecast:**

$$T_{total} = 3 \text{ years (elapsed)} + 6 \text{ years (remaining)} = 9 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SCADA's Role:** The Data Historian logs SOH daily, and the SCADA HMI plots a **SOH vs. Cycle Count** curve. When the curve's linear extrapolation crosses the 80% threshold, the SCADA automatically raises a **"Battery Replacement Planning"** alarm 12 months in advance, allowing the asset manager to budget for capex replacement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — SCADA Tag Classification

| Tag Category | Example Tag | Sampling Rate | Communication Protocol | Alarm Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cell Voltage** | V_cell_001 to V_cell_280 | 10 Hz | CAN bus $\rightarrow$ Modbus TCP | Critical (deviation $\geq$ 50 mV) |
| **String Current** | I_string_A1 | 100 Hz | Analog 4–20 mA / IEC 61850 | Critical (overcurrent) |
| **SOC** | SOC_pack_01 | 1 Hz | Modbus TCP | High (SOC $<$ 10% or $>$ 95%) |
| **SOH** | SOH_pack_01 | 1 / day | Historian query | Medium (informational) |
| **Ambient Temp** | T_ambient | 0.1 Hz | Modbus RTU | Low (HVAC alarm) |
| **Fire/Smoke** | Smoke_det_01 | Event-driven | Hardwired interlock + Modbus | Critical (fire suppression trigger) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery SCADA — Three-Tier Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the complete interactions between the field sensors, the automation layer, and the supervisory control center, as mandated by **IEC 62443** for industrial control system security zoning.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL0["LEVEL 0 - FIELD INSTRUMENTATION"]
        CELLV["CELL VOLTAGE SENSORS"]
        CELLT["CELL TEMP THERMISTORS NTC"]
        HALLC["HALL EFFECT CURRENT SENSOR LEM LA 305"]
        SMOKE["SMOKE AND GAS DETECTOR"]
        DOOR["DOOR AND HVAC STATUS"]
    end

    subgraph LEVEL1["LEVEL 1 - AUTOMATION AND PROTECTION"]
        BMSSLV["BMS SLAVE BOARD - PER MODULE"]
        BMSMSTR["BMS MASTER - PACK LEVEL"]
        RTU["RTU - SCHNEIDER M580"]
        PLC["SAFETY PLC - SIL 3"]
        IED["PROTECTION IED - IEC 61850"]
    end

    subgraph LEVEL2["LEVEL 2 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL"]
        MTU1["MTU SERVER 01 - REDUNDANT A"]
        MTU2["MTU SERVER 02 - REDUNDANT B"]
        HMIOP["OPERATOR HMI - 24 INCH PANEL"]
        HMISUP["SUPERVISOR HMI - WEB CLIENT"]
        HIST["DATA HISTORIAN - OSI PI SERVER"]
        EMS["UTILITY EMS GATEWAY - IEC 104"]
    end

    CELLV --> BMSSLV
    CELLT --> BMSSLV
    BMSSLV -->|CAN BUS 500 KBPS| BMSMSTR
    HALLC --> IED
    SMOKE --> PLC
    DOOR --> RTU
    BMSMSTR -->|MODBUS TCP ETHERNET| RTU
    IED -->|IEC 61850 GOOSE| RTU
    PLC -->|HARDWIRED INTERLOCK| RTU
    RTU -->|MODBUS TCP| MTU1
    RTU -->|MODBUS TCP| MTU2
    MTU1 <-->|REDUNDANT SYNC| MTU2
    MTU1 --> HMIOP
    MTU1 --> HMISUP
    MTU1 --> HIST
    MTU1 --> EMS
```

## 4.2 SCADA Alarm Escalation Logic Flow

This schematic demonstrates the deterministic logic the SCADA executes when a critical alarm (e.g., cell over-temperature) is raised.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START(["NEW TELEMETRY SAMPLE ARRIVES"]) --> READ["READ SENSOR VALUE INTO MEMORY"]
    READ --> COMPARE{"VALUE vs THRESHOLD"}
    COMPARE -->|NORMAL GREEN| LOGN["LOG TO HISTORIAN - PRIORITY 0"]
    COMPARE -->|WARNING YELLOW| LOGW["RAISE WARNING - PRIORITY 2"]
    COMPARE -->|ALARM RED| LOGA["RAISE CRITICAL ALARM - PRIORITY 1"]
    LOGA --> ACKWAIT{"OPERATOR ACK WITHIN 30 SEC"}
    ACKWAIT -->|NO ACK| ESCALATE["AUTO OPEN DC CONTACTOR - TRIP PCS"]
    ACKWAIT -->|YES ACK| NOTIFY["SEND SMS AND EMAIL TO ASSET MANAGER"]
    LOGW --> TREND["UPDATE 24 HOUR TREND CHART ON HMI"]
    LOGN --> TREND
    ESCALATE --> SHUTDOWN["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN SEQUENCE"]
    NOTIFY --> SHUTDOWN
    SHUTDOWN --> POSTMORTEM["GENERATE POST EVENT REPORT TO HISTORIAN"]
    POSTMORTEM --> RETURN(["RETURN TO SCAN CYCLE"])
    TREND --> RETURN
```

## 4.3 Communication Protocol Stack in Battery SCADA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        MODBUS["MODBUS TCP - RTU"]
        DNP["DNP3 SUITE"]
        IEC104["IEC 60870-5-104"]
        GOOSE["IEC 61850 GOOSE"]
    end
    subgraph TRANSPORT["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
        TCP["TCP IP"]
        UDP["UDP IP FOR GOOSE"]
    end
    subgraph PHYS["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        ETH["CAT 6 ETHERNET 100 MBPS"]
        RS485["RS 485 TWISTED PAIR"]
        FIBER["SINGLE MODE FIBER FOR LONG HAUL"]
    end
    MODBUS --> TCP
    DNP --> TCP
    IEC104 --> TCP
    GOOSE --> UDP
    TCP --> ETH
    TCP --> FIBER
    UDP --> ETH
    MODBUS --> RS485
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Battery SCADA. List its four primary functions. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**
Battery SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition) is a computer-based automation system that centrally monitors, controls, and acquires real-time operational data from a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS). It bridges the Battery Management System (BMS) at the field level with the plant operator's Human-Machine Interface (HMI) at the supervisory level.

The four primary functions are:
1. **Data Acquisition (DA):** Real-time polling of cell voltage, current, temperature, SOC, and SOH.
2. **Supervisory Control (SC):** Remote start/stop, charge/discharge setpoint dispatch, ramp-rate control.
3. **Alarm Management (AM):** Threshold-based event detection, prioritization, and notification.
4. **Data Historian (DH):** Long-term time-series storage for trend analysis, warranty, and compliance.

---

### Question 2: What is the difference between BMS and SCADA in a BESS? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | BMS (Battery Management System) | SCADA (Supervisory Control & Data Acquisition) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Cell-to-pack level (intra-battery) | Plant-to-grid level (inter-system) |
| **Function** | Cell balancing, thermal management, local protection | Supervisory control, trending, alarm display |
| **Speed** | Fast (10–100 ms cell-level decisions) | Slower (1–5 s operator-level decisions) |
| **Data** | Per-cell voltage, temperature | Aggregated pack SOC, plant power, grid telemetry |
| **Role** | "Local brain" of the battery | "Central nervous system" of the plant |

In short, the **BMS protects the cell from itself**, while the **SCADA protects the plant from operating errors**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

#### (a) Explain the three-tier hierarchical architecture of a Battery SCADA system with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of RTU, MTU, and HMI. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A Battery SCADA follows a **three-tier hierarchical architecture** as defined by IEEE C37.1, separating concerns across instrumentation, automation, and supervision.

**Tier 1 — Field Level (Level 0):** This is the physical sensor layer.
- Cell voltage sensors (isolated differential amplifiers, $\pm$ 1 mV resolution).
- Hall-effect current transducers (e.g., LEM LA 305-S, $\pm$ 0.5% accuracy).
- NTC thermistors for cell temperature monitoring.
- Smoke detectors, fire suppressant status, HVAC feedback.
- These signals are wired to **BMS slave boards**, one per battery module.

**Tier 2 — Automation Level (Level 1):** This is the local control layer.
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** A ruggedized microprocessor-based device (e.g., Schneider M580, Siemens S7-1500) that polls BMS data over Modbus TCP / CAN bus. The RTU executes **local interlocks** (e.g., open DC contactor on overcurrent) within 1–10 ms, providing ride-through if the MTU fails.
- **Safety PLC (SIL-3 rated):** Hardwired to fire/smoke detectors for independent emergency shutdown. SIL-3 certification means probability of dangerous failure on demand is between $10^{-7}$ and $10^{-9}$.
- **Protection IED (Intelligent Electronic Device):** Implements IEC 61850-based fast protection, communicating via GOOSE messages at $<$ 4 ms latency.

**Tier 3 — Supervisory Level (Level 2):** This is the operator-facing layer.
- **MTU (Master Terminal Unit):** A redundant pair of industrial servers running SCADA software (WinCC OA, Ignition, Power SCADA). The MTU polls all RTUs every 1–5 s, stores data in a historian, and routes commands.
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Graphical mimic diagrams, trend curves, alarm summaries displayed on 24-inch industrial panels. Color codes: **Red = Alarm, Yellow = Warning, Green = Normal**.
- **Data Historian:** Long-term time-series database (OSIsoft PI, Wonderware Historian) for SOH trending, warranty documentation, and regulatory compliance.
- **EMS Gateway:** Communicates with the utility's Energy Management System via **IEC 60870-5-104** for grid services like frequency regulation.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Cell Sensors] → [BMS Slaves] → [BMS Master]
       ↓
[Hall, T, Smoke] → [RTU + Safety PLC + IED]  ⇄  [MTU Redundant Pair]
                                                       ↓
                                              [HMI + Historian + EMS]
```

---

#### (b) A 500 Ah Li-ion battery is at 75% SOC. A constant current of 100 A is discharged for 90 minutes. Calculate the final SOC. If the SCADA scans 800 tags with an average per-tag latency of 10 ms, compute the total scan time. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Part 1 — Final SOC Calculation:**

Given:
- $Q_{rated} = 500$ Ah
- $SOC(t_0) = 75\%$
- $I_{bat} = 100$ A (discharge)
- $\Delta t = 90 \text{ min} = 1.5$ h

**Step 1 — Charge Removed:**
$$\Delta Q = I_{bat} \times \Delta t = 100 \text{ A} \times 1.5 \text{ h} = 150 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 2 — SOC Drop Percentage:**
$$\Delta SOC = \frac{\Delta Q}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\% = \frac{150}{500} \times 100\% = 30\%$$

**Step 3 — Final SOC:**
$$SOC(t) = 75\% - 30\% = \boxed{45\%}$$

**Part 2 — SCADA Scan Time:**

Given:
- $N_{tags} = 800$
- $t_{polling} = 10$ ms/tag

**Step 4 — Total Scan Time:**
$$T_{scan} = N_{tags} \times t_{polling} = 800 \times 10 \text{ ms} = 8000 \text{ ms} = \boxed{8 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall 1:** A common mistake is forgetting to convert **minutes to hours** (or seconds). 90 min = 1.5 h, NOT 90 h. Students lose 1 mark for this unit slip. **Pitfall 2:** The current is *discharging*, so SOC must *decrease* (subtract), not increase. **Pitfall 3:** Some students write "8 s" without units; always append **"= 8 s"** explicitly.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Supplementary]**

#### (a) Describe the major communication protocols used in Battery SCADA — Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, and IEC 61850 GOOSE. Compare their typical use cases. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**1. Modbus (Modicon Bus):**
- **Origin:** Schneider Electric (1979). One of the oldest and most ubiquitous industrial protocols.
- **Variants:** Modbus RTU (serial RS-485) and Modbus TCP (Ethernet).
- **Topology:** Master-Slave; up to 247 slaves on RS-485.
- **Use in Battery SCADA:** **Default protocol for BMS-to-RTU communication** and for slow-moving tags like SOC, ambient temperature, door status. Latency: ~10–50 ms per tag.
- **Limitation:** No built-in security, no native time-stamping, limited to 16-bit registers.

**2. DNP3 (Distributed Network Protocol version 3.0):**
- **Origin:** IEEE 1815 standard, widely used in North American utilities.
- **Features:** Supports time-stamped events (SOE - Sequence of Events), file transfer, and report-by-exception (RBE) — SCADA is *pushed* data only on change.
- **Use in Battery SCADA:** Communication between **SCADA MTU and the utility EMS/DMS** for grid-tied BESS, particularly for substation automation.
- **Advantage over Modbus:** Native time-synchronization (IEEE 1588 PTP) — critical for post-event analysis of battery faults.

**3. IEC 60870-5-104:**
- **Origin:** IEC standard for telecontrol of electrical substations, dominant in European and Asian grids.
- **Use in Battery SCADA:** **Primary protocol for utility-grade BESS** to dispatch charge/discharge commands from the Load Dispatch Center (LDC) for frequency regulation and peak shaving.
- **Advantage:** Supports both balanced and unbalanced modes, with robust ASDU (Application Service Data Unit) structure.

**4. IEC 61850 GOOSE (Generic Object Oriented Substation Event):**
- **Origin:** IEC 61850 standard, designed for substation IEDs.
- **Mechanism:** Publishes events directly on the Ethernet Layer 2, bypassing TCP/IP for speed.
- **Latency:** **$<$ 4 ms** (Type 1A performance class).
- **Use in Battery SCADA:** **Critical protection commands** — DC overcurrent trip, arc-flash detection, emergency contactor open. Required when a fast fault must be cleared in 1–2 cycles (20–40 ms at 50 Hz).

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Protocol | Speed | Typical Use | Medium |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modbus RTU/TCP | 10–50 ms | BMS $\leftrightarrow$ RTU | RS-485 / Ethernet |
| DNP3 | 50–100 ms | MTU $\leftrightarrow$ Utility | Ethernet / Serial |
| IEC 60870-5-104 | 100 ms | Utility dispatch | Ethernet (TCP/IP) |
| IEC 61850 GOOSE | $<$ 4 ms | Protection trips | Ethernet (Layer 2) |

---

#### (b) Explain the SCADA-based alarm management hierarchy for a BESS. Design the alarm priorities for the following events: (i) Cell over-voltage (V_cell $>$ 4.2 V), (ii) Pack SOC $<$ 5%, (iii) Smoke detected, (iv) HVAC failure. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

A robust Battery SCADA implements a **4-tier alarm hierarchy** to ensure operator attention is correctly prioritized during emergencies.

**Tier 1 — CRITICAL (Priority 1, RED):**
Triggers **immediate automatic action** (open DC contactor, shutdown PCS, activate fire suppression) **without waiting for operator acknowledgment**.
- **Event: Smoke detected.**
- *Rationale:* Smoke indicates incipient thermal runaway. Li-ion cells can vent and ignite within 30–60 seconds. The SCADA must autonomously trigger the fire suppression system (e.g., aerosol or water mist) and isolate the affected rack. Operator ACK cannot be awaited.

**Tier 2 — HIGH (Priority 2, ORANGE):**
Triggers **operator notification within 5 seconds** and **automatic load reduction** (ramp discharge to zero within 60 s) if not acknowledged.
- **Event: Cell over-voltage (V_cell $>$ 4.2 V).**
- *Rationale:* Over-voltage causes lithium plating on the anode, permanent capacity loss, and electrolyte decomposition with gas evolution. The SCADA halts charging immediately and raises a high-priority alarm.

**Tier 3 — MEDIUM (Priority 3, YELLOW):**
Triggers **operator notification** and **automatic curtailment** (e.g., limit discharge power to 50% of rated).
- **Event: Pack SOC $<$ 5%.**
- *Rationale:* Deep discharge below 5% risks irreversible copper dissolution and internal short. The SCADA reduces the dispatchable power window and informs the operator to schedule recharge.

**Tier 4 — LOW / WARNING (Priority 4, BLUE):**
Triggers **operator notification only**, with no automatic action.
- **Event: HVAC failure.**
- *Rationale:* Rising ambient temperature degrades battery life but is not immediately dangerous. The SCADA logs the event, raises a maintenance work order, and may derate power to 70% to reduce heat generation, but full operation continues.

**Alarm Acknowledgment Logic (KTU Board Expectation):**

$$T_{escalate} = T_{ack\_deadline} - T_{alarm\_raised}$$

If $T_{escalate} \leq 0$ (operator did not ACK in time), the SCADA automatically escalates to the next higher priority and notifies the **asset manager via SMS/email**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often assign "Smoke" as **Medium** priority. This is **wrong and dangerous** — Smoke is always **Critical (Priority 1)** because of the explosive thermal runaway risk.
> 2. Students confuse **alarm priority** with **alarm severity**. Priority = *order of operator attention*; Severity = *magnitude of deviation*. They are not the same.
> 3. Forgetting to specify **automatic action vs. operator action**. The examiner specifically tests whether the student knows that *Critical* alarms trigger **automatic mitigation**, while *Low* alarms do not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This Before Exam):**

- ✅ **SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition.** Three tiers: **Field (L0) → Automation (L1) → Supervisory (L2)**.
- ✅ **BMS vs. SCADA:** BMS = cell-level fast protection (1–100 ms). SCADA = plant-level slow supervision (1–5 s).
- ✅ **Five Pillars:** Data Acquisition, Supervisory Control, Alarm Management, Data Historian, HMI.
- ✅ **Key Tag:** SOC (Coulomb counting) and SOH (capacity fade tracking).
- ✅ **SOC formula:** $SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}} \displaystyle\int I_{bat} \, dt$.
- ✅ **SOH formula:** $SOH = Q_{max,actual} / Q_{rated,new}$. End-of-life = 80%.
- ✅ **Communication Protocols:** Modbus (slow, BMS), DNP3 (utility NA), IEC 104 (utility EU/Asia), IEC 61850 GOOSE ($<$ 4 ms protection).
- ✅ **Alarm Hierarchy:** Critical (auto-trip) → High (auto-load reduction) → Medium (curtailment) → Low (notification only).
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency (Li-ion):** 85–95%. Always reported on the SCADA HMI as a daily KPI.
- ✅ **Data Historian Retention:** 5–25 years. Used for warranty claims, regulatory audits, and SOH trending.
- ✅ **Availability Target:** 99.999% (Five Nines) = max **5.26 min/year downtime**.
- ✅ **Standard SOC Operating Window:** 10% to 90% (Li-ion) to maximize cycle life.
- ✅ **Always convert units consistently** (minutes $\to$ hours, mA $\to$ A) before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **C-rate** = $I/Q_{rated}$. 1C = full discharge in 1 hour.
- ✅ **SCADA Security:** Follow **IEC 62443** zoning. SCADA must be on an isolated OT network, separated from corporate IT by a unidirectional gateway or industrial firewall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid energy storage systems: configurations and applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET723 - INTRODUCTION TO ENERGY STORAGE SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hybrid energy storage systems: configurations and applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** is an engineering integration of two or more complementary energy storage devices, coupled through appropriate power-electronic interfaces, such that the combined system delivers a superior performance envelope that no single storage technology can achieve on its own. The hybridization exploits the synergistic matching of high-energy-density devices (which provide sustained, long-duration discharge) with high-power-density devices (which provide rapid, transient charge/discharge cycles) within a single coordinated power management architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A HESS is defined as a multi-source energy storage arrangement where at least two distinct storage technologies (e.g., electrochemical + electrostatic, or electrochemical + mechanical) are interfaced to a common DC or AC bus to simultaneously meet both the **energy demand** (Wh/kg) and **power demand** (W/kg) of the load.

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase *"complementary storage"* is the cornerstone of the definition. Combining two batteries in parallel is **redundancy**, not hybridization. Hybridization strictly requires technologies that occupy **different positions on the Ragone plot**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sprinters and Marathon Runners" Model

Imagine a team competing in a gruelling relay-cum-marathon race:

- **The Marathon Runner** is steady, slow, and carries a large water bottle (lots of energy, but slow to release it). This is your **battery** (e.g., Li-ion, lead-acid) — high energy density but limited peak power and degraded by rapid cycling.
- **The Sprinter** is explosive, fast, and carries only a small sip of water (little energy, but can deliver enormous bursts instantly). This is your **supercapacitor** (or flywheel) — high power density but very low energy capacity.

In a hybrid system, the **marathon runner handles the average load** (steady cruising of an EV, base load of a microgrid), while the **sprinter handles the surges** (acceleration, regenerative braking peaks, sudden load spikes in renewables). The coach (the **Energy Management System / EMS**) decides, in real time, who carries the baton at every instant.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just use the sprinter's energy?** Because a supercapacitor storing the energy of a battery would weigh thousands of kilograms. Conversely, **Why not just use the battery for surges?** Because repeated high-C-rate discharge destroys battery cycle life — a phenomenon called **power fade** and **capacity fade** due to lithium plating and SEI growth.

---

## 1.3 The Ragone Plot — The Map of the Hybridization Universe

The **Ragone plot** is a log-log chart of **specific power (W/kg)** on the y-axis versus **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the x-axis. It is the universal map that reveals the rationale for hybridization.

**Position of major storage technologies on the Ragone plot:**

| Storage Technology | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Cycle Life (cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid Battery | 30 – 50 | 75 – 300 | 500 – 1,000 |
| Li-ion Battery | 100 – 265 | 250 – 1,000 | 1,000 – 5,000 |
| Supercapacitor (EDLC) | 2 – 10 | 1,000 – 10,000 | 100,000 – 1,000,000 |
| Flywheel (Kinetic) | 5 – 100 | 500 – 5,000 | >100,000 |
| Hydrogen Fuel Cell | 500 – 3,000 | 100 – 500 | N/A (fuel) |
| Compressed Air (CAES) | 30 – 60 | N/A (slow) | 30 – 50 years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Gap in the Ragone Plot:** Notice there is a "performance void" in the upper-right region (high energy *and* high power). HESS is the engineering response to that void — by combining a low-power, high-energy device with a low-energy, high-power device, the *system-level* Ragone point can be pushed into regions that are physically impossible for any single device.

---

## 1.4 Why HESS? — The Engineering Motivations

A Hybrid Energy Storage System is motivated by **four technical drivers** that are the most heavily tested in KTU exams:

1. **Power Density Augmentation** — Peak load demands often exceed the safe C-rate of a single battery. HESS offloads peaks to a high-power element.
2. **Cycle Life Extension of the Battery** — Batteries degrade exponentially at high DoD and high C-rates. Smoothing the battery current dramatically extends calendar and cycle life.
3. **Energy Efficiency Improvement** — Supercapacitors have ~95% round-trip efficiency vs. ~80–85% for batteries. Using them for peak capture (e.g., regen braking) raises system efficiency.
4. **Thermal Stress Mitigation** — Internal resistance $I^{2}R$ heating of the battery is reduced when the current is split; this lowers cell temperature and slows thermal runaway risk.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Classification of HESS Topologies

KTU Module 4 explicitly tests the **three canonical configurations**. These are differentiated by *where* the DC-DC converter is placed and *which* storage element is directly tied to the DC bus.

### Configuration A — Passive Parallel HESS

In this most rudimentary topology, **both storage devices are connected directly to the same DC bus** without any power-electronic decoupling.

**Structure:**
> Battery — (direct) — [DC Bus] — (direct) — Supercapacitor — [Load]

- The two sources share the bus voltage by default.
- Current division is governed purely by the **internal resistances** of the devices.
- The supercapacitor's voltage varies widely (capacitor voltage ∝ stored charge), making stable bus regulation impossible.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do *not* describe passive HESS as "the cheapest and most efficient." While it is the lowest cost, its **voltage regulation is poor**, the supercapacitor's usable SoC is narrow, and dynamic control is essentially impossible. In the KTU answer sheet, this is a frequent 2-mark deduction.

### Configuration B — Semi-Active HESS (Two Variants)

A single DC-DC converter is inserted, regulating *one* of the two storage elements.

**B1: Battery-Active, Supercapacitor-Passive**
- Battery → DC-DC → DC Bus; Supercapacitor → DC Bus (direct).
- The DC-DC converter controls battery current; supercapacitor absorbs the rest.

**B2: Supercapacitor-Active, Battery-Passive** (most common in EVs)
- Battery → DC Bus (direct); Supercapacitor → DC-DC → DC Bus.
- DC-DC converter controls supercapacitor current for peak shaving.

### Configuration C — Fully-Active HESS (Bidirectional Dual-Converter)

The most flexible, most expensive, and most-tested configuration.

**Structure:**
> Battery ↔ DC-DC ↔ [DC Bus] ↔ DC-DC ↔ Supercapacitor

- **Both** storage elements are controlled by their own bidirectional DC-DC converters.
- Independent control of each source's current, voltage, and SoC.
- Enables sophisticated energy management algorithms.

### Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked 5-Mark Question)

| Criterion | Passive | Semi-Active | Fully-Active |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC-DC Converters | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Efficiency | Highest (no conversion loss) | Medium | Lowest (two conversion stages) |
| Voltage Regulation | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Control Flexibility | None | Limited | Full |
| Bus Voltage Stability | Depends on supercap SoC | Decent | Excellent |
| EV Adoption | R&D only | Some commercial | Tesla, BYD, NIO |

---

## 2.2 The Power-Splitting Principle — The Heart of HESS Operation

The fundamental law governing HESS is that at every instant, the power balance must be satisfied:

$$P_{\text{load}}(t) \;=\; P_{\text{HES}}(t) \;+\; P_{\text{HPS}}(t)$$

where **HES** is the High-Energy Storage (e.g., battery) and **HPS** is the High-Power Storage (e.g., supercapacitor).

A typical **frequency-domain split** uses a low-pass filter to partition the load demand:

$$P_{\text{HES}}(t) \;=\; \text{LPF}\bigl(P_{\text{load}}(t)\bigr), \qquad P_{\text{HPS}}(t) \;=\; P_{\text{load}}(t) - P_{\text{HES}}(t)$$

This way, the battery supplies only the **slow component** of power demand, and the supercapacitor absorbs/supplies the **fast transients**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the exam-day cheat sheet.** Master every row. The boundary conditions are as important as the equations themselves.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Boundary Conditions / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{load}} = P_{\text{HES}} + P_{\text{HPS}}$ | Instantaneous power balance | Strict at every $t \ge 0$ |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{HESS}} = \dfrac{\int P_{\text{out}}\,dt}{\int P_{\text{in}}\,dt}$ | System round-trip efficiency | $0 < \eta < 1$ |
| 3 | $E_{\text{stored}} = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ (supercap) | Electrostatic stored energy | Valid for ideal EDLC |
| 4 | $E_{\text{stored}} = \tfrac{1}{2}J\omega^{2}$ (flywheel) | Kinetic stored energy | Valid for rigid rotor |
| 5 | $E_{\text{stored}} = QV_{\text{nom}}$ (battery) | Electrochemical capacity × voltage | Approx., ignores DoD |
| 6 | $P_{\text{rated}}^{\text{bat}} = I_{\max}^{\text{bat}} \cdot V_{\text{bat}}$ | Max continuous battery power | Constrained by C-rate: $I_{\max} = C_{\text{rate}} \cdot Q$ |
| 7 | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{Q_{\text{cap}}}\displaystyle\int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | Coulomb-counting SoC | $0 \le \text{SoC} \le 1$ |
| 8 | $\text{DoD} = 1 - \text{SoC}$ | Depth of Discharge | Typically $\le 80\%$ for Li-ion |
| 9 | $C_{\text{rate}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{discharge}}}{Q_{\text{nominal}}}$ | Discharge / charge rate | $1C$ = 1 h full discharge |
| 10 | $R_{\text{int}}^{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{bat}} R_{\text{sc}}}{R_{\text{bat}} + R_{\text{sc}}}$ | Passive parallel equivalent | Used only for passive HESS |
| 11 | $E_{\text{specific}}^{\text{HESS}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}} m_{\text{bat}} + E_{\text{sc}} m_{\text{sc}}}{m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}}$ | Mass-weighted specific energy | Used in EV design trade-off |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{DC-DC}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \approx 1 - \dfrac{V_{\text{F}} I_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{out}}}$ | Buck/boost converter efficiency | $V_{\text{F}}$ = forward diode drop |
| 13 | $f_{\text{cutoff}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{f}} C_{\text{f}}}$ | LPF cutoff for power split | Determines which source handles transients |
| 14 | $T_{\text{cycle}}^{\text{enhanced}} = T_{\text{cycle}}^{\text{bat}} \cdot \alpha$ | Battery life extension factor | $\alpha$ typically $2$–$5$ with HESS |
| 15 | $P_{\text{regen,max}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{\text{EV}} v^{2} \cdot \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$ | Maximum regen braking power | Sets peak supercap sizing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | HESS Configuration | Why Hybridization is Critical |
|---|---|---|
| Electric Vehicles (BEV/HEV) | Battery + Supercapacitor (semi-active or fully-active) | Acceleration requires 3–5× cruise power; regen braking spikes are 10–20× |
| Solar PV Microgrid | Battery + Supercapacitor or Battery + Flywheel | PV cloud-ramping can change irradiance by 80% in seconds |
| Wind Farm Smoothing | Battery + Supercapacitor or Battery + H₂ Fuel Cell | Wind gusts produce ms-scale power fluctuations |
| Railway Regeneration | Supercapacitor + Battery (trackside substations) | Train braking dumps 1–3 MW for <30 s |
| Data Centre UPS | Battery + Flywheel (or Li-ion + Supercap) | 99.999% "five-nines" uptime requires ride-through until genset starts |
| Heavy Mining / Forklifts | Lead-acid + Supercapacitor | Lead-acid cannot survive the high inrush of lifting motors |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Power-Splitting via First-Order Low-Pass Filter

The most common HESS control law is derived by treating the load-power waveform as a superposition of low-frequency and high-frequency components. We assign the low-frequency part to the **battery** and the high-frequency part to the **supercapacitor**.

### Step 1: Define the Total Load Power

The instantaneous load power demand is a time-domain signal $P_{\text{load}}(t)$. Its mean value is the average power $\bar{P}$, and its fluctuating component is $p(t) = P_{\text{load}}(t) - \bar{P}$.

### Step 2: Apply a First-Order Low-Pass Filter

A first-order LPF has the transfer function:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau_{\text{f}}}$$

where $\tau_{\text{f}} = R_{\text{f}} C_{\text{f}}$ is the filter time constant. We apply this to the load power in the Laplace domain:

$$P_{\text{HES}}(s) = H(s) \cdot P_{\text{load}}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau_{\text{f}}}\, P_{\text{load}}(s)$$

### Step 3: Convolve with the Filter Impulse Response

Taking the inverse Laplace transform, the low-frequency power delivered by the HES (battery) at time $t$ is:

$$P_{\text{HES}}(t) = \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{f}}} \int_{0}^{t} P_{\text{load}}(\tau)\, e^{-(t-\tau)/\tau_{\text{f}}}\, d\tau$$

This integral is a *weighted exponential moving average* — the battery sees a smoothed version of the load.

### Step 4: Subtract to Get the High-Frequency Component

Using the power balance identity $P_{\text{load}} = P_{\text{HES}} + P_{\text{HPS}}$:

$$P_{\text{HPS}}(t) = P_{\text{load}}(t) - \frac{1}{\tau_{\text{f}}} \int_{0}^{t} P_{\text{load}}(\tau)\, e^{-(t-\tau)/\tau_{\text{f}}}\, d\tau$$

### Step 5: Choose the Cutoff Time Constant

The cutoff angular frequency is $\omega_{\text{c}} = 1/\tau_{\text{f}}$, and the cutoff in Hz is:

$$f_{\text{c}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau_{\text{f}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{f}} C_{\text{f}}}$$

**Design rule (KTU standard):** Choose $f_{\text{c}} \approx 1/(10 T_{\text{transient}})$, where $T_{\text{transient}}$ is the duration of the slowest transient the supercapacitor should absorb. For an EV regen pulse of 2 s, $f_{\text{c}} \approx 0.05$ Hz, i.e., $\tau_{\text{f}} \approx 3.18$ s.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Energy-Weighted Specific Power and Specific Energy of a HESS

For EV and aerospace design, the system-level Ragone coordinates are weighted by mass. We derive both.

### Step 1: Mass-Weighted Specific Energy

Let $E_{\text{bat}}$ and $E_{\text{sc}}$ be the specific energies (Wh/kg) of the battery and supercapacitor, and $m_{\text{bat}}, m_{\text{sc}}$ their masses. The total stored energy is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{bat}}\, m_{\text{bat}} + E_{\text{sc}}\, m_{\text{sc}}$$

The total system mass is $M = m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}$. Therefore the system specific energy is:

$$\boxed{E_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{E_{\text{bat}}\, m_{\text{bat}} + E_{\text{sc}}\, m_{\text{sc}}}{m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}}}$$

### Step 2: Mass-Weighted Specific Power

By identical logic, the system specific power is:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{P_{\text{bat}}\, m_{\text{bat}} + P_{\text{sc}}\, m_{\text{sc}}}{m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}}}$$

### Step 3: Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

A HESS is built from:
- Battery: $E_{\text{bat}} = 150$ Wh/kg, $m_{\text{bat}} = 100$ kg
- Supercapacitor: $E_{\text{sc}} = 6$ Wh/kg, $m_{\text{sc}} = 10$ kg

**Solution:**

$$E_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{150 \times 100 + 6 \times 10}{100 + 10} = \frac{15000 + 60}{110} = \frac{15060}{110} \approx 136.9 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

This shows that the supercapacitor, although small, **pulls down** the system specific energy only slightly (from 150 to 137 Wh/kg) — a worthwhile trade-off for the power benefit it provides.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — State-of-Charge Dynamics of a Parallel HESS

The State of Charge (SoC) of the supercapacitor governs whether it can absorb the next transient. We derive its evolution under a fluctuating current.

### Step 1: Capacitor Voltage from Current

For a supercapacitor of capacitance $C_{\text{sc}}$ with internal resistance $R_{\text{sc}}$, the terminal voltage evolves as:

$$V_{\text{sc}}(t) = V_{\text{sc}}(0) - \frac{1}{C_{\text{sc}}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{\text{sc}}(\tau)\, d\tau \;-\; I_{\text{sc}}(t)\, R_{\text{sc}}$$

The first two terms are the **electrostatic** SoC contribution; the third is the **ohmic drop**.

### Step 2: Define SoC Based on Voltage Window

Supercapacitors are rated between $V_{\min}$ and $V_{\max}$ (typically 0.5 × $V_{\text{rated}}$ to $V_{\text{rated}}$). Define:

$$\text{SoC}_{\text{sc}}(t) = \frac{V_{\text{sc}}(t) - V_{\min}}{V_{\max} - V_{\min}}$$

### Step 3: Maximum Storable and Deliverable Energy

The available energy window is:

$$E_{\text{usable}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{\text{sc}} \left( V_{\max}^{2} - V_{\min}^{2} \right)$$

**Important:** If we naively operated from $V_{\min} = 0$ to $V_{\max}$, only $75\%$ of the total stored energy would actually be usable, because $E \propto V^{2}$. This is why industrial supercapacitor packs are typically restricted to the upper half of the voltage range.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Cycle Life Extension Quantification

The single most important *commercial* justification of HESS in EVs is the extension of battery cycle life. We derive an approximate amplification factor.

### Step 1: Empirical Battery Aging Law

A widely used empirical aging law (Laresgoiti et al.) for lithium-ion cells is:

$$L_{\text{bat}} = L_{0} \left( \frac{C_{\text{rate,op}}}{C_{\text{rate,ref}}} \right)^{-k}$$

where $L_{0}$ is the reference cycle life, $C_{\text{rate,op}}$ is the operational C-rate, and $k \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ is the aging exponent.

### Step 2: Compare With and Without HESS

**Without HESS:** the battery must deliver the full load peaks, so $C_{\text{rate,op}} = C_{\text{rate,peak}}$. Thus:

$$L_{\text{noHESS}} = L_{0} \left( \frac{C_{\text{rate,peak}}}{C_{\text{rate,ref}}} \right)^{-k}$$

**With HESS:** the LPF smooths the battery current, so $C_{\text{rate,op}} = C_{\text{rate,avg}} \ll C_{\text{rate,peak}}$. Thus:

$$L_{\text{HESS}} = L_{0} \left( \frac{C_{\text{rate,avg}}}{C_{\text{rate,ref}}} \right)^{-k}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Life-Amplification Factor

$$\alpha = \frac{L_{\text{HESS}}}{L_{\text{noHESS}}} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{rate,avg}}}{C_{\text{rate,peak}}} \right)^{-k}$$

**Example:** If peaks reach $5C$ but the smoothed average is $0.5C$, then $\alpha = (0.1)^{-1.7} \approx 50$. A battery rated for 2,000 cycles could effectively last 100,000 cycles — a dramatic system-level benefit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** This derivation is the most common "battery life extension" sub-question in 7-mark problems. Always state the assumption that $C_{\text{rate,avg}} \ll C_{\text{rate,peak}}$ and identify the *physical cause* of aging (lithium plating, SEI growth, particle cracking) for full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix for HESS Sizing

The following checklist guides the **first-pass sizing** of each storage element. It is the procedure that KTU board examiners expect in a design question.

| Sizing Step | Question to Answer | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 — Energy sizing | What is the total mission energy $E_{\text{mission}}$? | $E_{\text{HES}} \ge E_{\text{mission}} / \eta_{\text{HES}}$ |
| 2 — Power sizing | What is the peak power $P_{\text{peak}}$ and duration $T_{\text{peak}}$? | $E_{\text{peak}} = P_{\text{peak}} T_{\text{peak}}$ |
| 3 — Assign HES | Does $E_{\text{peak}} \le 0.2 E_{\text{mission}}$? | If yes, $\Rightarrow$ supercapacitor sized by $E_{\text{peak}}$ |
| 4 — Compute HPS mass | $m_{\text{sc}} = E_{\text{peak}} / E_{\text{sc}}$ | $E_{\text{sc}}$ from supercap datasheet |
| 5 — Compute HES mass | $m_{\text{bat}} = (E_{\text{mission}} - E_{\text{sc}} m_{\text{sc}})/E_{\text{bat}}$ | Ensure $m_{\text{bat}} \ge P_{\text{avg}} t_{\text{mission}}/E_{\text{bat}}$ |
| 6 — Validate | Does $m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}} \le m_{\text{max}}$? | Mass constraint from application |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are drawn at the **block-functional** level (not physical hardware layouts) for maximum KTU-board clarity. All node IDs are alphanumeric per the compilation safeguards.

## 4.1 Fully-Active HESS — Block Architecture

This is the canonical HESS topology. Two bidirectional DC-DC converters allow independent control of both storage elements.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeSrc1[BATTERY PACK] --> nodeConv1[BIDIRECTIONAL DC DC CONVERTER 1]
    nodeConv1 --> nodeBus[DC BUS]
    nodeBus --> nodeInv[DCDC AC INVERTER]
    nodeInv --> nodeLoad[AC LOAD OR GRID]
    nodeSrc2[SUPERCAPACITOR BANK] --> nodeConv2[BIDIRECTIONAL DC DC CONVERTER 2]
    nodeConv2 --> nodeBus
    nodeBus --> nodeEMS[ENERGY MANAGEMENT CONTROLLER EMS]
    nodeEMS -. control signal .-> nodeConv1
    nodeEMS -. control signal .-> nodeConv2
    nodeEMS -. sense .-> nodeBus
    nodeEMS -. sense .-> nodeLoad
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The solid arrows are **power flow paths**.
- The dotted arrows are **control and measurement signals**.
- The EMS is the brain — it samples the bus voltage, load current, and SoCs, then issues PWM duty-cycle commands to both converters.

---

## 4.2 Semi-Active HESS (Supercapacitor-Active, Battery-Passive) — EV Configuration

The most commonly deployed topology in commercial battery-supercapacitor EVs (e.g., Maxwell Technologies reference designs).

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeBat[LI ION BATTERY] --> nodeBus[DC BUS]
    nodeBus --> nodeInv[TRACTION INVERTER]
    nodeInv --> nodeMotor[TRACTION MOTOR]
    nodeSc[EDLC SUPERCAPACITOR MODULE] --> nodeConv[BIDIRECTIONAL DC DC CONVERTER]
    nodeConv --> nodeBus
    nodeRegen[REGENERATIVE BRAKING] -. charging pulse .-> nodeBus
    nodeBus -. voltage sensing .-> nodeECU[VEHICLE ECU EMS]
    nodeECU -. duty cycle .-> nodeConv
```

**Why battery is passive here:** The battery is a stiff voltage source, so it is connected directly. The DC-DC converter around the supercapacitor allows its terminal voltage to vary (1.0 V to 2.7 V per cell) while forcing a stable 350–400 V bus — a key design simplification.

---

## 4.3 Hybrid Storage with Renewable Source — Microgrid Configuration

This diagram shows a PV-wind microgrid with battery-supercapacitor HESS for ramp-rate smoothing and frequency regulation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePV[PV ARRAY] --> nodePVDCDC[PV DC DC BOOST]
    nodeWind[WIND TURBINE] --> nodeWACDC[RECTIFIER]
    nodePVDCDC --> nodeBus[DC MICROGRID BUS]
    nodeWACDC --> nodeBus
    nodeBat[LI ION BATTERY] --> nodeBatConv[BI DIRECTIONAL DC DC]
    nodeBatConv --> nodeBus
    nodeSc[SUPERCAPACITOR] --> nodeScConv[BI DIRECTIONAL DC DC]
    nodeScConv --> nodeBus
    nodeBus --> nodeInverter[GRID FORMING INVERTER]
    nodeInverter --> nodeGrid[LOCAL AC LOAD AND GRID]
    nodeBus --> nodeMgmt[MICROGRID ENERGY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM MEMS]
    nodeMgmt -. SoC sensing .-> nodeBat
    nodeMgmt -. SoC sensing .-> nodeSc
    nodeMgmt -. MPPT control .-> nodePVDCDC
    nodeMgmt -. pitch control .-> nodeWind
    nodeMgmt -. dispatch .-> nodeInverter
```

**Operating Logic:**
- The battery supplies the **steady deficit/surplus** after MPPT.
- The supercapacitor smooths the **sub-minute fluctuations** (clouds, wind gusts).
- The MEMS coordinates all four power-electronic stages.

---

## 4.4 Configuration Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ[HESS TOPOLOGY DECISION] --> nodeA1[DO YOU NEED INDEPENDENT CONTROL OF BOTH SOURCES]
    nodeA1 -- NO --> nodeB1[CHOOSE PASSIVE PARALLEL]
    nodeA1 -- YES --> nodeB2[DO YOU NEED FULL BIDIRECTIONAL CONTROL]
    nodeB2 -- NO --> nodeC1[CHOOSE SEMI ACTIVE]
    nodeB2 -- YES --> nodeC2[CHOOSE FULLY ACTIVE]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD1[WHICH SOURCE IS STIFF VOLTAGE SOURCE]
    nodeD1 -- BATTERY --> nodeE1[BATTERY PASSIVE]
    nodeD1 -- SUPERCAP --> nodeE2[SUPERCAP PASSIVE]
```

This decision tree is the **exact answer structure** KTU examiners look for in a "justify the choice of HESS configuration" question.
