# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define a Hybrid Energy Storage System and explain its necessity using the Ragone plot. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** is an engineered integration of two or more energy storage devices, coupled through power-electronic interfaces, that delivers a system-level Ragone performance (specific energy vs. specific power) which cannot be achieved by any single device.

**Necessity (3 points, ~3 marks):**
1. **Gap in the Ragone plot:** No single technology simultaneously occupies the high-energy, high-power quadrant. Batteries dominate energy density, supercapacitors dominate power density.
2. **Cycle life preservation:** A battery forced to deliver high power spikes suffers accelerated capacity fade due to lithium plating and SEI growth. HESS routes spikes to a power-dense source, multiplying battery life by 2–50×.
3. **System efficiency and thermal relief:** Power-dense storage handles short-duration transients with ~95% efficiency, reducing $I^{2}R$ losses in the battery and lowering cell temperature.

---

### Question A2: List and briefly define the three configurations of a battery-supercapacitor HESS. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Configuration | Description | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Parallel** | Both sources tied directly to the DC bus | No DC-DC converter; current shared by internal resistance |
| **Semi-Active** | One source connected via a DC-DC converter | Asymmetric control; battery-active or supercap-active variant |
| **Fully-Active** | Both sources have independent DC-DC converters | Full independent control of each source; highest flexibility |

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Battery + Supercapacitor for EV Application**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A hybrid energy storage system for a small electric vehicle consists of a **Li-ion battery** (specific energy = 150 Wh/kg, mass = 90 kg) and a **supercapacitor module** (specific energy = 6 Wh/kg, mass = 8 kg). Calculate:
- (i) the system specific energy, and
- (ii) the system specific power, given the battery specific power is 400 W/kg and supercapacitor specific power is 6,000 W/kg.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Explain, with a labelled block diagram, the **fully-active HESS topology** and state two advantages it has over the passive configuration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — HESS for Solar PV Microgrid**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
A 50 kWp solar PV plant is integrated with a HESS consisting of a **200 kWh Li-ion battery** and a **30 kWh supercapacitor bank**. During a 4-second cloud passage, the PV output drops from 50 kW to 12 kW.
- (i) Calculate the average load demand on the storage.
- (ii) Calculate the power that the supercapacitor must supply to keep the battery's discharge rate below 1C, and verify whether the supercapacitor's energy is sufficient to ride through the event.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Discuss, with a suitable diagram, the **role of the Energy Management System (EMS)** in a HESS. Mention at least two control strategies.

---

## Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a)

**Given:**
- Battery: $E_{\text{bat}} = 150$ Wh/kg, $P_{\text{bat}} = 400$ W/kg, $m_{\text{bat}} = 90$ kg
- Supercap: $E_{\text{sc}} = 6$ Wh/kg, $P_{\text{sc}} = 6000$ W/kg, $m_{\text{sc}} = 8$ kg

**(i) System Specific Energy:**

$$E_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{E_{\text{bat}} m_{\text{bat}} + E_{\text{sc}} m_{\text{sc}}}{m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{(150)(90) + (6)(8)}{90 + 8} = \frac{13500 + 48}{98} = \frac{13548}{98} \approx 138.2 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

**(ii) System Specific Power:**

$$P_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{P_{\text{bat}} m_{\text{bat}} + P_{\text{sc}} m_{\text{sc}}}{m_{\text{bat}} + m_{\text{sc}}}$$

$$P_{\text{HESS}} = \frac{(400)(90) + (6000)(8)}{98} = \frac{36000 + 48000}{98} = \frac{84000}{98} \approx 857.1 \text{ W/kg}$$

**Interpretation:** The mass-adding supercapacitor (only ~8% of the system mass) **more than doubled** the system specific power (from 400 to 857 W/kg) while only marginally reducing specific energy (from 150 to 138 Wh/kg). This is the **classic value proposition** of HESS.

#### Part (b) — Fully-Active HESS Block Diagram

```
   BATTERY  -->  [DC-DC 1]  --+
                               |
                               v
                          [ DC BUS ] --> [INVERTER] --> LOAD
                               ^
                               |
   SUPERCAP  --> [DC-DC 2]  --+

   ENERGY MANAGEMENT SYSTEM (EMS)
        |               |
        +-- control --> DC-DC 1
        +-- control --> DC-DC 2
        +-- senses  --> bus voltage, SoCs
```

**Two advantages over passive HESS:**
1. **Independent power control:** Each source's current is regulated independently by its own converter, so the EMS can precisely command the battery to deliver only the low-frequency component.
2. **Stable DC bus voltage:** The supercapacitor's terminal voltage can vary from 0.5 V to 2.7 V per cell, but the DC-DC converter holds the bus voltage constant (e.g., 400 V), regardless of SoC. In passive HESS, bus voltage droops as the supercapacitor discharges.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a)

**Given:**
- PV: 50 kWp, drops to 12 kW for 4 s
- Battery: 200 kWh, rated 1C discharge → $P_{\text{bat,max}} = 200$ kW
- Supercap: 30 kWh

**(i) Average Load on Storage (i.e., the deficit the storage must supply):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{drop}} = 50 \text{ kW} - 12 \text{ kW} = 38 \text{ kW}$$

**Energy deficit during the 4-second event:**

$$\Delta E = 38 \text{ kW} \times 4 \text{ s} = 152 \text{ kJ} = \frac{152000}{3600} \text{ Wh} \approx 42.2 \text{ Wh}$$

**(ii) Power required from supercapacitor to keep battery at 1C:**

If the battery is allowed to discharge at 1C, it can supply only 200 kW — but the deficit is just 38 kW, so technically the battery *can* handle it. The HESS is still justified because the **rate of change** is ~9.5 kW/s, which would cause thermal stress. To keep the battery at, say, 0.2C = 40 kW, the supercapacitor must supply:

$$P_{\text{sc}} = 38 \text{ kW} - 40 \text{ kW} \cdot \eta_{\text{conv}} \approx 0$$

In this case the **battery handles the average** and the **supercapacitor handles the transients** (the high-frequency components of the dip).

**Supercapacitor energy check:**
The transient has high-frequency content with energy ≈ 42.2 Wh. Available supercap energy = 30 kWh = 30,000 Wh, which is **vastly more than needed** — even using 75% of the rated energy window, ~22,500 Wh is available. **The HESS is well-sized** for this event.

#### Part (b) — Role of the EMS in HESS

The **Energy Management System (EMS)** is the supervisory controller that determines, in real time, the power split between the two storage elements. It executes two principal control strategies:

1. **Rule-Based (Deterministic) Strategy** — Uses predefined rules such as *"if supercap SoC < 30%, then all power to battery; if load > threshold, supercap takes the peak."* Simple, robust, easy to implement on a microcontroller.

2. **Frequency-Domain (Filter-Based) Strategy** — Decomposes the load power into low-frequency and high-frequency components using a low-pass filter (e.g., cutoff = 0.1 Hz). The battery follows the low-frequency component; the supercapacitor follows the high-frequency component. This is the **mathematically optimal** approach for life extension.

3. **Optimization-Based (Predictive) Strategy** — Uses Model Predictive Control (MPC) or equivalent consumption minimization strategy (ECMS) to minimize a cost function (battery aging + electricity cost) over a prediction horizon. Most powerful, but computationally intensive.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse hybrid with redundancy.** Two banks of the *same* battery in parallel is NOT a hybrid system. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you write "hybrid means backup."
> 2. **Always state the Ragone plot in your answer.** It is the *first* thing the examiner looks for. Even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for it, mentioning it demonstrates conceptual depth.
> 3. **Distinguish "configuration" from "topology."** Configuration = which device has the converter (passive/semi-active/fully-active). Topology = the circuit (e.g., interleaved boost, cascaded buck-boost). Do not use them interchangeably.
> 4. **Numerical questions: always include units and show intermediate steps.** A 7-mark problem demands ~5 distinct steps. Skipping the power-balance equation loses 2 marks.
> 5. **For lifecycle extension questions, always specify the aging mechanism** (lithium plating, particle cracking, SEI growth). Generic statements like "battery gets damaged" score zero.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **HESS = high-energy device + high-power device** (different technologies, not duplicates).
- **Ragone plot** = the map of hybridization rationale; HESS fills the upper-right gap.
- **Three configurations:** Passive (no converter), Semi-Active (one converter), Fully-Active (two converters). Each is a trade-off of cost, efficiency, and control.
- **Power balance law:** $P_{\text{load}} = P_{\text{HES}} + P_{\text{HPS}}$ holds at every instant.
- **LPF power-split strategy** assigns low frequencies to the battery and high frequencies to the supercapacitor; cutoff $f_{\text{c}} = 1/(2\pi R C)$.
- **Cycle-life extension factor** $\alpha = (C_{\text{avg}}/C_{\text{peak}})^{-k}$ with $k \approx 1.5$–$2.0$.
- **Supercapacitor usable energy** is only 75% of full nameplate if operated over the full voltage window; restrict to upper half in practice.
- **Specific energy & power are mass-weighted averages** for the combined system.
- **Top applications:** EVs, PV microgrids, wind farm smoothing, railway regen, data-centre UPS, mining equipment.
- **Always cite both the physical and economic benefit** in exam answers (life extension + efficiency + thermal relief).
- **Most-tested KTU questions:** (1) configuration comparison table, (2) specific energy/power numerical, (3) LPF cutoff design, (4) cycle-life extension derivation.
